Periarticular osteopenia with marginal (not central) erosions at the MCPs is the hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis. Punched-out erosions with overhanging edges and preserved joint space describe gout. Bony ankylosis of the sacroiliac joints belongs to ankylosing spondylitis. The central erosion/gull-wing pattern at the DIP/PIP joints is the radiographic signature that separates erosive OA from these mimics.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 22 mm/hr
CRP 0.8 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP antibody: negative
ANA: negative
Serum urate 5.1 mg/dL
Hand radiographs: central subchondral erosions with marginal osteophytes producing a gull-wing appearance at multiple DIP and PIP joints; first CMC joint-space narrowing with subchondral sclerosis; MCP joints and wrists preserved without marginal erosions or periarticular osteopenia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on the clinical picture and these results, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Initiate allopurinol to target a serum urate below 6 mg/dL as initial therapy
Begin topical NSAIDs
Start high-dose prednisone taper for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Start methotrexate with folic acid as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For hand OA, including the erosive variant, first-line therapy is topical NSAIDs plus oral analgesics (acetaminophen), combined with hand exercises, joint protection, and education; oral NSAIDs are used for flares when topical measures are insufficient. The negative RF/anti-CCP and absence of MCP/wrist synovitis argue against RA, so methotrexate is not indicated. Normal serum urate and gull-wing (not punched-out) erosions exclude gout, so allopurinol is inappropriate. A prolonged high-dose prednisone taper exposes her to steroid toxicity without disease-modifying benefit in OA. Conservative topical and physical measures are the evidence-based starting point.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next year she develops persistent pain and instability at the base of the right thumb (first CMC joint) limiting pinch and grip, despite splinting and topical NSAIDs. What is the most appropriate next step for this specific joint?
options: Offer an intra-articular corticosteroid injection of the first CMC joint
Begin a biologic TNF inhibitor as the leading concern
Start oral methotrexate to halt erosions as the leading concern
Refer for first CMC fusion as the immediate first procedure
stages.explanation: When localized first CMC OA remains symptomatic despite splinting and topical NSAIDs, an intra-articular corticosteroid injection is a reasonable next step that can provide meaningful short-term relief and is far less invasive than surgery. TNF inhibitors and methotrexate treat inflammatory arthritides like RA, not OA, and would not address mechanical thumb-base disease. Surgical reconstruction or fusion of the first CMC is effective but reserved for refractory cases after conservative options including injection have failed; it is not the immediate first procedure. Escalating to a targeted injection appropriately matches a single refractory joint.

pearls: Hand OA targets the DIP (Heberden nodes), PIP (Bouchard nodes), and first CMC joints while sparing the MCPs and wrists; RA does the reverse.
Erosive OA shows central subchondral erosions with marginal osteophytes producing a 'gull-wing' sign, distinct from the marginal erosions of RA and the overhanging-edge erosions of gout.
RF, anti-CCP, and serum urate are typically normal in erosive OA and help exclude RA and gout when flares are inflammatory.
First-line treatment is topical NSAIDs, acetaminophen, hand exercises, and joint protection; intra-articular steroid injection helps a refractory first CMC joint.

cases.title: Recurrent Flank Pain With Blood in the Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of severe right flank pain radiating to the groin with visible blood in the urine.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents for follow-up after his third episode in two years of severe, colicky right flank pain radiating to the groin, with gross hematuria. Each prior episode resolved after he passed a small stone; one was sent for analysis. He has noticed persistently low urine output and concentrated urine because of a physically demanding outdoor job with poor hydration. He follows a high-protein, low-carbohydrate weight-loss diet rich in meat. Past medical history: obesity (BMI 34), type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily), gout treated only during flares, and chronic diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel. Medications: metformin, occasional colchicine. Allergies: none. Social: drinks one to two beers nightly, eats organ meats and seafood, sedentary outside of work. Family history: father had kidney stones. Review of systems: no fever, dysuria, or urinary frequency; reports occasional first-MTP joint pain. He drinks little water during the workday.
cases.exam: Obese, well-appearing man in no acute distress today. Mild right costovertebral angle tenderness without rebound. Abdomen soft, nontender, no organomegaly. No suprapubic tenderness. Genitourinary exam normal. No flank ecchymosis. First metatarsophalangeal joints with mild bony prominence but no active warmth, redness, or effusion today. No tophi. Remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: pH 5.2, 2+ blood, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites; numerous RBCs, no crystals seen this visit
Serum urate 9.2 mg/dL
Serum creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Serum calcium 9.4 mg/dL
Non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis: 6-mm stone in the right proximal ureter; bilateral nonobstructing renal calculi
Stone Hounsfield density: ~480 HU (relatively low)
Prior stone analysis: 100% uric acid
KUB plain radiograph: no radiopaque calculus visualized

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best explains why this patient's stones are radiolucent on plain radiograph yet visible on CT?
options: Cystine composition due to a tubular transport defect
Struvite composition associated with urease-producing infection
Uric acid composition with persistently acidic urine
Calcium oxalate composition with alkaline urine
stages.explanation: Uric acid stones are radiolucent on plain films (not seen on KUB) but are readily detected on non-contrast CT, which detects nearly all stone types; their formation is driven by persistently acidic urine, where uric acid is poorly soluble. This patient's urine pH of 5.2, serum urate of 9.2 mg/dL, low-attenuation stone (~480 HU), and prior 100% uric acid analysis all fit. Calcium oxalate stones are radiopaque and do not require acidic urine. Struvite stones arise with urease-producing infections and alkaline urine, with no infection here. Cystine stones occur in cystinuria and are faintly radiopaque with characteristic crystals, not seen here.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His 6-mm proximal ureteral stone is causing intermittent symptoms but no obstruction, infection, or acute kidney injury. What is the most appropriate initial management of the acute stone?
options: Immediate shockwave lithotripsy as the first step today
Open surgical stone removal as the first step today
Emergent percutaneous nephrostomy as the first step today
Conservative management with hydration, analgesia,
stages.explanation: A 6-mm ureteral stone without obstruction, infection, or AKI can often pass; conservative management with hydration, analgesia, and medical expulsive therapy using an alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) improves passage rates for distal-to-mid stones up to about 10 mm. Emergent nephrostomy is reserved for obstruction with infection or deteriorating renal function (urologic emergency). Shockwave lithotripsy is notoriously ineffective for radiolucent uric acid stones, which are also amenable to dissolution therapy. Open surgery is rarely needed and is not first-line. Trial of passage with expulsive therapy is the appropriate initial strategy for this uncomplicated stone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medical therapy is most specific and effective for both dissolving existing uric acid stones and preventing recurrence in this patient?
options: Urinary alkalinization with potassium citrate to a target urine pH of 6.5-7.0
Acetazolamide to acidify the urine as initial therapy
Thiazide diuretic to reduce urinary calcium as initial therapy
Increasing dietary calcium restriction as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Uric acid solubility is exquisitely pH-dependent, so urinary alkalinization with potassium citrate to a urine pH of 6.5-7.0 both dissolves existing uric acid stones and prevents new ones; this is the cornerstone of therapy. Thiazides reduce urinary calcium and help calcium stones, not uric acid stones. Acetazolamide alkalinizes the urine but causes a systemic metabolic acidosis that paradoxically promotes uric acid stones and is not used here. Calcium restriction is counterproductive in stone disease generally and irrelevant to uric acid stones. Alkalinization with citrate, plus hydration and dietary measures, is the targeted approach.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite alkalinization, hydration, and dietary purine reduction, he forms another uric acid stone and his serum urate remains 9.0 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add allopurinol titrated to lower serum urate
Restrict all dietary protein to near zero in this setting
Start probenecid to increase uric acid excretion
Begin pegloticase infusions as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When alkalinization, hydration, and dietary measures fail to prevent recurrent uric acid stones in a hyperuricemic patient, adding allopurinol (a xanthine oxidase inhibitor) lowers serum and urinary uric acid and reduces stone formation, and also treats his gout. Probenecid is uricosuric, increasing urinary uric acid excretion, which raises stone risk and is contraindicated in uric acid nephrolithiasis. Pegloticase is reserved for severe refractory tophaceous gout, not uncomplicated stone prevention. Near-zero protein restriction is impractical, nutritionally harmful, and unnecessary when xanthine oxidase inhibition is available. Allopurinol is the appropriate escalation.

pearls: Uric acid stones are radiolucent on plain film (KUB) but visible on non-contrast CT, which is the imaging gold standard for nephrolithiasis.
Persistently acidic urine (pH < 5.5) is the key driver of uric acid stone formation; alkalinization with potassium citrate to pH 6.5-7.0 dissolves and prevents these stones.
Shockwave lithotripsy is poorly effective for uric acid stones; pharmacologic dissolution is preferred.
Allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor) is added when alkalinization and diet fail; avoid uricosurics like probenecid, which increase urinary uric acid and stone risk.

cases.title: Fever and One-Sided Buttock Pain After Childbirth
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever and severe right buttock pain that began three weeks after a vaginal delivery.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman, 3 weeks postpartum after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery, is admitted with 6 days of progressively severe right buttock and low-back pain and 3 days of fevers to 39 °C with rigors. The pain is constant, worse with weight-bearing and any movement of the right hip, and disturbs her sleep. She limps and can barely climb stairs. She denies dysuria, vaginal discharge, abdominal pain, or trauma. Her pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated aside from a brief labor epidural. Past medical history: none. Medications: prenatal vitamins; ibuprofen with minimal relief. Allergies: none. Social: married, nonsmoker, no alcohol, no injection drug use. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: notes night sweats and reduced appetite; no rash, eye redness, oral ulcers, diarrhea, or psoriasis. No recent travel. She is breastfeeding and has had no recent antibiotics.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic young woman lying still, reluctant to move the right hip. Marked tenderness over the right sacroiliac joint with a positive FABER (Patrick) test and positive sacroiliac compression and thigh-thrust provocation tests reproducing buttock pain. Pain with resisted hip abduction. No midline spinal tenderness. No overlying erythema or fluctuance. Neurologic exam intact: 5/5 strength, normal sensation, symmetric reflexes, negative straight-leg raise. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Lochia is normal; no uterine tenderness. Heart with tachycardia but no murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given unilateral sacroiliac pain with high fever and rigors, which is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic priority?
options: Order HLA-B27 and refer to rheumatology clinic as an outpatient as the first step today
Initiate physical therapy for presumed sacroiliac joint dysfunction as the first step today
Obtain blood cultures, inflammatory markers,
Start an NSAID and reassess in 48 hours assuming mechanical postpartum pain
stages.explanation: Unilateral sacroiliitis with high fever and rigors is septic until proven otherwise; the immediate priority is to obtain blood cultures, inflammatory markers (CBC, ESR, CRP), and urgent cross-sectional imaging (MRI) to confirm infection and identify a drainable collection. Treating empirically as mechanical pain with an NSAID risks missing a rapidly progressive joint and bone infection. HLA-B27 and outpatient rheumatology referral are appropriate for chronic inflammatory back pain, not an acutely febrile patient. Physical therapy for presumed dysfunction would dangerously delay diagnosis. The systemic toxicity mandates an urgent infection workup before any anti-inflammatory or rehabilitative path.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
ESR 78 mm/hr
CRP 142 mg/L
Procalcitonin 2.4 ng/mL
Blood cultures x2: pending
Urinalysis: unremarkable
MRI pelvis with and without contrast: right sacroiliac joint effusion with bone marrow edema across the joint, periarticular myositis, and a small adjacent fluid collection; findings consistent with septic sacroiliitis
HLA-B27: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which step is most important to identify the causative organism and guide targeted therapy before starting antibiotics, when feasible?
options: Empiric antibiotics immediately without any cultures as the primary diagnosis
Blood cultures and, when feasible, image-guided aspiration/biopsy of the sacroiliac joint for Gram stain
Serologic testing for HLA-B27 to confirm the diagnosis as the primary diagnosis
Synovial fluid crystal analysis from the knee as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Microbiologic confirmation drives targeted therapy: blood cultures plus image-guided aspiration or biopsy of the sacroiliac joint for Gram stain and culture maximize the chance of identifying the organism (Staphylococcus aureus is most common). Whenever the patient is stable, obtaining cultures before antibiotics avoids sterilizing the sample. Empiric antibiotics without any cultures forfeit the opportunity for an organism-specific regimen. HLA-B27 tests for spondyloarthropathy and does not identify a pathogen. Knee synovial fluid crystal analysis is irrelevant to a sacroiliac infection. Securing cultures from blood and the joint before antibiotics, when the patient permits, is the key diagnostic step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Cultures are obtained and she remains hemodynamically stable. Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate while awaiting culture results?
options: Oral doxycycline alone as initial therapy
No antibiotics until final culture results return as initial therapy
Oral fluconazole as initial therapy
Intravenous vancomycin plus an antipseudomonal beta-lactam pending cultures
stages.explanation: Empiric coverage for septic sacroiliitis must reliably cover Staphylococcus aureus, including MRSA, the leading pathogen; IV vancomycin provides MRSA coverage, and adding a broad-spectrum beta-lactam such as cefepime covers gram-negatives until cultures return, then therapy is narrowed. Oral doxycycline alone is inadequate empiric therapy for a deep musculoskeletal infection with bacteremia risk. Fluconazole targets fungi, which are not the typical cause. Withholding antibiotics until final cultures in a septic, febrile patient is unsafe and risks progression to abscess or osteomyelitis. Prompt broad IV therapy with subsequent de-escalation is the standard approach.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Blood and joint cultures grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus and she defervesces on therapy. Which plan best reflects appropriate definitive management and follow-up?
options: Two days of IV antibiotics then stop with no follow-up with appropriate follow-up
Begin a TNF inhibitor for the bone marrow edema with appropriate follow-up
Switch to lifelong suppressive antibiotics regardless of response with appropriate follow-up
Narrow to an antistaphylococcal agent , continue a prolonged course guided by response
stages.explanation: With MSSA confirmed, therapy is narrowed to an antistaphylococcal beta-lactam (cefazolin or nafcillin) and continued as a prolonged course (commonly several weeks, often transitioning IV-to-oral with infectious disease input) guided by clinical response and trending ESR/CRP, with surgical drainage only if an abscess fails to resolve. A two-day course is far too short for deep musculoskeletal infection and would relapse. Lifelong suppression is unnecessary for a curable acute infection. A TNF inhibitor treats inflammatory spondyloarthritis, not infection, and would be harmful here by impairing host defense. Targeted, sufficiently long therapy with monitored follow-up is correct.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On day 5 her fever recurs and CRP rises despite appropriate antibiotics. Repeat MRI shows an enlarging periarticular abscess. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange image-guided or surgical drainage of the abscess in addition to continued antibiotics
Continue the same antibiotics unchanged and observe as the leading concern
Add an oral NSAID and discharge home as the leading concern
Stop all antibiotics and start corticosteroids as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Failure to improve with an enlarging abscess indicates inadequate source control; the appropriate step is image-guided or surgical drainage of the collection while continuing antibiotics. Antibiotics alone cannot reliably penetrate and sterilize a walled-off abscess, so continuing unchanged or discharging the patient invites further deterioration and bone destruction. Adding an NSAID and sending her home ignores the source-control problem. Stopping antibiotics and starting corticosteroids would suppress her immune response against an active bacterial infection and is dangerous. Drainage plus continued targeted antibiotics restores source control and is the correct intervention.

pearls: Unilateral sacroiliitis with fever and rigors is septic arthritis of the SI joint until proven otherwise; the postpartum period and injection drug use are risk factors.
Provocative tests (FABER, sacroiliac compression, thigh-thrust) localize pain to the SI joint; contrast MRI shows joint effusion, bone marrow edema, and any abscess.
Obtain blood cultures and image-guided joint aspiration/biopsy before antibiotics when feasible; Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism.
Treat with prolonged organism-directed antibiotics (empiric vancomycin plus a broad beta-lactam, narrowed once cultures return); drain abscesses for source control.

cases.title: Months of Morning Back Stiffness in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Eight months of low back and buttock pain with prolonged morning stiffness that improves with activity.
cases.history: A 31-year-old graphic designer reports 8 months of insidious low back and alternating buttock pain. The pain is worse in the second half of the night, wakes her around 4 a.m., and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. Symptoms improve with exercise and a hot shower and worsen with prolonged sitting. She tried ibuprofen 600 mg, which helped substantially. Past medical history: an episode of unilateral painful red eye 1 year ago diagnosed as anterior uveitis; mild intermittent diarrhea. Medications: ibuprofen as needed. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional wine, sedentary job. Family history: father has ankylosing spondylitis; an uncle has psoriasis. Review of systems: occasional heel pain at the Achilles insertion and a swollen toe last year that resolved. No recent infection, no urethritis, no oral ulcers, no significant weight loss. She has had no trauma and reports the pain has lasted well beyond three months.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile. Tenderness over both sacroiliac joints with a positive FABER test; mild reduction in lumbar flexion with a modestly reduced modified Schober test. Tenderness at the right Achilles insertion (enthesitis) and mild fullness of one toe consistent with prior dactylitis. Chest expansion is normal. Peripheral joints without active synovitis. No psoriatic skin plaques or nail changes appreciated today. Neurologic exam normal with negative straight-leg raise. No spinal step-off or deformity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation best characterizes her back pain as inflammatory rather than mechanical?
options: Insidious onset before age 40, morning stiffness > 30 minutes, improvement with exercise,
Pain only with prolonged standing, fully relieved by sitting as the first step today
Acute onset after lifting, radiation below the knee, worse with coughing as the first step today
Onset after age 50, relief with rest, no morning stiffness as the first step today
stages.explanation: Inflammatory back pain features include insidious onset before age 40, duration over 3 months, morning stiffness exceeding 30 minutes, improvement with exercise but not rest, and night pain (often in the second half of the night) that eases on getting up. This patient fits all of these. Onset after 50 with relief by rest and no stiffness describes mechanical/degenerative pain. Acute onset after lifting with radiation below the knee and worsening with cough/Valsalva suggests discogenic radiculopathy. Pain only with standing relieved by sitting suggests spinal stenosis. Her pattern is classically inflammatory, prompting evaluation for axial spondyloarthritis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CRP 14 mg/L (elevated)
ESR 32 mm/hr
HLA-B27: positive
Rheumatoid factor: negative
ANA: negative
Anteroposterior pelvis radiograph: sacroiliac joints without definite erosions, sclerosis, or ankylosis (normal/non-diagnostic)
MRI sacroiliac joints: bone marrow edema (osteitis) in the subchondral bone of both sacroiliac joints, highly suggestive of active sacroiliitis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Plain pelvic radiographs are normal but the MRI shows active sacroiliitis with a positive HLA-B27 and inflammatory features. Which diagnosis best fits?
options: Ankylosing spondylitis (radiographic axial spondyloarthritis)
Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis
Mechanical low back pain as the primary diagnosis
Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis
stages.explanation: Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis is defined by clinical features of axial spondyloarthritis with active sacroiliitis on MRI (bone marrow edema) but without definite structural damage on plain radiographs; HLA-B27 positivity, inflammatory back pain, uveitis, enthesitis, and dactylitis support it. Ankylosing spondylitis (radiographic axSpA) requires definite radiographic sacroiliitis, which her normal pelvic film excludes. Mechanical low back pain does not produce MRI osteitis, elevated CRP, or these extra-articular features. Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis causes flowing anterolateral ossification in older patients with preserved SI joints and no inflammation. The normal radiograph plus MRI-active sacroiliitis defines the non-radiographic form.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her active non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis?
options: A scheduled NSAID at an adequate dose combined with a structured exercise/physiotherapy program
Low-dose oral prednisone indefinitely as initial therapy
Immediate initiation of a TNF inhibitor before any NSAID trial as initial therapy
Oral methotrexate as monotherapy
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for axial spondyloarthritis is a continuous (scheduled) NSAID at an adequate anti-inflammatory dose plus a structured exercise and physiotherapy program; many patients respond well, as she did to ibuprofen. Methotrexate and other conventional DMARDs are ineffective for axial disease (useful only for peripheral arthritis). Chronic systemic corticosteroids are not recommended for axial spondyloarthritis given limited efficacy and toxicity. A TNF inhibitor is reserved for patients with persistent high disease activity despite an adequate trial of at least two NSAIDs, not as the initial step. Optimized NSAID therapy with exercise is the correct starting point.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After adequate trials of two different NSAIDs over several months she still has high disease activity and night pain. Considering her history of recurrent anterior uveitis, which biologic class is the most appropriate next step?
options: A monoclonal-antibody TNF inhibitor (e.g., adalimumab)
Rituximab as the immediate next step
An oral conventional DMARD such as sulfasalazine for the axial disease
An IL-17 inhibitor as the preferred agent given her uveitis
stages.explanation: When axial spondyloarthritis remains highly active despite adequate trials of two NSAIDs, a biologic is indicated; in a patient with recurrent anterior uveitis, a monoclonal-antibody TNF inhibitor (e.g., adalimumab) is preferred because TNF inhibitors reduce uveitis flares, whereas IL-17 inhibitors are generally less protective and may be less favorable for recurrent uveitis. Rituximab (anti-CD20) is not effective for axial spondyloarthritis. Sulfasalazine can help peripheral arthritis but does not control axial disease. Selecting a TNF monoclonal antibody addresses both her spinal inflammation and her extra-articular uveitis, making it the best next step.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before starting the biologic, which screening test is most essential to obtain?
options: Colonoscopy as the leading concern
Screening for latent tuberculosis
Echocardiogram as the leading concern
Baseline DXA bone density scan as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Before initiating a TNF inhibitor, screening for latent tuberculosis with an interferon-gamma release assay or tuberculin skin test (plus consideration of hepatitis B serologies) is essential, because TNF blockade can reactivate latent TB into disseminated disease. A baseline DXA is reasonable in spondyloarthritis given osteoporosis risk but is not the critical pre-biologic safety screen. An echocardiogram is not routinely required and is more relevant when considering TNF inhibitors in heart failure. Colonoscopy may be considered if inflammatory bowel disease is suspected but is not the essential biologic-safety screen. Latent TB screening is the mandatory step before TNF inhibitor therapy.

pearls: Inflammatory back pain: insidious onset before 40, duration > 3 months, morning stiffness > 30 minutes, improvement with exercise (not rest), and night pain easing on arising.
Non-radiographic axSpA shows active sacroiliitis (bone marrow edema) on MRI with normal plain radiographs; HLA-B27, uveitis, enthesitis, and dactylitis support the diagnosis.
First-line therapy is scheduled NSAIDs plus exercise; conventional DMARDs (methotrexate) do not work for axial disease, and a TNF inhibitor follows failure of two NSAIDs.
Prefer a monoclonal TNF inhibitor when recurrent anterior uveitis coexists, and always screen for latent tuberculosis before starting any TNF inhibitor.

cases.title: A Young Girl With Trouble Climbing Stairs and a Facial Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive difficulty climbing stairs and getting up from the floor, with a violet rash around the eyes.
cases.history: An 8-year-old previously healthy girl is brought in by her parents for 3 months of progressive difficulty climbing stairs, standing from the floor, and lifting her backpack overhead. She tires easily, has become reluctant to play, and her parents note she now uses her hands to 'walk up' her legs when rising. Over the same period she developed a violet-pink rash around her eyes and red, scaly bumps over her knuckles. She has mild difficulty swallowing dry foods and a nasal-quality voice at times. No fever currently, no joint swelling, no Raynaud phenomenon. Past medical history: unremarkable; immunizations up to date. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: second grade, lives with both parents and a sibling; no sick contacts; no recent travel. Family history: no autoimmune disease. Review of systems: low-grade fatigue, occasional low-grade fevers earlier in the course, no rash photosensitivity reported, normal bowel and bladder, no recent infections. She has had no recent medication exposures and no muscle pain at rest, though she reports tenderness with activity.
cases.exam: Alert child with a violaceous (heliotrope) rash over the upper eyelids and erythematous scaly papules over the MCP and PIP knuckles (Gottron papules). Symmetric proximal muscle weakness: hip flexors and shoulder abductors 3/5, with preserved distal strength (5/5 grip and ankle dorsiflexion). Positive Gower sign. Mild neck-flexor weakness. Nailfold capillaries show dilated loops. No active arthritis. A few firm subcutaneous nodules over the elbows and knees (early calcinosis). Palate elevates symmetrically; gag intact. Reflexes normal; sensation intact. No hepatosplenomegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings most strongly suggests an inflammatory myopathy with a characteristic cutaneous syndrome in this child?
options: Episodic weakness triggered by exercise that resolves at rest
Asymmetric weakness with fasciculations and tongue atrophy
Distal weakness with a malar rash and oral ulcers
Symmetric proximal weakness with a heliotrope rash
stages.explanation: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness combined with a heliotrope (violaceous periorbital) rash and Gottron papules over the knuckles is the hallmark of juvenile dermatomyositis. The proximal pattern (difficulty with stairs, rising from the floor, overhead tasks, positive Gower sign) plus these pathognomonic skin findings is highly specific. Distal weakness with a malar rash and oral ulcers fits lupus rather than a primary myopathy. Asymmetric weakness with fasciculations and tongue atrophy suggests a motor neuron disorder. Episodic exertional weakness resolving at rest suggests a metabolic myopathy or channelopathy. The proximal-weakness-plus-classic-rash combination directs you to dermatomyositis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatine kinase 1,850 U/L (elevated)
Aldolase: elevated
AST 96 U/L, ALT 78 U/L (with normal GGT)
LDH: elevated
ESR 30 mm/hr, CRP mildly elevated
ANA: positive 1:160
Myositis-specific antibodies: anti-NXP2 positive
MRI thighs (T2/STIR): symmetric proximal muscle edema (myositis), no focal mass
EMG: irritable myopathy with low-amplitude short-duration motor units

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the classic skin findings, proximal weakness, elevated muscle enzymes, and MRI muscle edema, what is the most appropriate way to confirm the diagnosis with least morbidity?
options: Genetic testing for dystrophinopathy as the primary diagnosis
Mandatory open muscle biopsy in all cases before treatment as the primary diagnosis
Repeat ANA in 6 months before any diagnosis is made as the primary diagnosis
The combination of pathognomonic rash , proximal weakness, elevated muscle enzymes, myositis-specific antibody,
stages.explanation: When a child has pathognomonic skin findings (heliotrope rash and Gottron papules), symmetric proximal weakness, elevated muscle enzymes, a myositis-specific antibody, and MRI muscle edema, the diagnosis of juvenile dermatomyositis can be made clinically, and muscle biopsy is reserved for atypical or diagnostically uncertain cases. Routine mandatory open biopsy adds morbidity without changing management in classic presentations. Genetic testing for dystrophinopathy targets muscular dystrophy, which lacks these inflammatory skin findings and antibodies. Delaying diagnosis to repeat the ANA is inappropriate when the clinical picture is already definitive and treatment should not be postponed. The noninvasive composite is sufficient here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment for active juvenile dermatomyositis?
options: NSAIDs as monotherapy
Systemic corticosteroids combined with methotrexate as a steroid-sparing agent
Observation with physical therapy only as initial therapy
Topical corticosteroids alone for the rash as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Active juvenile dermatomyositis is treated with systemic corticosteroids to rapidly control inflammation, combined early with methotrexate as a steroid-sparing agent to improve outcomes and limit steroid toxicity; intravenous immunoglobulin is added for refractory or severe disease. Topical steroids treat only the skin and ignore the dangerous muscle and systemic inflammation. NSAID monotherapy is grossly inadequate for an autoimmune myopathy. Observation with physical therapy alone risks progressive weakness, dysphagia, and calcinosis. Although physical therapy is an important adjunct, the core treatment is systemic immunosuppression with steroids plus methotrexate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next two years she develops firm, tender subcutaneous deposits over pressure points that occasionally ulcerate and extrude chalky material. Which statement best guides management of this complication?
options: It indicates a drug allergy to methotrexate and mandates stopping all immunosuppression as the leading concern
Calcinosis cutis is associated with inadequately controlled or delayed-treatment disease
It is a benign cosmetic issue requiring no change in therapy as the leading concern
It represents disease relapse requiring only topical antibiotics as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Calcinosis cutis (subcutaneous calcium deposits that may ulcerate and extrude chalky material) is a hallmark complication of juvenile dermatomyositis, strongly linked to inadequately controlled or delayed-treatment disease; the best strategy is aggressive control of the underlying inflammation to prevent progression, with surgical excision reserved for selected symptomatic lesions. It is not merely a skin relapse cured by topical antibiotics, nor a purely cosmetic issue to ignore, since lesions cause pain, ulceration, and infection. It does not signify methotrexate allergy, and stopping immunosuppression would worsen the disease driving calcinosis. Optimizing disease control is the key principle.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which symptom in a child with juvenile dermatomyositis should prompt the most urgent evaluation because it signals a potentially life-threatening complication?
options: Transient morning stiffness as the immediate next step
Mild knuckle rash flare as the immediate next step
Mild elevation of liver transaminases as the immediate next step
New dysphagia with nasal regurgitation or aspiration, or progressive shortness of breath
stages.explanation: New dysphagia with nasal regurgitation or signs of aspiration (from pharyngeal/respiratory muscle involvement) and progressive dyspnea (possible interstitial lung disease or respiratory muscle weakness) are red flags signaling potentially life-threatening complications that demand urgent evaluation and intensified therapy, including airway/aspiration precautions. A mild knuckle rash flare reflects cutaneous activity but is not immediately dangerous. Transient morning stiffness is nonspecific. Mild transaminase elevation often reflects muscle (not liver) injury and is expected; it is monitored but not an emergency. Bulbar and respiratory involvement carry the highest acute risk and must be prioritized.

pearls: Juvenile dermatomyositis combines symmetric proximal weakness (positive Gower sign) with pathognomonic skin findings: heliotrope rash and Gottron papules.
Elevated CK/aldolase, a positive myositis-specific antibody, MRI muscle edema, and classic skin findings allow clinical diagnosis; biopsy is reserved for atypical cases.
Treat with systemic corticosteroids plus early methotrexate as a steroid-sparing agent; IVIG is added for severe or refractory disease.
Calcinosis cutis reflects undertreated disease, while new dysphagia, aspiration, or dyspnea signal dangerous bulbar/respiratory involvement requiring urgent care.

cases.title: Rapidly Worsening Breathlessness With Skin Ulcers
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of rapidly worsening shortness of breath with painful skin ulcers and minimal muscle complaints.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman is transferred to the ICU with 3 weeks of rapidly progressive dyspnea, now requiring high-flow oxygen, accompanied by dry cough and low-grade fevers. Over 2 months she developed painful ulcerations over the knuckles and elbows, tender red papules on the palms, and a violaceous rash on the eyelids, but she denies significant muscle weakness and continues most daily activities. She has arthralgias of the hands. Past medical history: hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine. Allergies: none. Social: never smoked, works as a teacher, no occupational dust exposure, no recent travel. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: marked exertional dyspnea, dry cough, mouth ulcers, hair thinning, weight loss of 4 kg; no chest pain, hemoptysis, or orthopnea. She had no preceding upper respiratory illness and no new medications. Her dyspnea has accelerated sharply over the last week despite outpatient antibiotics.
cases.exam: Tachypneic woman using accessory muscles. Fine bibasilar 'Velcro' inspiratory crackles. Cutaneous ulcerations over the dorsal MCP joints and elbows; tender erythematous palmar papules; heliotrope discoloration of the eyelids; periungual erythema with dilated nailfold capillaries. Notably, muscle strength is near normal (5/5 proximally and distally) with minimal tenderness. No synovitis on exam. No murmurs; jugular venous pressure not elevated. No lower-extremity edema. Mild oral ulceration noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How would you best characterize this clinical pattern of prominent dermatomyositis skin findings with little or no muscle weakness?
options: Clinically amyopathic dermatomyositis
Polymyositis with severe proximal weakness
Drug-induced necrotizing myopathy
Inclusion body myositis
stages.explanation: The picture of characteristic dermatomyositis skin findings (heliotrope rash, Gottron lesions, cutaneous ulcers, palmar papules) with little or no clinical muscle weakness is clinically amyopathic dermatomyositis. Polymyositis presents with prominent proximal weakness and lacks the dermatomyositis rash. Inclusion body myositis is a slowly progressive disorder of older adults featuring distal and asymmetric weakness (finger flexors, quadriceps) without these skin findings. Drug-induced necrotizing myopathy causes weakness with markedly elevated CK, again without the dermatomyositis rash and not amyopathic. Recognizing the amyopathic phenotype is critical because it carries a high risk of rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatine kinase: 110 U/L (near normal)
Aldolase: mildly elevated
Ferritin: 2,400 ng/mL (markedly elevated)
LDH: elevated
ANA: positive
Myositis-specific antibody panel: anti-MDA5 positive
High-resolution chest CT: bilateral ground-glass opacities with subpleural and lower-lobe consolidation and early traction changes, progressing from a study 2 weeks ago
PaO2/FiO2 ratio: 180 (impaired)
Blood and respiratory cultures: pending; procalcitonin low

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature of this case carries the most ominous prognostic significance and should drive aggressive management?
options: The presence of arthralgias as the primary diagnosis
Mild hypothyroidism as the primary diagnosis
Anti-MDA5 positivity with rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease
The near-normal creatine kinase as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Anti-MDA5-positive dermatomyositis, especially in the clinically amyopathic form, is associated with rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease; a markedly elevated ferritin further predicts poor outcomes and mortality, making this the most ominous feature and the driver of aggressive, early immunosuppression. A near-normal CK is actually typical of amyopathic disease and does not indicate mild illness; it can falsely reassure. Arthralgias are common but not prognostically dominant. Hypothyroidism is incidental. Recognizing the lethal potential of anti-MDA5 disease with rapidly progressive ILD and hyperferritinemia is essential to prompt urgent treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial immunosuppressive strategy for anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis with rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease?
options: Low-dose oral prednisone alone with outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
High-dose corticosteroids combined with additional immunosuppression , with early multidisciplinary escalation
NSAIDs and supplemental oxygen only as initial therapy
Empiric long-term antibiotics without immunosuppression as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Rapidly progressive ILD in anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis demands aggressive, combined immunosuppression: high-dose corticosteroids plus additional agents such as a calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus) and/or cyclophosphamide, with early escalation (often including IVIG or rituximab and consideration of advanced therapies) given the high mortality. Low-dose prednisone alone with outpatient follow-up is dangerously insufficient for this fulminant process. NSAIDs and oxygen alone do not treat the underlying autoimmune lung injury. Empiric antibiotics without immunosuppression misses the diagnosis; while infection must be excluded and treated if present, the low procalcitonin and progressive ground-glass changes point to disease-driven ILD requiring immunosuppression.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite combination immunosuppression, her oxygenation worsens with a falling PaO2/FiO2 ratio and diffuse infiltrates. Which is the most appropriate next consideration?
options: Intensify therapy , provide ICU-level respiratory support,
Stop all immunosuppression and switch to comfort care immediately as the leading concern
Begin high-dose inhaled bronchodilators as definitive treatment as the leading concern
Add a statin to reduce inflammation as the leading concern
stages.explanation: When anti-MDA5 ILD progresses despite combination therapy, the appropriate path is to intensify immunosuppression (e.g., add rituximab or further escalate), deliver maximal ICU respiratory support, and evaluate for advanced rescue options such as extracorporeal membrane oxygenation as a bridge and lung transplant referral at expert centers. Abruptly abandoning treatment for comfort care is premature when rescue options remain. Inhaled bronchodilators treat airway obstruction, not interstitial inflammation/fibrosis, and are not definitive here. Statins do not treat this ILD and can themselves cause myopathy, so they are inappropriate. Aggressive escalation with consideration of advanced support best matches this refractory, high-mortality disease.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before and during intensive immunosuppression for this patient, which monitoring/prophylaxis step is most important?
options: Daily electrocardiograms regardless of symptoms as the immediate next step
Infection surveillance
Serial nerve conduction studies as the immediate next step
Routine daily abdominal ultrasound as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Patients receiving intensive combination immunosuppression (high-dose steroids plus additional agents) are profoundly immunocompromised, so vigilant infection surveillance and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) are essential to prevent opportunistic infection, which can mimic or worsen ILD. Routine daily abdominal ultrasound has no role in this monitoring. Daily ECGs without an indication add no value. Serial nerve conduction studies are unnecessary because the threat is pulmonary and infectious, not peripheral nerve. Preventing and detecting opportunistic infection is the highest-yield supportive measure during aggressive immunosuppression for anti-MDA5 disease.

pearls: Clinically amyopathic dermatomyositis presents with classic DM skin findings (heliotrope, Gottron, cutaneous ulcers, palmar papules) but little or no weakness and often near-normal CK.
Anti-MDA5 antibodies predict rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease; markedly elevated ferritin signals high mortality risk.
Treatment is aggressive early combination immunosuppression (high-dose steroids plus calcineurin inhibitor and/or cyclophosphamide, with rituximab/IVIG escalation), not steroid monotherapy.
Provide PCP prophylaxis and infection surveillance during intensive immunosuppression, and consider ECMO/transplant referral for refractory ILD.

cases.title: Worsening Leg and Arm Weakness Despite Stopping a Cholesterol Pill
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive difficulty rising from chairs and lifting his arms that continued worsening even after he stopped his cholesterol medication.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man reports 4 months of progressive proximal weakness: he struggles to rise from a chair, climb stairs, and lift objects onto shelves. His primary doctor suspected statin myopathy and stopped his atorvastatin 40 mg 10 weeks ago, but the weakness has continued to worsen rather than improve. He has mild muscle aches but the weakness dominates. No rash, no joint swelling, no dysphagia yet. Past medical history: coronary artery disease, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, hypertension. Medications (current): aspirin, metformin, lisinopril, amlodipine; atorvastatin discontinued 10 weeks ago. Allergies: none. Social: retired electrician, nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history: no neuromuscular disease. Review of systems: weight stable, no fevers, no Raynaud, no skin changes, no ocular or bulbar symptoms, no sensory complaints. He notes the weakness is symmetric and has slowly progressed despite statin cessation, which surprised his physician.
cases.exam: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness: hip flexors and shoulder abductors 3/5 to 4-/5, with preserved distal strength (5/5 grip, wrist, and ankle). Difficulty rising from a chair without using his arms; mild waddling gait. No muscle atrophy or fasciculations. No rash, Gottron papules, or heliotrope. No synovitis. Sensation intact; deep tendon reflexes normal and symmetric; no Babinski. No ptosis or extraocular weakness; speech and swallow normal. No scapular winging.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly argues against simple self-limited statin myalgia/myopathy and warrants further workup?
options: Normal reflexes and sensation as the first step today
A single mildly elevated CK that normalized off the drug
Progressive objective proximal weakness that persists
Mild muscle aches that improved with rest as the first step today
stages.explanation: Common statin-associated muscle symptoms (myalgia, mild self-limited myopathy) resolve within weeks of stopping the drug. Progressive, objective proximal weakness that persists and even worsens after statin discontinuation is a red flag for an immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy rather than a toxic, reversible effect, and mandates further workup (CK, myositis antibodies, EMG, possibly biopsy). Mild aches improving with rest are reassuring and typical of benign myalgia. Normal reflexes and sensation simply localize to muscle, not nerve. A single mildly elevated CK that normalizes off the drug fits benign statin myopathy. The hallmark distinguishing feature here is ongoing worsening despite stopping the statin.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatine kinase 6,400 U/L (markedly elevated)
AST 140 U/L, ALT 120 U/L
Aldolase: elevated
TSH: normal
ESR/CRP: mildly elevated
Myositis antibody panel: anti-HMGCR (anti-3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA reductase) positive; anti-Jo-1 negative; anti-SRP negative
EMG: irritable myopathy (fibrillations, positive sharp waves, short-duration motor units)
Muscle biopsy: scattered necrotic and regenerating fibers with minimal lymphocytic inflammation (necrotizing myopathy pattern)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the persistent weakness, very high CK, anti-HMGCR antibodies, and necrotizing biopsy after statin exposure?
options: Self-limited toxic statin myopathy in this clinical context
Inclusion body myositis as the primary diagnosis
Polymyalgia rheumatica as the primary diagnosis
Statin-associated immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy
stages.explanation: Persistent, worsening proximal weakness after stopping a statin, a markedly elevated CK, positive anti-HMGCR antibodies, and a biopsy showing necrotic/regenerating fibers with sparse inflammation define statin-associated immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy. Unlike self-limited toxic statin myopathy, this autoimmune process continues after drug cessation and requires immunosuppression. Polymyalgia rheumatica causes proximal pain and stiffness with normal CK and no true weakness, not a necrotizing myopathy. Inclusion body myositis causes slowly progressive distal and asymmetric weakness (finger flexors, quadriceps) with characteristic rimmed vacuoles, not this acute necrotizing, anti-HMGCR-positive pattern. The antibody plus biopsy clinches the immune-mediated diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management once anti-HMGCR immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy is confirmed?
options: Start a high-intensity exercise program as the sole therapy as initial therapy
Permanently discontinue the statin
Rechallenge with a lower-dose statin as initial therapy
Simply continue to withhold the statin and reassure that it will resolve as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Anti-HMGCR necrotizing myopathy is an autoimmune disease that does not remit with statin withdrawal alone, so management is to permanently discontinue the statin and start immunosuppression: corticosteroids combined with steroid-sparing agents (commonly methotrexate or azathioprine) and frequently IVIG, which is particularly effective in this condition. Merely withholding the statin and reassuring the patient ignores the ongoing immune attack and risks permanent weakness. Statin rechallenge is contraindicated and can worsen disease. Exercise alone cannot control the autoimmune necrotizing process, though rehabilitation is a helpful adjunct. Immunosuppression after permanent statin cessation is the standard of care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He has established coronary artery disease and now cannot take a statin. What is the most appropriate approach to his lipid management?
options: Accept no lipid-lowering therapy indefinitely as the immediate next step
Use a non-statin lipid-lowering agent such as ezetimibe and/or a PCSK9 inhibitor while treating the myopathy
Switch to a different statin at high dose as the immediate next step
Resume the statin at half dose despite the diagnosis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Because statins are contraindicated in anti-HMGCR necrotizing myopathy, his secondary-prevention lipid goals are best pursued with non-statin agents such as ezetimibe and/or a PCSK9 inhibitor, which lower LDL without statin exposure, while immunosuppression controls the myopathy. Accepting no lipid-lowering therapy leaves a high-risk CAD patient unprotected. Resuming any statin, even at half dose or as a different statin at high dose, risks reactivating or worsening the immune-mediated myopathy and is contraindicated. Non-statin LDL-lowering therapy allows continued cardiovascular risk reduction without re-triggering the autoimmune muscle disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: During follow-up, which is the best objective measure to monitor his treatment response over time?
options: Daily body weight with appropriate follow-up
Trends in serum creatine kinase together with serial objective muscle strength testing
Serial LDL cholesterol levels with appropriate follow-up
Repeat EMG every visit with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Treatment response in immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy is best tracked by trending serum creatine kinase alongside serial objective strength assessments (e.g., manual muscle testing and functional measures); both should improve with effective immunosuppression. Serial LDL levels monitor lipid therapy, not myopathy activity. Repeating EMG at every visit is invasive, uncomfortable, and unnecessary for routine monitoring. Daily body weight does not reflect myositis disease activity. Combining the CK trend with measured strength provides the most reliable, low-burden assessment of whether the autoimmune myopathy is responding to therapy.

pearls: Weakness that persists or worsens after stopping a statin is a red flag for immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy, not benign self-limited statin myopathy.
Anti-HMGCR antibodies plus a biopsy showing necrotic/regenerating fibers with minimal inflammation and a markedly elevated CK confirm the diagnosis.
Management requires permanent statin discontinuation plus immunosuppression (steroids, steroid-sparing agents, often IVIG); statin rechallenge is contraindicated.
Continue cardiovascular risk reduction with non-statin agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors), and monitor response with CK trends plus serial strength testing.

cases.title: Sudden Hot, Swollen Joints in a Post-Op Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute onset of multiple hot, swollen, exquisitely painful joints two days after hip surgery.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man is on the orthopedic ward 2 days after an elective total knee arthroplasty. He develops abrupt, severe pain, redness, and swelling in his right first MTP joint, left ankle, and right wrist overnight. He has been NPO and on IV fluids, received perioperative diuresis for mild volume overload, and his usual medications were held around surgery. He has a history of gout with prior podagra but has not been on urate-lowering therapy. Past medical history: gout, hypertension, chronic kidney disease (stage 3, baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL), heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Medications: home hydrochlorothiazide and amlodipine (HCTZ recently increased perioperatively); allopurinol never started. Allergies: none. Social: occasional beer, retired. Family history: gout in father. Review of systems: no fever before this, no new rash; he reports the surgical knee itself is doing well and is not the painful joint. Oral intake remains limited because of postoperative nausea.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man. Right first MTP joint warm, erythematous, markedly tender with overlying shiny skin; left ankle and right wrist swollen, warm, and tender with painful limited range of motion. The surgical right knee dressing is clean and dry; that knee is mildly swollen as expected post-op but not disproportionately painful, warm, or erythematous beyond the surgical site. No tophi visualized. No new murmurs. Mild lower-extremity edema. He is NPO and reports nausea.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which perioperative factors most likely precipitated this acute polyarticular flare?
options: Starting a PCSK9 inhibitor perioperatively as the first step today
Excessive dietary purine intake while NPO as the first step today
Initiation of high-dose colchicine before surgery as the first step today
Diuretic use, fluid shifts/volume changes,
stages.explanation: Perioperative gout flares are precipitated by rapid shifts in serum urate and volume status: diuretics (especially increased thiazide dosing) raise urate and cause fluid shifts, surgical stress and dehydration alter urate concentrations, and interruption of routine medications can destabilize a patient with underlying gout. This patient received perioperative diuresis, has been NPO with fluid shifts, and had medications held, all classic triggers. He was not given high-dose colchicine before surgery. Being NPO means he is not consuming excess dietary purines. A PCSK9 inhibitor lowers LDL and is unrelated to gout. The diuretic-and-fluid-shift mechanism explains this flare.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Although the picture is classic for a gout flare, what is the most important step to confirm the diagnosis and exclude a dangerous mimic before committing to therapy?
options: Serum uric acid level alone during the flare as the primary diagnosis
Empiric treatment without any joint evaluation as the primary diagnosis
Arthrocentesis of an accessible affected joint for synovial fluid crystal analysis, cell count, Gram stain,
Plain radiographs of all involved joints alone as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Even when gout seems obvious, septic arthritis must be excluded, so arthrocentesis of an accessible affected joint for crystal analysis (negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals), cell count, Gram stain, and culture is the key confirmatory and safety step, particularly in a hospitalized, postoperative patient. Empiric treatment without any joint evaluation risks missing joint sepsis. A serum uric acid level can be normal or low during an acute flare and neither confirms gout nor excludes infection. Radiographs may show chronic changes but cannot distinguish acute gout from infection. Synovial fluid analysis is the decisive test.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Arthrocentesis (right wrist): cloudy fluid
Synovial WBC 28,000/µL (predominantly neutrophils)
Crystals: needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (monosodium urate)
Gram stain: no organisms
Synovial culture: pending (final negative)
Serum uric acid: 7.8 mg/dL
Serum creatinine: 1.7 mg/dL (near baseline)
WBC 11.5 ×10³/µL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given confirmed gout but ongoing NPO status, nausea, stage 3 CKD, and heart failure, what is the most appropriate treatment for this acute flare?
options: Oral colchicine at full loading dose as initial therapy
High-dose oral NSAIDs around the clock as initial therapy
Intra-articular and/or systemic corticosteroids, an appropriate option when oral intake is limited
Start allopurinol now at high dose to abort the flare as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When a patient cannot take oral medications and has contraindications to NSAIDs (CKD, heart failure) and to high-dose colchicine (CKD), corticosteroids are the preferred anti-inflammatory therapy: intra-articular injection for one or two accessible joints, or systemic IV/IM/parenteral steroids for polyarticular disease in an NPO patient. High-dose oral NSAIDs are contraindicated given his renal disease and heart failure and require oral intake. Allopurinol does not treat acute flares and may prolong them if started during an attack without anti-inflammatory cover, so it is not used to abort a flare. Full-dose colchicine is risky in CKD and requires oral intake. Steroids fit best.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His flare resolves and he is eating again. He has had recurrent gout, a tophaceous tendency, and CKD. What is the most appropriate long-term plan for urate-lowering therapy?
options: Use only dietary changes and never pharmacologic therapy as the immediate next step
Start allopurinol immediately at a high fixed dose during the next flare as the immediate next step
Avoid all urate-lowering therapy permanently because of CKD as the immediate next step
After the flare settles, initiate allopurinol at a low dose, titrate to a target serum urate < 6 mg/dL,
stages.explanation: For recurrent gout, urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol is indicated; the correct approach is to start at a low dose once the acute flare has settled, titrate gradually to a target serum urate below 6 mg/dL (lower, < 5 mg/dL, for tophaceous disease), and provide anti-inflammatory prophylaxis (e.g., low-dose colchicine or NSAID/steroid as tolerated) during titration to prevent mobilization flares. Starting allopurinol at a high fixed dose mid-flare is inappropriate and risks toxicity. CKD does not preclude allopurinol; dosing is adjusted and titrated. Diet alone rarely achieves target urate in established recurrent or tophaceous gout. Low-and-slow allopurinol to target with prophylaxis is standard.

pearls: Surgery, diuretics, fluid shifts, and interruption of routine medications commonly precipitate acute gout flares in hospitalized patients.
Always perform arthrocentesis to confirm monosodium urate crystals and exclude septic arthritis; serum urate is often normal or low during a flare.
When patients are NPO or have CKD/heart failure contraindicating NSAIDs and colchicine, corticosteroids (intra-articular or systemic) are the preferred flare therapy.
Start urate-lowering allopurinol low and slow after the flare resolves, titrate to serum urate < 6 mg/dL, and give anti-inflammatory prophylaxis during titration.

cases.title: A Non-Healing Sore in the Jaw After a Tooth Extraction
cases.chiefComplaint: Exposed bone and persistent jaw pain that failed to heal after a tooth extraction three months ago.
cases.history: A 73-year-old woman presents with 3 months of persistent right lower jaw pain and a non-healing socket after a molar extraction. She now notices a patch of exposed, rough bone in the gum, intermittent foul drainage, and a bad taste. The site has not healed despite a course of oral antibiotics and chlorhexidine rinses from her dentist. Past medical history: postmenopausal osteoporosis treated for 6 years; breast cancer in remission. Medications: oral alendronate 70 mg weekly for years, with several years of prior intravenous zoledronic acid during cancer therapy; calcium and vitamin D; lisinopril. Allergies: penicillin (hives) - took clindamycin. Social: nonsmoker, social alcohol, retired teacher. Family history: osteoporosis in mother. Review of systems: localized jaw pain and altered sensation of the lower lip on the right; no fever currently, no facial swelling beyond the gum, no visual changes. She recalls no recent trauma other than the dental extraction, and the lesion has persisted well beyond the expected healing window.
cases.exam: An area of exposed, yellow-gray necrotic bone approximately 1 cm is visible in the right posterior mandibular alveolar ridge at the prior extraction site, with surrounding erythematous, mildly swollen gingiva and scant purulent drainage. The exposed bone is nontender to light touch but the surrounding soft tissue is tender. Mild numbness of the right lower lip (Vincent sign). No facial cellulitis, no trismus, no fluctuant abscess, no cervical lymphadenopathy. Dentition otherwise fair. No intraoral mass suggestive of malignancy on inspection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination from her history is the key risk factor for this non-healing exposed jaw bone?
options: Penicillin allergy and chlorhexidine use as the first step today
Long-term antiresorptive therapy with a recent invasive dental procedure
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation as the first step today
Treated hypertension with lisinopril as the first step today
stages.explanation: The pivotal risk factor is prolonged antiresorptive (antiangiogenic) therapy, here years of oral alendronate plus prior IV zoledronic acid, combined with a recent invasive dental procedure (tooth extraction), the classic setup for medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw. The penicillin allergy and chlorhexidine use are incidental and do not cause osteonecrosis. Calcium and vitamin D support bone health and are not causative. Lisinopril for hypertension is unrelated. Cumulative antiresorptive exposure (especially potent IV bisphosphonates) plus dentoalveolar trauma drives impaired bone healing and exposed necrotic bone, which defines this entity.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical definition must be met to diagnose medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ)?
options: Tooth mobility in an osteoporotic patient as the primary diagnosis
A positive bone scan alone without exposed bone as the primary diagnosis
Any jaw pain in a patient on a bisphosphonate as the primary diagnosis

Exposed bone or bone probed through a fistula in the maxillofacial region persisting > 8 weeks, in a patient on antiresorptive/antiangiogenic therapy, with no history of jaw radiation or metastatic disease to the site
stages.explanation: MRONJ is defined by all of the following: exposed bone, or bone that can be probed through an intraoral or extraoral fistula in the maxillofacial region, persisting for more than 8 weeks; current or prior antiresorptive or antiangiogenic therapy; and no history of radiation therapy to the jaws or obvious metastatic disease to the site (which would suggest osteoradionecrosis or tumor). Jaw pain alone is nonspecific. A positive bone scan without exposed/probeable bone does not meet criteria. Tooth mobility alone is not diagnostic. Her 3-month-old exposed necrotic bone after extraction on antiresorptives, without jaw radiation, satisfies the definition.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Panoramic radiograph (orthopantomogram): ill-defined osteolysis and patchy sclerosis of the right posterior mandible at the extraction site, with a sequestrum of necrotic bone and non-healing socket
No widened periodontal ligament space suggestive of tumor; no fracture
CBCT: focal cortical erosion with sequestrum, no large soft-tissue mass
Biopsy (only if malignancy suspected): not performed given classic clinical and imaging features

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this patient with early, symptomatic but localized MRONJ (exposed necrotic bone with mild infection), what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate extensive surgical resection of the mandible as initial therapy
Hyperbaric oxygen as sole curative therapy as initial therapy
Doubling the bisphosphonate dose to strengthen the bone as initial therapy
Conservative measures: antimicrobial mouth rinses , oral antibiotics for the secondary infection, analgesia, meticulous oral hygiene,
stages.explanation: Early, localized symptomatic MRONJ is managed conservatively: antimicrobial rinses such as chlorhexidine, systemic antibiotics targeting the secondary infection, analgesia, scrupulous oral hygiene, and limited removal of loose bony sequestra that irritate soft tissue, with referral to oral/maxillofacial surgery for staging and ongoing care. Immediate extensive mandibular resection is reserved for advanced, refractory disease, not early lesions. Hyperbaric oxygen is at best an adjunct with uncertain benefit, not sole curative therapy. Increasing the bisphosphonate dose worsens the underlying problem. Conservative source control plus specialist referral is the appropriate initial strategy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her oncologist and rheumatologist ask about the antiresorptive medication. Which statement best guides decision-making about her bisphosphonate going forward?
options: She should start a new IV bisphosphonate to promote socket healing as the immediate next step
Bisphosphonates have a very long skeletal half-life so a 'drug holiday' has uncertain short-term benefit
Stopping the bisphosphonate immediately will rapidly heal the exposed bone within days because the drug clears quickly as the immediate next step
She should switch to denosumab, which carries no risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Bisphosphonates bind avidly to bone and have an extremely long skeletal half-life, so discontinuation does not produce rapid clearance or quick healing; the value of a drug holiday is uncertain and individualized, and any decision must balance her osteoporosis and cancer-related skeletal-event risk against potential MRONJ benefit, made jointly with her oncologist and the prescribing clinicians. Denosumab is also associated with MRONJ, so it is not a risk-free substitute (though its effect is non-cumulative and reversible). Starting a new IV bisphosphonate would increase, not reduce, risk. Thoughtful, multidisciplinary, risk-weighted decision-making is the correct guidance.

pearls: MRONJ requires exposed/probeable maxillofacial bone persisting > 8 weeks, current/prior antiresorptive or antiangiogenic therapy, and no jaw radiation; invasive dental procedures are the key trigger.
Cumulative and potent antiresorptive exposure (IV bisphosphonates, denosumab) plus dentoalveolar surgery raises risk; preventive dental evaluation before therapy reduces it.
Early localized disease is treated conservatively (chlorhexidine rinses, antibiotics for secondary infection, analgesia, limited sequestrectomy) with oral/maxillofacial surgery referral.
Bisphosphonates have a long skeletal half-life, so drug holidays have uncertain short-term benefit; pausing therapy must be a multidisciplinary, risk-weighted decision.

cases.title: Swollen Fingers and a Stiff Knee
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful swelling of a few fingers and his right knee for two months.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man presents with two months of pain and swelling involving the second finger of his right hand, the fourth finger of his left hand, and his right knee. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with activity. The affected fingers are diffusely swollen "like sausages," and he has noticed pitting and ridging of several fingernails. He has a 10-year history of plaque psoriasis affecting his scalp and extensor elbows, managed with topical clobetasol. He denies low back pain, eye redness, oral ulcers, urethral discharge, or recent diarrhea. He works as an electrician and is sexually active with one partner. He drinks alcohol socially and does not smoke. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: topical clobetasol 0.05% as needed. No known drug allergies. Family history is notable for psoriasis in his father and an uncle with "arthritis of the spine." Review of systems is otherwise negative for fevers, weight loss, or rash changes.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. Skin shows well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale on the scalp and extensor elbows. The right second and left fourth digits are diffusely swollen with uniform fusiform enlargement (dactylitis). Several fingernails show pitting, onycholysis, and an oil-drop sign. The right knee is warm with a moderate effusion and reduced flexion. There is tenderness at the right Achilles insertion. The joint involvement is asymmetric. There is no symmetric small-joint MCP swelling, no rheumatoid nodules, no tophi, and no spinal tenderness. Eyes are without conjunctival injection. Distal interphalangeal joints of the index finger are tender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly points toward a spondyloarthropathy rather than rheumatoid arthritis?
options: Morning stiffness lasting longer than one hour
Dactylitis with nail pitting
Presence of a knee effusion as the first step today
Symmetric small-joint involvement of the hands
stages.explanation: Dactylitis ("sausage digit"), nail changes (pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop sign), and distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint involvement are hallmark features of psoriatic arthritis and the seronegative spondyloarthropathies, distinguishing them from rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Morning stiffness over an hour (A) occurs in any inflammatory arthritis, including RA, so it does not discriminate. Symmetric small-joint involvement (B) is actually the classic RA pattern, the opposite of the asymmetric oligoarticular pattern here. A knee effusion (D) is nonspecific and occurs in many arthritides. Enthesitis (Achilles tenderness) and asymmetric oligoarthritis further support spondyloarthropathy. The combination of psoriasis, dactylitis, nail disease, and DIP involvement makes psoriatic arthritis the leading diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to support the diagnosis and exclude key mimics?
options: Order rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ESR/CRP,
Perform a knee arthrocentesis to look for negatively birefringent crystals
Obtain an MRI of the sacroiliac joints before any blood work
Send HLA-B27 as the confirmatory diagnostic test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Psoriatic arthritis is a clinical diagnosis; supportive workup includes inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP), seronegativity (RF and anti-CCP typically negative, helping distinguish from RA), and radiographs that may show erosions with adjacent new bone formation or the "pencil-in-cup" deformity. HLA-B27 (B) is associated with axial disease but is neither sensitive nor specific and is not confirmatory. Arthrocentesis for crystals (C) is reasonable when gout or pseudogout is suspected, but the picture here (psoriasis, dactylitis, nail disease) does not suggest crystal disease as primary. SI joint MRI (D) is useful if axial symptoms are present, but this patient has no back pain, so it is not the first step. Baseline labs and radiographs are the highest-yield initial evaluation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
RF: negative
Anti-CCP: negative
ESR: 38 mm/hr
CRP: 22 mg/L
Uric acid: 5.8 mg/dL
ANA: negative
Hand/foot radiographs: erosive changes at right 2nd DIP with adjacent new bone formation; early "pencil-in-cup" deformity
Right knee arthrocentesis: WBC 9,800/µL, no crystals under polarized light, Gram stain negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has dactylitis, an inflamed knee, and enthesitis but no axial disease. What is the most appropriate first-line systemic therapy?
options: Allopurinol with colchicine prophylaxis
A conventional synthetic DMARD such as methotrexate
Hydroxychloroquine as initial therapy
Long-term systemic glucocorticoids alone
stages.explanation: For peripheral psoriatic arthritis with active joint and skin disease, a conventional synthetic DMARD such as methotrexate is a reasonable first-line agent and also treats the cutaneous psoriasis; if response is inadequate or there is significant enthesitis/axial disease, a biologic (TNF inhibitor or IL-17 inhibitor) is added. Long-term systemic steroids alone (A) are avoided because tapering can precipitate severe pustular psoriasis flares and they do not modify disease. Allopurinol (B) treats gout, which arthrocentesis excluded. Hydroxychloroquine (D) is relatively contraindicated in psoriasis because it can trigger flares and is a poor choice here. NSAIDs may help mild disease, but this patient warrants a DMARD.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient later develops inflammatory low back pain with morning stiffness and an MRI showing sacroiliitis, which therapeutic change is most appropriate?
options: Add a TNF-alpha inhibitor or IL-17 inhibitor, as conventional DMARDs are ineffective for axial disease
Start oral prednisone indefinitely as the leading concern
Switch to hydroxychloroquine for axial coverage as the leading concern
Increase the methotrexate dose, since it controls axial disease well as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Conventional synthetic DMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine, leflunomide) do not effectively treat axial spondyloarthritis. When inflammatory axial disease with MRI-proven sacroiliitis develops, a biologic—TNF-alpha inhibitor or IL-17 inhibitor—is indicated. Increasing methotrexate (A) will not help the spine. Indefinite prednisone (C) carries unacceptable toxicity and risks pustular psoriasis on taper. Hydroxychloroquine (D) does not treat axial disease and can flare psoriasis. NSAIDs are first-line for symptomatic axial disease, but when persistent active sacroiliitis is documented, biologic therapy is the disease-modifying step of choice.

cases.diagnosis: Psoriatic arthritis (asymmetric oligoarticular subtype with dactylitis, enthesitis, and nail disease)
pearls: Dactylitis ("sausage digit"), nail pitting/onycholysis, and DIP involvement are classic clues to psoriatic arthritis.
Psoriatic arthritis is seronegative: RF and anti-CCP are typically negative, helping distinguish it from rheumatoid arthritis.
The "pencil-in-cup" deformity on radiographs reflects simultaneous erosion and new bone formation unique to psoriatic arthritis.
Conventional DMARDs treat peripheral disease and skin, but axial disease requires a TNF or IL-17 inhibitor.

cases.title: Sudden Hot Swollen Knee After Hip Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute pain and swelling of the right knee three days after hip replacement.
cases.history: A 75-year-old man is on the orthopedic ward recovering from an elective left total hip arthroplasty performed three days ago. Overnight he developed acute, severe pain, swelling, warmth, and redness of his right (non-operative) knee, with near-inability to bear weight. He denies prior similar episodes but recalls being told years ago of "calcium in the knee cartilage" on an X-ray. PMH includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.5 mg/dL). He has no history of gout. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, enoxaparin prophylaxis, and acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no fevers at home and no recent infections. Surgical site is clean and dry. He reports the right knee felt mildly achy for years but never acutely inflamed like this.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable elderly man guarding the right knee. The right knee is warm, erythematous, and has a tense effusion with markedly limited and painful range of motion; pain on minimal passive movement. No overlying break in the skin. The left hip surgical incision is clean, dry, and intact without surrounding erythema or drainage. No tophi at the ears, elbows, or first MTP joints. No other joints are inflamed. Calves are soft and nontender bilaterally with no cords. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. There are no oral ulcers or rashes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important next step in evaluating this acute monoarthritis?
options: Perform arthrocentesis of the knee for cell count, Gram stain, culture,
Start empiric IV vancomycin and obtain blood cultures only
Order a serum uric acid level to confirm gout as the first step today
Obtain an MRI of the knee before any intervention as the first step today
stages.explanation: Any acute monoarthritis must be presumed septic until proven otherwise, and arthrocentesis with synovial fluid analysis (cell count, Gram stain, culture, and crystal exam under polarized light) is the essential diagnostic step.
