Empiric antibiotics alone (A) without aspirating the joint risks missing both crystal disease and confirming/excluding infection—cultures should be obtained from the joint. Serum uric acid (B) is unreliable acutely (often normal or low during attacks) and cannot diagnose pseudogout or exclude infection. MRI (D) delays definitive diagnosis and is unnecessary for acute crystal or septic arthritis evaluation. Postoperative crystal flares are common, but infection must always be excluded first by tapping the joint.

stages.label: SYNOVIAL FLUID & IMAGING
stages.content:
Knee aspirate: cloudy yellow fluid
WBC: 28,000/µL (predominantly neutrophils)
Gram stain: no organisms
Crystal analysis: rhomboid-shaped crystals, weakly POSITIVELY birefringent (blue when parallel to the compensator)
Knee radiograph: linear calcification of the meniscal and articular cartilage (chondrocalcinosis)
Serum uric acid: 6.1 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the synovial fluid and radiograph, what is the diagnosis?
options: Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease
Reactive arthritis as the primary diagnosis
Gout (monosodium urate crystal arthritis)
Septic arthritis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Rhomboid-shaped, weakly positively birefringent crystals (blue when parallel to the compensator) plus chondrocalcinosis on radiograph are diagnostic of calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (pseudogout). Gout (A) shows needle-shaped, strongly negatively birefringent crystals (yellow when parallel) and would not produce chondrocalcinosis. Septic arthritis (B) is excluded by a negative Gram stain and a WBC of 28,000/µL with crystals identified—though culture results are still pending, the crystal findings explain the picture; counts in pseudogout commonly fall in this inflammatory range. Reactive arthritis (D) follows GI/GU infection and would not show crystals or chondrocalcinosis. Surgery, acute illness, and volume shifts are classic triggers of acute CPPD attacks.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his age, CKD (creatinine 1.6), and recent surgery, what is the most appropriate treatment for this acute attack confined to one joint?
options: High-dose colchicine 1.2 mg followed by 0.6 mg hourly until symptoms resolve
High-dose indomethacin for two weeks as initial therapy
Intra-articular glucocorticoid injection after septic arthritis is reasonably excluded
Begin urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol immediately as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For an acute CPPD attack limited to one accessible joint, an intra-articular glucocorticoid injection is highly effective and avoids systemic toxicity—ideal in an elderly postoperative patient with CKD. NSAIDs like indomethacin (A) are relatively contraindicated given CKD, age, and recent surgery (bleeding/renal risk). Aggressive colchicine dosing (C) is nephrotoxic-adjacent and poorly tolerated in CKD; if used, doses must be reduced, and the old "hourly until toxicity" regimen is obsolete. Urate-lowering therapy (D) treats gout, not CPPD, and is never started during an acute flare. Confirming a negative Gram stain (with cultures pending and low clinical suspicion) supports proceeding with intra-articular steroid.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After his attack resolves, which underlying condition should be screened for given new or recurrent CPPD, especially at a relatively younger age or with severe disease?
options: Chronic hepatitis C infection in this setting
Lyme disease serology as the immediate next step
Vitamin B12 deficiency as the immediate next step
Hypothyroidism, hemochromatosis, hyperparathyroidism,
stages.explanation: Recurrent or early-onset CPPD warrants screening for associated metabolic conditions: hyperparathyroidism (calcium/PTH), hemochromatosis (ferritin/transferrin saturation), hypomagnesemia, and hypothyroidism (TSH). These secondary causes can drive pyrophosphate deposition and may be treatable. Hepatitis C (B) is not associated with CPPD. Vitamin B12 deficiency (C) causes neurologic and hematologic disease, not crystal arthritis. Lyme disease (D) can cause arthritis (typically a large-joint mono/oligoarthritis) but does not produce CPP crystals or chondrocalcinosis and is not part of the metabolic CPPD screen. Identifying and treating an underlying disorder can reduce recurrent attacks.

cases.diagnosis: Acute calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (pseudogout) of the knee, postoperative flare
pearls: Always tap an acute monoarthritis: synovial fluid analysis distinguishes crystal disease from septic arthritis.
CPPD crystals are rhomboid and weakly positively birefringent; gout crystals are needle-shaped and strongly negatively birefringent.
Chondrocalcinosis (linear cartilage calcification) on radiograph is the radiographic signature of CPPD.
Recurrent/early CPPD should prompt screening for hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia, and hypothyroidism.

cases.title: Tightening Skin and Painful White Fingers
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly thickening, tight skin over her hands, arms, and trunk for three months.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents with three months of progressively tightening skin involving her fingers, hands, forearms, upper arms, and now her chest and abdomen. She describes her fingers becoming puffy then hard, with difficulty making a fist. She has new color changes of her fingers in the cold—turning white, then blue, then red—over the past six months. She reports diffuse arthralgias, fatigue, and shortness of breath on exertion. She denies heartburn currently but notes occasional dysphagia for solids. No prior autoimmune disease. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and works as a teacher. Family history includes a sister with hypothyroidism. She is worried because the skin changes are spreading quickly. She has had no rash on her face, no muscle weakness, and no recent infections. She has not been pregnant recently. Review of systems is positive for new mild exertional dyspnea and Raynaud phenomenon.
cases.exam: Anxious woman in no acute distress. Skin is markedly thickened and indurated over the fingers (sclerodactyly), dorsa of the hands, forearms, upper arms, chest, and abdomen, with reduced ability to pinch the skin and decreased oral aperture. Digital pulp atrophy is present; one fingertip has a small healing pit. There are no telangiectasias or calcinosis nodules. Nailfold capillaries appear dilated. Bibasilar fine inspiratory crackles are heard. There is no heliotrope rash, no Gottron papules, and proximal muscle strength is 5/5. No synovitis. Cardiac exam is normal without a loud P2. Blood pressure is mildly elevated compared with prior readings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The rapid, proximal-to-trunk skin thickening with Raynaud phenomenon most suggests which diagnosis, and which antibody should be checked first?
options: Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis; anti-centromere antibody
Eosinophilic fasciitis; peripheral eosinophil count
Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis
Dermatomyositis; anti-Jo-1 antibody as the first step today
stages.explanation: Skin thickening that extends proximal to the elbows/knees and onto the trunk, progressing rapidly, defines diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis, which is most associated with anti-topoisomerase I (anti-Scl-70) and a high risk of interstitial lung disease and scleroderma renal crisis. Limited cutaneous SSc (A) spares the trunk, advances slowly, and is linked to anti-centromere antibody and pulmonary hypertension. Dermatomyositis (C) features heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, and proximal weakness—absent here. Eosinophilic fasciitis (D) typically spares the fingers and the face, lacks Raynaud, and shows peau d'orange induration with eosinophilia; the prominent sclerodactyly and Raynaud here argue against it.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ANA: positive, 1:640, nucleolar pattern
Anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I): positive
Anti-centromere: negative
Anti-RNA polymerase III: pending
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no protein, no blood
CBC: normal; no schistocytes on smear
High-resolution chest CT: bibasilar reticulation and ground-glass with early honeycombing
PFTs: restrictive pattern, FVC 72% predicted, DLCO 60% predicted
Echocardiogram: normal estimated PA pressure, normal EF

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given biopsy-supported interstitial lung disease with reduced FVC and DLCO, which therapy is most appropriate for her lung disease?
options: Methotrexate as initial therapy
Mycophenolate mofetil , with consideration of antifibrotic therapy
High-dose daily prednisone alone as initial therapy
Observation with repeat PFTs in one year as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Systemic sclerosis–associated interstitial lung disease with significant or progressive functional impairment is treated with immunosuppression—mycophenolate mofetil is commonly first-line (cyclophosphamide is an alternative), and antifibrotics such as nintedanib may be added for progressive fibrosis. High-dose glucocorticoids alone (A) are ineffective for SSc-ILD and—critically—doses ≥15 mg/day prednisone increase the risk of scleroderma renal crisis, so steroids must be minimized. Methotrexate (C) helps skin/joint disease but is not the agent of choice for ILD and is relatively contraindicated with significant lung disease. Observation (D) is inappropriate given a DLCO of 60% with active fibrosis; delaying treatment risks irreversible decline.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two months later she presents with a blood pressure of 196/118 mmHg, headache, creatinine rising to 2.8 mg/dL, and a smear showing schistocytes. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Intravenous labetalol infusion and supportive care
Start a short-acting ACE inhibitor
High-dose IV methylprednisolone pulse as the leading concern
Urgent plasmapheresis for thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
stages.explanation: This is scleroderma renal crisis—malignant hypertension, acute kidney injury, and microangiopathic hemolysis—and the treatment of choice is a short-acting ACE inhibitor such as captopril, titrated rapidly to control blood pressure, even as creatinine rises. ACE inhibitors dramatically improve outcomes here and should not be withheld for renal function. Labetalol/other antihypertensives (A) do not target the renin-angiotensin axis driving the crisis and are inferior. High-dose steroids (C) are contraindicated—they precipitate renal crisis and worsen it. Plasmapheresis for TTP (D) is wrong: the microangiopathy is from renal crisis, not ADAMTS13 deficiency, and the unifying diagnosis is scleroderma renal crisis. Diffuse SSc with anti-Scl-70/RNA pol III and recent steroid exposure are key risk factors.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which exposure most likely contributed to precipitating this patient's scleroderma renal crisis?
options: Treatment with mycophenolate mofetil based on current risk
Cold exposure triggering Raynaud based on current risk
Recent corticosteroid exposure
Use of an ACE inhibitor in the preceding weeks
stages.explanation: Corticosteroid exposure, particularly prednisone ≥15 mg/day, is a well-established precipitant of scleroderma renal crisis, which is why steroids are minimized in diffuse SSc, especially early in disease with anti-RNA polymerase III or anti-Scl-70 antibodies. ACE inhibitors (A) are the treatment, not a cause—they are protective. Mycophenolate (C) is used for skin and lung disease and does not precipitate renal crisis. Cold-induced Raynaud (D) causes digital ischemia but does not trigger renal crisis. Recognizing the steroid–renal crisis link is high-yield: avoid high-dose steroids in early diffuse systemic sclerosis whenever possible.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: For long-term monitoring of a patient with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis, which surveillance strategy is most appropriate?
options: Periodic PFTs, echocardiography for pulmonary hypertension,
Yearly bone marrow biopsy as the immediate next step
Routine muscle biopsy every six months as the immediate next step
Annual colonoscopy and brain MRI as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Diffuse SSc requires ongoing organ surveillance: serial pulmonary function tests and HRCT for interstitial lung disease, echocardiography (with right heart catheterization if indicated) to detect pulmonary hypertension, and frequent home blood pressure and creatinine monitoring to catch scleroderma renal crisis early. Colonoscopy/brain MRI (A) are not part of SSc surveillance. Bone marrow biopsy (C) has no role. Muscle biopsy (D) is reserved for evaluating myositis when clinically suspected, not for routine SSc monitoring. Structured surveillance allows early detection of the leading causes of SSc morbidity and mortality—ILD, pulmonary hypertension, and renal crisis.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis with interstitial lung disease, complicated by scleroderma renal crisis
pearls: Skin thickening proximal to the elbows/knees and on the trunk defines diffuse cutaneous SSc, associated with anti-Scl-70 and anti-RNA polymerase III.
Scleroderma renal crisis presents as malignant hypertension, AKI, and microangiopathic hemolysis—treat with a short-acting ACE inhibitor regardless of rising creatinine.
High-dose corticosteroids (≥15 mg/day prednisone) can precipitate renal crisis and should be avoided in early diffuse SSc.
SSc-ILD is treated with mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide, with antifibrotics for progressive fibrosis; steroids alone are ineffective.

cases.title: Cold Fingers, Hard Skin, and Swallowing Trouble
cases.chiefComplaint: Years of color-changing fingers in the cold, now with tight finger skin and difficulty swallowing.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 12-year history of fingers turning white then blue then red with cold exposure or stress. Over the past few years she has developed tightening of the skin on her fingers, small firm white nodules at her fingertips and elbows that occasionally extrude chalky material, and visible red spots on her face and hands. She reports several years of solid-food dysphagia and frequent heartburn. She denies proximal muscle weakness, dyspnea, or rapidly spreading skin changes; her skin tightening is limited to her hands and forearms below the elbows and her face. PMH includes GERD. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks occasionally. Family history is notable for a mother with Raynaud phenomenon. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, hemoptysis, or hematuria. She seeks evaluation because of the worsening fingertip nodules and dysphagia.
cases.exam: Comfortable woman. Multiple telangiectasias are seen on the face, lips, and palms. There is sclerodactyly of the fingers with tapering and skin tightening limited to the digits and distal forearms; the trunk and proximal arms are spared. Several firm subcutaneous nodules with overlying chalky-white deposits are present at the fingertips and extensor elbows (calcinosis cutis). Nailfold capillary dilation is noted. There are no digital ulcers acutely. Lungs are clear; no loud P2 or right ventricular heave. No proximal muscle weakness; strength 5/5. No synovitis. There is no heliotrope rash or Gottron papules. Cardiac and abdominal exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her constellation of Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, and Telangiectasia is best classified as which condition?
options: Primary Raynaud phenomenon in this clinical context
Mixed connective tissue disease
Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis
Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis
stages.explanation: The CREST pentad—Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia—with skin thickening limited to distal to the elbows/knees and the face defines limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis. Diffuse cutaneous SSc (A) involves the trunk and proximal limbs with rapid progression and higher risk of ILD and renal crisis—neither matches her distal-only, slowly progressive disease. Mixed connective tissue disease (C) features overlap of SLE, scleroderma, and myositis with high-titer anti-U1 RNP, and typically includes myositis or arthritis prominently. Primary Raynaud (D) has no structural skin disease, normal nailfold capillaries, and no autoantibodies—excluded by her sclerodactyly, calcinosis, and telangiectasias.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ANA: positive, 1:320, centromere pattern
Anti-centromere antibody: positive
Anti-Scl-70: negative
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
Urinalysis: unremarkable
Hand radiographs: subcutaneous calcium deposits at fingertips; soft-tissue changes
Barium swallow: reduced distal esophageal peristalsis with patulous lower esophageal sphincter
PFTs: FVC 92% predicted, DLCO 78% predicted
Echocardiogram: estimated PASP 30 mmHg (normal), normal EF

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which antibody is most specific for limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis and is associated with this clinical picture?
options: Anti-centromere antibody
Anti-RNA polymerase III
Anti-Jo-1 as the primary diagnosis
Anti-topoisomerase I (anti-Scl-70)
stages.explanation: Anti-centromere antibody is highly specific for limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST) and confers an elevated long-term risk of pulmonary arterial hypertension. Anti-topoisomerase I/Scl-70 (A) is associated with diffuse cutaneous SSc and interstitial lung disease, not the limited form. Anti-RNA polymerase III (C) is associated with diffuse SSc, rapid skin progression, and scleroderma renal crisis. Anti-Jo-1 (D) is an anti-synthetase antibody seen in inflammatory myopathies (antisynthetase syndrome), not scleroderma. Her positive anti-centromere antibody with a centromere ANA pattern confirms limited cutaneous SSc and flags the need for vigilant pulmonary hypertension surveillance.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of her Raynaud phenomenon to prevent digital ischemia?
options: A non-selective beta-blocker as initial therapy
Low-dose aspirin alone as initial therapy
A long-acting dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker plus cold avoidance
Oral corticosteroids as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for Raynaud phenomenon in systemic sclerosis is a long-acting dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker such as nifedipine or amlodipine, combined with cold avoidance and smoking cessation; PDE-5 inhibitors or topical nitrates are added for refractory disease or digital ulcers. Non-selective beta-blockers (A) cause peripheral vasoconstriction and can worsen Raynaud. Aspirin alone (C) does not provide vasodilation and is not first-line for Raynaud (though it may be used in specific thrombotic settings). Corticosteroids (D) do not treat Raynaud and, in scleroderma, high doses risk renal crisis. Calcium channel blockade is the evidence-based first step.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given anti-centromere positive limited SSc, which complication warrants the most vigilant long-term screening?
options: Pulmonary arterial hypertension
Scleroderma renal crisis
Inflammatory polymyositis
Rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease
stages.explanation: Limited cutaneous SSc with anti-centromere antibody carries a notable long-term risk of isolated pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), so annual echocardiography (with PFT DLCO trends and right heart catheterization when indicated) is essential. Scleroderma renal crisis (A) is far more characteristic of diffuse SSc with anti-RNA polymerase III, not anti-centromere limited disease. Rapidly progressive ILD (B) is more typical of anti-Scl-70/diffuse SSc; limited SSc can have ILD but it is usually less aggressive. Polymyositis (D) is not a feature of anti-centromere limited SSc. Early detection of PAH meaningfully improves outcomes, making it the priority surveillance target.

cases.diagnosis: Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST syndrome), anti-centromere antibody positive
pearls: CREST = Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia; skin thickening stays distal to elbows/knees plus the face.
Anti-centromere antibody is specific for limited cutaneous SSc and predicts long-term pulmonary arterial hypertension risk.
First-line Raynaud therapy is a long-acting dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker plus cold avoidance.
Screen anti-centromere limited SSc patients annually with echocardiography and DLCO for pulmonary arterial hypertension.

cases.title: Trouble Climbing Stairs with a Facial Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of difficulty rising from chairs and climbing stairs, plus a new facial and hand rash.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents with six weeks of progressive difficulty climbing stairs, rising from a seated position, and lifting objects overhead. She also reports trouble combing her hair. She has noticed a violaceous discoloration around her eyelids and reddish, scaly bumps over the knuckles of both hands. She denies pain in the muscles but feels weak. She reports mild dysphagia and decreased appetite with a 3 kg weight loss. She denies fevers, joint swelling, dry eyes/mouth, or Raynaud. PMH is unremarkable; she is up to date on cervical screening but is two years overdue for mammography and colonoscopy. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is noncontributory. She works as an accountant. Review of systems is positive for mild exertional dyspnea but negative for rash photosensitivity elsewhere or oral ulcers. She is concerned about the rash and her worsening weakness.
cases.exam: Alert woman with a violaceous, slightly edematous rash over both upper eyelids (heliotrope rash) and erythematous scaly papules over the MCP and PIP joints (Gottron papules). There is also erythema over the upper chest in a V-distribution. Muscle strength is reduced symmetrically in proximal muscles: hip flexors and shoulder abductors 4-/5, with preserved distal strength (5/5). Neck flexor weakness is present. There is no muscle tenderness, no synovitis, and reflexes and sensation are intact. Lungs have faint bibasilar crackles. There is no lymphadenopathy. Breast exam and abdominal exam are unremarkable. No telangiectasias or sclerodactyly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of symmetric proximal weakness with a heliotrope rash and Gottron papules most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Polymyalgia rheumatica
Dermatomyositis
Myasthenia gravis
Hypothyroid myopathy
stages.explanation: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness plus the pathognomonic cutaneous findings—heliotrope rash and Gottron papules—define dermatomyositis. Polymyalgia rheumatica (A) causes proximal pain and stiffness (especially shoulders/hips) but not true weakness or these rashes, and typically affects patients over 50 with markedly elevated ESR. Hypothyroid myopathy (C) can cause proximal weakness and elevated CK but lacks the characteristic rash and would show thyroid abnormalities. Myasthenia gravis (D) causes fatigable weakness with ocular/bulbar involvement (ptosis, diplopia) and normal CK, without skin findings. The skin signs here are specific enough to make dermatomyositis the clear diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of tests best confirms the diagnosis and characterizes the muscle disease?
options: Acetylcholine receptor antibodies and repetitive nerve stimulation
TSH and morning cortisol as the primary diagnosis
Creatine kinase, aldolase, myositis-specific antibodies, EMG,
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP only as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Confirming an inflammatory myopathy uses muscle enzymes (CK, aldolase), myositis-specific/associated antibodies, electromyography (showing a myopathic pattern with irritability), and MRI to localize inflammation or guide/confirm with muscle biopsy. RF/anti-CCP (B) evaluate rheumatoid arthritis, not myositis. TSH/cortisol (C) screen for endocrine myopathies and adrenal disease but would miss inflammatory myositis. Acetylcholine receptor antibodies and repetitive nerve stimulation (D) test for myasthenia gravis, which the rash and elevated CK do not support. The combination in option A both confirms muscle inflammation and helps subtype the disease, including identifying antibodies linked to ILD or malignancy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatine kinase: 4,200 U/L
Aldolase: elevated
ALT: 78 U/L, AST: 90 U/L
ANA: positive, 1:160
Anti-Mi-2: positive
Anti-Jo-1: negative
TSH: normal
EMG: short-duration, low-amplitude polyphasic motor units with fibrillations (myopathic, irritable)
Muscle MRI: patchy proximal muscle edema
Chest CT: mild bibasilar reticulation

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Because this patient is a 50-year-old woman with newly diagnosed dermatomyositis, which evaluation is especially important?
options: Routine echocardiogram only as the immediate next step
Lumbar puncture for oligoclonal bands as the immediate next step
Bone densitometry as the priority study as the immediate next step
Age- and risk-appropriate malignancy screening, as dermatomyositis carries an increased cancer risk
stages.explanation: Dermatomyositis is a paraneoplastic-associated condition with a significantly increased risk of underlying malignancy, particularly in adults, so age- and risk-appropriate cancer screening is essential—including completing overdue mammography and colonoscopy, plus CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis and gynecologic evaluation as indicated. A routine echocardiogram alone (A) does not address the cancer risk. Lumbar puncture (C) is irrelevant to this myopathy. Bone densitometry (D) matters once chronic steroids start but is not the priority at diagnosis. Identifying an occult malignancy can change management dramatically and is a defining teaching point in adult dermatomyositis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her active dermatomyositis?
options: Hydroxychloroquine alone for both skin and muscle disease as initial therapy
Plasmapheresis as monotherapy
High-dose corticosteroids, typically with a steroid-sparing agent such as methotrexate or azathioprine
Observation with physical therapy only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for active dermatomyositis is high-dose corticosteroids, almost always paired early with a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant (methotrexate or azathioprine) to control disease and limit steroid toxicity; IVIG is added for refractory or severe disease. Plasmapheresis (B) is not effective as monotherapy in inflammatory myopathy. Hydroxychloroquine (C) helps the cutaneous manifestations but does not adequately treat the muscle disease. Observation with physical therapy alone (D) is inadequate for active myositis with a CK of 4,200 and progressive weakness—immunosuppression is required, and PT is adjunctive. Monitoring CK and strength guides taper.

cases.diagnosis: Dermatomyositis (anti-Mi-2 positive) with paraneoplastic malignancy risk
pearls: Heliotrope rash and Gottron papules with symmetric proximal weakness are pathognomonic for dermatomyositis.
Workup includes CK/aldolase, myositis antibodies, EMG, and MRI or muscle biopsy.
Adult dermatomyositis is associated with occult malignancy—pursue age- and risk-appropriate cancer screening.
Treat with high-dose corticosteroids plus an early steroid-sparing agent (methotrexate or azathioprine).

cases.title: Weak Legs Without a Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: Two months of progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from chairs.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents with two months of gradually worsening weakness, primarily in her thighs and shoulders. She struggles to climb stairs, get up from low chairs, and lift grocery bags onto a high shelf. She denies any rash, joint swelling, dry eyes or mouth, or Raynaud phenomenon. She reports mild dysphagia for solids but no facial weakness, ptosis, or double vision, and no sensory changes. She has had no recent statin use or new medications. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. She works as a librarian. Family history is noncontributory for muscle disease. Review of systems is negative for fevers, weight loss, chest pain, and dyspnea, though she notes some general fatigue. She is concerned because the weakness is interfering with her daily activities and is slowly worsening.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman. Skin exam is normal: no heliotrope rash, no Gottron papules, no V-sign or shawl-sign erythema, no sclerodactyly or telangiectasia. Muscle strength is symmetrically reduced in proximal muscle groups: deltoids and hip flexors 4-/5, with normal distal strength (5/5). Neck flexor weakness is present. There is no muscle tenderness. Deep tendon reflexes are normal and symmetric; sensation is intact throughout. There is no fatigable ptosis or ocular muscle weakness. Lungs are clear. No synovitis, lymphadenopathy, or organomegaly. Cranial nerve exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Symmetric proximal weakness without any skin findings and with normal reflexes/sensation most suggests which category of disease?
options: An inflammatory myopathy
A neuromuscular junction disorder (myasthenia gravis)
An upper motor neuron lesion
A peripheral neuropathy in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Symmetric proximal weakness with intact sensation and normal reflexes, no skin rash, and no fatigability points to an inflammatory myopathy such as polymyositis. A peripheral neuropathy (A) typically causes distal weakness with sensory loss and diminished reflexes—not seen here. Myasthenia gravis (C) produces fatigable weakness with ocular/bulbar involvement (ptosis, diplopia) and normal muscle enzymes; her steady proximal weakness without ocular signs argues against it. An upper motor neuron lesion (D) causes hyperreflexia, spasticity, and an upgoing toe, which are absent. The pattern—proximal, symmetric, painless weakness with no rash—is classic for polymyositis and warrants enzyme and electrodiagnostic testing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which initial workup is most appropriate to evaluate suspected inflammatory myopathy?
options: Acetylcholine receptor and MuSK antibodies
Creatine kinase, aldolase, TSH, myositis antibodies,
Lumbar puncture for cytoalbuminologic dissociation
Nerve conduction studies looking for demyelination only
stages.explanation: Evaluation of suspected polymyositis begins with muscle enzymes (CK, aldolase), TSH to exclude hypothyroid myopathy, myositis-specific/associated antibodies, and EMG to confirm a myopathic, irritable pattern; muscle biopsy then confirms the diagnosis. Nerve conduction studies for demyelination (B) target peripheral neuropathies, not myopathy. Acetylcholine receptor/MuSK antibodies (C) test for myasthenia gravis, which the clinical picture does not support. Lumbar puncture for cytoalbuminologic dissociation (D) evaluates Guillain-Barré syndrome, an acute demyelinating polyneuropathy with areflexia—inconsistent with this subacute proximal myopathy. Enzymes plus EMG, with TSH to exclude a mimic, are the right first steps.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatine kinase: 6,800 U/L
Aldolase: elevated
AST: 110 U/L, ALT: 95 U/L
TSH: normal
ANA: positive, 1:160
Anti-Jo-1: negative
Anti-SRP: negative
EMG: short-duration, low-amplitude polyphasic units with fibrillations and positive sharp waves (irritable myopathy)
Muscle biopsy: endomysial inflammatory infiltrate (CD8+ T cells) surrounding and invading non-necrotic myofibers; no perifascicular atrophy; no rimmed vacuoles

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The muscle biopsy shows CD8+ T-cell endomysial infiltrate invading non-necrotic fibers without perifascicular atrophy or rimmed vacuoles. Which diagnosis does this support?
options: Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy
Polymyositis
Inclusion body myositis
Dermatomyositis
stages.explanation: Endomysial inflammation with CD8+ T cells surrounding and invading non-necrotic myofibers is the characteristic biopsy finding of polymyositis. Dermatomyositis (A) shows perifascicular atrophy with perivascular CD4+/B-cell infiltrates and complement deposition, plus the typical rashes—absent here. Inclusion body myositis (B) shows rimmed vacuoles and tends to cause distal and asymmetric weakness (e.g., finger flexors, quadriceps) in older patients, not the symmetric proximal pattern here; biopsy shows no rimmed vacuoles. Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (D) shows scattered necrotic/regenerating fibers with sparse inflammation, often with anti-SRP or anti-HMGCR antibodies. The CD8+/endomysial pattern confirms polymyositis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her polymyositis?
options: Intravenous immunoglobulin as first-line monotherapy as initial therapy
High-dose corticosteroids with an early steroid-sparing agent
Statin discontinuation and observation as initial therapy
Rituximab as the initial agent as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for polymyositis is high-dose corticosteroids, typically combined early with a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant (methotrexate or azathioprine) to improve disease control and limit steroid toxicity, with response tracked by strength and falling CK. IVIG (B) is reserved for refractory or severe disease or when steroids must be avoided, not as routine first-line monotherapy. Statin discontinuation/observation (C) applies to statin-associated myopathy, but she takes no statin and has biopsy-proven inflammatory myositis, so observation is inappropriate. Rituximab (D) is used for refractory disease, not initial therapy. Glucocorticoids plus an early steroid-sparing agent is the standard initial regimen.

cases.diagnosis: Polymyositis (CD8+ endomysial inflammatory myopathy without rash)
pearls: Polymyositis causes symmetric proximal weakness with elevated CK but no rash—distinguishing it from dermatomyositis.
Biopsy shows CD8+ T-cell endomysial inflammation invading non-necrotic fibers; dermatomyositis shows perifascicular atrophy.
Always check TSH and a medication/statin history to exclude myopathy mimics before committing to immunosuppression.
First-line therapy is high-dose corticosteroids plus an early steroid-sparing agent such as methotrexate or azathioprine.

cases.title: Dry Eyes, Dry Mouth, and Swollen Cheeks
cases.chiefComplaint: Two years of dry, gritty eyes and a persistently dry mouth, now with swelling of the cheeks.
cases.history: A 56-year-old woman presents with two years of progressively dry, gritty eyes that feel like sand, requiring frequent artificial tears, and a dry mouth that makes swallowing dry foods difficult and has led to multiple new dental cavities. She keeps water by her bedside and wakes to sip it. Over the past few months she has noticed intermittent bilateral swelling near the angles of her jaw. She reports fatigue and arthralgias but denies a malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or Raynaud. She denies fevers, weight loss, or night sweats. PMH includes hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history includes a daughter with lupus and an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. She works as a chef. Review of systems is positive for vaginal dryness and a dry cough, but negative for hematuria or significant dyspnea. She is bothered most by the eye and mouth dryness and the new facial swelling.
cases.exam: Comfortable woman. Eyes appear dry with reduced tear film; conjunctivae are mildly injected without discharge. The oral mucosa is dry and sticky with scant pooled saliva, the tongue is dry and fissured, and there is dental decay at the gumline. There is bilateral, firm, nontender enlargement of the parotid glands. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No malar or discoid rash. Mild tenderness without overt synovitis at the small hand joints. Thyroid is normal in size. Lungs are clear. No hepatosplenomegaly. Skin shows no purpura currently. Neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of dry eyes, dry mouth, and parotid enlargement most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Sarcoidosis in this clinical context
IgG4-related disease
Sjögren syndrome
Medication-induced xerostomia
stages.explanation: Sicca symptoms (dry eyes and dry mouth) with bilateral parotid enlargement and accelerated dental caries are classic for Sjögren syndrome, an autoimmune exocrinopathy. Sarcoidosis (A) can cause parotid swelling and sicca (e.g., Heerfordt syndrome) but typically has hilar adenopathy, granulomas, and other systemic features; it is less likely given the chronic isolated sicca picture. IgG4-related disease (C) can cause salivary/lacrimal enlargement but usually shows elevated IgG4 with characteristic histology and other organ involvement, and is less common. Medication-induced xerostomia (D) would not explain parotid enlargement or the autoimmune family history, and she takes only levothyroxine. The pattern fits Sjögren best, warranting confirmatory serology and objective dryness testing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of tests best supports the diagnosis of Sjögren syndrome?
options: Anti-centromere and anti-Scl-70 antibodies as the primary diagnosis
Anti-dsDNA and complement levels as the primary diagnosis
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP only as the primary diagnosis
Anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies, objective ocular dryness testing , and minor salivary gland biopsy
stages.explanation: Sjögren is supported by anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies, objective measures of dryness (Schirmer test, ocular surface staining, reduced salivary flow), and a minor salivary gland (lip) biopsy showing focal lymphocytic sialadenitis with a focus score ≥1. Anti-dsDNA/complement (A) are SLE-oriented tests; while overlap exists, they are not the core Sjögren panel. RF and anti-CCP (C) evaluate rheumatoid arthritis (RF can be positive in Sjögren but is nonspecific). Anti-centromere/Scl-70 (D) are scleroderma antibodies. The triad of specific serologies, objective dryness, and lip biopsy is the standard diagnostic approach for Sjögren syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ANA: positive, 1:320
Anti-Ro/SSA: positive
Anti-La/SSB: positive
Rheumatoid factor: positive
Anti-CCP: negative
ESR: 42 mm/hr
Schirmer test: 3 mm wetting in 5 min (markedly reduced)
Minor salivary gland (lip) biopsy: focal lymphocytic sialadenitis, focus score 2
Urinalysis: pH 6.8, no protein; basic metabolic panel notable for low serum bicarbonate (distal RTA suspected)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management of her sicca symptoms?
options: Cyclophosphamide as initial therapy
High-dose systemic corticosteroids as initial therapy
Topical artificial tears/lubricants, good oral/dental hygiene,
Immediate rituximab as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management of Sjögren sicca symptoms is symptomatic and conservative: artificial tears and ocular lubricants, meticulous dental care and saliva substitutes, and muscarinic secretagogues (pilocarpine or cevimeline) to stimulate residual gland function. Hydroxychloroquine is often added for fatigue and arthralgias. High-dose systemic steroids (A) are reserved for significant systemic/extraglandular involvement, not routine sicca. Rituximab (C) is considered for severe systemic disease (e.g., vasculitis, severe parotid disease), not first-line for dryness. Cyclophosphamide (D) is reserved for severe organ-threatening manifestations. Symptom-directed therapy plus secretagogues is the appropriate starting point for the dominant sicca complaints.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which serious long-term complication should patients with Sjögren syndrome be monitored for, especially with persistent parotid swelling?
options: Non-Hodgkin B-cell lymphoma
Multiple myeloma in this presentation
Hepatocellular carcinoma
Pheochromocytoma in this presentation
stages.explanation: Sjögren syndrome carries a substantially increased risk of B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma, most often mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) lymphoma; persistent or unilateral firm parotid enlargement, new lymphadenopathy, cryoglobulinemia, low complement, or a falling RF should prompt evaluation. Hepatocellular carcinoma (A) is linked to chronic hepatitis/cirrhosis, not Sjögren. Multiple myeloma (C) is a plasma-cell malignancy not characteristically associated with Sjögren. Pheochromocytoma (D) is unrelated. Recognizing the lymphoma association—and the warning signs in the salivary glands—is a key part of long-term Sjögren care.

cases.diagnosis: Primary Sjögren syndrome (anti-Ro/anti-La positive, lip biopsy confirmed) with parotid involvement
pearls: Sicca symptoms plus parotid enlargement and accelerated dental caries point to Sjögren syndrome.
Diagnosis rests on anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB, objective dryness testing, and lip biopsy showing focal lymphocytic sialadenitis (focus score ≥1).
Initial therapy is symptomatic—lubricants, dental care, and muscarinic agonists (pilocarpine/cevimeline); hydroxychloroquine for fatigue/arthralgia.
Sjögren raises the risk of MALT B-cell lymphoma—watch for persistent parotid swelling, lymphadenopathy, and falling complement.

cases.title: Chronic Sinus Trouble, Cough, and Bloody Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of refractory sinus symptoms and cough, now admitted with blood in the urine.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man is admitted after months of chronic nasal congestion, recurrent epistaxis, crusting, and purulent sinus drainage unresponsive to multiple antibiotic courses. He developed a cough productive of occasionally blood-streaked sputum and was found to have abnormal kidney function and blood in his urine at an outpatient visit. He reports malaise, low-grade fevers, 5 kg weight loss, and migratory arthralgias. He notes a change in the shape of his nose. He denies asthma, prior allergies, or eosinophilia history. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks rarely. He works as a carpenter. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for new hearing reduction and intermittent red, painful eyes; negative for hemoptysis in large volume, rash, or peripheral neuropathy currently. He is admitted for evaluation of suspected systemic disease with renal involvement.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing man. Nasal exam reveals mucosal crusting, septal perforation, and a depressed nasal bridge (saddle-nose deformity). There is conjunctival injection of the right eye consistent with scleritis. Otoscopy shows a dull, retracted tympanic membrane with conductive hearing loss. Lungs have scattered crackles without wheeze. Cardiac exam is normal. There is mild lower-extremity edema. No palpable purpura currently and no synovitis. There is no wheezing and no nasal polyps. Neurologic exam is intact without foot drop. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Upper airway disease (sinusitis, saddle-nose), lung involvement, and glomerulonephritis together most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
options: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease)
IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura)
Microscopic polyangiitis
stages.explanation: The triad of upper respiratory tract disease (chronic sinusitis, nasal crusting, saddle-nose deformity), lower respiratory involvement (nodules/hemorrhage), and glomerulonephritis is classic for granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA). Goodpasture syndrome (A) causes pulmonary-renal disease but spares the upper airway/sinuses and does not cause saddle-nose or granulomas. Microscopic polyangiitis (C) causes pulmonary-renal vasculitis but classically lacks granulomatous upper-airway destruction and saddle-nose deformity. IgA vasculitis (D) causes palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthralgia, and IgA nephropathy—not destructive sinus disease or saddle-nose. The prominent ENT involvement points specifically to GPA.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of tests is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Anti-GBM antibody as the sole confirmatory test as the primary diagnosis
Blood cultures and echocardiography only as the primary diagnosis
Skin biopsy of a purpuric lesion only as the primary diagnosis
ANCA testing , urinalysis with microscopy, chest imaging,
stages.explanation: Diagnosis of GPA combines serology (c-ANCA with anti-proteinase 3/PR3), urinalysis with microscopy (looking for dysmorphic RBCs and red cell casts of glomerulonephritis), chest imaging (nodules, cavities, infiltrates), and tissue biopsy (renal showing pauci-immune crescentic GN, or sinus/lung showing necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with vasculitis). Blood cultures/echo (B) evaluate endocarditis, a mimic, but won't confirm GPA. Anti-GBM alone (C) tests for Goodpasture, a different disease. A skin biopsy of purpura alone (D) is nonspecific and he has no purpura. The multi-pronged approach—ANCA, urine, imaging, biopsy—establishes the diagnosis and assesses organ involvement.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
c-ANCA: positive
Anti-PR3: strongly positive
Anti-MPO: negative
Anti-GBM: negative
Creatinine: 2.6 mg/dL (baseline ~1.0)
Urinalysis: 3+ blood, 2+ protein; dysmorphic RBCs and red cell casts
CT chest: multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, some cavitating
ESR: 88 mm/hr; CRP: elevated
Renal biopsy: pauci-immune focal necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has organ-threatening GPA with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. What is the most appropriate induction therapy?
options: Long-term antibiotics alone as initial therapy
Methotrexate monotherapy as initial therapy
Hydroxychloroquine and NSAIDs as initial therapy
High-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab
stages.explanation: Severe, organ-threatening ANCA-associated vasculitis (here, rapidly progressive GN and cavitary lung disease) is treated with induction using high-dose glucocorticoids combined with rituximab or cyclophosphamide; plasma exchange may be considered in select cases (e.g., severe renal failure or pulmonary hemorrhage). Methotrexate (B) is only appropriate for limited, non-organ-threatening disease, not crescentic GN. Hydroxychloroquine/NSAIDs (C) have no role in inducing remission of systemic vasculitis. Long-term antibiotics alone (D) treat infection (and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole has an adjunct role in limited upper-airway GPA and PJP prophylaxis), but cannot control this severe systemic vasculitis. Glucocorticoids plus rituximab/cyclophosphamide is the standard induction.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After achieving remission with rituximab-based induction, what is the appropriate next phase of management?
options: Maintenance immunosuppression to prevent relapse, with monitoring for relapse
Stop all immunosuppression immediately once remission is achieved with appropriate follow-up
No further monitoring is needed if ANCA becomes negative with appropriate follow-up
Lifelong high-dose cyclophosphamide with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After induction, ANCA-associated vasculitis requires a maintenance phase with lower-intensity immunosuppression (commonly rituximab or azathioprine) for a sustained period to prevent relapse, along with ongoing monitoring of renal function, urinalysis, and symptoms, plus infection prophylaxis. Stopping all immunosuppression immediately (A) leads to high relapse rates. Lifelong high-dose cyclophosphamide (C) is unacceptably toxic (malignancy, infertility, cytopenias) and is not used for maintenance. Assuming no monitoring is needed once ANCA normalizes (D) is wrong—ANCA titers correlate imperfectly with disease activity, and relapses occur, so continued surveillance is essential. PJP prophylaxis is also important during immunosuppression.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Months later, while on maintenance therapy, he develops progressive hoarseness, biphasic stridor, and exertional dyspnea with normal lungs on auscultation. Which GPA-related complication is most likely?
options: Reactivation of crescentic glomerulonephritis
Subglottic tracheal stenosis
New diffuse alveolar hemorrhage
Pulmonary embolism as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Progressive hoarseness, biphasic stridor, and dyspnea with clear lung fields point to subglottic (tracheal) stenosis, a characteristic GPA complication from inflammatory scarring of the airway that can occur even when systemic disease is otherwise quiescent; it is evaluated by laryngoscopy/airway imaging and often managed with intralesional steroid injection and dilation rather than escalation of systemic immunosuppression alone. Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (A) would cause hypoxia, hemoptysis, and alveolar infiltrates—not isolated stridor with clear lungs. Pulmonary embolism (C) causes acute dyspnea and hypoxia but not fixed stridor and hoarseness. Reactivation of glomerulonephritis (D) presents with rising creatinine, hematuria, and casts, not airway obstruction. Stridor localizes the lesion to the large airway.

cases.diagnosis: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (c-ANCA/anti-PR3) with crescentic glomerulonephritis and saddle-nose deformity
pearls: Upper airway destruction (sinusitis, saddle-nose), lung nodules/cavities, and glomerulonephritis are the classic GPA triad.
GPA is associated with c-ANCA/anti-PR3; renal biopsy shows pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis.
Severe organ-threatening disease is induced with glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide; consider plasma exchange in select cases.
After induction, maintenance immunosuppression and ongoing monitoring are required—ANCA titers correlate imperfectly with activity.

cases.title: Breathless and Coughing Blood with Failing Kidneys
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of worsening cough with blood-tinged sputum and breathlessness, now with declining kidney function.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man is admitted with one week of progressive dyspnea and cough productive of blood-tinged sputum. Over the preceding two months he had fatigue, low-grade fevers, weight loss, and migratory myalgias. Outpatient labs before admission showed a rising creatinine and abnormal urine. He denies chronic sinusitis, nasal crusting, asthma, or nasal polyps. He has had no large-volume hemoptysis previously. PMH includes hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily. No known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks rarely. He is a retired postal worker. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for new tingling and weakness in one foot; negative for saddle-nose changes, hearing loss, or wheezing. On admission he is hypoxic and tachypneic. He is being evaluated for a pulmonary-renal syndrome with suspected systemic vasculitis.
cases.exam: Tachypneic man using accessory muscles. Diffuse inspiratory crackles are heard bilaterally. Nasal exam is normal with no crusting, perforation, or saddle-nose deformity. There are no nasal polyps and no wheeze. There is right foot drop with reduced sensation over the dorsum (mononeuritis multiplex). Skin shows scattered palpable purpura on the lower legs. There is mild pedal edema. Cardiac exam is normal. No lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Pulmonary hemorrhage and glomerulonephritis WITHOUT upper-airway/granulomatous disease or asthma most suggests which diagnosis?
options: Microscopic polyangiitis
Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Polyarteritis nodosa in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A pulmonary-renal syndrome (alveolar hemorrhage plus glomerulonephritis) with mononeuritis multiplex and palpable purpura, but WITHOUT destructive upper-airway/granulomatous disease and without asthma/eosinophilia, is characteristic of microscopic polyangiitis (MPA). Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (A) features prominent sinus/nasal destruction, saddle-nose, and granulomatous inflammation—absent here. Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (C) requires asthma and eosinophilia, which he lacks. Polyarteritis nodosa (D) is a medium-vessel vasculitis that classically spares the lungs and does not cause glomerulonephritis (it causes renovascular hypertension/microaneurysms), making it inconsistent with alveolar hemorrhage plus GN. MPA fits the small-vessel pulmonary-renal picture best.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which tests are most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: ANCA , urinalysis with microscopy, chest imaging,
Sputum culture and bronchoscopy alone as the primary diagnosis
Anti-GBM and complement levels as the only studies in this clinical context
Hepatitis B serology to diagnose the vasculitis directly
stages.explanation: MPA is confirmed with ANCA serology (typically p-ANCA with anti-myeloperoxidase/MPO), urinalysis with microscopy (dysmorphic RBCs, red cell casts), chest imaging (diffuse alveolar infiltrates of hemorrhage), and renal biopsy showing pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis. Sputum culture/bronchoscopy (B) help exclude infection and document hemorrhage but do not establish vasculitis. Anti-GBM and complement (C) help exclude Goodpasture and immune-complex disease but are not sufficient to confirm MPA. Hepatitis B serology (D) is relevant to polyarteritis nodosa, not MPA. The combination of ANCA, urine microscopy, imaging, and biopsy both confirms MPA and grades organ involvement.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
p-ANCA: positive
Anti-MPO: strongly positive
Anti-PR3: negative
Anti-GBM: negative
Creatinine: 3.4 mg/dL (baseline ~1.1)
Urinalysis: 3+ blood, 2+ protein; dysmorphic RBCs and red cell casts
Hemoglobin: 8.9 g/dL
CT chest: bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities consistent with alveolar hemorrhage
Renal biopsy: pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis; no immune complex deposits on immunofluorescence

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has diffuse alveolar hemorrhage and rapidly progressive GN (creatinine 3.4). What is the most appropriate management?
options: Broad-spectrum antibiotics alone for presumed pneumonia as initial therapy
Hemodialysis without immunosuppression as initial therapy
High-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide, with plasma exchange considered for severe disease
Methotrexate monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Severe ANCA-associated vasculitis with diffuse alveolar hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis requires aggressive induction: high-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide, with plasma exchange considered for severe presentations such as alveolar hemorrhage or severe renal failure. Antibiotics alone (A) treat infection, a mimic to exclude, but will not control the vasculitis driving hemorrhage and GN. Hemodialysis without immunosuppression (C) addresses uremia but ignores the underlying immunologic disease and risks progression. Methotrexate (D) is inappropriate for organ-threatening disease and is avoided in significant renal impairment. Prompt immunosuppression is critical to halt alveolar hemorrhage and preserve renal function.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The right foot drop with sensory loss in this vasculitis reflects which complication?
options: Diabetic peripheral polyneuropathy as the leading concern
A central (upper motor neuron) stroke as the leading concern
Mononeuritis multiplex from vasculitic involvement of the vasa nervorum
Guillain-Barré syndrome as the leading concern
A lumbar disc herniation as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Asymmetric involvement of named peripheral nerves (e.g., common peroneal causing foot drop) with sensory loss is mononeuritis multiplex, caused by vasculitic ischemia of the vasa nervorum—a hallmark of ANCA-associated and other systemic vasculitides. A central stroke (A) would produce upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, spasticity), not an isolated peripheral foot drop with distal sensory loss. Diabetic polyneuropathy (C) is symmetric and length-dependent, not abrupt and asymmetric, and he has no diabetes. A lumbar disc herniation (D) could cause foot drop but not the systemic vasculitic context or multifocal pattern. Guillain-Barré (E) is symmetric, ascending, and areflexic with cytoalbuminologic dissociation—inconsistent with focal mononeuritis multiplex.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After induction achieves remission of his MPA, which long-term plan is most appropriate?
options: Schedule routine plasma exchange every month with appropriate follow-up
Maintenance immunosuppression with periodic monitoring of renal function, urinalysis,
Discontinue all therapy and rely on symptom recurrence to detect relapse with appropriate follow-up
Indefinite high-dose cyclophosphamide to guarantee remission with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Like other ANCA-associated vasculitides, MPA relapses frequently, so after induction patients need a maintenance phase with lower-intensity immunosuppression (rituximab or azathioprine) and ongoing surveillance—serial creatinine, urinalysis for hematuria/casts, and symptom review—with infection prophylaxis (e.g., PJP) during immunosuppression. Discontinuing all therapy (A) leads to high relapse rates and risks irreversible renal loss before symptoms reappear. Indefinite high-dose cyclophosphamide (C) is far too toxic for maintenance, causing cytopenias, infertility, and malignancy. Routine monthly plasma exchange (D) is reserved for acute severe disease (alveolar hemorrhage, severe AKI), not maintenance. Structured maintenance plus monitoring optimizes long-term renal and patient survival.

cases.diagnosis: Microscopic polyangiitis (p-ANCA/anti-MPO) with pulmonary-renal syndrome and mononeuritis multiplex
pearls: MPA causes a pulmonary-renal syndrome without the granulomatous upper-airway destruction or saddle-nose seen in GPA.
MPA is associated with p-ANCA/anti-MPO; renal biopsy shows pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis.
Severe disease (alveolar hemorrhage, RPGN) is induced with steroids plus rituximab/cyclophosphamide; consider plasma exchange.
Mononeuritis multiplex (e.g., asymmetric foot drop) reflects vasculitic ischemia of the vasa nervorum.

cases.title: New Adult Asthma, Numb Foot, and a High Eosinophil Count
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening asthma and a newly weak, numb foot, admitted after labs showed very high eosinophils.
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman is admitted with poorly controlled asthma that began in adulthood three years ago and has required escalating inhaled and oral steroids, along with chronic sinusitis and nasal polyps. Over the past few weeks she developed migratory leg pains, a new rash, and weakness with numbness of her right foot. She reports fatigue, low-grade fevers, and weight loss. Outpatient labs showed a markedly elevated eosinophil count. She denies hemoptysis, saddle-nose changes, or hematuria currently. PMH includes adult-onset asthma, allergic rhinitis, and nasal polyposis. Medications: high-dose inhaled corticosteroid-LABA, montelukast, intermittent prednisone tapers, and a nasal steroid. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. She is a schoolteacher. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for paresthesias of the right foot and new skin lesions; negative for chronic nasal crusting, large-volume hemoptysis, and significant chest pain. She is admitted for evaluation of suspected eosinophilic systemic disease.
cases.exam: Mildly ill-appearing woman with audible expiratory wheeze. Nasal exam shows boggy mucosa with polyps; there is no septal perforation or saddle-nose deformity. Lungs have diffuse expiratory wheezes and scattered crackles. There is right foot drop with reduced sensation over the dorsum and lateral foot (mononeuritis multiplex). Skin shows palpable purpura on the lower legs and a few tender subcutaneous nodules. Cardiac exam reveals a normal rhythm without murmur, but there is mild dependent edema. No nasal crusting and no large-joint synovitis. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Adult-onset asthma with nasal polyps, marked eosinophilia, and mononeuritis multiplex most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Microscopic polyangiitis in this clinical context
Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia without vasculitis
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
stages.explanation: The combination of adult-onset asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, prominent peripheral eosinophilia, and vasculitic features (mononeuritis multiplex, purpura) is characteristic of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly Churg-Strauss). Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (A) features destructive sinus disease, saddle-nose, and PR3-ANCA, typically without asthma or marked eosinophilia. Microscopic polyangiitis (C) causes pulmonary-renal vasculitis with anti-MPO but lacks asthma, nasal polyps, and eosinophilia. Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia (D) causes eosinophilic lung infiltrates but does not explain mononeuritis multiplex, purpura, and systemic vasculitis. The asthma–eosinophilia–vasculitis triad makes EGPA the leading diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which workup best supports the diagnosis and assesses organ involvement?
options: Echocardiography as the sole diagnostic test as the primary diagnosis
Allergy skin-prick panel only as the primary diagnosis
CBC with differential , ANCA , urinalysis, chest imaging,
Sweat chloride testing as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: EGPA evaluation includes a CBC with differential documenting eosinophilia, ANCA testing (p-ANCA/anti-MPO is positive in roughly 40%), urinalysis to assess renal involvement, chest imaging for migratory/eosinophilic infiltrates, and biopsy of an affected site (showing eosinophilic infiltration, necrotizing vasculitis, and/or extravascular granulomas). An allergy skin-prick panel (B) characterizes atopy but does not diagnose vasculitis. Sweat chloride (C) tests for cystic fibrosis, unrelated here. Echocardiography alone (D) is important because cardiac involvement drives mortality in EGPA, but it cannot establish the diagnosis by itself. The comprehensive panel confirms EGPA and stages organ damage, including the critical cardiac assessment.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC: 16.5 ×10³/µL with eosinophils 28% (absolute eosinophils ~4,600/µL)
p-ANCA: positive
anti-MPO: positive
anti-PR3: negative
IgE: markedly elevated
Urinalysis: trace blood, no casts
Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL
CT chest: patchy bilateral ground-glass and migratory infiltrates
Nerve conduction study: axonal sensorimotor neuropathy of the right peroneal nerve
Echocardiogram: mildly reduced EF; troponin mildly elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has EGPA with cardiac and neurologic (mononeuritis multiplex) involvement. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Inhaled corticosteroids and montelukast escalation only as initial therapy
Empiric antiparasitic therapy alone as initial therapy
Antihistamines and allergen avoidance as initial therapy
High-dose systemic glucocorticoids plus a second immunosuppressant for organ-threatening disease
stages.explanation: EGPA with poor-prognosis organ involvement—here cardiac disease (reduced EF, elevated troponin) and mononeuritis multiplex—requires high-dose systemic glucocorticoids plus a second immunosuppressant such as cyclophosphamide or rituximab to induce remission. Inhaled steroids/montelukast escalation (A) treats asthma but is grossly inadequate for systemic organ-threatening vasculitis (and leukotriene-receptor antagonists are not a treatment for the vasculitis itself). Antihistamines/allergen avoidance (B for atopy) (C) do not treat vasculitis. Empiric antiparasitic therapy (D) targets hypereosinophilia from helminths, which is excluded by the vasculitic, ANCA-positive picture. Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of death in EGPA, mandating aggressive immunosuppression.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For long-term maintenance of EGPA—particularly to control eosinophilic asthma and reduce steroid dependence in patients without active organ-threatening vasculitis—which targeted biologic is useful?
options: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5)
Adalimumab (anti-TNF)
Ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23)
Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6)
stages.explanation: Mepolizumab, an anti-IL-5 monoclonal antibody, targets the eosinophil-driving cytokine IL-5 and is approved for EGPA; it reduces relapses, controls eosinophilic asthma, and lowers glucocorticoid requirements, particularly in non–organ-threatening or relapsing disease. Adalimumab (B, anti-TNF) is used in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis and inflammatory bowel disease, not EGPA. Tocilizumab (C, anti-IL-6) is used in giant cell arteritis and RA, not as standard EGPA therapy. Ustekinumab (D, anti-IL-12/23) is used in psoriasis/psoriatic arthritis and Crohn disease, not EGPA. Because IL-5 drives eosinophilia, anti-IL-5 therapy is the rational targeted agent for steroid-sparing maintenance in EGPA.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature in this patient most strongly indicates a poor prognosis and justifies the addition of cyclophosphamide or rituximab to steroids?
options: Cardiac involvement
Her elevated total IgE level in this presentation
Her peripheral eosinophil percentage of 28%
The presence of nasal polyps in this presentation
stages.explanation: Cardiomyopathy/cardiac involvement is a poor-prognosis factor in EGPA (a component of the Five-Factor Score, along with significant renal disease, GI involvement, age, and absence of ENT symptoms) and is the leading cause of EGPA-related death—its presence is precisely what escalates therapy from steroids alone to steroids plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab. An elevated IgE (A) and a high eosinophil percentage (D) support the diagnosis and reflect disease activity but are not prognostic factors that mandate cytotoxic therapy. Nasal polyps/ENT involvement (C) are typical of EGPA and, notably, the absence (not presence) of ENT disease is the poor-prognosis item. Recognizing cardiac involvement as the key driver of both mortality and treatment intensity is high-yield.

cases.diagnosis: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, Churg-Strauss), p-ANCA/anti-MPO positive, with cardiac and neurologic involvement
pearls: EGPA classically presents with adult-onset asthma, nasal polyps, marked eosinophilia, and vasculitic features like mononeuritis multiplex.
p-ANCA/anti-MPO is positive in roughly 40% of EGPA; biopsy shows eosinophilic infiltration with necrotizing vasculitis and granulomas.
Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of death in EGPA—always assess with troponin and echocardiography.
Organ-threatening EGPA needs steroids plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab; mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) helps steroid-sparing maintenance.

cases.title: Foot Drop, Belly Pain, and Bloody Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive limb weakness, numbness, flank pain, and weeks of fevers and weight loss.
cases.history: A 49-year-old man is admitted after three weeks of intermittent fevers, malaise, and a 7 kg unintentional weight loss. Two weeks ago he developed right foot drop, then progressive numbness and burning in the left hand and right lower leg in a patchy, asymmetric distribution. He reports new, severe right flank pain and gross hematuria yesterday, plus diffuse postprandial abdominal pain and myalgias. He has hypertension diagnosed 2 months ago, now poorly controlled. He takes amlodipine 5 mg daily. No NSAID use. Allergies: none. Social history: former IV drug use 12 years ago, 20 pack-year smoking history, occasional alcohol. He has no regular physician and reports remote unprotected sexual exposures. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is positive for scrotal/testicular tenderness and livedo-like skin mottling on the legs. He denies sinus disease, hemoptysis, oral ulcers, and dyspnea. No recent travel.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, febrile. Skin: livedo reticularis over both lower extremities and a few tender subcutaneous nodules; no palpable purpura. Neuro: right foot drop with absent dorsiflexion (deep peroneal), reduced sensation in left ulnar and right superficial peroneal territories — an asymmetric, multifocal pattern; reflexes diminished asymmetrically. Abdomen: diffuse tenderness without peritoneal signs; right flank tenderness. GU: tender right testicle. Lungs clear. No sinus tenderness, no nasal crusting, no synovitis. No conjunctival or oral lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single descriptor best characterizes this patient's neurologic findings and most narrows the differential?
options: Length-dependent symmetric polyneuropathy
Proximal symmetric myopathic weakness
Symmetric distal stocking-glove sensory loss
Mononeuritis multiplex
Cauda equina syndrome as the first step today
stages.explanation: The picture — right foot drop (deep peroneal), plus left ulnar and right superficial peroneal sensory loss occurring stepwise and asymmetrically — is classic mononeuritis multiplex, infarction of named nerves from vasculitis of the vasa nervorum. This pattern points strongly toward a systemic vasculitis. A stocking-glove pattern (option A) and length-dependent polyneuropathy (option C) are symmetric and typical of diabetes/toxic causes, not focal nerve infarcts. Cauda equina (option D) causes saddle anesthesia and bowel/bladder dysfunction, not isolated peripheral named-nerve deficits. Proximal myopathy (option E) is weakness without the sensory and focal features seen here. Recognizing mononeuritis multiplex reframes the multisystem illness (renal, GI, skin, constitutional) as likely vasculitic.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 10.9 g/dL
Platelets 488 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
BUN 32 mg/dL
ESR 88 mm/hr
CRP 14.2 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 2+ blood, 30–40 RBC/hpf, no dysmorphic RBCs, no RBC casts, trace protein
ANCA (MPO and PR3): negative
ANA: negative
C3/C4: normal
Hepatitis B surface antigen: positive; HBeAg positive; HBV DNA 4.2 ×10⁶ IU/mL
Hepatitis C antibody: negative
HIV: negative
Cryoglobulins: negative
CT abdomen/pelvis: wedge-shaped right renal infarct; multiple small renal and mesenteric arterial aneurysms

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given negative ANCA with renal infarcts, microaneurysms, and active hepatitis B, what is the most appropriate confirmatory diagnostic step?
options: Renal biopsy to look for pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis in this setting
Schirmer test and minor salivary gland biopsy as the immediate next step
Anti-GBM antibody testing as the immediate next step
Conventional mesenteric/renal angiography demonstrating medium-vessel microaneurysms
Temporal artery biopsy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: This is medium-vessel vasculitis: ANCA-negative, no glomerulonephritis (bland sediment, no RBC casts, no dysmorphic RBCs), renal infarction, and HBV association. Visceral angiography showing microaneurysms and stenoses of medium arteries is the confirmatory test and can substitute for biopsy when accessible tissue is lacking. Renal biopsy (option A) is for glomerular/small-vessel disease — here the sediment is bland and the kidney shows infarction, so biopsy risks bleeding into an aneurysm and is low-yield. Anti-GBM (option C) targets pulmonary-renal small-vessel disease, absent here. Temporal artery biopsy (option D) is for large-vessel GCA in older patients with cranial symptoms. Sjögren workup (option E) is unrelated. Nerve/muscle or skin nodule biopsy is the alternative tissue confirmation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: IgA vasculitis as the primary diagnosis
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Polyarteritis nodosa associated with hepatitis B
Microscopic polyangiitis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Medium-vessel vasculitis with mononeuritis multiplex, renal and mesenteric microaneurysms with renal infarction (not glomerulonephritis), orchitis, livedo, hypertension, constitutional symptoms, negative ANCA, and active HBV is classic polyarteritis nodosa, frequently HBV-driven. GPA (option A) and MPA (option B) are ANCA-associated small-vessel diseases causing glomerulonephritis (RBC casts) and, for GPA, upper-airway/lung granulomas — all absent. IgA vasculitis (option D) causes palpable purpura, IgA-dominant glomerulonephritis, and arthralgia, usually in children, not microaneurysms or HBV. EGPA (option E) features asthma, eosinophilia, and ANCA in ~40%; this patient lacks asthma and eosinophilia. PAN characteristically spares the lungs, consistent with his clear chest.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature, if present, would most strongly indicate organ- or life-threatening disease (a poor-prognosis 'Five-Factor Score' item) requiring more aggressive therapy?
options: Mild arthralgias as the leading concern
Renal insufficiency , significant proteinuria, or cardiac, GI, or CNS involvement
Mild normocytic anemia as the leading concern
Isolated skin nodules as the leading concern
Low-grade fever alone as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The Five-Factor Score identifies poor-prognosis PAN: renal insufficiency (elevated creatinine), proteinuria >1 g/day, and cardiac, gastrointestinal, or CNS involvement predict higher mortality and justify adding cyclophosphamide to steroids (in idiopathic PAN) or intensifying therapy. This patient's rising creatinine, renal infarction, and GI involvement place him in a higher-risk category. Isolated skin nodules (option A), mild arthralgias (option B), low-grade fever (option C), and mild anemia (option E) are not severity markers that change the treatment intensity. Recognizing which organ involvements signal severe disease guides escalation and prognosis discussions, and in the HBV setting reinforces the need for antivirals plus plasma exchange when severe.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment strategy for HBV-associated polyarteritis nodosa?
options: Rituximab monotherapy as initial therapy
A short course of glucocorticoids plus antiviral therapy , often with plasma exchange for severe disease
Methotrexate plus a TNF inhibitor as initial therapy
High-dose glucocorticoids plus prolonged cyclophosphamide as sole therapy as initial therapy
Glucocorticoids alone, tapered over several weeks, with no antiviral as initial therapy
stages.explanation: HBV-associated PAN is treated differently from idiopathic PAN. Prolonged immunosuppression promotes viral replication, so the strategy is a brief course of corticosteroids to control life-threatening inflammation, started together with effective nucleos(t)ide antiviral therapy (entecavir or tenofovir), and plasma exchange to clear immune complexes in severe organ-threatening disease. Steroids are stopped early once inflammation is controlled, letting seroconversion clear immune complexes. Prolonged cyclophosphamide alone (option A) worsens viral replication and is reserved as add-on only for severe idiopathic PAN. Steroids alone without antiviral (option B) fuels HBV. Rituximab (option D) depletes B cells and risks HBV reactivation; it is not standard for PAN. MTX plus TNF inhibitor (option E) is not indicated and TNF inhibitors risk HBV reactivation.

pearls: PAN is a medium-vessel vasculitis: think microaneurysms and infarcts (renal, mesenteric) rather than glomerulonephritis, and it is classically ANCA-negative.
Mononeuritis multiplex plus orchitis, livedo, new hypertension, and constitutional symptoms in an ANCA-negative patient should prompt evaluation for PAN.
Always check hepatitis B in suspected PAN — HBV-associated disease is treated with antivirals plus short-course steroids (and plasma exchange if severe), not prolonged immunosuppression.
PAN characteristically spares the lungs; prominent pulmonary involvement should redirect toward ANCA-associated vasculitis.

cases.title: Recurrent Mouth and Groin Sores with a Red, Painful Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated painful mouth and genital ulcers now accompanied by a red, painful, light-sensitive eye.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man presents with a two-year history of recurrent, painful oral ulcers occurring 6–8 times per year, each lasting 1–2 weeks and healing without scarring. Over the past year he has had three episodes of painful ulcers on the scrotum, two of which left scars. He now reports five days of a red, painful, photophobic right eye with blurring. He has intermittent inflammatory knee and ankle arthralgias and tender red nodules on his shins. He recalls pustules forming at sites where blood was recently drawn. He takes no medications. Allergies: none. He emigrated from Turkey six years ago. He smokes occasionally and denies IV drug use; he is in a monogamous relationship and HSV/syphilis testing was negative last year during a prior ulcer workup. Family history: a maternal uncle had recurrent oral ulcers and an eye problem. Review of systems is negative for diarrhea, bloody stool, headache, and focal weakness.
cases.exam: Two shallow aphthous ulcers with erythematous halos on the buccal mucosa; a healing scarred ulcer on the scrotum. Right eye: conjunctival injection with ciliary flush; slit-lamp (performed by ophthalmology) shows anterior chamber cells consistent with anterior uveitis; no hypopyon today. Skin: two tender erythema nodosum-like nodules on the anterior shins; a fresh erythematous papulopustule at an antecubital venipuncture site. Joints: mild ankle tenderness without effusion. No genital discharge, no lymphadenopathy, no oral candidal plaques, no neurologic deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside finding, if reproducible, is most specific and supportive of the unifying diagnosis?
options: Positive Nikolsky sign as the first step today
Pathergy — a sterile papule/pustule forming at a needlestick site within 24–48 hours
Positive Tzanck smear as the first step today
Positive Romberg sign as the first step today
Auspitz sign as the first step today
stages.explanation: Pathergy is exaggerated skin injury hyperreactivity: a needle prick produces a sterile papule or pustule at 24–48 hours. It is specific (though not highly sensitive, and more often positive in Mediterranean/Middle Eastern patients) for the disease suggested by recurrent oral plus genital ulcers and uveitis, and his venipuncture-site pustule is a spontaneous example. Nikolsky sign (option B) indicates pemphigus/SJS-TEN epidermal detachment. Tzanck smear (option C) detects herpetic multinucleated giant cells — already excluded by negative HSV testing. Auspitz sign (option D) is pinpoint bleeding in psoriasis. Romberg (option E) tests proprioception and is unrelated.
