The combination of mucocutaneous ulcers, uveitis, erythema nodosum, and pathergy is the key pattern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome)
Systemic lupus erythematosus in this clinical context
Behcet disease
Recurrent herpes simplex with reactive arthritis
Crohn disease with oral and perianal involvement
stages.explanation: Recurrent oral aphthae plus recurrent genital ulcers, anterior uveitis, erythema nodosum, and pathergy in a young man from Turkey (along the historic Silk Road) is textbook Behcet disease. HSV (option A) was excluded serologically and would not cause uveitis/pathergy/erythema nodosum in this pattern. Crohn disease (option B) can mimic Behcet with oral and genital ulcers but he has no GI symptoms, weight loss, or diarrhea, and pathergy/recurrent painful scarring genital ulcers favor Behcet. SLE (option D) causes painless oral ulcers, malar rash, serositis, and positive ANA — not this clinical picture. Reactive arthritis (option E) follows GU/GI infection with conjunctivitis, urethritis, and keratoderma blennorrhagicum, not recurrent genital and oral ulcers with pathergy.

stages.label: LABS & OPHTHALMOLOGY
stages.content:
WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL
Hgb 14.6 g/dL
ESR 42 mm/hr
CRP 3.8 mg/dL
ANA: negative
HLA-B51: positive
HSV PCR (oral lesion swab): negative
RPR: nonreactive
HIV: negative
Ophthalmology: right anterior uveitis; dilated exam shows early retinal vascular sheathing in the right eye (posterior involvement)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His exam reveals posterior segment (retinal vasculitis) involvement, not just isolated anterior uveitis. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Empiric oral acyclovir and observation as initial therapy
Topical corticosteroid eye drops alone as initial therapy
Colchicine alone for mucocutaneous control as initial therapy
Low-dose aspirin and reassurance as initial therapy
Systemic immunosuppression
stages.explanation: Posterior uveitis/retinal vasculitis in Behcet is sight-threatening and demands prompt systemic immunosuppression: systemic glucocorticoids plus a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant (azathioprine, or cyclosporine), with biologics such as infliximab/adalimumab (anti-TNF) for severe or refractory ocular disease. Topical steroids alone (option A) are appropriate only for isolated anterior uveitis and are inadequate for posterior involvement, risking blindness. Colchicine (option B) helps mucocutaneous and arthritic manifestations but does not control retinal vasculitis. Acyclovir (option D) treats herpetic disease, excluded here. Aspirin and reassurance (option E) ignores active sight-threatening inflammation. Recognizing that retinal involvement upgrades the urgency and the therapy is the key teaching point.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: For long-term control of his recurrent oral and genital ulcers and skin lesions (mucocutaneous disease), which agent is the recommended first-line therapy?
options: Cyclophosphamide in this setting
Warfarin before discharge planning
Colchicine
Long-term broad-spectrum antibiotics
Allopurinol in this setting
stages.explanation: Colchicine is first-line for the mucocutaneous and arthritic manifestations of Behcet disease (oral/genital ulcers, erythema nodosum, arthralgia), often combined with topical measures and, for resistant ulcers, apremilast. Cyclophosphamide (option B) is reserved for severe organ-threatening disease such as major vascular or CNS involvement, not routine ulcers. Long-term antibiotics (option C) do not treat this immune-mediated disease. Warfarin (option D) is not used for mucocutaneous disease and is hazardous in Behcet vascular disease where inflammation, not anticoagulation, is the target. Allopurinol (option E) treats gout. While his sight-threatening eye disease needs systemic immunosuppression, baseline mucocutaneous control with colchicine remains a cornerstone.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication of this disease carries the highest mortality and warrants specific vigilance?
options: Erythema nodosum as the leading concern
Folliculitis as the leading concern
Large-vessel involvement, particularly pulmonary artery aneurysms
Mild peripheral arthritis as the leading concern
Recurrent oral ulcers as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Behcet is a variable-vessel vasculitis; its most lethal complications are vascular — pulmonary artery aneurysms (which can rupture and cause fatal hemoptysis), large-vessel arterial aneurysms, and venous thromboses (including Budd-Chiari and dural sinus thrombosis). These, along with parenchymal CNS disease, drive mortality. Importantly, Behcet thrombosis is inflammatory (immunosuppression is central; anticoagulation alone is insufficient and is hazardous if a pulmonary aneurysm coexists). Recurrent oral ulcers (option A), erythema nodosum (option B), mild arthritis (option D), and folliculitis (option E) are morbid and uncomfortable but not life-threatening. Awareness of vascular and neurologic Behcet changes both prognosis and management intensity.

pearls: The classic Behcet triad is recurrent oral ulcers plus genital ulcers plus uveitis; pathergy and HLA-B51 (common in Silk Road populations) support the diagnosis.
Posterior uveitis/retinal vasculitis is sight-threatening and requires systemic immunosuppression (steroids plus azathioprine, escalating to anti-TNF), not topical drops alone.
Behcet is a variable-vessel vasculitis; pulmonary artery aneurysms and CNS involvement carry the highest mortality.
Behcet-related thrombosis is inflammatory — treat with immunosuppression; do not rely on anticoagulation alone, and screen for pulmonary artery aneurysm before anticoagulating.

cases.title: Arm Tiredness and a Faint Pulse in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching, fatiguing left arm with exertion and dizziness, plus several months of malaise.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman of East Asian descent presents with three months of left-arm aching and heaviness that worsen with use — for example, brushing her hair — and resolve with rest. She has had intermittent low-grade fevers, fatigue, night sweats, and a 4 kg weight loss over six months, with neck and shoulder-girdle aching she first attributed to stress. She reports occasional lightheadedness when standing and a few near-syncope episodes. She has no chronic illnesses and takes only an oral contraceptive. Allergies: none. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and works as a graphic designer. There is no family history of vasculitis or early stroke. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, dyspnea at rest, visual loss, focal weakness, rash, oral ulcers, and Raynaud phenomenon. She has not been pregnant. A clinic nurse noted difficulty obtaining a blood pressure on the left arm at a prior visit.

vitals.BP: Right arm 148/86 mmHg; Left arm 104/64 mmHg

cases.exam: Well-developed young woman, mildly fatigued. Diminished left radial and brachial pulses with a > 30 mmHg interarm systolic BP difference. A bruit is audible over the left subclavian region and the left carotid. Cool left hand with delayed capillary refill; no digital ulcers or gangrene. Heart: regular, soft early diastolic murmur at the right upper sternal border. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft; no abdominal bruit appreciated. No temporal artery tenderness, no scalp tenderness, no jaw claudication. Neuro exam nonfocal. No synovitis, rash, or oral ulcers.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The interarm blood pressure difference, diminished left pulses, and subclavian/carotid bruits in a young woman most strongly suggest which disease category?
options: Small-vessel ANCA-associated vasculitis
Large-vessel vasculitis
Thoracic outlet syndrome in this clinical context
Medium-vessel vasculitis in this clinical context
Premature atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease
stages.explanation: Limb (arm) claudication, asymmetric/absent pulses, an interarm BP difference >10 mmHg, and arterial bruits over the subclavian and carotid arteries localize disease to large vessels (aorta and its major branches) — large-vessel vasculitis. Small-vessel ANCA disease (option B) causes glomerulonephritis, alveolar hemorrhage, and palpable purpura, not large-artery stenoses. Medium-vessel disease (option C, e.g., PAN) causes microaneurysms and visceral infarcts, not subclavian stenosis with pulse loss. Premature atherosclerosis (option D) is unlikely at 29 without risk factors and would not explain systemic inflammatory symptoms. Thoracic outlet syndrome (option E) is positional and unilateral but does not cause carotid bruits, systemic constitutional symptoms, or an inflammatory profile. The constellation defines a large-vessel arteritis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.4 ×10³/µL
Hgb 10.8 g/dL (normocytic)
Platelets 462 ×10³/µL
ESR 74 mm/hr
CRP 5.9 mg/dL
ANA: negative
ANCA: negative
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
MR angiography of aorta and great vessels: long-segment concentric wall thickening and stenosis of the left subclavian artery, narrowing of the left common carotid, and mild dilation of the aortic root with thickened ascending aortic wall

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Considering her age, ethnicity, vessels involved, and imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Polyarteritis nodosa
Fibromuscular dysplasia
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Giant cell (temporal) arteritis
Takayasu arteritis
stages.explanation: A woman under 40, of Asian descent, with limb claudication, pulse deficits, an interarm BP gradient, bruits, elevated inflammatory markers, and MRA showing concentric long-segment thickening/stenosis of the aorta and great vessels has Takayasu arteritis. GCA (option A) is histologically similar large-vessel arteritis but occurs in patients over 50 with cranial symptoms (headache, jaw claudication, visual loss, scalp tenderness), absent here. Fibromuscular dysplasia (option C) gives a 'string-of-beads' appearance (often renal/carotid) without systemic inflammation or elevated ESR/CRP. PAN (option D) is medium-vessel with microaneurysms, not large-vessel concentric stenoses. GPA (option E) is ANCA-associated small-vessel disease with sinopulmonary and renal involvement. Age <40 strongly favors Takayasu over GCA.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment for active Takayasu arteritis?
options: Observation with serial imaging only as initial therapy
High-dose systemic glucocorticoids, typically with a steroid-sparing agent
Aspirin alone as initial therapy
Immediate angioplasty/stenting of the subclavian stenosis as initial therapy
Anticoagulation with warfarin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Active Takayasu is treated medically first: high-dose glucocorticoids induce remission, and a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant (methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate, or biologics such as tocilizumab/TNF inhibitors) is added to maintain remission and limit steroid toxicity, especially in this young woman. Revascularization (option A) is reserved for critical, symptomatic, fixed stenoses and ideally performed during quiescent (non-inflammatory) disease; intervening on actively inflamed vessels risks restenosis and complications. Aspirin (option B) may be adjunctive for ischemic risk but does not treat the vasculitis. Warfarin (option C) is not indicated for the arteritis itself. Observation alone (option E) is inappropriate given active inflammation and progressive stenoses that threaten organ perfusion.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which imaging-based parameter is most useful for tracking disease activity and response to therapy over time in this large-vessel arteritis?
options: Bone densitometry with appropriate follow-up
Carotid intima-media thickness on a single baseline scan only with appropriate follow-up
Annual echocardiography only with appropriate follow-up
Plain radiographs of the chest with appropriate follow-up
Serial cross-sectional vascular imaging for wall thickening/edema
stages.explanation: Takayasu activity is monitored with serial cross-sectional vascular imaging — MR or CT angiography (and increasingly PET) — to detect arterial wall thickening/edema and the development of new stenoses or aneurysms, interpreted together with clinical features and ESR/CRP (which can be imperfect markers). Plain chest radiographs (option A) cannot assess the vessel wall. Echocardiography alone (option B) evaluates valves/aortic root but not the full arterial tree. Bone densitometry (option D) monitors steroid-induced osteoporosis but not vasculitis activity. A single baseline carotid scan (option E) does not allow tracking over time. Longitudinal vascular imaging is essential because clinically silent progression can occur even when inflammatory markers appear controlled.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Because her cuff blood pressure may be unreliable, what monitoring caveat is most important during follow-up?
options: Use only home automated wrist cuffs for accuracy as the immediate next step
Subclavian/aortic stenoses can mask true central hypertension
Cuff pressures are always accurate in large-vessel vasculitis as the immediate next step
Blood pressure is irrelevant once immunosuppression is started as the immediate next step
Always use the left-arm reading as the true central pressure as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In Takayasu, stenoses of the subclavian arteries make peripheral cuff readings falsely low and can hide dangerous central hypertension; additionally, renal artery stenosis is a common cause of true (and severe) hypertension. The correct approach is to identify and use the least-affected limb for monitoring, corroborate with central measurement when needed, and actively screen for renal artery involvement, because unrecognized hypertension causes stroke, heart failure, and aortic complications. Option 0 is wrong because the diseased left arm under-reads. Options 2 and 3 are dangerously false — cuff pressures are frequently unreliable, and BP control remains critical. Wrist cuffs (option E) are not validated solutions and do not address the underlying stenoses. Vigilant, anatomically informed BP assessment is essential.

pearls: Takayasu ('pulseless disease') is a large-vessel granulomatous arteritis of the aorta and its branches, classically in women under 40, more common in Asian populations.
Hallmarks: limb claudication, diminished/absent pulses, interarm BP difference >10 mmHg, arterial bruits, and elevated ESR/CRP; MRA/CTA shows long-segment concentric wall thickening and stenoses.
Treat active disease with glucocorticoids plus a steroid-sparing agent; reserve revascularization for fixed critical stenoses during quiescent disease.
Peripheral cuff pressures can be falsely low due to subclavian stenosis — measure in the least-affected limb and screen for renal artery stenosis to avoid missing true hypertension.

cases.title: Raised Purple Spots on the Legs with Belly Pain After a Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: A raised purple rash on the legs and buttocks with crampy abdominal pain and sore, swollen ankles.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the ED with a three-day history of a raised purple rash that began on his lower legs and buttocks. He has crampy, colicky abdominal pain and has vomited twice; his mother noticed a small streak of blood in his stool this morning. He complains of painful, swollen ankles and a sore knee, making him reluctant to walk. He had a runny nose and sore throat about ten days ago that resolved on its own. He is otherwise healthy, fully immunized, and on no medications. Allergies: none. He attends second grade; there are no sick contacts with serious illness. Family history is unremarkable. He has had normal urine color but his mother reports the urine looked slightly tea-colored once today. He has had no recent new foods or medications, no insect bites, and no easy bruising or bleeding history.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly uncomfortable child. Skin: symmetric palpable purpura and a few petechiae over the buttocks and extensor surfaces of both lower legs, not blanching, with normal-appearing skin elsewhere; no purpura above the waist. Abdomen: soft with periumbilical tenderness, mild guarding, hyperactive bowel sounds, no rebound; no palpable mass. Joints: tender, mildly swollen ankles and right knee with limited painful range of motion but no warmth or erythema. Scrotum nontender. No meningismus, no mucosal bleeding, no lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial laboratory finding would best distinguish this child's palpable purpura from a primary bleeding/platelet disorder?
options: Low fibrinogen with elevated D-dimer
Normal platelet count
Prolonged PT/INR as the first step today
Markedly low platelet count
Positive direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test
stages.explanation: The rash is palpable purpura — a vasculitic, not thrombocytopenic, lesion. In this disease the platelet count and coagulation studies are normal, which separates it from immune thrombocytopenia, leukemia, and consumptive coagulopathy. A markedly low platelet count (option A) suggests ITP or leukemic infiltration (and ITP purpura is typically flat/non-palpable). Prolonged PT/INR (option C) and low fibrinogen with high D-dimer (option D) indicate coagulopathy/DIC, which would also produce non-palpable bleeding and a sicker child. A positive Coombs (option E) points to autoimmune hemolysis, not this vasculitis. Demonstrating normal platelets and normal coagulation in a child with palpable purpura is the pivotal early step that reframes the rash as small-vessel vasculitis.

stages.label: LABS & URINALYSIS
stages.content:
WBC 11.2 ×10³/µL
Hgb 12.8 g/dL
Platelets 356 ×10³/µL
PT/INR and aPTT: normal
Creatinine 0.4 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 2+ blood, 25–30 RBC/hpf with dysmorphic RBCs, 1+ protein
Stool guaiac: positive
ESR 38 mm/hr
C3/C4: normal
Throat culture: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the tetrad of palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthralgia, and hematuria following a URI, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia
Henoch-Schonlein purpura
Meningococcemia in this clinical context
Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
Hemolytic uremic syndrome
stages.explanation: Palpable purpura on the buttocks and lower extremities, colicky abdominal pain with GI bleeding, arthralgia/arthritis of the lower limbs, and glomerular hematuria (dysmorphic RBCs, proteinuria) with normal platelets after a URI in a school-aged child is classic Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis). ITP (option B) causes non-palpable purpura with low platelets and no nephritis/arthritis. ALL (option C) would show cytopenias, blasts, and constitutional findings. HUS (option D) features the triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury, usually after bloody diarrhea — platelets are low and the rash is not palpable purpura. Meningococcemia (option E) presents with a toxic, febrile, rapidly deteriorating child and spreading petechiae/purpura, not this stable tetrad.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which abdominal complication is most characteristic of this disease and must be specifically watched for in a child with severe colicky abdominal pain?
options: Toxic megacolon in this presentation
Gallstone pancreatitis
Intussusception
Acute appendicitis
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis
stages.explanation: HSP can cause bowel-wall edema and submucosal hemorrhage that serve as a lead point for intussusception, classically ileo-ileal (small-bowel), which standard contrast/air enema (effective for ileo-colic forms) may not reduce — ultrasound is the key diagnostic tool. Severe, worsening, or colicky abdominal pain warrants imaging to exclude it. Appendicitis (option A) can mimic HSP pain but is not the disease-specific complication. Toxic megacolon (option C) relates to severe colitis (e.g., IBD, C. difficile), not HSP. Hirschsprung enterocolitis (option D) presents in neonates/infants with delayed meconium passage. Gallstone pancreatitis (option E) is unrelated. Recognizing intussusception risk drives the decision to obtain abdominal ultrasound in HSP patients with prominent GI symptoms.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His renal involvement is limited to microscopic hematuria with mild proteinuria and normal creatinine. What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Transfuse platelets as initial therapy
Start high-dose IV cyclophosphamide immediately as initial therapy
Supportive care with close outpatient follow-up
Prescribe broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed sepsis as initial therapy
Begin lifelong dialysis planning as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Most HSP is self-limited; with only mild renal findings, management is supportive — hydration, analgesia, and close monitoring with serial urinalyses and blood pressure to detect evolving nephritis, since renal disease is the main determinant of long-term prognosis and can appear or worsen over weeks. Corticosteroids may help severe abdominal pain or arthritis but do not prevent nephritis. Cyclophosphamide (option A) is reserved for severe, rapidly progressive (crescentic) glomerulonephritis, not mild hematuria. Dialysis planning (option B) is premature with normal creatinine. Antibiotics for sepsis (option C) are unwarranted in this stable, afebrile child. Platelet transfusion (option E) is pointless — platelets are normal and the problem is vasculitis, not thrombocytopenia. Vigilant renal follow-up is the cornerstone.

pearls: HSP is the most common childhood vasculitis: palpable purpura (buttocks/lower extremities), abdominal pain, arthritis/arthralgia, and renal disease, often after a URI, with NORMAL platelets and coagulation.
Skin biopsy/renal biopsy shows IgA-dominant immune complex deposition (leukocytoclastic vasculitis); complement is typically normal.
Watch for intussusception (often ileo-ileal) — get an abdominal ultrasound for severe colicky pain.
Renal involvement determines long-term prognosis: follow serial urinalyses and blood pressure; most cases resolve with supportive care.

cases.title: Five Days of Fever, Red Eyes, and a Bright Red Tongue in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Five days of high fever with red eyes, cracked lips, a rash, and now peeling fingertips.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is admitted for five days of persistent high fevers (up to 39.8 °C) poorly responsive to antipyretics. His parents report red eyes without discharge, very red and cracked lips, a bumpy red tongue, and a diffuse rash over his trunk and groin. He has been irritable and off his feeding. They noticed a swelling in the right side of his neck and that his hands and feet looked puffy and red; today the skin around his fingertips is beginning to peel. He was previously healthy, is fully immunized, and takes no medications. Allergies: none. He attends daycare. No known sick contacts with confirmed infections, no recent travel, and no new medications or foods. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no cough, coryza, or significant diarrhea, and no one has noted vesicular lesions, oral ulcers, or exudative pharyngitis.
cases.exam: Irritable but consolable toddler. Eyes: bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection with perilimbal sparing, no exudate. Lips bright red, dry, and fissured; tongue erythematous with prominent papillae (strawberry tongue); pharynx erythematous without exudate. Neck: a single 1.8 cm tender right anterior cervical lymph node. Skin: diffuse polymorphous erythematous (morbilliform) rash, accentuated in the groin; hands and feet with firm edema and palmar/plantar erythema; early periungual desquamation of the fingertips. Heart: tachycardic, regular, no murmur or gallop. Lungs clear. No vesicles, no oral ulcers, no Koplik spots, no hepatosplenomegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which set of clinical criteria, combined with the prolonged fever, best supports this diagnosis at the bedside?
options: Bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, oral mucosal changes , polymorphous rash, extremity changes ,
Target lesions with mucosal erosions at two sites as the first step today
Sandpaper rash with circumoral pallor and exudative pharyngitis as the first step today
Cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and Koplik spots as the first step today
Migratory polyarthritis with carditis and subcutaneous nodules as the first step today
stages.explanation: The diagnostic principle is fever for at least 5 days plus at least 4 of 5 classic features: bilateral nonexudative (limbic-sparing) conjunctivitis, oral changes (red fissured lips, strawberry tongue), polymorphous rash, extremity changes (acral edema/erythema then periungual desquamation), and cervical lymphadenopathy (often a single node >1.5 cm). This child meets all five. Option 0 describes measles (cough/coryza/Koplik spots), which he lacks. Option 2 describes scarlet fever (sandpaper rash, exudative pharyngitis). Option 3 describes Stevens-Johnson/erythema multiforme (target lesions, mucosal erosions). Option 4 describes acute rheumatic fever (Jones criteria). The specific Kawasaki constellation — and the limbic-sparing conjunctivitis without exudate — is the key recognition pattern.

stages.label: LABS & ECHO
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hgb 10.4 g/dL
Platelets 620 ×10³/µL (thrombocytosis)
ESR 78 mm/hr
CRP 12.4 mg/dL
ALT 68 U/L
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
Na 132 mEq/L
Urinalysis: sterile pyuria (WBC present, culture negative)
Throat culture and rapid strep: negative
Blood cultures: pending, no growth to date
Echocardiogram: dilated left anterior descending coronary artery (z-score elevated); no thrombus

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Measles as the primary diagnosis
Scarlet fever in this clinical context
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
Kawasaki disease
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (systemic)
stages.explanation: Five days of fever with bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue and cracked lips, polymorphous rash, extremity changes with periungual desquamation, cervical adenopathy, sterile pyuria, marked thrombocytosis, elevated inflammatory markers, and an echocardiogram showing coronary artery dilation is diagnostic of Kawasaki disease. Scarlet fever (option A) has a sandpaper rash with positive strep and responds to antibiotics; throat culture/strep here are negative. Measles (option B) has cough/coryza/Koplik spots and a cephalocaudal rash, absent here. Staph scalded skin (option D) causes superficial desquamation with Nikolsky sign and bullae, not coronary changes. Systemic JIA (option E) features quotidian fevers, evanescent salmon rash, and arthritis but not conjunctivitis, mucosal changes, or coronary aneurysms. The coronary involvement clinches the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment to administer promptly to reduce the risk of coronary artery aneurysms?
options: Intravenous ceftriaxone and vancomycin
Corticosteroids alone as initial therapy
Oral acyclovir as initial therapy
Supportive care and antipyretics only
Intravenous immunoglobulin plus aspirin
stages.explanation: High-dose IVIG (2 g/kg) given within the first 10 days of fever, combined with aspirin, is the standard treatment and markedly lowers the incidence of coronary artery aneurysms from ~25% to ~4%. Aspirin (anti-inflammatory dosing initially, then low antiplatelet dose) is used despite the usual pediatric Reye concern because the benefit here is established. Antibiotics (option B) treat bacterial infections, but cultures are negative and Kawasaki is not bacterial. Corticosteroids alone (option C) are not first-line, though steroids are added for high-risk or IVIG-refractory disease. Supportive care only (option D) leaves the child at high aneurysm risk. Acyclovir (option E) treats herpetic infections and is irrelevant. Timely IVIG plus aspirin is the single most important coronary-protective intervention.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After acute treatment, what is the most important long-term monitoring plan for this child?
options: No further follow-up once fever resolves as the immediate next step
Routine renal ultrasound surveillance only as the immediate next step
Annual throat cultures as the immediate next step
Lifelong systemic corticosteroids as the immediate next step
Serial echocardiography to monitor the coronary arteries, with continued antiplatelet therapy
stages.explanation: Because the dreaded complication is coronary artery aneurysm with risk of thrombosis, stenosis, and myocardial infarction, follow-up centers on serial echocardiography (typically at diagnosis, ~1–2 weeks, and ~4–6 weeks, with closer/longer surveillance if abnormalities persist) plus antiplatelet therapy and cardiology involvement; larger aneurysms may require anticoagulation. Stopping all follow-up (option A) is unsafe given his already-dilated LAD. Annual throat cultures (option C) are irrelevant — this is not streptococcal disease. Lifelong corticosteroids (option D) are not indicated. Renal ultrasound surveillance (option E) misses the cardiac focus of the disease. Cardiac surveillance and antiplatelet management drive long-term outcomes in Kawasaki disease.

pearls: Diagnose Kawasaki with fever ≥5 days plus ≥4 of 5: conjunctivitis (nonexudative, limbic-sparing), mucosal changes (strawberry tongue/cracked lips), polymorphous rash, extremity changes (edema then periungual desquamation), and cervical lymphadenopathy.
Supportive clues include sterile pyuria, marked thrombocytosis (second week), elevated ESR/CRP, and anemia; the feared complication is coronary artery aneurysm.
Treat with IVIG plus aspirin within 10 days of fever onset to cut aneurysm risk from ~25% to ~4%.
Follow with serial echocardiograms and antiplatelet therapy; consider corticosteroids/second-line agents for IVIG-refractory or high-risk patients.

cases.title: Purple Spots, Aching Joints, and Tingling Feet in a Man with Liver Disease
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent raised purple spots on the legs, achy joints, and numbness in the feet.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents with several months of recurrent crops of raised purple spots on his lower legs that worsen after standing or in cold weather and fade leaving brown discoloration. He has fatigue, achy symmetric small- and medium-joint arthralgias without frank swelling, and progressive numbness and burning in both feet. He notes that his fingers turn white and painful in the cold. He was diagnosed with hepatitis C a decade ago but never completed treatment. He drinks alcohol socially and used IV drugs in his 30s. He takes only acetaminophen occasionally. Allergies: none. He has mild leg edema. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for foamy urine recently; negative for fever, hemoptysis, sinus symptoms, and oral ulcers. He has not had any new medications. His symptoms are notably worse in winter and improve in warm weather.
cases.exam: Palpable purpura over both lower legs with overlying hyperpigmentation from prior lesions; a small shallow ulcer near the right malleolus. Skin: livedo and acrocyanosis of the fingers on cold exposure. Neuro: reduced pinprick and vibration in a stocking distribution with mild distal weakness, asymmetric in places. Liver edge firm and palpable 2 cm below the costal margin; mild splenomegaly; trace ascites; 1+ lower-extremity edema. No active synovitis, no rash above the waist, no nasal crusting, lungs clear. No clubbing or jaundice.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Palpable purpura, arthralgia, peripheral neuropathy, and cold-induced symptoms in a patient with chronic hepatitis C should prompt testing for which serum protein abnormality?
options: Cold agglutinins only as the first step today
Ceruloplasmin deficiency in this clinical context
Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
Cryoglobulins
Bence-Jones proteinuria in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The triad of palpable purpura, arthralgia, and weakness/neuropathy (Meltzer triad) with cold-sensitivity in a hepatitis C patient strongly suggests cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. Cryoglobulins are immunoglobulins that precipitate in the cold; HCV drives mixed (type II/III) cryoglobulinemia via chronic immune-complex formation. Proper sample handling (warm collection/transport at 37 °C) is essential or the test is falsely negative. Bence-Jones proteinuria (option A) indicates myeloma light chains, not this syndrome. Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (option B) causes emphysema and liver disease, not vasculitic purpura. Cold agglutinins (option D) cause hemolysis, not immune-complex small-vessel vasculitis with this triad. Ceruloplasmin (option E) relates to Wilson disease. Cryoglobulin testing is the pivotal next step.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 11.6 g/dL
Platelets 110 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 1.5 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 2+ protein, 15–20 RBC/hpf with dysmorphic RBCs, occasional RBC casts
C3: low-normal
C4: markedly low (disproportionately depressed)
Rheumatoid factor: strongly positive
Hepatitis C antibody: positive; HCV RNA 1.8 ×10⁶ IU/mL
Hepatitis B surface antigen: negative
Cryoglobulins: positive (type II, monoclonal IgM kappa with polyclonal IgG)
ANA: negative; ANCA: negative
Serum protein electrophoresis: small monoclonal IgM band

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the positive cryoglobulins, strongly positive rheumatoid factor, and disproportionately low C4 with active hepatitis C, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis with rheumatoid vasculitis
Polyarteritis nodosa as the primary diagnosis
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis in this clinical context
Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis secondary to hepatitis C
Henoch-Schonlein purpura as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Palpable purpura, arthralgia, neuropathy, membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, proteinuria), strongly positive RF, a characteristically low C4, and positive type II cryoglobulins in active HCV define HCV-associated mixed cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. PAN (option A) is medium-vessel, ANCA-negative but with microaneurysms and no cryoglobulins/MPGN, and is HBV-associated. RA with vasculitis (option C) occurs in long-standing erosive RA, which he lacks (no chronic deforming arthritis). GPA (option D) is ANCA-associated with sinopulmonary/renal granulomatous disease, absent here. HSP (option E) is IgA vasculitis, typically pediatric, with normal complement and no cryoglobulins. The HCV link, low C4, high RF, and type II cryoglobulins make cryoglobulinemic vasculitis the answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For HCV-associated cryoglobulinemic vasculitis with this level of organ involvement, what is the cornerstone of long-term therapy?
options: Splenectomy as initial therapy
Lifelong high-dose corticosteroids alone as initial therapy
Long-term broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Allopurinol and colchicine as initial therapy
Direct-acting antiviral therapy to eradicate hepatitis C, with immunosuppression reserved for severe/organ-threatening disease
stages.explanation: Because the vasculitis is driven by chronic HCV antigenemia, eradicating the virus with direct-acting antivirals is the cornerstone and often resolves the cryoglobulinemia. For severe or organ-threatening disease (rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, severe neuropathy, digital ischemia), add immunosuppression — rituximab (preferred B-cell-directed agent) with corticosteroids, and plasma exchange to rapidly remove cryoglobulins — typically alongside or before antivirals. Steroids alone (option A) do not address the viral driver and risk increasing viremia. Splenectomy (option C) is not a treatment for this vasculitis. Long-term antibiotics (option D) are irrelevant to a viral immune-complex disease. Allopurinol/colchicine (option E) treat gout, not cryoglobulinemia. Antiviral cure plus targeted immunosuppression for severe disease is the modern standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because his cryoglobulinemia is associated with a clonal B-cell process, which malignancy is he at increased long-term risk of developing?
options: Acute myeloid leukemia
Colorectal carcinoma
B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma
Hepatocellular carcinoma only
Melanoma as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Mixed cryoglobulinemia (especially type II with a monoclonal IgM, as here) reflects chronic B-cell stimulation by HCV and carries an increased risk of B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma; eradicating HCV can reduce this risk and sometimes induces remission of low-grade lymphoma. Hepatocellular carcinoma (option A) is a real HCV/cirrhosis risk but is not the lymphoproliferative complication specific to the clonal B-cell expansion driving his cryoglobulinemia. Melanoma (option C), colorectal carcinoma (option D), and AML (option E) are not characteristically linked to HCV-associated cryoglobulinemia. Awareness of the lymphoma association justifies monitoring the monoclonal band and lymph nodes over time.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His urinalysis shows dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts. Which renal lesion is most characteristically associated with this disease?
options: Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis
Anti-GBM (crescentic) glomerulonephritis
Thin basement membrane disease
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
Minimal change disease in this setting
stages.explanation: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis classically causes membranoproliferative (mesangiocapillary) glomerulonephritis from immune-complex and cryoglobulin deposition, producing hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs/RBC casts, proteinuria, low complement (especially C4), and sometimes a nephritic/nephrotic picture — exactly this patient. Minimal change disease (option A) causes selective nephrotic-range proteinuria with bland sediment, typically in children. FSGS (option B) causes nephrotic syndrome with bland sediment, not this immune-complex MPGN. Thin basement membrane disease (option C) causes isolated benign hematuria. Anti-GBM disease (option E) is a pulmonary-renal crescentic GN with linear IgG and anti-GBM antibodies, distinct from immune-complex MPGN. The MPGN pattern with hypocomplementemia is a hallmark of cryoglobulinemic renal involvement.

cases.diagnosis: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis (hepatitis C-associated, mixed type II)
pearls: Mixed cryoglobulinemic vasculitis classically presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia, and neuropathy (Meltzer triad), often cold-exacerbated, and is strongly linked to chronic hepatitis C.
Labs show positive cryoglobulins, high rheumatoid factor, and a disproportionately low C4; collect/transport the cryoglobulin sample at 37 °C to avoid false negatives.
Renal involvement is typically membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, hypocomplementemia).
Treat the underlying HCV with direct-acting antivirals; add rituximab, steroids, and plasma exchange for severe organ-threatening disease.

cases.title: Daily Spiking Fevers with a Fleeting Pink Rash and Sore Joints
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of daily high fevers with a transient rash, sore throat, and aching swollen joints.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman is admitted after three weeks of daily spiking fevers, typically peaking in the late afternoon/evening to 39.5 °C and returning to normal in between. She has a salmon-colored rash that appears with the fevers and fades as they resolve. She reports a persistent sore throat with negative strep tests, diffuse myalgias, and progressively painful, swollen wrists and knees. She has fatigue and a 3 kg weight loss. She has no significant past medical history and takes only acetaminophen and ibuprofen. Allergies: none. She works as a teacher, does not smoke, drinks rarely, and has no recent travel or sick contacts. Family history is noncontributory. Extensive outpatient workup for infection has been unrevealing. Review of systems is negative for genitourinary symptoms, oral/genital ulcers, photosensitivity, and Raynaud phenomenon. Multiple blood cultures have been drawn and remain negative.
cases.exam: Febrile, mildly ill-appearing. Skin: evanescent salmon-pink macular rash on the trunk and proximal extremities, most visible during a fever spike, with a few areas exhibiting linear streaks at sites of scratching (Koebner phenomenon). Pharynx erythematous without exudate. Lymphadenopathy: mobile, mildly tender cervical and axillary nodes. Joints: symmetric synovitis of both wrists and knees with warmth and effusions. Cardiac exam normal, no rub. Mild hepatosplenomegaly. No oral ulcers, no malar rash, no pericardial rub, no nail-fold infarcts, lungs clear.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before settling on a rheumatologic diagnosis, what is the most essential initial approach in a patient with prolonged daily fevers, rash, and arthritis?
options: Order anti-CCP and treat as rheumatoid arthritis as the first step today
Exclude infection and malignancy
Initiate broad-spectrum antibiotics for life regardless of cultures as the first step today
Start empiric high-dose corticosteroids immediately as the first step today
Begin a TNF inhibitor empirically as the first step today
stages.explanation: The suspected disease (a systemic inflammatory syndrome with quotidian fever, evanescent rash, and arthritis) is a diagnosis of exclusion: infection (endocarditis, viral, abscess), malignancy (especially lymphoma and leukemia), and other autoimmune disease must be reasonably ruled out first via cultures, serologies, and imaging. Starting steroids immediately (option A) can mask or worsen an undiagnosed infection or lymphoma. Empiric TNF inhibitor (option C) is inappropriate before diagnosis and infection clearance. Indefinite antibiotics regardless of cultures (option D) is irrational. Anti-CCP/treating as RA (option E) is premature; RA does not cause this fever/rash pattern and anti-CCP is usually negative here. Systematic exclusion protects the patient and confirms the clinical diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 21.5 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant, ~88%)
Hgb 10.2 g/dL
Platelets 540 ×10³/µL
ESR 96 mm/hr
CRP 18 mg/dL
AST 92 U/L; ALT 110 U/L
Ferritin 14,200 ng/mL
Glycosylated (fraction of) ferritin: low (<20%)
ANA: negative
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Blood cultures ×3: no growth
HIV, EBV, CMV, parvovirus serologies: negative for acute infection
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: mild hepatosplenomegaly and small reactive lymph nodes; no abscess or mass
Peripheral smear: reactive neutrophilia, no blasts

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Considering quotidian fevers, evanescent salmon rash, arthritis, neutrophilic leukocytosis, and ferritin >10,000 with negative ANA/RF and negative infectious workup, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Adult-onset Still disease
Infective endocarditis
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Lymphoma as the primary diagnosis
Rheumatoid arthritis
stages.explanation: Daily (quotidian) spiking fevers, an evanescent salmon-colored rash that comes and goes with fever, arthritis, sore throat, neutrophilic leukocytosis, hyperferritinemia (often >5,000–10,000) with a low glycosylated ferritin fraction, hepatosplenomegaly, transaminitis, and negative ANA/RF after exclusion of infection and malignancy fulfill the Yamaguchi criteria for adult-onset Still disease. SLE (option A) typically has a positive ANA, photosensitive malar rash, cytopenias, and serositis, not this fever/rash/ferritin pattern. RA (option B) lacks quotidian fevers and evanescent rash; RF is negative here anyway. Lymphoma (option C) and endocarditis (option E) are the key mimics — but imaging, smear, and repeatedly negative cultures argue against them, and the markedly low glycosylated ferritin supports Still disease.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her ferritin abruptly rises further with new cytopenias, falling ESR, very high triglycerides, and worsening liver function. Which life-threatening complication should be suspected?
options: Tumor lysis syndrome as the leading concern
Macrophage activation syndrome
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction in this presentation
Disseminated intravascular coagulation from sepsis
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura in this presentation
stages.explanation: Macrophage activation syndrome (a form of secondary HLH) is a feared complication of adult-onset Still disease. Clues are a rising ferritin, new cytopenias (paradoxical drop in cell lines), a falling ESR (from hypofibrinogenemia), hypertriglyceridemia, hepatic dysfunction, and hemophagocytosis on marrow exam. It requires urgent recognition and aggressive immunosuppression (high-dose steroids, anakinra, sometimes etoposide-based regimens). DIC (option A) features prolonged PT/aPTT and is usually driven by sepsis, which is excluded here. Tumor lysis (option C) shows hyperkalemia/hyperphosphatemia/hyperuricemia after cytotoxic therapy. TTP (option D) shows schistocytes, normal coagulation, and neurologic/renal findings. A hemolytic transfusion reaction (option E) is temporally tied to transfusion. The ferritin/cytopenia/falling-ESR/triglyceride pattern is classic for MAS.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional clinical feature is part of the major diagnostic criteria and would further support this diagnosis if present?
options: Markedly elevated anti-CCP antibodies as the immediate next step
Symmetric small-joint erosions on hand radiographs as the immediate next step
Positive blood cultures for Staphylococcus aureus as the immediate next step
High-titer anti-dsDNA antibodies as the immediate next step
Fever ≥39 °C, arthralgia/arthritis, the typical rash,
stages.explanation: The Yamaguchi major criteria are fever ≥39 °C lasting ≥1 week, arthralgia/arthritis ≥2 weeks, the typical evanescent nonpruritic salmon rash, and leukocytosis ≥10,000/µL with ≥80% neutrophils; minor criteria include sore throat, lymphadenopathy/splenomegaly, abnormal liver function, and negative ANA/RF. This patient meets multiple major and minor criteria. Erosive symmetric arthritis (option A) and high anti-CCP (option E) point to rheumatoid arthritis, not Still disease. High-titer anti-dsDNA (option C) indicates SLE. Positive blood cultures (option D) would indicate infection and exclude this diagnosis of exclusion. Knowing the formal criteria helps confirm adult-onset Still disease once infection and malignancy are excluded.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For her systemic adult-onset Still disease, what is the most appropriate first-line treatment approach?
options: Allopurinol as initial therapy
NSAIDs alone indefinitely as initial therapy
Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy as initial therapy
Systemic glucocorticoids for active disease, with steroid-sparing agents
Long-term antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: NSAIDs may control mild disease, but most patients with significant systemic adult-onset Still disease need glucocorticoids to induce remission, with steroid-sparing methotrexate added for chronic articular disease and IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra, canakinumab) or IL-6 inhibitors (tocilizumab) for refractory, severe systemic disease or macrophage activation syndrome. NSAIDs alone (option B) are insufficient for this degree of systemic illness. Long-term antibiotics (option C) treat infection, which is excluded. Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy (option D) is useful in SLE/mild RA but inadequate for active Still disease. Allopurinol (option E) treats gout and is unrelated. Glucocorticoids first, then biologic IL-1/IL-6 blockade for refractory disease, is the guideline-based approach.

pearls: Adult-onset Still disease classically presents with quotidian (daily-spiking) fevers, an evanescent salmon-colored rash, arthritis, and sore throat; it is a diagnosis of exclusion (Yamaguchi criteria).
Labs show neutrophilic leukocytosis, very high ferritin (often >5,000–10,000) with a low glycosylated ferritin fraction, and typically negative ANA and RF.
Macrophage activation syndrome (secondary HLH) is a life-threatening complication — suspect it with rising ferritin, new cytopenias, falling ESR, hypertriglyceridemia, and liver dysfunction.
Treat with glucocorticoids first, adding methotrexate or IL-1/IL-6 inhibitors (anakinra, tocilizumab) for refractory or severe systemic disease.

cases.title: A Young Woman with Repeated Pregnancy Loss and a Leg Clot
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful, swollen leg with a history of three miscarriages and a prior clot.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman is admitted with a swollen, painful right calf for three days. She has had three first/second-trimester pregnancy losses, including one fetal death at 14 weeks, and one prior unprovoked deep vein thrombosis at age 30 while not on estrogen. She is not currently pregnant. She reports occasional joint aches and a lacy purplish discoloration on her thighs in the cold. She takes no regular medications and is not on hormonal contraception. Allergies: none. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and has no recent immobilization, surgery, or long travel. Family history: a sister with lupus. Review of systems is negative for malar rash, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, hemoptysis, and focal neurologic deficits, though she recalls one transient episode of arm numbness last year. She has no history of bleeding despite an abnormal coagulation test noted incidentally before a prior dental procedure.
cases.exam: Right calf swollen, warm, and tender with a positive Homans sign and ~3 cm calf-circumference asymmetry. Skin: livedo reticularis over the thighs; no active rash, no digital ulcers. No synovitis. Cardiopulmonary exam normal; no murmurs (no findings of Libman-Sacks endocarditis today). Abdomen benign. Neuro exam nonfocal. No oral ulcers, no alopecia, no malar rash. Peripheral pulses intact. No signs of active bleeding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her records show a prolonged aPTT that did NOT correct on a mixing study. In the context of thrombosis (not bleeding), what does this most likely indicate?
options: Disseminated intravascular coagulation as the first step today
Heparin contamination only, with no clinical significance
Vitamin K deficiency as the first step today
A clotting factor deficiency (e.g., factor VIII or IX deficiency)
An inhibitor — specifically a lupus anticoagulant
stages.explanation: A prolonged aPTT that fails to correct when mixed 1:1 with normal plasma indicates an inhibitor rather than a factor deficiency (a deficiency would correct because normal plasma supplies the missing factor). In a patient with thrombosis and pregnancy loss, the relevant inhibitor is a lupus anticoagulant — an antiphospholipid antibody that prolongs phospholipid-dependent clotting tests in vitro yet paradoxically causes thrombosis in vivo. A factor deficiency (option A) would correct on mixing and cause bleeding, not clotting. Vitamin K deficiency (option B) prolongs the PT predominantly and corrects with factor replacement. DIC (option C) consumes factors/platelets with elevated D-dimer and bleeding/clotting, not an isolated non-correcting aPTT. Heparin (option E) must be excluded but does not explain the clinical syndrome. Non-correction = inhibitor = think lupus anticoagulant.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
aPTT: prolonged; does NOT correct on 1:1 mixing study
Dilute Russell viper venom time (dRVVT): prolonged, with confirmation of phospholipid dependence (lupus anticoagulant positive)
Anticardiolipin IgG: high titer (>40 GPL)
Anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG: positive, high titer
Platelets: 118 ×10³/µL (mild thrombocytopenia)
PT/INR: normal
ANA: positive 1:160
Anti-dsDNA: negative
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
Lower extremity duplex ultrasound: occlusive thrombus in the right popliteal and femoral veins

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With a confirmed DVT, recurrent pregnancy loss, lupus anticoagulant, and persistently positive anticardiolipin and anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies, what is the diagnosis?
options: Antiphospholipid syndrome
Factor V Leiden thrombophilia
Systemic lupus erythematosus without thrombophilia
Protein C deficiency in this clinical context
Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia
stages.explanation: Antiphospholipid syndrome requires a clinical criterion (vascular thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity — she has both a DVT and recurrent loss including a fetal death) plus a laboratory criterion (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, or anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I) confirmed positive on two occasions ≥12 weeks apart. She meets these. Factor V Leiden (option A) causes venous thrombosis but not the antibody profile or this aPTT pattern, and is confirmed by genetic/APC-resistance testing. SLE (option C) may coexist (her ANA is positive, sister has lupus), but the thrombotic/obstetric syndrome with antiphospholipid antibodies defines APS specifically; APS can be primary or secondary to SLE. Protein C deficiency (option D) is another inherited thrombophilia without these antibodies. HIT (option E) requires heparin exposure with platelet drop and PF4 antibodies. The antibody-plus-clinical pattern is diagnostic of APS.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate long-term anticoagulation strategy for this patient with venous thrombosis and antiphospholipid syndrome?
options: A direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) such as rivaroxaban indefinitely
A three-month course of anticoagulation, then stop
Aspirin alone as initial therapy
No anticoagulation; immunosuppression only as initial therapy
Long-term warfarin targeting an INR of 2.0–3.0
stages.explanation: Thrombotic APS is best managed with long-term (typically indefinite) warfarin targeting an INR of 2.0–3.0 for a first venous event. DOACs (option A) are generally avoided in APS — especially triple-positive patients — because trials showed higher recurrent thrombosis (notably arterial) compared with warfarin. Aspirin alone (option C) is insufficient for established venous thrombosis (it is used more for primary prophylaxis or in pregnancy combined with heparin). A 3-month-then-stop course (option D) is inadequate because APS confers a high, ongoing recurrence risk requiring indefinite therapy. Immunosuppression alone (option E) does not treat the prothrombotic state in typical APS (it is relevant in catastrophic APS as adjunct). Indefinite warfarin with INR 2–3 is the standard for venous APS.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She suddenly develops multi-organ failure with thromboses in several small vessels over days, despite anticoagulation. Which severe variant should be suspected?
options: Sepsis-induced multiorgan dysfunction
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
Disseminated intravascular coagulation
Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome
Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia
stages.explanation: Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome is a rare, life-threatening variant in which widespread small-vessel thromboses cause rapid multi-organ failure (kidneys, lungs, brain, heart, skin) over days, often triggered by infection, surgery, or anticoagulation lapse. Treatment is aggressive combination therapy: anticoagulation, high-dose glucocorticoids, and plasma exchange and/or IVIG, with rituximab or eculizumab in refractory cases. DIC (option A) features consumptive coagulopathy with marked PT/aPTT prolongation and bleeding. HIT (option B) requires heparin exposure with a platelet drop and PF4 antibodies. TTP (option D) shows schistocytes and ADAMTS13 deficiency. Sepsis (option E) is a trigger but does not itself explain the antiphospholipid-driven thrombotic storm. Early recognition of CAPS is critical to survival.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: If she becomes pregnant in the future, what is the recommended approach to reduce pregnancy loss and thrombosis?
options: Continue warfarin throughout pregnancy as the immediate next step
Use a DOAC during pregnancy as the immediate next step
Stop all anticoagulation during pregnancy as the immediate next step
Switch to low-molecular-weight heparin plus low-dose aspirin during pregnancy
Use high-dose corticosteroids as the primary therapy in this setting
stages.explanation: In pregnancy, warfarin is teratogenic (warfarin embryopathy), so the regimen switches to low-molecular-weight heparin (prophylactic or therapeutic dosing based on history) combined with low-dose aspirin, which improves live-birth rates and reduces thrombosis in obstetric APS. Continuing warfarin (option A) risks fetal embryopathy and is avoided, especially in the first trimester. DOACs (option B) are contraindicated in pregnancy and discouraged in APS generally. Stopping all anticoagulation (option C) leaves her at high risk for thrombosis and recurrent loss. High-dose corticosteroids (option E) are not the primary therapy for obstetric APS and add maternal risk. Heparin plus low-dose aspirin is the evidence-based obstetric APS regimen.

pearls: Suspect APS with venous/arterial thrombosis or recurrent pregnancy loss plus persistently positive antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I) confirmed ≥12 weeks apart.
A prolonged aPTT that does NOT correct on mixing signals an inhibitor; lupus anticoagulant paradoxically prolongs clotting tests in vitro but causes thrombosis in vivo.
Treat thrombotic APS with long-term warfarin (INR 2.0–3.0); avoid DOACs, especially in triple-positive patients.
In pregnancy, replace warfarin (teratogenic) with low-molecular-weight heparin plus low-dose aspirin.

cases.title: Foamy Urine and Leg Swelling in a Woman with Known Lupus
cases.chiefComplaint: Foamy urine, leg swelling, and rising blood pressure in a patient with established lupus.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed three years ago (malar rash, arthritis, positive ANA and anti-dsDNA) presents with two weeks of foamy urine, progressive lower-extremity and periorbital swelling, and headaches. She admits to inconsistent use of hydroxychloroquine 300 mg daily and has not followed up regularly. She has mild fatigue and arthralgias but denies fever, dysuria, or flank pain. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social history: nonsmoker, works as an accountant, no recent NSAID overuse beyond occasional ibuprofen. Family history: mother with hypothyroidism. Review of systems is positive for mild oral ulcers and new mild dyspnea on exertion; negative for hemoptysis, chest pain, and seizures. Her blood pressure has been creeping up at home readings. She has gained 4 kg over two weeks, which she attributes to swelling.
cases.exam: Mildly uncomfortable young woman. Skin: faint malar erythema; a few shallow painless oral ulcers. Periorbital edema and 2+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally. Cardiovascular exam normal without rub. Lungs with mild bibasilar crackles. Abdomen soft, mild shifting dullness suggesting small ascites. No active synovitis today; no rash on the trunk; no focal neurologic deficits. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with known SLE, edema, and hypertension, what is the single most important initial test to evaluate for renal involvement?
options: Serum uric acid level as the first step today
Urinalysis with microscopy
24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring as the first step today
Renal ultrasound with Doppler as the first step today
CT angiography of the renal arteries as the first step today
stages.explanation: The first and most informative step in suspected lupus nephritis is a urinalysis with microscopy plus quantification of proteinuria. Active urinary sediment (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts) signals glomerular inflammation, and proteinuria quantifies severity — both are central to detecting and grading nephritis. BP monitoring (option A) is useful but does not assess renal parenchymal disease. Renal ultrasound (option C) evaluates obstruction/size, not glomerular activity, and is usually normal early. Uric acid (option D) is nonspecific. CT angiography (option E) targets renovascular disease, not glomerulonephritis, and contrast risks nephrotoxicity. The urinalysis and protein quantification directly reveal the active nephritic/nephrotic process and guide the decision to biopsy.

stages.label: LABS & URINALYSIS
stages.content:
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 0.8)
BUN 30 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 3+ protein, 30–40 RBC/hpf with dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts present
Spot urine protein/creatinine ratio: 3.8 g/g
Serum albumin 2.8 g/dL
C3: low; C4: low
Anti-dsDNA: markedly elevated (rising from prior)
ANA: positive 1:640
Hgb 10.6 g/dL
Platelets 180 ×10³/µL
Complete blood count otherwise unremarkable

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given active sediment, nephrotic-range proteinuria, rising creatinine, and falling complements with high anti-dsDNA, what is the most appropriate next step to guide therapy?
options: Renal biopsy to determine the ISN/RPS class
Empirically start immunosuppression without further testing
Order anti-GBM and ANCA and treat as pulmonary-renal syndrome
Repeat urinalysis in three months before acting in this setting
Begin chronic hemodialysis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Renal biopsy is the standard next step in newly active lupus nephritis: it establishes the ISN/RPS class (I–VI), distinguishes proliferative (III/IV) from membranous (V) disease, and quantifies activity versus chronicity — all of which dictate the intensity and choice of immunosuppression and prognosis. Empiric immunosuppression without histology (option A) risks over- or under-treating and missing irreversible scarring. Hemodialysis (option B) is premature with a creatinine of 1.6 and is not initial therapy here. Waiting three months (option C) allows progressive, potentially irreversible glomerular damage. Anti-GBM/ANCA workup as a pulmonary-renal syndrome (option E) is not the leading diagnosis given known SLE with classic serologies and active sediment; her mild dyspnea is from volume overload. Biopsy directs class-specific treatment.

stages.label: RENAL BIOPSY
stages.content:
Light microscopy: diffuse (>50% of glomeruli) endocapillary hypercellularity with wire-loop deposits and focal cellular crescents
Immunofluorescence: 'full house' staining — IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, and C1q
Electron microscopy: subendothelial dense deposits
Diagnosis: ISN/RPS Class IV lupus nephritis (diffuse proliferative), high activity index
No significant chronicity (minimal fibrosis/sclerosis)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For biopsy-proven Class IV (diffuse proliferative) lupus nephritis, what is the most appropriate induction therapy?
options: Plasma exchange alone as initial therapy
High-dose glucocorticoids plus an immunosuppressant such as mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide
Hydroxychloroquine alone as initial therapy
Rituximab as the sole agent without steroids as initial therapy
ACE inhibitor monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Proliferative lupus nephritis (Class III/IV) requires aggressive induction: high-dose glucocorticoids combined with either mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide; modern regimens often add belimumab or a calcineurin inhibitor (e.g., voclosporin) for better renal responses. Hydroxychloroquine (option A) is essential background therapy for all lupus patients but is not sufficient to treat proliferative nephritis. An ACE inhibitor (option B) is important adjunctive therapy for proteinuria and blood pressure but does not treat the immune-mediated glomerulonephritis. Rituximab alone without steroids (option D) is not standard first-line induction. Plasma exchange alone (option E) is reserved for specific situations (e.g., concurrent TTP or severe pulmonary hemorrhage), not routine proliferative nephritis. Steroids plus mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide is the cornerstone of induction.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term outcome is the renal biopsy class (diffuse proliferative, Class IV) most associated with, underscoring the need for aggressive treatment?
options: Exclusively extrarenal complications as the leading concern
Benign course with no risk of renal failure as the leading concern
Isolated nephrotic syndrome with no inflammatory component as the leading concern
Spontaneous remission without therapy in most cases as the leading concern
The highest risk of progression to end-stage renal disease among lupus nephritis classes if untreated
stages.explanation: Class IV (diffuse proliferative) lupus nephritis carries the highest risk of progression to end-stage renal disease among the classes if inadequately treated, which is why prompt aggressive induction immunosuppression is essential. A benign course (option A) and spontaneous remission (option B) misrepresent this aggressive proliferative lesion. Isolated nephrotic syndrome without inflammation (option C) describes membranous (Class V) disease, not the endocapillary hypercellularity and crescents seen here. Class IV is fundamentally a renal disease with major renal consequences, so 'exclusively extrarenal complications' (option E) is wrong. Understanding the prognosis of Class IV drives the urgency of induction therapy, adherence, and close monitoring to preserve kidney function.

stages.label: ADJUNCTIVE CARE
stages.question: Beyond immunosuppression, which adjunctive measures are most important in this patient's overall lupus nephritis management?
options: Start a DOAC for routine prophylaxis as the immediate next step
Discontinue hydroxychloroquine to reduce pill burden as the immediate next step
Continue hydroxychloroquine, add an ACE inhibitor/ARB for proteinuria
Avoid all vaccinations indefinitely as the immediate next step
High-sodium diet to maintain intravascular volume as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Comprehensive lupus nephritis care includes continuing hydroxychloroquine (reduces flares, organ damage, and improves renal outcomes), adding an ACE inhibitor or ARB to lower proteinuria and control hypertension, reinforcing medication adherence (her flare followed inconsistent use), counseling on effective contraception (teratogenic agents like mycophenolate/cyclophosphamide are used), and providing infection prophylaxis/vaccination given immunosuppression. Stopping hydroxychloroquine (option A) worsens outcomes. A high-sodium diet (option C) aggravates edema and hypertension. Avoiding all vaccines (option D) is wrong — appropriate (non-live where indicated) vaccination is encouraged before/around immunosuppression. Routine DOAC prophylaxis (option E) is not indicated absent thrombosis or antiphospholipid syndrome. These adjuncts substantially improve renal and overall outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Lupus nephritis (ISN/RPS Class IV, diffuse proliferative)
pearls: Active urinary sediment (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts) plus proteinuria, rising creatinine, low complement, and high anti-dsDNA in SLE signals active lupus nephritis.

Renal biopsy classifies disease by ISN/RPS class and quantifies activity vs chronicity, directing therapy; Class IV (diffuse proliferative) shows 'full house' immunofluorescence and wire-loop/subendothelial deposits.
Induction for proliferative (III/IV) nephritis is high-dose glucocorticoids plus mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide, often with belimumab or a calcineurin inhibitor.
Always continue hydroxychloroquine and add an ACE inhibitor/ARB; emphasize adherence and contraception, since many agents are teratogenic.

cases.title: New Joint Aches and Chest Pain on a Blood-Pressure Pill
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of achy joints, fatigue, and chest pain that worsens with deep breaths.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man presents with six weeks of symmetric joint aches in the hands, wrists, and knees, fatigue, low-grade fevers, and pleuritic chest pain. He has hypertension treated with hydralazine 100 mg three times daily, started about ten months ago after other agents were inadequate, plus amlodipine and lisinopril. He also takes a daily aspirin. Allergies: none. Social history: retired electrician, former smoker (quit 5 years ago), minimal alcohol. Family history: no autoimmune disease. Review of systems is negative for malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, alopecia, Raynaud phenomenon, hematuria, and frothy urine. He denies kidney problems and neurologic symptoms. He recalls feeling well before the hydralazine dose was increased. He has not had similar symptoms in the past and has no personal history of lupus or other connective-tissue disease.
cases.exam: Mildly uncomfortable man. Cardiopulmonary: a soft pericardial friction rub heard at the left lower sternal border; lungs clear with no effusion signs but pain on deep inspiration. Joints: mild symmetric tenderness of the MCPs, wrists, and knees with trivial swelling and no deformities. Skin: no malar or discoid rash, no photosensitive eruption, no oral ulcers, no alopecia. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Neuro exam normal. No edema; no nail-fold changes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient on hydralazine presenting with arthralgia, serositis, and constitutional symptoms, which medication-related diagnosis should be highest on the differential?
options: Calcium channel blocker-induced peripheral edema
NSAID-induced interstitial nephritis
Drug-induced lupus
ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema
Statin-associated myopathy
stages.explanation: Hydralazine is a classic cause of drug-induced lupus, which presents with arthralgia/arthritis, serositis (pleuritis, pericarditis), fever, and malaise developing months after starting (or increasing) the drug. His new pericardial rub and pleuritic pain plus polyarthralgia on hydralazine fit this. NSAID-induced interstitial nephritis (option A) causes renal dysfunction, pyuria, and sometimes a triad of fever/rash/eosinophilia, not serositis. ACE inhibitor angioedema (option C) causes facial/airway swelling, not arthritis/serositis. Statin myopathy (option D) causes myalgia and elevated CK, not pleuropericarditis. Calcium channel blocker edema (option E) is peripheral edema without systemic inflammation. The temporal link to hydralazine and the lupus-like serositis/arthritis make drug-induced lupus the leading consideration.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ANA: positive 1:320 (homogeneous pattern)
Anti-histone antibodies: positive
Anti-dsDNA: negative
Anti-Smith: negative
C3/C4: normal
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no protein, no blood, no casts (bland)
WBC 6.2 ×10³/µL
Hgb 13.8 g/dL
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
ESR 46 mm/hr
ECG: diffuse ST-segment changes consistent with pericarditis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given positive ANA and anti-histone antibodies, negative anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith, normal complement, and a bland urinalysis on hydralazine, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Mixed connective tissue disease
Systemic lupus erythematosus (idiopathic)
Drug-induced lupus from hydralazine
Rheumatoid arthritis in this clinical context
Viral pericarditis with reactive arthritis
stages.explanation: Drug-induced lupus is supported by positive ANA and anti-histone antibodies (present in the majority of cases), negative anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith, normal complement, and absence of renal/CNS involvement (bland urinalysis), all temporally tied to hydralazine. Idiopathic SLE (option A) typically features anti-dsDNA and/or anti-Smith, low complement, and often renal/hematologic/CNS disease, which are absent here. RA (option B) causes erosive symmetric arthritis with positive RF/anti-CCP and does not explain anti-histone positivity or serositis in this pattern. Viral pericarditis with reactive arthritis (option D) would not produce a positive ANA/anti-histone profile. MCTD (option E) features high-titer anti-U1 RNP with overlap features (Raynaud, sclerodactyly), not seen here. The anti-histone-positive, anti-dsDNA-negative, normal-complement, drug-linked picture is classic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management step?
options: Start high-dose cyclophosphamide as initial therapy
Discontinue hydralazine
Initiate chronic anticoagulation as initial therapy
Continue hydralazine and add hydroxychloroquine indefinitely as initial therapy
Begin lifelong immunosuppression as for idiopathic SLE as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of drug-induced lupus management is stopping the offending drug — here, hydralazine — after which symptoms typically resolve over weeks to months. Symptomatic treatment with NSAIDs addresses arthralgia and mild serositis, and a short course of corticosteroids is appropriate for more significant serositis such as symptomatic pericarditis. Cyclophosphamide (option B) is far too aggressive for this self-limited, organ-sparing condition. Lifelong immunosuppression as for idiopathic SLE (option C) is unnecessary since the disease remits after drug withdrawal. Continuing hydralazine (option D) perpetuates the disease; hydroxychloroquine is not the primary fix. Anticoagulation (option E) is not indicated. Drug withdrawal plus symptomatic care is the definitive approach.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: What should the patient be counseled to expect regarding his symptoms and antibodies after stopping hydralazine?
options: He will inevitably develop lupus nephritis as the immediate next step
Clinical symptoms usually resolve within weeks to months
The antibodies will normalize within 24 hours as the immediate next step
Symptoms will persist lifelong despite stopping the drug as the immediate next step
He must avoid all antihypertensive medications permanently as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Patients should be reassured that clinical symptoms of drug-induced lupus typically resolve within weeks to a few months after stopping the culprit drug, while serologies (ANA) can persist longer and decline gradually; future rechallenge with the same agent should be avoided to prevent recurrence. Lifelong persistence of symptoms (option B) is incorrect — the condition is reversible. Antibodies do not normalize in 24 hours (option C); serologic resolution lags clinical recovery. Drug-induced lupus rarely causes significant renal disease, so inevitable lupus nephritis (option D) is false. He does not need to avoid all antihypertensives (option E) — only the implicated hydralazine; alternative agents can be substituted. Accurate counseling reduces anxiety and prevents harmful rechallenge.

pearls: Classic culprits for drug-induced lupus include hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, minocycline, and TNF inhibitors; it presents with arthralgia, serositis, fever, and malaise months after starting the drug.
Serology shows positive ANA and anti-histone antibodies with typically NEGATIVE anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith and NORMAL complement.
Renal and CNS involvement are characteristically rare, distinguishing it from idiopathic SLE.
Treatment is withdrawal of the offending drug (with NSAIDs or short-course steroids for serositis); symptoms resolve over weeks to months and rechallenge should be avoided.

cases.title: Newborn Girl With a Slow Heartbeat Noted at Birth
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent fetal and neonatal bradycardia detected on routine monitoring.
cases.history: A term girl is born at 38 weeks to a 31-year-old G2P2 mother. Fetal bradycardia (heart rate ~55 bpm) was first noted at the 20-week anatomy scan and persisted; serial fetal echocardiograms showed atrioventricular dissociation without structural heart disease. The mother has had intermittent dry eyes, dry mouth, and arthralgias for two years but was never formally evaluated; she takes no medications and has no known autoimmune diagnosis. Pregnancy was otherwise uncomplicated; she received routine prenatal care and denies infections, fevers, or teratogen exposure. There were no maternal medications, alcohol, or tobacco. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with systemic lupus erythematosus. The infant was delivered vaginally with Apgars of 8 and 9. At birth she is pink and vigorous but with a sustained heart rate in the 50s. No prior siblings were affected. Newborn screen is pending. The mother breastfeeds without difficulty, and the infant is feeding, voiding, and stooling normally on the ward.
cases.exam: Alert, well-perfused term infant in no distress. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Cardiovascular: regular but profoundly bradycardic rhythm at ~52 bpm with no murmur; normal S1 and variable-intensity S1; femoral pulses 2+ and symmetric; capillary refill <2 seconds. Lungs clear with mild tachypnea, no retractions. Abdomen soft, liver edge 1 cm below costal margin. Skin: faint annular erythematous patches on the scalp and periorbital area, without scale or vesicles. Neurologic exam age-appropriate with good tone and symmetric Moro. No hepatosplenomegaly beyond the noted liver edge and no peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial diagnostic test will most directly characterize the cause of this infant's bradycardia?
options: Blood culture and empiric antibiotics for sepsis
Continuous pulse oximetry with pre- and post-ductal saturations
12-lead electrocardiogram
Serum electrolytes and ionized calcium in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A 12-lead ECG is the immediate test to define the rhythm; in congenital complete heart block the atrial rate is normal while the ventricular rate is slow and dissociated, confirming third-degree AV block. Pulse oximetry with pre/post-ductal saturations screens for ductal-dependent cyanotic lesions but does not characterize the conduction abnormality and the exam shows no cyanosis. Electrolytes and ionized calcium are reasonable to exclude reversible contributors but will not explain fixed congenital AV dissociation seen since 20 weeks. Sepsis can cause bradycardia in a sick neonate, but this infant is well-appearing and febrile illness is absent; empiric antibiotics do not address the documented chronic conduction defect.

stages.label: ECG & INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
ECG: atrial rate 140 bpm, ventricular rate 52 bpm, complete AV dissociation (third-degree AV block), narrow QRS
No structural defect on echocardiogram; normal ventricular function, mild tricuspid regurgitation
Na 138 mEq/L
K 4.4 mEq/L
Ionized Ca 1.22 mmol/L
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 16.1 g/dL
Platelets 188 ×10³/µL
AST 96 U/L
ALT 71 U/L
Total bilirubin 5.2 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the complete heart block, annular rash, and transaminitis, which maternal antibodies are most likely responsible?
options: Anti-Smith antibodies in this clinical context
Anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies
Anti-Ro/SSA antibodies
Antiphospholipid antibodies
stages.explanation: Neonatal lupus is caused by transplacental passage of maternal anti-Ro/SSA (and frequently anti-La/SSB) IgG, which target the fetal conduction system and skin; congenital complete heart block is the hallmark cardiac manifestation, often with cutaneous, hepatic, and hematologic involvement. Anti-dsDNA correlates with maternal lupus nephritis activity but is not the antibody that crosses to cause neonatal heart block. Anti-Smith is highly specific for maternal SLE but is not implicated in neonatal lupus conduction disease. Antiphospholipid antibodies cause thrombosis and pregnancy morbidity (recurrent loss, preeclampsia), not congenital AV block.
