Cultures of blood and synovial fluid are frequently negative; multisite mucosal NAAT (cervical/urethral, pharyngeal, rectal) maximizes detection.
Treat with IV/IM ceftriaxone and presumptively cover chlamydia; complete an STI workup and ensure partner treatment.
Suspect terminal complement (C5-C9) deficiency in patients with recurrent neisserial infections.

cases.title: A Swollen Knee That Keeps Coming Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent swelling and stiffness of the right knee over the past few months.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man presents to clinic with several months of intermittent right knee swelling. Episodes last 1-3 weeks, with the knee becoming large, stiff, and uncomfortable but only mildly painful; between flares it returns nearly to normal. He recalls no acute injury. Last summer—about 4 months ago—he took a hiking trip in rural Connecticut and removed several ticks afterward; he does not recall a rash but did have a brief flu-like illness with fatigue and headache a couple of weeks after the trip that resolved on its own. He has no morning stiffness elsewhere, no back pain, no urethritis, no eye redness, and no diarrhea. He has no chronic medical problems, takes only occasional ibuprofen, and has no allergies. He works as a landscaper, drinks beer on weekends, and does not use tobacco or drugs. Family history is unremarkable for autoimmune disease. He denies fevers, weight loss, or recent unprotected sexual contacts. He has not traveled internationally.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile man in no distress. The right knee shows a moderate effusion with a positive bulge sign, mild warmth, and slightly reduced flexion; it is only mildly tender and he bears weight without difficulty. There is no overlying erythema and no overlying skin rash or erythema migrans. The left knee and all other joints are normal, with no enthesitis at the Achilles insertion and no dactylitis. Skin survey reveals no annular lesions. There is no conjunctivitis, no oral ulcers, and no cardiac murmur or irregular rhythm. Distal pulses and neurologic exam are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the history and a single recurrent knee monoarthritis, which diagnosis should top the differential?
options: Septic arthritis from Staphylococcus aureus
Gout as the first step today
Rheumatoid arthritis
Late Lyme disease with Lyme arthritis
stages.explanation: An intermittent, oligo-/mono-articular arthritis of a large joint (classically the knee) appearing months after exposure in a Lyme-endemic region (New England), in a patient with a preceding viral-like illness and tick exposure, is the textbook picture of late Lyme disease. Lyme arthritis is typically only mildly painful relative to the degree of swelling. Gout (option A) usually causes acute, intensely painful podagra or monoarthritis with rapid onset, uncommon as a recurrent knee picture at this age without risk factors. Rheumatoid arthritis (option C) is symmetric and small-joint predominant with prolonged morning stiffness. Bacterial septic arthritis (option D) is acutely painful, febrile, and does not remit and relapse over months.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the recommended serologic testing strategy to confirm the diagnosis?
options: A single Lyme IgM Western blot as the primary diagnosis
Synovial fluid culture for Borrelia burgdorferi as the primary diagnosis
Two-tier testing: a sensitive ELISA,
Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) on serum as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Standard confirmation uses two-tier serology: a sensitive first-tier enzyme immunoassay (ELISA), followed—if reactive or equivocal—by a confirmatory immunoblot, or a second EIA in the modified two-tier algorithm. By the time Lyme arthritis appears (weeks to months out), essentially all patients have a strongly positive IgG response, so IgG blots are key. A lone IgM blot (option A) is unreliable in late disease and prone to false positives. Borrelia is fastidious and synovial culture (option C) is not clinically useful. Serum PCR (option D) has poor sensitivity; synovial fluid PCR can support the diagnosis but serology remains the standard first step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 7.6 ×10³/µL
ESR 32 mm/hr
CRP 2.1 mg/dL
Uric acid 5.4 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor: negative; anti-CCP: negative; ANA: negative
Lyme first-tier EIA: positive
Lyme IgG immunoblot: positive (≥5 of 10 bands); IgM immunoblot: negative
Knee arthrocentesis: cloudy yellow fluid, WBC 28,000/µL (70% neutrophils), no crystals under polarized light, Gram stain negative, culture pending
Knee radiograph: moderate effusion, no erosions

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment for this patient's Lyme arthritis?
options: Lifelong suppressive antibiotics as initial therapy
A 28-day course of oral doxycycline
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection alone
Immediate arthroscopic synovectomy as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Initial therapy for Lyme arthritis is a 28-day course of an oral antibiotic—doxycycline (or amoxicillin/cefuroxime if doxycycline is contraindicated). Most patients respond, though resolution of the effusion can take weeks to months. IV ceftriaxone is reserved for those with no/partial response after oral therapy. Intra-articular steroids alone (option B) are not first-line and may worsen outcomes before infection is treated. Arthroscopic synovectomy (option C) is reserved for antibiotic-refractory (post-infectious) arthritis after appropriate courses fail. Lifelong suppressive antibiotics (option D) are never indicated; persistent symptoms after adequate treatment reflect immune-mediated 'post-antibiotic' arthritis, not ongoing infection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The knee improves but a mild effusion persists 2 months after completing oral and then IV antibiotics, with negative synovial PCR. What is the best next step?
options: Another prolonged course of IV antibiotics as the immediate next step
Diagnose chronic Lyme disease and begin combination long-term antimicrobials in this setting
Treat as antibiotic-refractory Lyme arthritis with NSAIDs, intra-articular steroids,
Start chronic oral antibiotics indefinitely as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Persistent synovitis after adequate oral plus IV antibiotic courses, with negative synovial PCR, indicates antibiotic-refractory (immune-mediated/post-infectious) Lyme arthritis rather than ongoing infection. Management shifts to anti-inflammatory therapy: NSAIDs, intra-articular corticosteroids, and DMARDs (e.g., methotrexate or hydroxychloroquine) with rheumatology input; synovectomy is a later option. Repeated or indefinite antibiotics (options A and C) provide no benefit once infection is cleared and add harm. 'Chronic Lyme disease' treated with prolonged combination antimicrobials (option D) is not an evidence-based entity and is associated with serious harms.

pearls: Lyme arthritis is typically an intermittent or persistent mono-/oligoarthritis of a large joint—classically the knee—with swelling out of proportion to pain.
By the time arthritis appears, two-tier serology with a positive IgG immunoblot is essentially always present; isolated IgM positivity in late disease suggests a false positive.
First-line treatment is 28 days of oral doxycycline (or amoxicillin); reserve IV ceftriaxone for inadequate response.
Synovitis persisting after adequate antibiotics with negative synovial PCR reflects immune-mediated antibiotic-refractory arthritis, treated with anti-inflammatories/DMARDs, not more antibiotics.

cases.title: Wandering Joint Pain and a Tired Heart in a Schoolboy
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of joint pains that move from joint to joint, plus fatigue and chest discomfort.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is admitted for 2 weeks of migratory joint pain. It began in the right knee with marked swelling and severe pain, improved over a few days, then jumped to the left ankle, then the right elbow and wrist—each joint dramatically painful for 2-4 days before resolving. His parents report he had a sore throat about 4 weeks ago that was not tested or treated. Over the past several days he has become fatigued, short of breath with exertion, and complains of intermittent chest discomfort. He has had low-grade fevers. There is no rash that they noticed initially, though his mother now points out faint pink rings on his trunk. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He lives with his parents and a younger sibling; the family recently immigrated and has limited access to primary care. There is no family history of rheumatic disease. He has had no recent travel, tick exposure, or new sexual contacts. Immunizations are reportedly up to date.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing boy, mildly tachypneic. The right wrist and left ankle are swollen, warm, and exquisitely tender with guarding; previously involved joints now look normal. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia disproportionate to the fever and a new high-pitched holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla; no rub appreciated today. Skin shows faint, evanescent, ring-shaped pink macules with central clearing on the trunk that spare the face. There are no subcutaneous nodules palpable today. Neurologic exam shows no chorea or abnormal movements. Lungs have mild bibasilar crackles. Pharynx is mildly erythematous without exudate.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which diagnostic framework best fits this clinical picture?
options: Acute rheumatic fever, applying the Jones criteria after group A streptococcal pharyngitis
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (systemic/Still disease) as the first step today
Reactive arthritis following enteric infection as the first step today
Septic arthritis with secondary bacteremia as the first step today
stages.explanation: A migratory polyarthritis of large joints plus carditis (new mitral regurgitation murmur), an evanescent annular rash (erythema marginatum), low-grade fever, and elevated inflammatory markers appearing 2-4 weeks after untreated pharyngitis is the classic Jones-criteria presentation of acute rheumatic fever (ARF). Reactive arthritis (option A) follows GI/GU infection and is typically asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis without carditis or erythema marginatum. Systemic JIA (option B/Still) causes quotidian fevers and a salmon-colored evanescent rash but not valvular carditis or strictly migratory large-joint arthritis tied to strep. Septic arthritis (option D) is usually monoarticular and would not migrate or cause this murmur.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Jones criteria, which combination would satisfy a diagnosis of acute rheumatic fever in this patient?
options: A single major criterion with no evidence of streptococcal infection
Positive ANA plus arthralgia as the primary diagnosis
Evidence of preceding GAS infection PLUS two major criteria
Two minor criteria alone as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The Jones criteria require evidence of antecedent group A streptococcal (GAS) infection PLUS either two major criteria, or one major plus two minor criteria. Here the patient has at least two major criteria—migratory polyarthritis and carditis (and likely erythema marginatum)—so documenting prior GAS (elevated/rising ASO or anti-DNase B, or positive throat culture/rapid test) confirms ARF. Two minor criteria alone (option A) are insufficient. A single major criterion without evidence of strep (option C) does not meet criteria. ANA and arthralgia (option D) are not Jones criteria—arthralgia is only a minor criterion in low-risk populations and cannot be counted when polyarthritis is already a major criterion.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.1 ×10³/µL
ESR 76 mm/hr
CRP 9.4 mg/dL
ASO titer: markedly elevated; anti-DNase B: elevated
Rapid strep antigen: negative; throat culture: no growth
Blood cultures: no growth at 48 h
ECG: sinus tachycardia with PR interval 0.22 s (first-degree AV block)
Chest radiograph: mild cardiomegaly with subtle pulmonary congestion
Echocardiogram: moderate mitral regurgitation with mild aortic regurgitation; preserved LV systolic function; trivial pericardial effusion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment regimen?
options: IV immunoglobulin and rituximab as initial therapy
Doxycycline plus physical therapy as initial therapy
Penicillin to eradicate GAS, anti-inflammatory therapy ,
High-dose colchicine and allopurinol as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acute management of ARF has three arms: (1) eradicate residual GAS with penicillin/amoxicillin (or a macrolide if penicillin-allergic); (2) suppress inflammation—aspirin for arthritis, with corticosteroids often added for moderate-to-severe carditis; and (3) treat heart failure/valvular complications supportively. Doxycycline (option A) is not standard GAS eradication and does not address carditis. Colchicine/allopurinol (option C) treat crystalline arthritis, not ARF. IVIG and rituximab (option D) have not shown benefit in ARF carditis and are not standard therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Because this patient has carditis, what is the recommended duration of secondary antibiotic prophylaxis?
options: Benzathine penicillin G every 3-4 weeks, continued long-term
Prophylaxis for exactly 1 year regardless of cardiac involvement with appropriate follow-up
A single 10-day course of oral penicillin only with appropriate follow-up
No prophylaxis is needed once the acute episode resolves with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Patients with ARF require secondary prophylaxis—typically intramuscular benzathine penicillin G every 3-4 weeks—to prevent recurrent GAS infection and progressive rheumatic heart disease. Duration depends on cardiac involvement: with carditis and persistent valvular disease, prophylaxis continues for at least 10 years or until age 40 (sometimes lifelong), whereas carditis without residual disease or no carditis warrants shorter durations. Stopping after the acute episode (option A) risks recurrence and worsening valve damage. A single 10-day course (option C) treats acute infection but is not prophylaxis. A fixed 1-year course regardless of carditis (option D) ignores risk stratification and is too short for this patient.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term cardiac complication is this patient at greatest risk of developing?
options: Aortic stenosis from a bicuspid valve as the leading concern
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy as the leading concern
Chronic rheumatic heart disease, most commonly mitral stenosis/regurgitation
Coronary artery aneurysms as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The most important sequela of ARF is chronic rheumatic heart disease, with the mitral valve most commonly affected—initially regurgitation and, over years, mitral stenosis—followed by the aortic valve. This drives the need for prophylaxis and echocardiographic follow-up. Bicuspid aortic stenosis (option A) is congenital and unrelated to ARF. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (option C) is genetic, not post-streptococcal. Coronary artery aneurysms (option D) are characteristic of Kawasaki disease, not rheumatic fever.

cases.diagnosis: Acute rheumatic fever with carditis (meeting Jones criteria)
pearls: Diagnose ARF with the Jones criteria: evidence of antecedent GAS plus two major, or one major plus two minor criteria.
Major criteria (JONES): Joints (migratory polyarthritis), heart (carditis), Nodules (subcutaneous), Erythema marginatum, Sydenham chorea.
Acute therapy: eradicate GAS with penicillin, anti-inflammatories (aspirin; steroids for significant carditis), and heart-failure care.
Secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin prevents recurrence; duration is longest (≥10 years or to age 40) when carditis with residual valve disease is present.

cases.title: Sudden Achy Hands After a Sick Kid at Home
cases.chiefComplaint: About a week of symmetric pain and stiffness in the fingers and wrists.
cases.history: A 31-year-old preschool teacher presents with 6 days of symmetric joint pain involving the small joints of both hands (PIPs and MCPs), both wrists, and to a lesser extent the knees. The onset was fairly abrupt and is associated with about 45 minutes of morning stiffness. Two weeks ago, several children in her class—and her own 5-year-old—developed a bright red 'slapped-cheek' facial rash followed by a lacy rash on the arms. She herself had a mild flu-like illness with low-grade fever and malaise around 10 days ago that has since resolved; she did not develop the facial rash. She is otherwise healthy, takes only a daily prenatal-style multivitamin (she is trying to conceive), and has no allergies. Her last menstrual period was 5 weeks ago. She denies psoriasis, prior joint disease, dry eyes/mouth, photosensitivity, or Raynaud phenomenon. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history includes rheumatoid arthritis in a maternal aunt. No recent travel or tick exposure.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Hands show mild symmetric soft-tissue swelling and tenderness of multiple PIP and MCP joints bilaterally with mild wrist tenderness; range of motion is preserved and there are no deformities, nodules, or visibly eroded joints. Knees have no effusions. There is no rash on her face, trunk, or extremities today; no psoriatic plaques or nail pitting. No oral ulcers, no malar rash, no sclerodactyly, and no synovitis-related warmth beyond mild tenderness. Lymph nodes are not enlarged. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this acute symmetric polyarthritis?
options: Gonococcal arthritis-dermatitis syndrome as the first step today
Systemic lupus erythematosus as the first step today
New-onset rheumatoid arthritis as the first step today
Parvovirus B19-associated viral arthritis following exposure to erythema infectiosum
stages.explanation: Acute, symmetric small-joint polyarthritis with morning stiffness in a young adult shortly after exposure to a child with 'slapped-cheek' rash (erythema infectiosum) is classic for parvovirus B19 arthropathy. Adults—especially women—often develop arthritis rather than the facial rash, and it typically follows a mild viral prodrome. New-onset RA (option A) is possible and can look identical early, but the temporal link to a sick child with fifth disease and the expected self-limited course make parvovirus most likely; RA is a diagnosis to reconsider if symptoms persist beyond about 6 weeks. SLE (option C) usually has additional features (rash, cytopenias, serositis). Gonococcal disease (option D) causes migratory arthritis with tenosynovitis and pustular lesions, not a symmetric small-joint pattern after fifth-disease exposure.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory test best confirms acute parvovirus B19 infection in an immunocompetent adult?
options: Parvovirus B19 IgM (with IgG) serology
Bone marrow biopsy as the primary diagnosis
Cold agglutinin titer in this clinical context
Quantitative parvovirus B19 DNA PCR as the primary screening test
stages.explanation: In an immunocompetent adult with acute symptoms, detection of parvovirus B19 IgM (often with rising or positive IgG) is the test of choice and indicates recent infection. PCR for B19 DNA (option B) is most useful in immunocompromised patients, in chronic infection/aplastic states, or in the fetus, because viremia may be transient and low-level DNA can persist after infection—so it is not the first-line confirmatory test here. Bone marrow biopsy (option C) is unnecessary in an immunocompetent host with classic features. Cold agglutinins (option D) are associated with Mycoplasma and certain lymphoproliferative disorders, not parvovirus.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 5.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 12.6 g/dL
Platelets 232 ×10³/µL
ESR 22 mm/hr
CRP 1.1 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor: negative; anti-CCP: negative; ANA: negative
Parvovirus B19 IgM: positive; IgG: positive
Urine pregnancy test: positive (consistent with early pregnancy)
Hand radiographs: soft-tissue swelling without erosions

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is newly found to be pregnant. What is the most appropriate management of her arthritis and overall care?
options: Begin high-dose prednisone taper indefinitely as initial therapy
Reassure that parvovirus arthritis is usually self-limited, manage symptoms with acetaminophen ,
Initiate IV immunoglobulin immediately as initial therapy
Start methotrexate for presumed early rheumatoid arthritis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Parvovirus B19 arthropathy is typically self-limited, resolving over a few weeks, so management is supportive—analgesia and reassurance. In pregnancy, acetaminophen is preferred and NSAIDs are generally avoided (particularly in the third trimester due to ductal/renal effects), and importantly she needs obstetric follow-up because maternal B19 infection can cause fetal anemia and hydrops fetalis, warranting monitoring (e.g., serial fetal ultrasound/MCA Dopplers). Methotrexate (option A) is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic/abortifacient) and premature for self-limited viral arthritis. Chronic high-dose prednisone (option C) is unwarranted. IVIG (option D) is reserved for chronic B19 in immunocompromised patients or severe fetal disease, not routine adult arthropathy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which patient population is at greatest risk for a transient aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19?
options: Patients with chronic hemolytic anemias such as sickle cell disease or hereditary spherocytosis
Patients on chronic anticoagulation as the leading concern
Patients with iron-deficiency anemia as the leading concern
Healthy adults with normal red-cell turnover as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Parvovirus B19 infects erythroid progenitors and halts red-cell production. People who depend on brisk red-cell production—those with chronic hemolytic anemias like sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis, or thalassemia—can develop a transient aplastic crisis (sudden severe anemia with reticulocytopenia). Healthy adults (option A) tolerate the brief red-cell production arrest without significant anemia. Iron-deficiency anemia (option C) and anticoagulation (option D) do not predispose to aplastic crisis from B19. Immunocompromised patients are instead at risk for chronic pure red-cell aplasia.

cases.diagnosis: Viral arthritis due to parvovirus B19 (in early pregnancy)
pearls: Acute symmetric small-joint polyarthritis after contact with a child who has 'slapped-cheek' rash suggests parvovirus B19; adults often get arthritis rather than the rash.
Confirm acute infection in immunocompetent adults with B19 IgM; reserve DNA PCR for immunocompromised, chronic, or fetal cases.
The arthropathy is usually self-limited; early RA should be reconsidered only if symptoms persist beyond ~6 weeks.
Maternal B19 infection can cause fetal anemia/hydrops, and B19 triggers transient aplastic crisis in patients with chronic hemolytic anemias.

cases.title: Slow, Lopsided Weakness in the Grip and Thighs
cases.chiefComplaint: Two years of gradually worsening hand grip and difficulty rising from chairs.
cases.history: A 68-year-old retired electrician presents with about 2 years of slowly progressive weakness. He first noticed trouble opening jars and a weakening grip, more on the right than the left, and frequent dropping of objects. Over the past year he has had difficulty rising from low chairs and climbing stairs, and he has tripped several times due to his knees 'buckling.' He denies pain, rash, dysphagia early on but now reports occasional trouble swallowing solids. There are no sensory complaints, no diplopia, and no fatigable ptosis. He has hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 40 mg daily (taken for 6 years). He has no allergies. He is a former smoker (20 pack-years), drinks minimally, and lives independently though now uses a cane. Family history is noncontributory for neuromuscular disease. A prior clinician diagnosed 'polymyositis' and tried a course of prednisone for 3 months with no meaningful improvement. Review of systems is otherwise negative for fevers, weight loss, and joint swelling.
cases.exam: Alert man with normal cognition and speech. Motor exam shows asymmetric weakness with prominent involvement of the finger flexors (notably weak grip, right worse than left) and the quadriceps bilaterally; there is visible atrophy of the volar forearms and quadriceps. Deltoids and proximal shoulder strength are relatively preserved. Deep tendon reflexes are reduced at the knees. Sensation to light touch, pinprick, and vibration is intact. There is no rash, no heliotrope discoloration, no Gottron papules, and no synovitis. Cranial nerves are normal with no ptosis or ophthalmoplegia. Gait is cautious with mild quadriceps buckling. No fasciculations or upper motor neuron signs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which pattern of weakness most strongly suggests the underlying diagnosis?
options: Distal symmetric sensory loss with absent ankle reflexes as the first step today
Fatigable weakness with ptosis and diplopia worsening through the day as the first step today
Symmetric proximal weakness with a heliotrope rash and Gottron papules as the first step today
Asymmetric weakness preferentially affecting the finger flexors
stages.explanation: Inclusion body myositis (IBM) characteristically causes slowly progressive, often asymmetric weakness that preferentially targets the finger/wrist flexors and the quadriceps (knee extensors), in an older patient (typically >50, male predominance), and is notoriously refractory to corticosteroids. This selective pattern—weak grip with relatively spared deltoids, plus quadriceps buckling—is the clue. A heliotrope rash with Gottron papules and symmetric proximal weakness (option A) describes dermatomyositis. Fatigable ptosis/diplopia (option C) is myasthenia gravis. Distal sensory loss with absent ankle reflexes (option D) is a peripheral neuropathy; IBM spares sensation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which initial laboratory/electrodiagnostic findings would you expect, and what is the most definitive next test?
options: Very high CK with positive acetylcholine receptor antibodies as the primary diagnosis
Normal-to-modestly elevated CK with a mixed myopathic/neurogenic EMG
Markedly elevated CK (>50× normal) with a pure myopathic EMG; muscle biopsy is unnecessary as the primary diagnosis
Normal CK with a decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: In IBM, creatine kinase is usually only mildly to moderately elevated (often <10-15× upper limit, sometimes near-normal), unlike the very high CK of polymyositis/necrotizing myopathy. EMG often shows a mixed picture with both myopathic and chronic neurogenic-appearing units. The most definitive test is muscle biopsy demonstrating the characteristic combination of endomysial inflammation with invasion of non-necrotic fibers, rimmed vacuoles, and protein aggregates. A CK >50× with pure myopathic EMG (option A) suggests necrotizing myopathy. Positive AChR antibodies (option C) and decremental repetitive stimulation (option D) point to myasthenia gravis, a neuromuscular junction disorder, not IBM.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CK 540 U/L (mildly elevated)
ESR 18 mm/hr; CRP 0.6 mg/dL
TSH normal
ANA: negative; anti-Jo-1: negative; anti-NT5C1A (cN1A): positive
AChR antibody: negative
EMG/NCS: irritable myopathy with admixed short- and long-duration motor unit potentials (mixed myopathic/neurogenic); normal sensory studies
Muscle biopsy: endomysial inflammatory infiltrate invading non-necrotic fibers, rimmed vacuoles, and congophilic/p62-positive protein aggregates

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the confirmed diagnosis, what is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Supportive care emphasizing physical/occupational therapy, exercise, fall prevention, swallowing evaluation,
Aggressive immunosuppression with high-dose prednisone plus azathioprine, expecting full recovery as initial therapy
Plasmapheresis every month indefinitely as initial therapy
Immediate initiation of IVIG as curative therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Inclusion body myositis is generally refractory to immunosuppression, and most immunomodulatory therapies (steroids, azathioprine, methotrexate) do not change the disease course; the mainstay is supportive—structured exercise and physical/occupational therapy, fall prevention, assistive devices, and swallowing assessment (with interventions like cricopharyngeal procedures for severe dysphagia). Aggressive immunosuppression expecting full recovery (option A) is incorrect and exposes patients to harm without benefit. Chronic plasmapheresis (option C) is not effective. IVIG (option D) is not curative; it is sometimes tried for significant dysphagia with limited, transient benefit but is not standard 'curative' therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His statin is reviewed in light of the muscle disease. What is the best interpretation and next step?
options: The chronic statin clearly caused IBM and stopping it will reverse the weakness as the immediate next step
Check anti-HMGCR antibodies and assume statin-induced necrotizing myopathy regardless of biopsy as the immediate next step
IBM is a distinct degenerative/inflammatory myopathy not caused by statins
Switch to a higher-intensity statin to treat the myopathy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: IBM is a chronic degenerative and inflammatory myopathy with rimmed vacuoles—it is not caused by statins, and stopping the statin will not reverse it. It is still reasonable to reassess whether the statin is needed based on cardiovascular risk and patient goals, but expectations should be set accordingly. Attributing IBM to the statin and expecting reversal (option A) is wrong. Increasing statin intensity (option C) is illogical and could add myotoxicity. Anti-HMGCR-associated immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (option D) is a separate statin-associated entity with very high CK and necrosis without rimmed vacuoles; this biopsy shows classic IBM, so that diagnosis does not fit.

pearls: Suspect IBM in an older patient (often male) with slowly progressive, asymmetric weakness of finger flexors and quadriceps that is poorly responsive to steroids.
CK is typically only mildly/moderately elevated; biopsy shows endomysial inflammation, rimmed vacuoles, and protein aggregates; anti-cN1A (NT5C1A) antibody supports the diagnosis.
Management is largely supportive—exercise, PT/OT, fall prevention, and swallowing evaluation—since immunosuppression rarely helps.
Distinguish IBM from statin-associated anti-HMGCR necrotizing myopathy (very high CK, necrosis, no rimmed vacuoles), which does respond to immunotherapy.

cases.title: Puffy Hands, Cold Fingers, and Aching Muscles
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of swollen hands, color changes in the fingers with cold, joint pain, and muscle weakness.
cases.history: A 37-year-old woman presents with about 8 months of progressive symptoms. Her hands have become diffusely 'puffy' and her fingers turn white then blue then red with cold exposure or stress. She has symmetric pain and morning stiffness in the small joints of the hands and wrists lasting over an hour, and over the last 2 months she has noticed proximal muscle weakness—trouble lifting groceries and rising from a chair. She reports occasional heartburn and mild exertional breathlessness. She denies oral ulcers, photosensitive rash, kidney problems, or prior thrombosis. She has no significant past medical history and takes only acetaminophen and a multivitamin; no allergies. She is married, works as an accountant, does not smoke, and drinks rarely. Family history includes Hashimoto thyroiditis in her mother and lupus in a cousin. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and intermittent low-grade fevers, and negative for hair loss, dry eyes/mouth, and recent infections. She has never been pregnant and uses no hormonal contraception.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman. Hands show diffuse 'puffy' swelling of the fingers (sausage-like digits) with mild sclerodactyly limited to the fingers, and a few dilated nailfold capillaries; no digital ulcers or pitting scars. Synovitis is present at several MCP and PIP joints and both wrists with tenderness and soft-tissue swelling. Proximal muscle strength is mildly reduced (4/5) in the deltoids and hip flexors without tenderness. No malar rash, no oral ulcers, and no alopecia. Lungs are clear with no crackles today; cardiac exam normal without an accentuated P2. There is no peripheral edema, and no skin thickening proximal to the MCPs over the forearms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which overarching diagnostic concept best unifies these overlapping features?
options: Seronegative spondyloarthritis as the first step today
Primary fibromyalgia as the first step today
An overlap syndrome combining features of SLE, systemic sclerosis,
An isolated, organ-specific autoimmune disease as the first step today
stages.explanation: The combination of Raynaud phenomenon, puffy/swollen hands, inflammatory arthritis, myositis, and features suggesting both lupus and scleroderma is the hallmark of an overlap syndrome—specifically mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD). MCTD blends SLE-, systemic sclerosis-, and polymyositis-like features and is associated with high-titer anti-U1-RNP antibodies. An isolated organ-specific disease (option A) cannot explain this multisystem overlap. Fibromyalgia (option C) causes diffuse pain without true synovitis, Raynaud with abnormal capillaries, or objective weakness with muscle enzyme elevation. Spondyloarthritis (option D) is axial/enthesitis-predominant and does not produce Raynaud, puffy hands, or myositis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which autoantibody is most characteristic of and required for mixed connective tissue disease?
options: Anti-Jo-1 antibody as the primary diagnosis
Anti-double-stranded DNA as the primary diagnosis
Anti-centromere antibody as the primary diagnosis
High-titer anti-U1-RNP antibody
stages.explanation: MCTD is defined by high-titer anti-U1-ribonucleoprotein (anti-U1-RNP) antibodies, almost always with a positive ANA in a speckled pattern; their presence in the right clinical setting is essential to the diagnosis. Anti-dsDNA (option A) is specific for SLE and, along with anti-Sm, is typically absent or low in pure MCTD. Anti-centromere (option C) is associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST). Anti-Jo-1 (option D) defines the antisynthetase syndrome within the myositis spectrum, not MCTD. The defining serology guides this diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 4.1 ×10³/µL
Hgb 11.8 g/dL
Platelets 168 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL; urinalysis: no proteinuria/hematuria
CK 410 U/L; aldolase elevated
ANA: positive 1:2560, speckled pattern
Anti-U1-RNP: strongly positive (high titer)
Anti-dsDNA: negative; anti-Sm: negative; anti-Scl-70: negative; anti-centromere: negative; anti-Jo-1: negative
Nailfold capillaroscopy: dilated capillaries
Echocardiogram: normal estimated pulmonary artery pressure; high-resolution chest CT: no significant interstitial lung disease

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her current disease is dominated by arthritis, Raynaud, and mild myositis without major organ involvement. What is the most appropriate management?
options: No treatment; reassure and observe only as initial therapy
Symptom- and organ-directed therapy: hydroxychloroquine and/or low-dose corticosteroids for arthritis/myositis, plus Raynaud measures
Begin lifelong anticoagulation as initial therapy
Start high-dose cyclophosphamide immediately as initial therapy
stages.explanation: MCTD is treated according to the dominant manifestations. For mild arthritis and myositis with Raynaud and no major organ threat, reasonable therapy includes hydroxychloroquine and/or low-dose glucocorticoids for inflammatory joint/muscle disease, and Raynaud management with cold avoidance and a dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nifedipine) if symptomatic. Doing nothing (option A) ignores active synovitis and myositis. High-dose cyclophosphamide (option C) is reserved for severe organ-threatening disease such as significant interstitial lung disease, not mild overlap features. Lifelong anticoagulation (option D) would apply to antiphospholipid syndrome/thrombosis, which she does not have.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the leading cause of mortality in MCTD and should be screened for over time?
options: Pulmonary arterial hypertension
Diabetic ketoacidosis as the leading concern
Hemorrhagic stroke as the leading concern
Acute appendicitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Pulmonary arterial hypertension is the leading cause of death in MCTD, often developing insidiously; interstitial lung disease is also a major contributor to morbidity and mortality. Because of this, longitudinal screening with echocardiography (and pulmonary function tests/HRCT as indicated) is important even when initial studies are normal, so that PAH and ILD can be detected and treated early. Appendicitis (option B), DKA (option C), and hemorrhagic stroke (option D) are not characteristic complications of MCTD and are not the basis for routine surveillance in this disease.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Two years later she develops worsening exertional dyspnea with a loud P2 and elevated estimated right ventricular systolic pressure on echo. What is the best next step?
options: Empiric high-dose diuretics alone without further workup in this setting
Start a beta-blocker for presumed diastolic dysfunction in this setting
Right heart catheterization to confirm pulmonary arterial hypertension
Reassure and repeat echo in 2 years as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: New exertional dyspnea with a loud P2 and elevated estimated RVSP in an MCTD patient strongly suggests pulmonary arterial hypertension, and right heart catheterization is the gold standard to confirm the diagnosis, characterize hemodynamics, and guide PAH-specific therapy (e.g., endothelin-receptor antagonists, PDE-5 inhibitors). Deferring evaluation for 2 years (option A) risks irreversible progression of a life-threatening complication. A beta-blocker for presumed diastolic dysfunction (option C) is premature and could worsen RV function in true PAH. Empiric diuretics alone without confirming the diagnosis (option D) fail to address the underlying pulmonary vascular disease.

pearls: MCTD overlaps SLE, systemic sclerosis, and myositis—think Raynaud, puffy hands, arthritis, and myositis together.
High-titer anti-U1-RNP with a positive speckled ANA is the defining serology; anti-dsDNA and anti-Sm are typically absent.
Treat according to the dominant manifestation; many overlap features respond reasonably to glucocorticoids.
Pulmonary arterial hypertension is the leading cause of death—screen longitudinally with echo and confirm suspected PAH with right heart catheterization.

cases.title: White Fingers in the Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: Fingers that turn white and blue in the cold, then red when warming up.
cases.history: A 22-year-old college student presents because her fingers change color in the cold. For the past 2-3 years, when she steps into cold weather, holds a cold drink, or feels stressed, the tips of several fingers on both hands turn sharply white, then dusky blue, and finally bright red and tingly as they rewarm. Episodes last 10-20 minutes and fully resolve with warming. They are symmetric and never associated with skin ulcers, fingertip pits, or tissue loss. She has no joint pain, rash, dry eyes/mouth, dysphagia, breathlessness, or weight loss. She is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and uses no stimulant decongestants or beta-blockers; she has no allergies. She does not smoke. There is no history of repetitive vibratory tool use, frostbite, or chemical exposure. Family history is notable for similar cold-induced finger color changes in her mother, with no diagnosed autoimmune disease. She drinks coffee occasionally and exercises regularly. Her menstrual cycles are regular and she takes no hormonal contraception.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young woman in no distress; hands are warm and pink at presentation. Fingers show no sclerodactyly, no digital pitting scars, no ulcers, and no telangiectasias. Nailfold examination at the bedside (and with magnification) reveals normal capillary architecture without dilated or dropout capillaries. There is no synovitis, no Gottron papules, and no malar rash. Radial and ulnar pulses are full and symmetric; Allen test is normal. There is no distal tissue loss or gangrene. The remainder of the skin, cardiopulmonary, and joint exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis based on the history and examination?
options: Secondary Raynaud phenomenon due to systemic sclerosis
Buerger disease (thromboangiitis obliterans)
Primary Raynaud phenomenon
Acrocyanosis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Symmetric, triphasic (white-blue-red) cold- and stress-induced color changes that fully reverse, beginning in a young woman, with a positive family history, normal nailfold capillaries, no digital ulcers/pits, and no systemic features, are classic for primary Raynaud phenomenon. Secondary Raynaud from systemic sclerosis (option A) would typically show abnormal nailfold capillaries, sclerodactyly, digital pits/ulcers, and positive serologies. Buerger disease (option C) occurs in smokers with distal ischemia and is not a transient triphasic color change. Acrocyanosis (option D) is persistent painless bluish discoloration of the hands without the sharp triphasic, episodic pattern and rewarming hyperemia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly suggest the Raynaud is secondary to an underlying connective tissue disease rather than primary?
options: Abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy with dilated/dropout capillaries
Symmetric involvement of multiple fingers as the primary diagnosis
Onset in the teens to early twenties as the primary diagnosis
Complete resolution of episodes with warming as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Features favoring secondary Raynaud include abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy (dilated capillaries, hemorrhages, dropout) and disease-specific autoantibodies such as anti-centromere or anti-Scl-70, often with older age at onset, asymmetric/severe episodes, digital ulcers/pits, and systemic symptoms. Symmetric multi-finger involvement (option A) and complete reversibility with warming (option D) are typical of primary Raynaud. Onset in the teens to twenties (option B) is also typical of primary disease. Thus, abnormal capillaroscopy plus a specific autoantibody (option C) is the strongest red flag for an underlying connective tissue disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CBC: normal
ESR 8 mm/hr; CRP 0.3 mg/dL
ANA: negative
Anti-centromere: negative; anti-Scl-70: negative
Nailfold capillaroscopy: normal capillary density and morphology, no dilation or dropout
TSH: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her episodes are mild and infrequent. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Start a non-selective beta-blocker as initial therapy
Initiate immunosuppression with prednisone as initial therapy
Begin sildenafil and refer for digital sympathectomy as initial therapy
Lifestyle measures: cold avoidance, warm gloves/layering, smoking avoidance,
stages.explanation: Primary Raynaud with mild, infrequent episodes is managed conservatively: keep the core and extremities warm (gloves, layering, hand warmers), avoid cold and abrupt temperature changes, avoid smoking and vasoconstrictive drugs (e.g., decongestants, non-selective beta-blockers, stimulants), and manage stress—along with reassurance about the benign course. Beta-blockers (option A) can precipitate vasospasm and should be avoided. Sildenafil and digital sympathectomy (option C) are reserved for severe/refractory secondary Raynaud with ischemic complications. Immunosuppression (option D) treats underlying autoimmune disease, which she does not have.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If her symptoms became frequent and bothersome despite lifestyle measures, what is the recommended first-line pharmacologic therapy?
options: A long-acting dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker
Oral prednisone as the immediate next step
Aspirin plus clopidogrel as the immediate next step
A non-selective beta-blocker as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When conservative measures are insufficient, the first-line drug for Raynaud is a long-acting dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker (e.g., amlodipine or extended-release nifedipine), which reduces the frequency and severity of vasospastic attacks by promoting vasodilation. Prednisone (option B) has no role in primary Raynaud. Dual antiplatelet therapy (option C) is not standard and adds bleeding risk without addressing vasospasm. Non-selective beta-blockers (option D) can worsen Raynaud by promoting peripheral vasoconstriction and are contraindicated.

pearls: Primary Raynaud: young woman, symmetric triphasic color change, normal nailfold capillaries, negative ANA, no digital ulcers, and often a family history.
Red flags for secondary Raynaud: older onset, asymmetry, digital pits/ulcers, abnormal capillaroscopy, and disease-specific autoantibodies (anti-centromere, anti-Scl-70).
First-line management is lifestyle modification—cold avoidance, warmth, and avoiding vasoconstrictors and smoking.
If pharmacotherapy is needed, a long-acting dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker is first-line; avoid non-selective beta-blockers.

cases.title: Crashing Blood Pressure and Failing Kidneys in a Woman with Tight Skin
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe headache, blurred vision, and dramatically elevated blood pressure with rising creatinine.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman is admitted to the ICU with a severe headache, blurred vision, and a blood pressure of 220/130 mmHg found at urgent care. Over the past 6 months she has developed rapidly progressive skin thickening of the hands, forearms, face, and trunk, along with Raynaud phenomenon, puffy then tight fingers, and fatigue. She was diagnosed 3 months ago with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis. About 6 weeks ago, for worsening arthralgias and tendon friction rubs, a clinician started her on prednisone 30 mg daily. She has no prior hypertension or kidney disease. She takes only the prednisone, omeprazole for reflux, and acetaminophen; she has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is unremarkable for renal disease. Review of systems is positive for new dyspnea, decreased urine output, and nausea, and negative for chest pain, hematuria, and prior pre-eclampsia. She reports no recent NSAID or contrast exposure. She works as a teacher and lives with her husband.
cases.exam: Anxious, mildly dyspneic woman. Skin shows diffuse thickening/tightening extending proximal to the elbows and over the trunk and face, with sclerodactyly and a few digital pitting scars; the face appears taut with reduced oral aperture. Fundoscopic exam reveals retinal hemorrhages and exudates with papilledema (hypertensive retinopathy). Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia, an S4, and bibasilar crackles. There is mild bilateral lower-extremity edema. Tendon friction rubs are palpable at the wrists. There is no pericardial rub today, abdomen is soft, and neurologic exam shows no focal deficit despite headache and visual blurring.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis driving this acute presentation?
options: Pheochromocytoma as the first step today
Scleroderma renal crisis
Essential hypertension with hypertensive emergency
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura unrelated to scleroderma
stages.explanation: New malignant/accelerated hypertension with acute kidney injury in a patient with early diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis—particularly after recent moderate-to-high-dose corticosteroids—is the classic picture of scleroderma renal crisis (SRC). SRC arises from intrarenal vascular injury and renin-angiotensin activation, producing severe hypertension and AKI, often with a microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Essential hypertension with emergency (option A) ignores her scleroderma and steroid trigger and rapid skin progression. Primary TTP (option C) shares microangiopathy but does not explain the rapidly progressive skin disease and steroid context; SRC is the unifying diagnosis here. Pheochromocytoma (option D) causes episodic catecholamine surges, not this scleroderma-linked renal picture.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of features most supports scleroderma renal crisis on initial workup?
options: Eosinophilia with eosinophiluria as the primary diagnosis
Hypokalemia with metabolic alkalosis and suppressed renin as the primary diagnosis
Acute kidney injury, markedly elevated plasma renin,
Heavy proteinuria with bland sediment and normal renin as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: SRC characteristically shows acute kidney injury with very high plasma renin activity (reflecting RAAS activation) and a thrombotic microangiopathy—schistocytes on smear, low haptoglobin, elevated LDH, and thrombocytopenia—often with only mild proteinuria and few casts. Hypokalemia/alkalosis with suppressed renin (option A) suggests hyperaldosteronism, the opposite of SRC. Heavy proteinuria with bland sediment and normal renin (option C) suggests a primary glomerular/nephrotic process, not SRC. Eosinophilia/eosinophiluria (option D) points to acute interstitial nephritis, not scleroderma renal crisis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatinine 3.4 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL one month ago)
Hgb 9.6 g/dL; platelets 92 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: schistocytes present
LDH 540 U/L; haptoglobin <10 mg/dL; reticulocytes elevated
Urinalysis: 1+ protein, few RBCs, no casts
Plasma renin activity: markedly elevated
ADAMTS13 activity: normal
ANA: positive; anti-RNA polymerase III: positive; anti-centromere: negative
Chest X-ray: pulmonary vascular congestion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for scleroderma renal crisis?
options: Intravenous beta-blocker as the primary agent as initial therapy
Immediate high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
Emergent bilateral nephrectomy as initial therapy
Prompt initiation and aggressive titration of a short-acting ACE inhibitor to control blood pressure
stages.explanation: The treatment that dramatically improved outcomes in scleroderma renal crisis is prompt, aggressive use of an ACE inhibitor—classically short-acting captopril—titrated rapidly to control blood pressure, even if creatinine rises initially, because it directly counters the renin-angiotensin-driven vascular injury. ACE inhibitors should be continued even if dialysis becomes necessary, as renal recovery can occur over months. A beta-blocker as primary therapy (option A) does not address the RAAS mechanism. High-dose corticosteroids (option C) are a precipitant of SRC and should be avoided/minimized. Nephrectomy (option D) is not a treatment for SRC.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Regarding her recent corticosteroid use, what is the key teaching point?
options: Moderate-to-high-dose corticosteroids are a recognized risk factor/precipitant for scleroderma renal crisis
Corticosteroids should be replaced with NSAIDs for joint symptoms as the leading concern
Corticosteroids have no relationship to scleroderma renal crisis as the leading concern
Corticosteroids are protective and the dose should be increased as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Moderate-to-high-dose glucocorticoids (often cited around ≥15 mg/day of prednisone) are associated with an increased risk of precipitating scleroderma renal crisis, especially in early diffuse cutaneous disease and in patients with anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies. Clinicians should therefore avoid or minimize steroids in at-risk patients and monitor blood pressure and renal function closely when they are used. Increasing the dose (option A) would worsen risk. Claiming no relationship (option C) is incorrect. Substituting NSAIDs (option D) is unwise given nephrotoxicity in a patient with renal vascular compromise.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite aggressive ACE inhibitor therapy, her creatinine rises and she becomes oliguric requiring dialysis. What is the best approach?
options: Switch to an ARB and discontinue dialysis with appropriate follow-up
Stop the ACE inhibitor permanently because the kidneys have failed with appropriate follow-up
Proceed directly to kidney transplantation within the week with appropriate follow-up
Continue the ACE inhibitor
stages.explanation: Even when dialysis is required, the ACE inhibitor should be continued (as tolerated by blood pressure) because a substantial proportion of patients recover enough renal function over weeks to months to come off dialysis; renal replacement therapy serves as a bridge. Stopping the ACE inhibitor because of dialysis (option A) abandons the therapy most likely to allow recovery. Switching to an ARB and stopping dialysis (option C) is not the established standard—ACE inhibitors are the evidence-based choice, and dialysis cannot simply be discontinued in an oliguric patient. Immediate transplantation (option D) is inappropriate during acute crisis; transplant is considered only after a prolonged period without recovery.

pearls: Suspect scleroderma renal crisis with new malignant hypertension and AKI in early diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis, especially with anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies.
Labs show high plasma renin and a thrombotic microangiopathy (schistocytes, low haptoglobin, thrombocytopenia) with only mild proteinuria.
ACE inhibitors (e.g., captopril) are the cornerstone—titrate aggressively and continue even if creatinine rises or dialysis is needed.
Avoid moderate-to-high-dose corticosteroids in at-risk diffuse scleroderma, as they can precipitate renal crisis.

cases.title: Hands That Go Numb at Night
cases.chiefComplaint: Numbness and tingling in the hand, worse at night, that wakes her up.
cases.history: A 45-year-old administrative assistant presents with several months of numbness and tingling in her right hand, worse than the left. The symptoms predominantly affect the thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger. They are worst at night, frequently waking her, and she relieves them by shaking out the hand ('flick sign'). Symptoms also flare with prolonged typing and while holding a phone or steering wheel. She reports occasional clumsiness and dropping objects but no neck pain, arm pain radiating from the neck, or weakness in the shoulder. She has no diabetes, thyroid disease, or recent pregnancy, and is not pregnant now. She takes only an oral contraceptive and occasional ibuprofen; no allergies. She does extensive keyboard work daily, does not smoke, and drinks socially. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is negative for rash, joint swelling, weight change, cold intolerance, and prior wrist trauma. She has gained a modest amount of weight over the past year.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman. Inspection of the right hand shows subtle thenar eminence flattening without ulcer or deformity. Sensation is diminished to light touch over the palmar thumb, index, middle, and radial ring finger, while sensation over the little finger and the hypothenar/dorsal-ulnar hand is preserved. Tinel sign over the volar wrist (tapping the median nerve) reproduces tingling into the radial digits, and Phalen maneuver (wrist flexion for 60 seconds) provokes paresthesias. Thumb abduction strength is mildly reduced. The neck has full painless range of motion with a negative Spurling test, and elbow flexion does not reproduce symptoms. Radial pulse is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains this distribution of symptoms and signs?
options: Carpal tunnel syndrome
Cervical radiculopathy at C8-T1 as the first step today
Thoracic outlet syndrome as the first step today
Ulnar nerve entrapment at the elbow (cubital tunnel syndrome)
stages.explanation: Nocturnal paresthesias relieved by shaking the hand, symptoms in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial half of ring finger) with sparing of the little finger, provocation by Tinel and Phalen maneuvers, and early thenar weakness are classic for carpal tunnel syndrome from median nerve compression at the wrist. C8-T1 radiculopathy (option A) follows the ulnar/medial distribution and is often associated with neck pain and a positive Spurling test. Ulnar entrapment at the elbow (option C) affects the little and ulnar-half ring fingers and the hypothenar area, which are spared here. Thoracic outlet syndrome (option D) typically causes more diffuse arm/hand symptoms with positional vascular or lower-trunk findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: In a typical case like this, what is the appropriate diagnostic approach?
options: Diagnosis requires a positive ANA and rheumatoid factor as the primary diagnosis
Carpal tunnel syndrome is primarily a clinical diagnosis
Cervical spine MRI is the first-line test as the primary diagnosis
MRI of the wrist is required in all patients before any treatment as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome is largely a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic history and exam (median distribution paresthesias, nocturnal symptoms, positive provocative tests). Nerve conduction studies/EMG are used to confirm the diagnosis when it is uncertain, to assess severity, or before surgical referral, not in every straightforward case. Routine wrist MRI (option B) is unnecessary and not first-line. Cervical spine MRI (option C) would be pursued if radiculopathy were suspected, which the exam argues against. Autoantibody testing (option D) is irrelevant to diagnosing CTS, which is a compressive mononeuropathy rather than an autoimmune disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Nerve conduction studies: prolonged median distal motor and sensory latencies across the wrist with normal ulnar studies
Electromyography: mild changes in the abductor pollicis brevis, no denervation in C8-T1 paraspinals
TSH: normal
HbA1c: 5.4%
Wrist radiographs: no fracture or significant arthritis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her symptoms are bothersome but there is no significant thenar atrophy or severe motor loss. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Systemic high-dose corticosteroids indefinitely as initial therapy
Long-term opioid analgesics as initial therapy
Immediate surgical carpal tunnel release as initial therapy
Conservative therapy: a neutral-position wrist splint , activity/ergonomic modification,
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderate carpal tunnel syndrome without severe motor deficit or significant thenar atrophy, first-line treatment is conservative: a neutral-position wrist splint worn at night (and during aggravating activities), ergonomic/activity modification, and a local corticosteroid injection, which can provide meaningful relief. Immediate surgery (option A) is reserved for severe disease (marked atrophy, denervation, or failure of conservative care). Long-term opioids (option C) are inappropriate for a compressive mononeuropathy. Indefinite systemic high-dose steroids (option D) carry unacceptable risks and are not indicated; a short course or local injection is preferred over chronic systemic steroids.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite several months of splinting and an injection, she develops worsening thenar atrophy and persistent weakness. What is the best next step?
options: Begin disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs
Continue the same splint indefinitely in this setting
Switch to an ulnar-targeted therapy in this setting
Refer for surgical carpal tunnel release
stages.explanation: Progressive thenar muscle atrophy and persistent motor weakness, or failure of adequate conservative therapy, are indications for surgical carpal tunnel release to decompress the median nerve and prevent further, potentially irreversible, motor loss. Continuing the same splint indefinitely (option A) risks permanent thenar damage. Ulnar-targeted therapy (option C) is wrong because this is a median, not ulnar, neuropathy. DMARDs (option D) treat inflammatory arthritis and have no role in a compressive mononeuropathy like CTS.

pearls: Carpal tunnel syndrome causes median-distribution paresthesias (thumb, index, middle, radial ring finger) that are worst at night and relieved by shaking the hand.
Phalen and Tinel signs support the diagnosis; it is primarily clinical, with NCS/EMG to confirm uncertain cases, grade severity, or plan surgery.
First-line treatment is a neutral wrist splint (especially nocturnal), ergonomic modification, and corticosteroid injection.
Refer for surgical release when there is thenar atrophy, motor weakness, or failure of conservative therapy.

cases.title: Thumb-Side Wrist Pain in a New Mother
cases.chiefComplaint: Pain along the thumb side of my right wrist for the past three weeks.
cases.history: A 32-year-old right-handed woman presents with three weeks of progressively worsening pain over the radial aspect of her right wrist. She delivered her first child seven weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding. The pain is worst when she lifts the baby under the arms or wrings out a washcloth, and it radiates up the forearm and down into the thumb. She denies trauma, numbness, tingling, or morning stiffness lasting more than a few minutes. She reports no fevers, rashes, or other joint complaints. PMH is unremarkable; she had an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Medications include a prenatal vitamin daily and acetaminophen 500 mg as needed, which gives partial relief. She has no known drug allergies. Social history: works as a graphic designer, currently on maternity leave; does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, no illicit drugs. Family history is notable for hypothyroidism in her mother and rheumatoid arthritis in a maternal aunt. She is anxious that the pain is interfering with caring for her newborn.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-appearing woman. Right wrist shows mild swelling and tenderness over the radial styloid at the first dorsal compartment; no warmth or erythema. There is palpable thickening over the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons. Resisted thumb extension and abduction reproduce pain. Finkelstein test is positive on the right. Grip strength mildly reduced by pain. No tenderness over the anatomic snuffbox, no scaphoid tenderness. MCP and PIP joints are non-tender with no synovitis. Tinel and Phalen signs are negative. Neurovascular exam of the hand is intact; sensation normal in median, ulnar, and radial distributions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical examination maneuver best supports the suspected diagnosis?
options: Watson scaphoid shift test
Finkelstein test
Phalen maneuver
Tinel sign over the carpal tunnel
stages.explanation: The Finkelstein test, performed by ulnar-deviating the wrist with the thumb flexed into the palm, stretches the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis and reproduces pain in De Quervain tenosynovitis, the suspected diagnosis. Tinel sign and the Phalen maneuver test for carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve), which would produce paresthesias rather than localized radial-styloid pain and is not supported by this patient's intact sensory exam. The Watson scaphoid shift test evaluates scapholunate instability after trauma, which the history does not suggest. The positive Finkelstein in the context of point tenderness over the first dorsal compartment is the classic confirmatory maneuver here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Scaphoid fracture
First carpometacarpal (thumb) osteoarthritis
Carpal tunnel syndrome
De Quervain tenosynovitis
stages.explanation: De Quervain tenosynovitis is stenosing tenosynovitis of the first dorsal compartment tendons (APL and EPB), classically seen in new mothers and caregivers from repetitive thumb abduction and wrist ulnar deviation while lifting an infant. Point tenderness over the radial styloid plus a positive Finkelstein test confirm it. Carpal tunnel syndrome causes median-distribution paresthesias, absent here. Scaphoid fracture follows trauma (fall on outstretched hand) with snuffbox tenderness, which is negative. First CMC osteoarthritis causes pain at the thumb base with a positive grind test and is far less common at age 32 without prior injury. The occupational/caregiving history and exam make De Quervain the clear answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Thumb spica splint, activity modification,
Wrist MRI to confirm the diagnosis as monotherapy
Surgical release of the first dorsal compartment
Oral glucocorticoid taper as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line management of De Quervain tenosynovitis is conservative: a thumb spica splint to rest the tendons, activity modification (adjusting lifting technique), and NSAIDs for pain and inflammation. This patient is breastfeeding, so topical or short-course low-dose NSAIDs such as ibuprofen are generally considered compatible with lactation. Surgical release is reserved for refractory cases after failed conservative care and corticosteroid injection. An oral glucocorticoid taper is not standard and carries systemic risk for a localized tendinopathy. MRI is unnecessary because the diagnosis is clinical; imaging is reserved for atypical presentations or to exclude alternatives. Conservative therapy is both diagnostic and therapeutic here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She returns after six weeks of splinting and NSAIDs with persistent pain limiting infant care. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat the same splinting regimen for another three months
Immediate surgical decompression before discharge planning
Corticosteroid injection into the first dorsal compartment
Switch to long-term oral opioids before discharge planning
stages.explanation: When splinting, activity modification, and NSAIDs fail after an adequate trial, the next step is a corticosteroid injection into the first dorsal compartment, which has high success rates (often 70 to 90 percent) and is the standard second-line therapy. Surgical decompression is reserved for patients who fail injection(s), not before. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a benign, self-limited tendinopathy and pose dependence and lactation concerns. Simply repeating the failed regimen ignores her ongoing functional impairment and delays effective treatment. A single targeted injection is the evidence-based escalation and frequently provides durable relief, especially when combined with continued ergonomic modification.

pearls: De Quervain tenosynovitis is stenosing tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis in the first dorsal compartment, classically in new mothers and caregivers.
A positive Finkelstein test with point tenderness over the radial styloid is diagnostic; imaging is rarely needed.
First-line treatment is a thumb spica splint, activity modification, and NSAIDs; corticosteroid injection is highly effective second-line therapy.
Distinguish from carpal tunnel syndrome (median paresthesias), scaphoid fracture (post-traumatic snuffbox tenderness), and thumb CMC osteoarthritis (positive grind test).

cases.title: Shoulder Pain When Reaching Overhead
cases.chiefComplaint: My right shoulder hurts whenever I reach up or paint overhead.
cases.history: A 55-year-old right-handed man presents with three months of gradually worsening right shoulder pain. He works as a house painter and the pain is provoked by overhead activity, reaching into cabinets, and lying on the affected side at night, which disrupts his sleep. He localizes the pain to the lateral deltoid region and denies neck pain, numbness, or tingling down the arm. There was no acute injury; the symptoms came on insidiously and have plateaued. He has not had fevers, weight loss, or other joint complaints. PMH includes well-controlled hypertension and dyslipidemia. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. He takes ibuprofen 400 mg occasionally with modest benefit. No known drug allergies. Social history: 20 years of overhead painting work; former smoker, quit five years ago (15 pack-years); drinks two beers on weekends. Family history is non-contributory. He is concerned about whether he can keep working and whether he will need surgery.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. Inspection of the right shoulder shows no atrophy, deformity, or swelling. There is tenderness over the greater tuberosity and subacromial region. Active abduction reproduces pain through a painful arc between roughly 60 and 120 degrees. Passive range of motion is full and less painful than active motion. Empty-can (Jobe) test is positive with pain and mild weakness in resisted abduction. Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer impingement signs are positive. External rotation strength is preserved; lift-off and belly-press tests are negative. Drop-arm test is negative. Cervical spine range of motion is full and painless; Spurling test is negative. Distal neurovascular exam is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly localizes the problem to the rotator cuff rather than glenohumeral joint pathology?
options: Full passive range of motion with a painful active arc
Tenderness of the acromioclavicular joint as the first step today
Pain radiating below the elbow with numbness as the first step today
Pain reproduced by passive motion equal to active motion
stages.explanation: Preserved (full) passive range of motion combined with a painful active arc and a positive empty-can (Jobe) test points to a rotator cuff problem, specifically supraspinatus tendinopathy or impingement, rather than intra-articular disease. In adhesive capsulitis or advanced glenohumeral arthritis, passive motion is also restricted, not full. AC joint tenderness suggests AC pathology, not cuff disease, and is absent here. Pain radiating below the elbow with numbness suggests cervical radiculopathy, excluded by a normal cervical exam and negative Spurling test. The dissociation between limited/painful active motion and preserved passive motion is the hallmark of cuff tendinopathy with impingement.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Full-thickness rotator cuff tear in this clinical context
Cervical radiculopathy as the primary diagnosis
Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)
Rotator cuff tendinopathy with subacromial impingement
stages.explanation: Insidious lateral shoulder pain with a painful arc, positive Neer and Hawkins impingement signs, and a positive empty-can test in a worker with chronic overhead activity is classic rotator cuff tendinopathy with subacromial impingement. Adhesive capsulitis would show globally restricted passive motion, which is full here. A full-thickness tear typically produces marked weakness and a positive drop-arm test, both absent. Cervical radiculopathy causes neck pain and dermatomal arm symptoms with a positive Spurling test, all negative here. The preserved passive motion, painful arc, and provocative-test pattern make tendinopathy with impingement the best fit.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Shoulder immobilization in a sling for six weeks as monotherapy
Urgent referral for arthroscopic rotator cuff repair
Activity modification, NSAIDs,
MRI of the shoulder before any treatment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management of rotator cuff tendinopathy with impingement is conservative: activity/ergonomic modification, NSAIDs, and a structured physical therapy program emphasizing rotator cuff and scapular stabilizer strengthening. Most patients improve without surgery. Urgent surgical repair is not indicated absent an acute full-thickness tear with significant weakness. MRI is not required before initiating treatment when the exam is consistent with tendinopathy; it is reserved for failure of conservative care or suspicion of a significant tear. Prolonged sling immobilization risks stiffness and adhesive capsulitis and is contraindicated. A subacromial corticosteroid injection may be added if NSAIDs and therapy are insufficient.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After three months of physical therapy and NSAIDs he has only modest improvement and still cannot work overhead comfortably. What is the most reasonable next step?
options: Stop all activity and rest the shoulder completely for three months
Subacromial corticosteroid injection
Begin chronic opioid therapy as the immediate next step
Proceed directly to total shoulder arthroplasty
stages.explanation: When NSAIDs and an adequate physical therapy trial give insufficient relief, a subacromial corticosteroid injection combined with continued rehabilitation is the appropriate next step and can reduce inflammation enough to allow effective therapy. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for a benign tendinopathy. Total shoulder arthroplasty treats glenohumeral arthritis, not cuff tendinopathy, and is not indicated. Complete rest with prolonged immobility risks stiffness and worsening function. If he fails injection plus continued conservative care, MRI and surgical (subacromial decompression or cuff evaluation) referral would be the subsequent steps. The injection-plus-therapy combination is the evidence-based escalation.

cases.diagnosis: Rotator cuff tendinopathy with subacromial impingement
pearls: Rotator cuff tendinopathy presents with a painful arc (60 to 120 degrees), positive empty-can (Jobe), Neer, and Hawkins tests, and night pain when lying on the shoulder.
Full passive range of motion distinguishes cuff tendinopathy from adhesive capsulitis, where passive motion is also restricted.
First-line treatment is activity modification, NSAIDs, and structured physical therapy; subacromial steroid injection is a useful adjunct.
A positive drop-arm test or marked weakness suggests a full-thickness tear and warrants MRI and surgical evaluation.

cases.title: A Stiff Shoulder That Won't Move
cases.chiefComplaint: My left shoulder has gotten so stiff I can't reach behind my back or fasten my bra.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman presents with five months of progressive left shoulder pain and stiffness. It began with aching deep in the shoulder, and over the following months the joint became increasingly stiff so that she now struggles to reach overhead, behind her back, or across her body. She is right-hand dominant. There was no injury. Pain was severe early on, especially at night, but has become somewhat less sharp as stiffness predominates. She denies neck pain, arm numbness, fever, or weight loss. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus (10 years) and hypothyroidism. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, and acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. Most recent HbA1c was 8.1 percent. Social history: office administrator, never smoker, occasional wine. Family history: type 2 diabetes in both parents. She is frustrated that the stiffness limits dressing and grooming and worries it will become permanent.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman guarding the left arm. No erythema, warmth, swelling, or visible atrophy of the left shoulder. Marked global restriction of BOTH active AND passive range of motion: external rotation limited to about 10 degrees, abduction to about 70 degrees, and limited internal rotation (hand reaches only to the sacrum). Attempts to move past these limits are painful with a firm end-feel. Rotator cuff strength is difficult to assess due to pain but appears grossly preserved within the available arc. Empty-can and drop-arm tests cannot be fully performed because of restricted motion. Cervical spine motion is full and painless; Spurling test negative. Distal neurovascular exam intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding is most characteristic of this condition and distinguishes it from rotator cuff tendinopathy?
options: Painful arc with full passive motion as the first step today
Tenderness localized to the AC joint as the first step today
Positive drop-arm test with weakness as the first step today
Loss of BOTH active and passive range of motion, especially external rotation
stages.explanation: The hallmark of adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is loss of both active and passive range of motion, with external rotation typically the most restricted. This contrasts with rotator cuff tendinopathy, in which passive motion is preserved and only active motion is painful (a painful arc). A positive drop-arm test with weakness indicates a rotator cuff tear, not capsular contracture. AC joint tenderness reflects AC pathology. The global, equal restriction of active and passive motion with a firm end-feel is what defines a frozen shoulder and separates it from cuff disease, where the joint can still be moved passively through a full arc.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Glenohumeral osteoarthritis
Septic arthritis of the shoulder
Rotator cuff tear
Adhesive capsulitis
stages.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis is most likely: a middle-aged woman with diabetes (a major risk factor) and insidious shoulder pain progressing to global loss of active and passive motion, external rotation most affected. Glenohumeral osteoarthritis can also limit motion but is usually associated with radiographic joint-space narrowing and crepitus and is less common in this age group without prior injury. A rotator cuff tear produces weakness and preserved passive motion, not a global passive deficit. Septic arthritis presents acutely with fever, warmth, erythema, and severe pain, none of which are present. The diabetic history and the active-equals-passive restriction pattern make frozen shoulder the clear diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
HbA1c 8.1 %
TSH 2.4 µIU/mL
ESR 14 mm/hr
CRP 0.4 mg/dL
Plain radiograph left shoulder: glenohumeral joint space preserved, no fracture, no significant osteoarthritis, no calcific deposits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given normal inflammatory markers and unremarkable radiographs, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Prolonged shoulder immobilization in a sling as initial therapy
Pain control plus physical therapy focused on range-of-motion
Empiric IV antibiotics for septic arthritis as initial therapy
Urgent arthroscopic surgery as initial therapy
