stages.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis is usually self-limited but protracted, and the cornerstone of treatment is analgesia (NSAIDs/acetaminophen) combined with physical therapy emphasizing gentle range-of-motion and stretching to restore motion; an intra-articular corticosteroid injection can help reduce pain and speed recovery, particularly in the painful freezing phase. Prolonged immobilization worsens stiffness and is contraindicated. Urgent surgery (manipulation under anesthesia or capsular release) is reserved for cases refractory to months of conservative therapy. Antibiotics are inappropriate because the normal ESR/CRP, absence of fever, and chronic course exclude septic arthritis. Optimizing glycemic control also supports recovery in this diabetic patient.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional intervention is most likely to improve her long-term outcome and reduce recurrence risk?
options: Tightening glycemic control of her diabetes
Adding chronic opioid therapy in this setting
Complete activity avoidance until pain-free
Routine MRI of the shoulder every three months
stages.explanation: Diabetes is the strongest risk factor for adhesive capsulitis; frozen shoulder is more frequent, more severe, and more often bilateral in diabetics, and poor glycemic control is associated with worse outcomes. Improving her HbA1c (currently 8.1 percent) supports recovery and reduces recurrence risk, including in the contralateral shoulder. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for this benign, self-limited condition. Complete activity avoidance promotes further capsular contracture and stiffness, the opposite of the needed mobilization. Serial MRI adds cost without changing management, as the diagnosis is clinical and imaging does not guide routine therapy. Addressing the underlying metabolic driver is the highest-yield additional step.

pearls: Adhesive capsulitis causes loss of BOTH active and passive shoulder motion, with external rotation most restricted, distinguishing it from rotator cuff tendinopathy (preserved passive motion).
Diabetes mellitus and thyroid disease are major risk factors; frozen shoulder in diabetics is more severe and often bilateral.
The course is typically self-limited over months to years through freezing, frozen, and thawing phases.
Treatment is analgesia plus physical therapy for range of motion; intra-articular steroid helps, and refractory cases may need manipulation or capsular release.

cases.title: Aching on the Outside of the Hip
cases.chiefComplaint: I have pain on the outside of my right hip, and I can't sleep on that side.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents with two months of pain over the outer aspect of her right hip. The pain is sharp when she presses on the area and aches the rest of the time. It is markedly worse when she lies on the right side at night, and she has begun sleeping with a pillow between her knees. Walking, climbing stairs, and rising from a low chair aggravate it; the pain sometimes radiates partway down the lateral thigh but never below the knee. She denies groin pain, numbness, tingling, low back pain, or knee pain. There was no fall or injury. PMH includes obesity (BMI 33), osteoarthritis of both knees, and hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. Social history: retired teacher, recently started long daily walks for weight loss; nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history: osteoarthritis in her mother. She is worried she has a hip joint problem that will require replacement.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman, ambulates with a mildly antalgic gait. Focal point tenderness directly over the greater trochanter of the right hip; no warmth, erythema, or swelling. Pain is reproduced by resisted hip abduction and by single-leg stance on the right. Passive internal and external rotation of the hip are full and painless, and there is no groin pain with these maneuvers. FABER (Patrick) test elicits lateral hip pain rather than groin pain. Straight-leg raise is negative bilaterally. Lumbar spine has full, painless range of motion with no midline tenderness. Lower-extremity strength, sensation, and reflexes are intact; pulses normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding best localizes the pain to the lateral soft tissues rather than the hip joint itself?
options: Point tenderness over the greater trochanter with pain on resisted abduction
Midline lumbar spine tenderness as the first step today
Groin pain with passive internal rotation as the first step today
Positive straight-leg raise as the first step today
stages.explanation: Focal point tenderness over the greater trochanter, pain on resisted hip abduction (loading the gluteal tendons), and full, painless passive hip rotation localize the problem to the lateral peritrochanteric soft tissues (greater trochanteric pain syndrome / gluteal tendinopathy and bursitis) rather than intra-articular hip disease. Groin pain with internal rotation indicates true hip joint pathology such as osteoarthritis. A positive straight-leg raise suggests lumbar radiculopathy. Midline lumbar tenderness points to the spine. Preserved, painless hip rotation essentially excludes a significant intra-articular process, and the reproduction of pain over the trochanter with abductor loading is the key localizing feature.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Femoral neck stress fracture
Hip osteoarthritis as the primary diagnosis
Trochanteric bursitis
L2-L3 lumbar radiculopathy in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Lateral hip pain worse when lying on the affected side, point tenderness over the greater trochanter, and pain on resisted abduction with preserved hip rotation are classic for greater trochanteric pain syndrome (trochanteric/gluteal bursitis and tendinopathy). Hip osteoarthritis causes groin pain and painful, restricted internal rotation, which are absent. L2-L3 radiculopathy would cause anterior thigh pain with neurologic signs, not focal trochanteric tenderness, and the SLR and neuro exam are normal. A femoral neck stress fracture causes groin/anterior pain worse with weight-bearing and would be unusual to present with isolated lateral point tenderness. The exam pattern makes trochanteric bursitis the clear answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: MRI of the lumbar spine as initial therapy
Immediate corticosteroid injection before any other treatment
Total hip arthroplasty referral as initial therapy
Activity/load modification, NSAIDs,
stages.explanation: Initial treatment of greater trochanteric pain syndrome is conservative: relative rest and load modification (e.g., adjusting her new walking regimen, avoiding side-lying on the affected hip, using a pillow between the knees), NSAIDs, and physical therapy focused on hip abductor and gluteal strengthening and stretching of the iliotibial band. Total hip arthroplasty treats end-stage hip arthritis, which she does not have. Lumbar MRI is not indicated without neurologic signs or radicular features. A corticosteroid injection is effective and can be offered, but it is generally reserved for patients who do not respond to initial conservative measures rather than being first-line before any other treatment. Conservative care first is standard.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After eight weeks of NSAIDs and physical therapy she has only partial relief and still has disabling night pain. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for surgical bursectomy
Order a bone scan to rule out malignancy
Peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection
Start chronic opioid therapy
stages.explanation: When conservative therapy provides inadequate relief, a peritrochanteric (greater trochanteric bursa) corticosteroid injection is the appropriate next step and provides good short- to medium-term relief in many patients with greater trochanteric pain syndrome. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for this benign musculoskeletal condition. Surgical bursectomy or gluteal tendon repair is reserved for refractory cases after injections and prolonged conservative care fail. A bone scan to rule out malignancy is unwarranted given a clear mechanical presentation with no systemic red flags (no weight loss, fever, or rest/night pain unrelated to position). The injection, alongside continued strengthening, is the evidence-based escalation.

pearls: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome causes lateral hip pain worse with side-lying, with point tenderness over the greater trochanter and pain on resisted abduction.
Preserved, painless passive hip rotation distinguishes it from hip osteoarthritis, which causes groin pain and restricted internal rotation.
First-line management is load modification, NSAIDs, and hip abductor strengthening physical therapy.
A peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection is effective second-line therapy when conservative measures fail.

cases.title: Stabbing Heel Pain With the First Steps of the Day
cases.chiefComplaint: A sharp pain in my heel when I first get out of bed in the morning.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents with six weeks of right heel pain. The pain is sharp and most severe with the first few steps when he gets out of bed in the morning or after sitting for a while, then eases as he walks around, though it returns toward the end of a long day on his feet. He localizes it to the bottom of the heel near the inner side. He recently started training for a charity 10K and increased his mileage quickly. He denies trauma, numbness, tingling, ankle swelling, or pain at rest at night. He has had no fevers, rashes, eye symptoms, or other joint pains. PMH includes obesity (BMI 31) and prediabetes. Medications: none regularly; takes ibuprofen occasionally. No known drug allergies. Social history: warehouse supervisor who stands much of the day, wears worn-out sneakers; nonsmoker, social drinker. Family history: father with type 2 diabetes. He wants to keep running and asks whether he has a heel spur or a stress fracture.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man, normal gait. Focal tenderness at the plantar medial aspect of the right calcaneus at the origin of the plantar fascia; pain is reproduced and worsened by passive dorsiflexion of the toes (windlass maneuver) with the ankle dorsiflexed. No tenderness with side-to-side (medial-lateral) squeeze compression of the calcaneus. No swelling, warmth, or erythema of the heel or ankle. Achilles tendon non-tender with normal Thompson test. Tinel sign over the tarsal tunnel is negative; sensation in the foot is intact. Ankle range of motion is full; gastrocnemius/soleus flexibility is reduced with limited ankle dorsiflexion. Pulses intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings best supports the leading diagnosis?
options: Pain on calcaneal squeeze (medial-lateral compression) and night pain in this clinical context
A positive Thompson test with calf swelling as the first step today
Plantar medial heel tenderness reproduced by the windlass maneuver, worst with first steps
Positive Tinel sign at the tarsal tunnel with foot numbness as the first step today
stages.explanation: Plantar fasciitis classically produces tenderness at the plantar medial calcaneal origin of the fascia, pain that is worst with the first steps in the morning or after rest, and reproduction of pain with the windlass maneuver (passive dorsiflexion of the toes tensioning the plantar fascia). A positive calcaneal squeeze test suggests a calcaneal stress fracture, not fasciitis. A positive Tinel sign with numbness indicates tarsal tunnel syndrome (posterior tibial nerve entrapment). A positive Thompson test indicates an Achilles tendon rupture. The first-step pain pattern plus a positive windlass test and focal plantar medial tenderness is the diagnostic combination for plantar fasciitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Tarsal tunnel syndrome
Calcaneal stress fracture
Achilles tendinopathy
Plantar fasciitis
stages.explanation: The presentation is classic plantar fasciitis: sharp inferomedial heel pain that is worst with the first steps in the morning and after rest, eases with activity, and recurs after prolonged standing, in a patient with risk factors of obesity, abruptly increased running, prolonged standing, worn footwear, and tight calves. A calcaneal stress fracture causes diffuse heel pain with a positive squeeze test, which is negative here. Tarsal tunnel syndrome produces burning, numbness, and a positive Tinel sign, absent here. Achilles tendinopathy causes posterior heel/tendon pain with Achilles tenderness, also absent. The localized plantar medial tenderness and positive windlass test confirm plantar fasciitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Surgical plantar fascia release as initial therapy
Plantar fascia and calf stretching, supportive footwear/orthotics, activity modification, and NSAIDs
Non-weight-bearing in a cast for six weeks as initial therapy
Corticosteroid injection into the plantar fascia as the initial treatment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line management of plantar fasciitis is conservative: plantar fascia-specific and calf (gastrocnemius/soleus) stretching, supportive footwear or over-the-counter heel cushions/arch supports, relative activity modification (reducing the rapidly increased mileage), and NSAIDs for pain. The vast majority improve with these measures. Surgical release is reserved for severe, refractory cases after at least 6 to 12 months of failed conservative care. Corticosteroid injection can help but is typically second-line because of small risks of fat-pad atrophy and fascial rupture, and is not the initial step. Non-weight-bearing casting is excessive for a benign tendinopathy and risks deconditioning. Conservative therapy is the appropriate start.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He asks how long recovery typically takes and what to expect. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: Imaging is mandatory before any treatment can begin as the immediate next step
He must stop all weight-bearing activity permanently as the immediate next step
Most cases resolve with consistent conservative therapy over weeks to months, though recovery can be slow
It almost always requires surgery within a few weeks as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The most accurate counseling is that plantar fasciitis is usually self-limited and the large majority of patients recover with consistent conservative therapy, though improvement can take weeks to many months and requires patience and adherence to stretching and footwear changes. Surgery is rarely needed and only after prolonged failed conservative care. Imaging is not mandatory; the diagnosis is clinical, and radiographs/ultrasound are reserved for atypical features or to exclude alternatives like a stress fracture. Permanent cessation of weight-bearing activity is neither necessary nor advisable; gradual return to activity with proper footwear and load management is the goal. Setting realistic expectations improves adherence and outcomes.

pearls: Plantar fasciitis causes sharp inferomedial heel pain worst with the first steps in the morning or after rest, easing with activity.
Risk factors include obesity, sudden increases in running/standing, tight calf muscles, and inadequate footwear.
Diagnosis is clinical: focal plantar medial calcaneal tenderness and a positive windlass test; a positive calcaneal squeeze suggests stress fracture instead.
First-line treatment is plantar fascia and calf stretching, supportive footwear/orthotics, activity modification, and NSAIDs; most cases resolve over weeks to months.

cases.title: Sudden Lower Back Pain After Moving Boxes
cases.chiefComplaint: My lower back seized up after I lifted boxes during a move two days ago.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents with two days of low back pain that began acutely while lifting and twisting with heavy boxes during a home move. The pain is across the lower lumbar region, described as a dull ache with intermittent muscle spasms, worse with bending and twisting and somewhat relieved by lying down. It does not radiate below the buttock, and he denies leg numbness, tingling, or weakness. He has no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no saddle numbness, no fever, no recent weight loss, no history of cancer, no IV drug use, and no recent infection. He has had occasional minor episodes of back strain in the past that resolved on their own. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Social history: works in IT, mostly sedentary, exercises sporadically; nonsmoker, drinks socially. Family history: non-contributory. He is anxious about whether he needs an MRI and whether he should stay on bed rest.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man who moves stiffly and guards the lumbar region. Paraspinal muscle tenderness and palpable spasm in the lower lumbar region; no midline bony (spinous process) tenderness. Lumbar range of motion is reduced and painful, particularly with flexion. Straight-leg raise is negative bilaterally. Lower-extremity strength is 5/5 throughout, sensation is intact in all dermatomes, and deep tendon reflexes (patellar and Achilles) are symmetric and normal. Gait is normal aside from guarding. Perianal sensation and rectal tone are normal. No costovertebral angle tenderness. No skin lesions over the spine.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature is a 'red flag' that, if present, would warrant urgent imaging or further workup?
options: Pain that improves when lying down as the first step today
Saddle anesthesia, new bowel or bladder dysfunction, or progressive neurologic deficit
Paraspinal muscle spasm as the first step today
Pain provoked by lumbar flexion as the first step today
stages.explanation: Red flags that warrant urgent imaging/workup in low back pain include saddle anesthesia, new bowel or bladder dysfunction, progressive or severe neurologic deficits (suggesting cauda equina), as well as fever/IV drug use (infection), history of cancer or unexplained weight loss (malignancy), and significant trauma or osteoporosis (fracture). Pain that improves with lying down, paraspinal spasm, and pain with flexion are typical of benign mechanical/muscular back pain and are not red flags. Recognizing the absence of red flags is what permits a conservative, imaging-free approach. The presence of any red flag would change management dramatically toward emergent evaluation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Vertebral osteomyelitis
Cauda equina syndrome in this clinical context
Lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
Mechanical low back pain
stages.explanation: Acute mechanical low back pain (lumbar strain) is most likely: pain began with a lifting/twisting injury, is paraspinal and non-radiating, is associated with muscle spasm, and has a completely normal neurologic exam with negative straight-leg raise and no red flags. Disc herniation with radiculopathy would produce dermatomal leg pain, sensory changes, and a positive straight-leg raise, all absent. Vertebral osteomyelitis presents with fever, focal bony tenderness, and risk factors such as IV drug use or bacteremia, none present. Cauda equina syndrome involves saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, and bilateral deficits, which are absent. The mechanical onset with a benign exam confirms lumbar strain.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management at this visit?
options: Reassurance, staying active as tolerated,
Refer for spinal surgery evaluation as initial therapy
Order an urgent lumbar MRI as initial therapy
Prescribe strict bed rest for one week as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For acute mechanical low back pain without red flags, evidence-based management is reassurance about the favorable natural history, advice to stay active and avoid prolonged bed rest, and first-line analgesia with NSAIDs, with the option of a short course of a muscle relaxant for spasm. Imaging is not indicated in the first 4 to 6 weeks absent red flags because it does not improve outcomes and leads to incidental findings and unnecessary intervention. Strict bed rest actually slows recovery and is discouraged. Surgical referral is unwarranted with a normal neurologic exam and no structural emergency. Conservative care plus reassurance is the standard of care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point about prognosis and follow-up is most appropriate?
options: He should remain on opioids long-term to prevent recurrence with appropriate follow-up
An MRI is required before he can return to work with appropriate follow-up
Most acute low back pain improves substantially within a few weeks
He will likely need surgery and should avoid all activity until then
stages.explanation: The correct counseling is that the prognosis for acute mechanical low back pain is excellent, with most patients improving substantially within a few weeks, and that he should return promptly if red-flag symptoms develop (new leg weakness, saddle numbness, bowel/bladder changes, fever). Telling him he likely needs surgery is incorrect and creates unnecessary anxiety. MRI is not required to return to work and is not indicated absent red flags. Long-term opioids are not recommended for low back pain due to limited efficacy and risks of dependence; if any analgesia is needed, NSAIDs are preferred. Reassurance plus a clear safety-net plan is optimal.

cases.diagnosis: Mechanical low back pain (acute lumbar strain)
pearls: Acute mechanical low back pain follows a strain/lifting injury, is paraspinal and non-radiating, and has a normal neurologic exam with no red flags.
Red flags (saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction, progressive deficit, fever, IV drug use, cancer history, weight loss, major trauma) mandate urgent evaluation.
Imaging is NOT indicated in the first 4 to 6 weeks without red flags; it leads to incidental findings and overtreatment.
Management is reassurance, staying active (not bed rest), and NSAIDs; most patients recover within weeks.

cases.title: Back Pain Shooting Down the Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp pain shooting from my lower back down the back of my left leg to my foot.
cases.history: A 43-year-old man presents with three weeks of low back pain that radiates down the posterior left thigh and calf to the lateral foot. The pain is sharp and electric, worse with sitting, bending forward, coughing, and sneezing, and partially relieved by standing and walking. It started after a weekend of heavy yard work. He reports numbness along the outer foot and a sense that the leg occasionally feels weak when pushing off. He denies bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle numbness, fever, weight loss, or history of cancer. PMH includes obesity and a prior episode of self-limited back pain. Medications: ibuprofen 600 mg as needed with partial relief; no other regular medications. No known drug allergies. Social history: construction estimator who sits and drives frequently; former smoker (10 pack-years), social alcohol. Family history: non-contributory. He is frustrated that the leg pain is worse than the back pain and is interfering with sitting at work and sleeping.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man, antalgic gait favoring the left. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness; range of motion limited by pain, worse with flexion. Straight-leg raise on the left reproduces radiating pain down the leg at about 40 degrees; crossed straight-leg raise is also positive. Sensation is decreased over the lateral left foot and small toe. Strength: plantarflexion (toe/foot push-off) is mildly weak (4/5) on the left; great toe extension is 5/5; dorsiflexion 5/5. Ankle (Achilles) reflex is diminished on the left compared with the right; patellar reflexes symmetric. Perianal sensation intact, normal rectal tone, no saddle anesthesia. No spine tenderness over a single bony level; gait otherwise intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the sensory, motor, and reflex findings, which nerve root is most likely affected?
options: L4 nerve root in this clinical context
L3 nerve root in this clinical context
L5 nerve root in this clinical context
S1 nerve root
stages.explanation: The findings localize to the S1 nerve root: pain radiating down the posterior leg to the lateral foot/small toe, decreased sensation over the lateral foot, weakness of plantarflexion (push-off), and a diminished Achilles (ankle) reflex are the classic S1 pattern, typically from an L5-S1 disc herniation. L5 radiculopathy would cause weak great-toe and foot dorsiflexion and numbness over the dorsum of the foot/first web space with preserved reflexes. L4 involves the medial leg, knee extension, and the patellar reflex. L3 affects the anterior thigh and knee extension. The combination of lateral-foot sensory loss, weak plantarflexion, and a reduced ankle reflex is specific for S1.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Piriformis syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Mechanical low back pain without radiculopathy
Lumbar disc herniation with S1 radiculopathy
Lumbar spinal stenosis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation with S1 radiculopathy best fits: acute onset after exertion, dermatomal leg pain worse with sitting and Valsalva (coughing/sneezing), positive straight-leg and crossed straight-leg raise, and S1 sensory/motor/reflex findings. Mechanical low back pain lacks radicular and neurologic findings, which are clearly present. Lumbar spinal stenosis typically affects older patients with neurogenic claudication (pain worse with standing/walking, relieved by sitting/flexion), the opposite of this sitting-aggravated pattern. Piriformis syndrome can cause sciatica-like pain but does not produce a reflex change or a positive crossed straight-leg raise and is a diagnosis of exclusion. The Valsalva-aggravated, dermatomal presentation with positive nerve-tension signs indicates a herniated disc.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no red flags and only mild plantarflexion weakness. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Conservative therapy: activity modification, NSAIDs,
Strict bed rest for two weeks as initial therapy
Start chronic opioid therapy as initial therapy
Immediate MRI and urgent surgical referral as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Most lumbar disc herniations with radiculopathy improve with conservative care, so initial management is activity modification (staying active as tolerated), NSAIDs, physical therapy, and reassurance that the majority resolve over weeks, since the herniation often regresses. Immediate MRI and urgent surgery are reserved for red flags (cauda equina, progressive or severe motor deficit) or failure of 6 weeks of conservative care; mild, stable weakness does not meet that threshold. Strict bed rest delays recovery and is discouraged. Chronic opioids are not recommended due to poor efficacy and dependence risk. A short course of analgesics with physical therapy and watchful waiting is the evidence-based first step.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP AT 7 WEEKS
stages.content:
Persistent left leg pain despite 6 weeks of NSAIDs and physical therapy
New progression of plantarflexion weakness: now 3/5 on the left
MRI lumbar spine: large left paracentral L5-S1 disc extrusion compressing the traversing S1 nerve root
No evidence of cauda equina compression; conus and thecal sac otherwise unremarkable

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given failure of conservative therapy plus progressive motor weakness with a corresponding MRI lesion, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Begin long-term opioids and reassurance
Refer for surgical evaluation
Continue the same physical therapy for three more months
Repeat the MRI in six months in this setting
stages.explanation: Surgical referral (typically microdiscectomy) is indicated because he has failed an adequate trial of conservative therapy AND has progressive motor weakness with an MRI lesion that correlates precisely with his S1 radiculopathy. These are accepted indications for surgery, which offers faster relief of radicular pain and addresses the progressing deficit. Continuing unchanged therapy ignores worsening neurologic function. Repeating the MRI in six months delays care while the deficit progresses. Long-term opioids do not treat the structural cause, carry dependence risk, and would leave the progressive weakness unaddressed. Progressive neurologic deficit plus failed conservative care and concordant imaging is precisely when discectomy is recommended.

cases.diagnosis: Lumbar disc herniation with S1 radiculopathy
pearls: S1 radiculopathy: posterior leg pain to the lateral foot, weak plantarflexion, diminished Achilles reflex; L5-S1 is the most common disc level.
Disc-herniation radicular pain worsens with sitting and Valsalva (coughing/sneezing); a positive crossed straight-leg raise is specific for herniation.
Most herniations improve with conservative care (activity, NSAIDs, PT); imaging and surgery are reserved for red flags, progressive deficit, or failure of 6 weeks of therapy.
Surgery (microdiscectomy) is indicated for progressive/severe motor deficit, cauda equina, or refractory radiculopathy with concordant imaging.

cases.title: Leg Pain When Walking That Eases on a Cart
cases.chiefComplaint: My legs ache and feel heavy when I walk, but I feel fine leaning on the grocery cart.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents with one year of progressively worsening pain, heaviness, and tingling in both legs and buttocks that come on after walking a few blocks. The symptoms are relieved within minutes by sitting, bending forward, or leaning on a shopping cart, and he notes he can walk much farther when pushing a cart or walking uphill than on level ground or downhill. He has no rest pain in the legs, no pain in the calves at rest, and his feet stay warm with good color. He denies acute back injury, bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle numbness, fever, or weight loss. PMH includes hypertension, dyslipidemia, and osteoarthritis; he is a former smoker. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. Social history: retired; former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 8 years ago); minimal alcohol. Family history: coronary artery disease. He worries the leg symptoms mean poor circulation.
cases.exam: Well-appearing older man. Posture slightly stooped forward. Lumbar extension reproduces leg/buttock symptoms; flexion relieves them. Straight-leg raise is negative bilaterally. Lower-extremity strength is 5/5, sensation grossly intact, reflexes symmetric and slightly diminished consistent with age. No focal motor deficit at rest. Importantly, distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) are 2+ and symmetric, feet are warm with normal color and capillary refill, and there is no hair loss or skin atrophy of the lower legs. Perianal sensation intact with normal rectal tone. Gait is wide-based and slightly stooped; he reports symptom relief when leaning forward.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes neurogenic claudication from vascular (arterial) claudication?
options: Symptoms relieved by standing still as the first step today
Diminished distal pulses with skin atrophy as the first step today
Pain that worsens with leg elevation as the first step today
Symptoms relieved by forward flexion/sitting
stages.explanation: Neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis is relieved by forward flexion or sitting (which widens the spinal canal) and provoked by standing and lumbar extension; patients walk farther leaning on a cart or going uphill (flexed posture) than downhill. Critically, distal pulses are normal because the problem is neural, not arterial. Vascular claudication, by contrast, is relieved simply by standing still (stopping the muscle work), is reproduced by a fixed walking distance regardless of posture, and is accompanied by diminished pulses, skin atrophy, and hair loss. Pain worsened by leg elevation also suggests arterial insufficiency. Posture-dependent relief plus normal, symmetric pulses points firmly to a neurogenic cause.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy in this clinical context
Peripheral arterial disease in this clinical context
Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication
Lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
stages.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication is most likely: an older patient with bilateral buttock/leg pain provoked by walking and standing, relieved by sitting and forward flexion (the classic shopping-cart sign), with normal pulses and a flexed antalgic posture. Peripheral arterial disease is excluded by 2+ symmetric distal pulses, warm well-perfused feet, and posture-dependent rather than effort-only symptoms. Disc herniation with radiculopathy causes dermatomal pain worse with sitting and a positive straight-leg raise, the opposite of this posture pattern. Diabetic neuropathy causes constant distal stocking sensory loss unrelated to walking or posture and he is not described as diabetic. The posture-dependent, pulse-preserved pattern defines spinal stenosis.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Ankle-brachial index (ABI): 1.05 bilaterally (normal)
MRI lumbar spine: multilevel degenerative changes with moderate-to-severe central canal stenosis at L4-L5, ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and facet arthropathy
No disc extrusion or nerve root sequestration; conus normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With a normal ABI and MRI confirming central canal stenosis, and symptoms that are bothersome but not disabling, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation as initial therapy
Immediate decompressive laminectomy as initial therapy
Conservative therapy: physical therapy , activity modification,
Lower-extremity arterial bypass surgery as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For lumbar spinal stenosis with symptoms that are bothersome but not disabling and without progressive neurologic deficit, initial management is conservative: physical therapy emphasizing flexion-based exercises and core strengthening, activity modification, and analgesics such as NSAIDs; epidural steroid injections may offer temporary relief in selected patients. Immediate decompressive laminectomy is reserved for severe, refractory, or progressively disabling symptoms after conservative care fails, or for significant neurologic compromise. Anticoagulation has no role in a neural compressive condition. Arterial bypass treats peripheral arterial disease, which is excluded by the normal ABI. Conservative therapy first, reserving surgery for failure or progression, is the standard approach.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After several months of conservative care his walking tolerance declines to half a block and the symptoms significantly limit his daily life. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat the ABI as the immediate next step
Refer for surgical decompression
Add long-term opioids indefinitely
Recommend strict bed rest in this setting
stages.explanation: When lumbar spinal stenosis becomes disabling and refractory to an adequate trial of conservative therapy, referral for surgical decompression (laminectomy, with or without fusion depending on stability) is appropriate and improves walking tolerance and quality of life in well-selected patients. Strict bed rest worsens deconditioning and does not address the mechanical compression. Indefinite opioids carry substantial risk with limited benefit and do not treat the structural cause. Repeating the ABI is unhelpful because vascular disease was already excluded and the ABI does not change the spinal management. Progressive functional decline despite conservative care is the accepted indication to escalate to surgical decompression.

cases.diagnosis: Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication
pearls: Neurogenic claudication is relieved by forward flexion/sitting (the shopping-cart sign) and provoked by standing and lumbar extension; patients tolerate uphill walking better than downhill.
Normal, symmetric distal pulses and a normal ABI distinguish neurogenic from vascular claudication.
MRI shows central canal narrowing, often from ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and facet arthropathy, typically at L4-L5.
Initial treatment is flexion-based physical therapy and analgesics; decompressive surgery is reserved for disabling or refractory symptoms or progressive deficit.

cases.title: Leg Weakness With Numbness in the Saddle Region
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe back pain with both legs going weak and numbness between my legs, and I can't urinate.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with two days of severe low back pain that radiated into both legs, now accompanied by rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness over the past 12 hours. He reports new numbness in the perineal/saddle region and over the past 8 hours has been unable to urinate, with a sensation of bladder fullness. He also notes decreased sensation when wiping after a bowel movement and some fecal incontinence. He had a prior episode of sciatica and recently strained his back lifting. He denies fever, chills, IV drug use, recent infection, known cancer, or unexplained weight loss. PMH: obesity, hypertension, prior lumbar disc herniation managed conservatively. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, ibuprofen as needed. No known drug allergies. Social history: warehouse worker; former smoker; social alcohol. Family history: non-contributory. He is in significant distress and frightened by the new bladder and saddle symptoms.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man in pain. Lower-extremity strength is reduced bilaterally: hip flexion, knee flexion, and ankle dor/plantarflexion 3-4/5, worse on the left. Sensation is decreased over the perineum, perianal region, and posterior thighs in a saddle distribution. Ankle reflexes are diminished bilaterally. Anal sphincter tone is reduced on digital rectal exam and the bulbocavernosus reflex is absent. Palpable, distended, tender suprapubic bladder; post-void bladder scan shows a large residual volume of 650 mL. Straight-leg raise is positive bilaterally. No fever, no spinal midline point tenderness suggesting fracture, no skin infection over the spine. Gait not safely assessed due to weakness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings is most specific for this emergency?
options: Fever, focal spinal tenderness, and elevated inflammatory markers as the first step today
Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention with overflow, reduced anal sphincter tone,
Posture-dependent bilateral leg pain relieved by sitting as the first step today
Unilateral dermatomal leg pain with a single diminished reflex as the first step today
stages.explanation: The combination of saddle (perineal/perianal) anesthesia, urinary retention with overflow incontinence, reduced anal sphincter tone (and an absent bulbocavernosus reflex), and bilateral leg weakness is the classic and most specific presentation of cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency from compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots. Unilateral dermatomal pain with one diminished reflex describes ordinary single-level radiculopathy, not cauda equina. Fever with focal tenderness and elevated inflammatory markers suggests spinal infection (osteomyelitis/epidural abscess). Posture-dependent bilateral leg pain relieved by sitting describes neurogenic claudication from spinal stenosis. Urinary retention plus saddle anesthesia and sphincter dysfunction are the red-flag hallmarks demanding emergent action.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Conus medullaris syndrome from a thoracic cord lesion
Acute transverse myelitis
Bilateral L5 radiculopathy
Cauda equina syndrome
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is the diagnosis: compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus produces saddle anesthesia, bladder dysfunction (retention with overflow), bowel involvement with reduced sphincter tone, bilateral lower-extremity weakness, and diminished ankle reflexes, typically from a large central disc herniation. Conus medullaris syndrome occurs higher (around T12-L1), is often more symmetric and abrupt, with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs and early, severe bladder/bowel involvement; the lower-motor-neuron pattern with diminished reflexes here favors cauda equina. Bilateral L5 radiculopathy would not cause saddle anesthesia or sphincter dysfunction. Transverse myelitis produces a sensory level and upper-motor-neuron signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski), not flaccid areflexia with saddle anesthesia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start empiric IV antibiotics and observe as initial therapy
Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine
Discharge with NSAIDs and outpatient MRI in one week
Lumbar puncture for cell count and cultures as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency, so the immediate steps are emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine to identify and localize the compressive lesion and urgent neurosurgical consultation for decompression, ideally as soon as possible (commonly within 24 to 48 hours, and earlier is better for neurologic recovery). A bladder catheter should also be placed to relieve retention. Discharge with delayed imaging risks permanent paralysis and incontinence and is contraindicated. Lumbar puncture is not the diagnostic test and could be harmful with a compressive mass. Empiric antibiotics target infection, which is not suggested here (no fever, normal markers); they do not address acute mechanical nerve-root compression. Time-sensitive imaging and surgery are paramount.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
Bladder catheterization drains 700 mL of urine
MRI lumbosacral spine: massive central L4-L5 disc herniation severely compressing the cauda equina with effacement of the thecal sac
WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL
CRP 0.5 mg/dL
ESR 10 mm/hr
No epidural fluid collection or rim-enhancing abscess

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: MRI confirms a massive central L4-L5 disc compressing the cauda equina with no infection. What is the definitive management?
options: Emergent surgical decompression
Outpatient physical therapy and a corticosteroid taper
High-dose IV antibiotics for spinal infection
Therapeutic anticoagulation and observation
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment of cauda equina syndrome from a compressive disc is emergent surgical decompression (urgent laminectomy and discectomy) to relieve pressure on the nerve roots; earlier surgery is associated with better recovery of motor, bladder, and bowel function, so it should not be delayed. IV antibiotics are inappropriate because imaging and normal inflammatory markers exclude infection/abscess. Outpatient physical therapy and a steroid taper are far too conservative for an acute compressive emergency and would risk permanent deficits. Anticoagulation has no role and is not indicated for this mechanical compression. Prompt operative decompression is the standard of care and the only intervention that can preserve or restore neurologic function.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the strongest predictor of his long-term recovery of bladder and bowel function after decompression?
options: Whether he receives postoperative antibiotics as the leading concern
The time from onset of symptoms to surgical decompression
His age at presentation as the leading concern
The width of the laminectomy performed as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The strongest modifiable predictor of neurologic recovery in cauda equina syndrome, particularly recovery of bladder and bowel function, is the duration of compression, that is, the time from symptom onset (especially the development of urinary retention) to decompression; earlier surgery yields better outcomes, which is why CES is treated as an emergency. Patients who already have established complete retention/incontinence have a worse prognosis than those decompressed before that point. Age is a weaker, non-modifiable factor and not the principal determinant. The width of the laminectomy does not drive functional recovery as long as adequate decompression is achieved. Postoperative antibiotics prevent surgical-site infection but do not influence recovery of neurologic function. Counseling should emphasize that delay worsens the chance of regaining continence.

pearls: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency: saddle anesthesia, urinary retention with overflow incontinence, reduced anal sphincter tone, and bilateral leg weakness, usually from a large central disc herniation.
Obtain emergent MRI and urgent neurosurgical consultation; place a bladder catheter for retention.
Decompression should be performed urgently because earlier surgery improves recovery of motor, bladder, and bowel function.
Distinguish from conus medullaris syndrome (higher lesion, mixed UMN/LMN signs) and from spinal infection (fever, elevated ESR/CRP, rim-enhancing abscess).

cases.title: Clubbed Fingers and Aching Shins in a Long-Time Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: My fingertips look swollen and bulbous, and my shins and wrists ache deeply.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man presents with two months of deep, aching pain in both lower legs (shins) and wrists, along with noticeable changes in his fingertips, which have become bulbous. The bone pain is worse with dependency and pressure over the shins and is poorly relieved by acetaminophen. He has also had a chronic cough, occasional blood-streaked sputum, fatigue, and an unintentional 6 kg weight loss over three months. He denies morning joint stiffness, rashes, photosensitivity, or symmetric small-joint synovitis. PMH includes COPD. Medications: tiotropium inhaler daily, albuterol as needed, acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. Social history: 45 pack-year smoking history, still smokes; worked in construction; moderate alcohol. Family history: father died of lung cancer. He is alarmed by the finger changes and the weight loss and wonders if he has arthritis.
cases.exam: Thin, ill-appearing man. Marked digital clubbing of all fingers with loss of the normal nail-fold angle and a positive Schamroth sign. Tenderness to palpation over the distal tibiae, distal radii, and ulnae with mild overlying warmth and soft-tissue swelling near the wrists and ankles. Wrists and ankles have mild effusions with discomfort on motion but no erythema; small joints of the hands are NOT swollen and have no synovitis. Decreased breath sounds with scattered wheezes and dullness at the right base. No lymphadenopathy palpable in the neck. No rash. Distal pulses intact; neurologic exam intact. No nail pitting or psoriatic skin changes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of digital clubbing plus painful periostitis of the long bones should most prompt evaluation for what underlying process?
options: Primary osteoarthritis of the wrists and ankles
Seronegative spondyloarthritis in this clinical context
An intrathoracic malignancy, especially lung cancer
Gout as the first step today
stages.explanation: Digital clubbing combined with painful periostitis of the long bones defines hypertrophic osteoarthropathy, and when it develops in an adult (secondary HOA) it is most often a paraneoplastic clue to an intrathoracic malignancy, classically lung cancer. In a heavy smoker with cough, hemoptysis, and weight loss, this triad strongly mandates evaluation for lung cancer. Seronegative spondyloarthritis causes inflammatory back/enthesitis and is not characterized by clubbing with periostitis. Primary osteoarthritis does not cause clubbing, periostitis, warmth, or systemic weight loss. Gout causes acute monoarticular crystal arthritis, not clubbing with diffuse long-bone periostitis. The clubbing-plus-periostitis pattern should trigger a hunt for an underlying thoracic neoplasm.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis explaining his musculoskeletal findings?
options: Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy
Polymyalgia rheumatica in this clinical context
Rheumatoid arthritis as the primary diagnosis
Reactive arthritis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy (HOA) is the diagnosis: the triad of digital clubbing, periostitis of long bones (causing the deep tibial/radial pain and warmth), and arthralgia/effusions of the wrists and ankles, here arising secondarily in the setting of probable lung cancer. Rheumatoid arthritis causes symmetric small-joint synovitis with morning stiffness and does not cause clubbing or periostitis; his small hand joints are spared. Polymyalgia rheumatica causes proximal shoulder/hip girdle pain and stiffness in older adults, not clubbing or periostitis. Reactive arthritis follows GI/GU infection with oligoarthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis, none present here. The clubbing-plus-periostitis pattern with constitutional symptoms in a smoker is classic for paraneoplastic HOA.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which workup is most appropriate to confirm the bone involvement and identify the underlying cause?
options: Empiric high-dose glucocorticoids without imaging as the immediate next step
Joint aspiration for crystal analysis as the immediate next step
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies only as the immediate next step
Plain radiographs of the long bones plus chest imaging to evaluate for lung malignancy
stages.explanation: The appropriate workup is plain radiographs of the long bones (which show periosteal new bone formation characteristic of HOA) together with chest imaging, starting with a chest CT, to identify the underlying lung malignancy, given his smoking history, hemoptysis, and weight loss. Bone scintigraphy can also demonstrate diffuse periosteal uptake. Ordering only rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP would miss the diagnosis and the life-threatening cause; RA does not explain clubbing or periostitis. Joint aspiration for crystals targets gout/pseudogout, not this syndrome. Empiric high-dose steroids without imaging would delay cancer diagnosis and inappropriately treat a paraneoplastic process without addressing its source. Imaging the bones and the chest is the key diagnostic step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Long-bone radiographs: periosteal new bone formation (periostitis) along the distal tibiae, radii, and ulnae
Chest CT: 4.2 cm spiculated mass in the right lower lobe with ipsilateral hilar lymphadenopathy
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP antibody: negative
ESR 48 mm/hr
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the spiculated right-lower-lobe mass with hilar lymphadenopathy, what is the most appropriate next step to establish the cause?
options: Refer for immediate lobectomy without biopsy or staging in this setting
Begin chemotherapy empirically without a tissue sample before discharge planning
Obtain a tissue diagnosis via biopsy and stage the disease
Start a six-week course of antibiotics and repeat the CT in this setting
stages.explanation: A spiculated lung mass with hilar lymphadenopathy in a heavy smoker with hemoptysis and weight loss requires a tissue diagnosis via biopsy (CT-guided percutaneous, bronchoscopic, or via mediastinal node sampling such as EBUS) together with staging, because histology and stage determine whether treatment is surgical, chemoradiation, or systemic therapy. Empiric chemotherapy without histology is unsafe and never appropriate. A trial of antibiotics inappropriately treats this as pneumonia and dangerously delays cancer diagnosis given the clearly malignant-appearing, spiculated lesion with nodal spread. Proceeding directly to lobectomy without biopsy or staging risks operating on unresectable or metastatic disease and skips mandatory mediastinal staging. Tissue plus staging is the standard pre-treatment step in suspected lung cancer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms periostitis and a right-lower-lobe lung mass. What management approach best addresses both his cancer and his bone pain?
options: Pursue tissue diagnosis
Provide reassurance alone and observe without further workup as initial therapy
Treat with allopurinol for presumed gout as initial therapy
Begin disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) for rheumatoid arthritis
stages.explanation: The definitive approach is to pursue tissue diagnosis (biopsy) and treatment of the underlying lung cancer, because secondary HOA is paraneoplastic and its symptoms characteristically improve or resolve when the tumor is treated or resected. NSAIDs (and acetaminophen) provide symptomatic relief of the periostitis/bone pain while oncologic workup and therapy proceed. DMARDs are inappropriate because this is not rheumatoid arthritis (negative RF and anti-CCP, no small-joint synovitis). Allopurinol treats gout, which this is not. Reassurance and observation would dangerously ignore a likely treatable/operable malignancy. Linking treatment of the bone syndrome to treatment of the cancer is the central management principle of paraneoplastic HOA.

pearls: Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy is the triad of digital clubbing, long-bone periostitis (deep bone pain), and arthralgia/effusions, often of the wrists and ankles.
Secondary (paraneoplastic) HOA in an adult is most commonly associated with intrathoracic malignancy, especially lung cancer; new clubbing in a smoker warrants chest imaging.
Long-bone radiographs show periosteal new bone formation; negative RF and anti-CCP and sparing of the small hand joints help exclude rheumatoid arthritis.
Treating the underlying tumor often improves the HOA; NSAIDs help bone pain symptomatically in the interim.

cases.title: Recurrent Red, Painful Ears
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated episodes of both ears becoming red, swollen, and exquisitely painful over the past year.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with three episodes over the past year of sudden bilateral ear pain, redness, and swelling. Each flare lasts several days to two weeks and resolves with a course of ibuprofen, but the cartilage of the outer ears now feels softer and one ear droops slightly. He notes the redness has consistently spared the fleshy earlobes. Over the past two months he has developed nasal stuffiness, intermittent epistaxis, and a sense that the bridge of his nose is collapsing. He reports a hoarse voice, a nagging dry cough, and mild breathlessness on exertion. He also describes migratory joint pains in the knees and wrists without persistent swelling, and recent redness of the right eye. PMH is unremarkable. He takes only ibuprofen 600 mg as needed. No known drug allergies. He is a never-smoker, works as an accountant, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, hematuria, oral ulcers, and Raynaud phenomenon.
cases.exam: Both auricles are erythematous, edematous, and tender over the cartilaginous helix and antihelix, with conspicuous sparing of the non-cartilaginous lobules; the cartilage feels boggy and one pinna is mildly floppy. The nasal bridge shows a saddle-nose depression with mild tenderness. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals crusting. The voice is hoarse and there is faint inspiratory stridor; mild diffuse tracheal tenderness is noted. Eyes show mild scleral injection of the right eye. Chest auscultation is clear. Joints are without active synovitis today. There is no rash, no lymphadenopathy, and no organomegaly. Neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single physical finding most specifically points toward the underlying diagnosis?
options: Hoarseness with a dry cough as the first step today
Unilateral scleral injection as the first step today
Bilateral auricular inflammation that spares the earlobes
Migratory arthralgias of the knees and wrists
stages.explanation: Inflammation confined to the cartilaginous auricle with sparing of the non-cartilaginous lobule is the classic, highly specific sign of relapsing polychondritis, because the disease targets cartilage and the lobule contains none. Migratory arthralgias (choice 1) are common but entirely nonspecific, occurring in countless rheumatologic and infectious conditions. Scleral injection (choice 2) reflects ocular inflammation seen across many vasculitides and connective-tissue diseases and does not localize to cartilage. Hoarseness and cough (choice 3) suggest laryngotracheal involvement that is consistent with the diagnosis but also occurs in infections, reflux, and GPA; it is supportive, not specific. The earlobe-sparing chondritis is the pivotal clue distinguishing chondritis from cellulitis or other auricular processes.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Relapsing polychondritis is primarily a clinical diagnosis. Which laboratory or imaging test is MOST important to obtain because it identifies the complication that most often determines prognosis?
options: Antinuclear antibody (ANA) panel
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody
Dynamic chest CT of the airways
Serum uric acid level as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Airway involvement (subglottic, tracheal, and bronchial chondritis with malacia and collapse) is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in relapsing polychondritis, so dynamic chest CT to assess for airway narrowing and dynamic collapse is the highest-yield study given his hoarseness and stridor. ANA (choice 0) is nonspecific and not diagnostic for RPC; it may be ordered to exclude overlap disease but does not assess the prognosis-defining complication. Uric acid (choice 2) evaluates gout, which does not cause chondritis. Anti-CCP (choice 3) is specific for rheumatoid arthritis, a different disease with persistent symmetric small-joint synovitis, not migratory cartilage-targeted inflammation. Identifying and quantifying airway disease directly guides urgency of immunosuppression and airway protection.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR: 64 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 4.1 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
WBC: 9.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL
Platelets: 388 ×10³/µL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no protein, no dysmorphic RBCs, no casts
ANA: negative
ANCA (PR3 and MPO): negative
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Dynamic chest CT: circumferential tracheal wall thickening with subglottic narrowing and >50% expiratory collapse of the mid-trachea; sparing of the posterior membranous wall
Flexible laryngoscopy: erythema and edema of the subglottic region

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The CT shows tracheal wall thickening that spares the posterior membranous wall and negative ANCA. How does this finding help distinguish his disease from granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA)?
options: GPA never causes saddle-nose deformity as the immediate next step
Both diseases are excluded once ANCA is negative as the immediate next step
GPA airway disease is always limited to the posterior membranous wall only as the immediate next step
Sparing of the posterior membranous trachea favors relapsing polychondritis, since GPA airway disease
stages.explanation: In relapsing polychondritis the inflammatory process targets cartilaginous airway walls and characteristically spares the posterior membranous (non-cartilaginous) trachea, whereas GPA produces subglottic stenosis and is usually associated with PR3-ANCA positivity; the combination of membranous sparing plus negative ANCA strongly favors RPC. Choice 0 is false—GPA can also cause a saddle-nose deformity from nasal cartilage destruction, which is why the diseases overlap clinically. Choice 2 inverts the anatomy. Choice 3 is wrong because a negative ANCA does not exclude either diagnosis (ANCA can be negative in limited GPA), so the distribution pattern and overall clinical picture remain essential. The membranous-sparing pattern is a useful radiologic discriminator.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given biopsy-supported chondritis with active airway involvement and stridor, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: NSAIDs alone and outpatient follow-up in 3 months as initial therapy
Immediate permanent tracheostomy as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Topical antibiotics to the ears and reassurance as initial therapy
Systemic high-dose corticosteroids with prompt addition of a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant
stages.explanation: Organ-threatening relapsing polychondritis with active airway disease and stridor requires systemic high-dose corticosteroids to rapidly control inflammation, with early addition of a steroid-sparing agent (e.g., methotrexate, azathioprine, or for refractory/severe disease a biologic) to limit cumulative steroid toxicity and prevent further airway destruction. Topical antibiotics (choice 0) treat an infection that is not present and ignore systemic cartilage inflammation. NSAIDs alone (choice 1) are appropriate only for mild chondritis or arthralgia, not for life-threatening airway involvement. Tracheostomy (choice 3) may be needed for fixed critical stenosis or collapse but is not first-line; medical immunosuppression to halt active inflammation comes first, with airway interventions reserved for refractory structural compromise.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Relapsing polychondritis is associated with an underlying hematologic disorder in a subset of older patients. Which condition should prompt screening, especially if blood counts are abnormal?
options: G6PD deficiency
Von Willebrand disease
Hereditary spherocytosis
Myelodysplastic syndrome
stages.explanation: Relapsing polychondritis—particularly in older men—can be associated with myelodysplastic syndrome (and other hematologic/myeloid disorders), so unexplained cytopenias or macrocytosis warrant a peripheral smear and hematology evaluation; this overlap is now recognized within the VEXAS syndrome spectrum in some patients. Hereditary spherocytosis (choice 1) is a congenital hemolytic anemia presenting earlier in life and is unrelated to chondritis. Von Willebrand disease (choice 2) is a bleeding disorder with no association with relapsing polychondritis. G6PD deficiency (choice 3) causes episodic hemolysis with oxidative stress and is not linked to cartilage inflammation. Recognizing the MDS association affects prognosis and may change management, making a complete blood count with smear an important part of the workup in older patients.

pearls: Auricular chondritis that spares the non-cartilaginous earlobe is the hallmark of relapsing polychondritis and distinguishes it from cellulitis.
Airway involvement (subglottic/tracheal chondritis and malacia) is the leading cause of death—obtain dynamic airway CT early in symptomatic patients.
Saddle-nose deformity occurs in both relapsing polychondritis and GPA; ANCA positivity and posterior membranous wall involvement favor GPA, while membranous sparing favors RPC.
Treatment is immunosuppression—corticosteroids for flares, with steroid-sparing agents or biologics for organ-threatening or refractory disease.

cases.title: Sudden Swollen Ankles with Tender Shin Bumps
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of painful, swollen ankles and tender red lumps on both shins.
cases.history: A 36-year-old woman presents with one week of bilateral ankle pain and swelling that came on fairly abruptly, making walking difficult. Two days later she developed several tender, raised, reddish-purple nodules on the front of both lower legs. She reports low-grade fevers, fatigue, and mild joint aches in the knees, but denies cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, night sweats, eye redness, or rashes elsewhere. She has no preceding diarrhea, sore throat, or new medications. PMH is unremarkable and she takes no regular medications other than a daily multivitamin. She has no known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, works as a schoolteacher, and recently returned from a camping trip. Family history is notable for a sister with hypothyroidism. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, dyspnea, abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, oral or genital ulcers, and Raynaud phenomenon. She is not pregnant and her last menstrual period was two weeks ago.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing but uncomfortable when bearing weight. Both ankles show warmth, swelling, and tenderness with painful, limited range of motion consistent with a true arthritis rather than periarticular tenderness alone. Over both anterior shins there are several 1–3 cm tender, indurated, erythematous-to-violaceous subcutaneous nodules without ulceration. The knees have small effusions and are mildly tender. There is no synovitis of the hands or wrists, no skin rash elsewhere, no oral or genital ulcers, and no conjunctival or scleral injection. Lungs are clear to auscultation. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How should the tender nodules on her shins be best characterized?
options: Tophi from chronic gout
Erythema nodosum, a septal panniculitis
Subcutaneous rheumatoid nodules
Palpable purpura from small-vessel vasculitis
stages.explanation: Tender, erythematous-to-violaceous nodules on the anterior shins that do not ulcerate are classic for erythema nodosum, a septal panniculitis representing a reactive hypersensitivity process. Palpable purpura (choice 0) consists of non-blanching petechiae/purpura from leukocytoclastic vasculitis and is flatter and more widespread, not deep tender nodules. Gouty tophi (choice 2) are firm, painless, chalky deposits over joints and ears that develop after years of hyperuricemia, not acute tender shin lesions in a young woman. Rheumatoid nodules (choice 3) are firm, nontender, and occur over extensor/pressure surfaces in patients with established seropositive RA, not as acutely tender inflammatory shin nodules with fever. Recognizing erythema nodosum narrows the differential toward sarcoidosis, infections, IBD, and drugs.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given acute ankle arthritis plus erythema nodosum and constitutional symptoms, which single test is most likely to reveal the unifying diagnosis?
options: Serum uric acid
HLA-B27 testing
Anti-streptolysin O titer
Chest radiograph
stages.explanation: The triad of acute (often ankle) arthritis, erythema nodosum, and constitutional symptoms in a young woman is Löfgren syndrome, an acute presentation of sarcoidosis; a chest radiograph showing bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy is the highest-yield, near-diagnostic study. Serum uric acid (choice 1) evaluates gout, which does not cause erythema nodosum or bilateral hilar adenopathy and is rare in premenopausal women. ASO titer (choice 2) screens for recent streptococcal infection, a possible EN trigger, but would not explain hilar adenopathy and is lower yield here. HLA-B27 (choice 3) is associated with spondyloarthropathies that cause asymmetric large-joint arthritis but not erythema nodosum with hilar adenopathy. The chest film both supports the diagnosis and provides prognostic staging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC: 8.4 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin: 12.9 g/dL
Platelets: 305 ×10³/µL
ESR: 52 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 3.2 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
Calcium: 9.6 mg/dL (ref 8.5–10.2)
Serum ACE: 78 U/L (mildly elevated)
Uric acid: 4.2 mg/dL
ANA: negative
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Urinalysis: normal
Chest radiograph: bilateral symmetric hilar lymphadenopathy without parenchymal infiltrates (stage I)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The chest radiograph shows bilateral hilar adenopathy and the picture fits Löfgren syndrome. Is a tissue biopsy required to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Yes—mediastinal lymph node biopsy is mandatory before any treatment as the immediate next step
No—the classic Löfgren triad with bilateral hilar adenopathy is highly specific,
No—because serum ACE is elevated, no further evaluation of any kind is needed before discharge planning
Yes—skin biopsy of the erythema nodosum will show non-caseating granulomas confirming sarcoid
stages.explanation: The complete Löfgren triad (acute arthritis, erythema nodosum, and bilateral symmetric hilar lymphadenopathy) is so characteristic of acute sarcoidosis that biopsy can generally be deferred when the presentation is classic and there is no atypical feature; this spares the patient an invasive procedure. Choice 0 overstates the need for biopsy in a textbook presentation. Choice 2 is a common trap—erythema nodosum is a reactive septal panniculitis and shows NO granulomas, so its biopsy cannot confirm sarcoidosis. Choice 3 is wrong because serum ACE is neither sensitive nor specific and cannot, by itself, establish or exclude the diagnosis; clinical context drives the decision. When atypical features are present, tissue confirmation (e.g., EBUS-guided node biopsy) is appropriate.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and prognostic counseling for this patient?
options: Initiate anti-tuberculous therapy empirically as initial therapy
Start lifelong high-dose oral corticosteroids immediately as initial therapy
Begin methotrexate as first-line steroid-sparing therapy as initial therapy
Symptomatic treatment with NSAIDs
stages.explanation: Löfgren syndrome carries an excellent prognosis, with spontaneous resolution in the large majority of patients within months; first-line therapy is symptomatic—NSAIDs for the arthritis and erythema nodosum (colchicine or a short steroid course for refractory symptoms)—with reassurance. Lifelong high-dose steroids (choice 0) are unnecessary and expose the patient to needless toxicity for a self-limited illness. Methotrexate (choice 1) is reserved for chronic, progressive, or organ-threatening sarcoidosis, not this acute benign syndrome. Empiric anti-TB therapy (choice 3) is inappropriate without evidence of tuberculosis; although TB can mimic sarcoid, her bilateral symmetric hilar pattern and clinical syndrome point to sarcoidosis, and TB should be excluded before, not treated reflexively. Counseling on the favorable natural history is central.

pearls: Löfgren syndrome = acute arthritis (often ankles) + erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, a self-limited form of sarcoidosis with excellent prognosis.
Erythema nodosum is a septal panniculitis and does NOT contain granulomas—biopsying it cannot confirm sarcoidosis.
A classic Löfgren triad is specific enough that lymph node biopsy can often be deferred; tissue is reserved for atypical presentations.
Serum ACE lacks sensitivity and specificity and should never be used alone to diagnose sarcoidosis; treat acute disease symptomatically with NSAIDs.

cases.title: Recurring Fevers and Belly Pain Since Childhood
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated short attacks of fever with severe abdominal pain that resolve on their own.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man presents with a lifelong pattern of recurrent attacks beginning in early childhood: abrupt high fevers accompanied by severe, diffuse abdominal pain that mimics a surgical abdomen, lasting one to three days and then resolving completely. He has had several emergency visits and one prior appendectomy that found a normal appendix. Some attacks feature sharp, pleuritic chest pain or a swollen, painful single ankle or knee. Between episodes he feels entirely well. The attacks occur every few weeks, sometimes triggered by stress or menstruation in affected relatives. He denies oral ulcers, rash other than occasional red, tender areas on the lower legs during attacks, diarrhea, weight loss, and night sweats. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no daily medications. No known drug allergies. He is of Armenian ancestry, a never-smoker, and a college student. Family history is striking: a paternal uncle had similar attacks and developed kidney failure in his 40s. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: During an acute attack, the patient appears flushed and uncomfortable. Abdominal exam reveals diffuse tenderness with voluntary guarding and rebound, but bowel sounds are present and there is no rigidity or palpable mass. There is mild tenderness over the left lower ribs that worsens with deep inspiration, consistent with pleuritic involvement. The right ankle is mildly swollen, warm, and tender with painful range of motion. A few faint, tender erythematous patches are noted over the left shin. There is no lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly appreciated, and no rash elsewhere. Cardiac and pulmonary auscultation are otherwise unremarkable. Neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this history is MOST suggestive of a hereditary periodic fever syndrome rather than an infectious or surgical cause?
options: Tachycardia during fever as the first step today
Presence of guarding and rebound on abdominal exam as the first step today
A single episode of severe abdominal pain as the first step today
Stereotyped, self-limited recurrent attacks of fever with serositis since childhood plus complete wellness between episodes
stages.explanation: The defining clue is a long-standing pattern of stereotyped, short, self-resolving attacks of fever with serositis (peritonitis, pleuritis, synovitis) since childhood, with complete symptom-free intervals—this periodicity and self-limitation distinguish autoinflammatory syndromes from infections or surgical emergencies. A single episode of abdominal pain (choice 0) is the opposite of the recurrent pattern that defines these syndromes. Guarding and rebound (choice 2) reflect the serositis but are nonspecific and indistinguishable from surgical peritonitis on a single exam—indeed, unnecessary appendectomies are common. Tachycardia during fever (choice 3) is an expected physiologic response and provides no diagnostic specificity. The combination of ethnicity, family history of attacks with renal failure, and recurrent serositis strongly points to familial Mediterranean fever.

stages.label: LABS DURING ATTACK
stages.content:
WBC: 15.6 ×10³/µL with neutrophilia
Hemoglobin: 13.8 g/dL
Platelets: 360 ×10³/µL
ESR: 70 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 9.5 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
Serum amyloid A: markedly elevated
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no protein, no casts
Blood cultures: pending, no growth at 24h
Abdominal ultrasound: small amount of free pelvic fluid, normal appendix, no abscess
Lipase: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test would best confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Genetic testing for MEFV gene mutations
Tissue transglutaminase IgA antibody
Antinuclear antibody and anti-dsDNA panel
Repeat blood cultures during three separate attacks
stages.explanation: Familial Mediterranean fever is caused by mutations in the MEFV gene (encoding pyrin), so MEFV genetic testing best confirms the clinical diagnosis, especially in a patient of Armenian ancestry with stereotyped serositis attacks and a family history of attacks complicated by renal failure (suggesting amyloidosis). ANA/anti-dsDNA (choice 1) evaluates lupus, which causes serositis but is chronic, multisystem, and typically autoantibody-positive—not brief self-limited attacks with complete recovery. Tissue transglutaminase IgA (choice 2) screens for celiac disease, which produces chronic GI symptoms and malabsorption, not recurrent febrile peritonitis. Repeated blood cultures (choice 3) are reasonable to exclude infection acutely but will not establish FMF; cultures are negative in this autoinflammatory disorder. The diagnosis remains clinical, with MEFV testing providing genetic support.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His uncle developed kidney failure. What is the most feared long-term complication of untreated familial Mediterranean fever, and what drives it?
options: Glomerulonephritis from immune-complex deposition as the leading concern
AA amyloidosis from chronic serum amyloid A elevation, causing nephrotic syndrome
Renal artery stenosis from vasculitis as the leading concern
Polycystic kidney disease progression as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The most serious complication of FMF is secondary (AA) amyloidosis: persistent subclinical and clinical inflammation keeps serum amyloid A (an acute-phase reactant) chronically elevated, and its deposition in the kidneys causes nephrotic-range proteinuria progressing to end-stage renal disease—exactly the course in his uncle. Immune-complex glomerulonephritis (choice 0) characterizes lupus and post-infectious processes, not FMF. Polycystic kidney disease (choice 2) is a structural genetic disorder unrelated to autoinflammation. Renal artery stenosis from vasculitis (choice 3) is not a recognized FMF complication. Crucially, ongoing inflammation even between overt attacks (reflected by elevated SAA) drives amyloid deposition, which is why suppression of inflammation—not just attack control—is the therapeutic goal.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate long-term therapy, and what is its key benefit?
options: As-needed NSAIDs during attacks only, with no daily prophylaxis
Empiric prophylactic antibiotics as initial therapy
Chronic low-dose prednisone between attacks as initial therapy
Daily colchicine to reduce attack frequency
stages.explanation: Lifelong daily colchicine is the cornerstone of FMF management: it both decreases the frequency and severity of attacks and—critically—prevents the development of AA amyloidosis by suppressing the chronic inflammation that elevates serum amyloid A, thereby protecting the kidneys. NSAIDs as needed (choice 0) only blunt acute symptoms and do nothing to prevent amyloidosis, the main threat to survival. Chronic prednisone (choice 2) is ineffective for FMF attacks and carries cumulative steroid toxicity. Prophylactic antibiotics (choice 3) are useless because FMF is autoinflammatory, not infectious. For the minority of patients who are colchicine-resistant or intolerant, IL-1 inhibitors (e.g., anakinra or canakinumab) are the next step, but colchicine remains first-line and amyloidosis-preventing.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He tolerates colchicine but continues to have frequent breakthrough attacks despite confirmed adherence at maximally tolerated doses. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Switch to chronic high-dose corticosteroids with appropriate follow-up
Begin a TNF inhibitor as the established next-line agent
Stop colchicine entirely as it has failed with appropriate follow-up
Add an IL-1 inhibitor for colchicine-resistant disease
stages.explanation: Colchicine-resistant FMF—persistent frequent attacks despite confirmed adherence at maximally tolerated doses—is best managed by adding an IL-1 inhibitor such as anakinra or canakinumab, since pyrin-driven inflammation in FMF is mediated by the inflammasome and IL-1; this also continues to suppress serum amyloid A and protect against amyloidosis.
