Stopping colchicine entirely (choice 0) is wrong because colchicine still provides partial benefit and amyloidosis prophylaxis and is typically continued alongside the biologic. Chronic high-dose corticosteroids (choice 2) are ineffective for FMF attacks and carry cumulative toxicity. TNF inhibitors (choice 3) are not the established next-line agent for colchicine-resistant FMF; IL-1 blockade is the targeted therapy of choice given the IL-1-driven pathophysiology.

pearls: FMF features stereotyped, short, self-limited attacks of fever with serositis (peritonitis, pleuritis, monoarthritis) since childhood, with complete wellness between episodes.
It is caused by MEFV (pyrin) mutations and is most common in Mediterranean ancestries (Armenian, Sephardic Jewish, Turkish, Arab); diagnosis is clinical with genetic support.
The dreaded complication is AA amyloidosis from chronically elevated serum amyloid A, leading to nephrotic syndrome and renal failure.
Daily colchicine both reduces attacks AND prevents amyloidosis; IL-1 inhibitors are used for colchicine-resistant disease.

cases.title: Frothy Urine and Leg Swelling in a Man with Crippling Joint Disease
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive leg swelling and foamy urine.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man with long-standing, poorly controlled seropositive rheumatoid arthritis is admitted for evaluation of progressive bilateral leg edema and foamy urine over four months, along with fatigue and a 6 kg weight gain. His RA was diagnosed 22 years ago; he has been intermittently adherent, frequently lost to follow-up, and never achieved sustained remission, with persistent active synovitis and progressive joint deformities. His only RA medication recently has been low-dose prednisone and occasional NSAIDs; he stopped methotrexate years ago. He denies hematuria, gross dysuria, recent infections, or new medications other than ibuprofen. PMH includes hypertension. Medications: prednisone 7.5 mg daily, ibuprofen 600 mg as needed, amlodipine 5 mg daily. No known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago) and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable for kidney disease. Review of systems is notable for early satiety, mild diarrhea, and orthostatic lightheadedness, but no chest pain, fevers, or rash.
cases.exam: The patient appears chronically ill. There is marked pitting edema to the thighs and mild periorbital puffiness. Musculoskeletal exam shows established RA deformities: ulnar deviation at the MCPs, swan-neck and boutonnière deformities, and active synovitis of several MCP and wrist joints bilaterally. The abdomen is soft with mild hepatomegaly; the spleen is not clearly enlarged. There are firm subcutaneous nodules over both elbows. Cardiac exam reveals normal heart sounds without rub or gallop. Lungs have mild bibasilar crackles. There is no jugular venous distention. Postural blood pressure drops on standing. Skin shows no purpura. Neurologic exam is grossly normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of edema and foamy urine in this patient most strongly suggests which renal syndrome?
options: Nephritic syndrome with active urinary sediment
Obstructive uropathy as the first step today
Pre-renal azotemia from volume depletion
Nephrotic syndrome from heavy proteinuria
stages.explanation: Foamy urine plus dependent and periorbital edema with weight gain in a patient with chronic uncontrolled inflammation points to nephrotic syndrome—heavy proteinuria causing hypoalbuminemia and edema. Nephritic syndrome (choice 0) features hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, hypertension, and rising creatinine; his picture lacks active sediment and he is hypotensive. Pre-renal azotemia (choice 2) causes oliguria and rising BUN/creatinine from hypovolemia but not heavy proteinuria with foamy urine and anasarca. Obstructive uropathy (choice 3) causes hydronephrosis and azotemia, not nephrotic-range protein loss. In a patient with two decades of untreated RA, nephrotic syndrome should raise immediate concern for secondary (AA) amyloidosis from sustained acute-phase inflammation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 4+ protein, no blood, no RBC casts, occasional oval fat bodies
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio: 7.8 g/g
Serum albumin: 2.1 g/dL
Total cholesterol: 312 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
ESR: 88 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 6.4 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
Rheumatoid factor: 210 IU/mL (positive)
Anti-CCP: strongly positive
ANA: negative
Hepatitis B/C serologies: negative
SPEP/UPEP with immunofixation: no monoclonal protein
Free light chain ratio: normal
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no obstruction

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given nephrotic-range proteinuria in chronic uncontrolled RA with a negative monoclonal workup, what is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Empiric high-dose steroids without biopsy
Bone marrow biopsy to look for plasma cells
Tissue biopsy with Congo red staining
24-hour urine for catecholamines as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The clinical setting—two decades of poorly controlled inflammatory disease, nephrotic syndrome, and a negative monoclonal protein workup (normal SPEP/UPEP, immunofixation, and free light chains)—strongly suggests AA (secondary) amyloidosis, and diagnosis requires tissue confirmation: Congo red staining showing apple-green birefringence under polarized light, with typing confirming AA fibrils. Renal biopsy is definitive; less invasive abdominal fat pad or rectal biopsy can also yield amyloid. Empiric steroids without tissue (choice 0) risk misdiagnosis and toxicity. A 24-hour catecholamine collection (choice 2) evaluates pheochromocytoma and is irrelevant here. Bone marrow biopsy (choice 3) targets AL amyloidosis/plasma cell dyscrasia, already made unlikely by the normal SPEP/UPEP and light chains; the inflammatory context favors AA, not AL, amyloid.

stages.label: BIOPSY RESULT
stages.content:
Renal biopsy: amorphous eosinophilic material deposited in glomerular mesangium and vessel walls
Congo red stain: positive, with apple-green birefringence under polarized light
Immunohistochemistry: deposits stain for serum amyloid A (AA) protein
No congophilic deposits typed as lambda or kappa light chains
Electron microscopy: randomly arranged 8–10 nm non-branching fibrils

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond supportive renal care, what is the cornerstone of treatment for this patient's AA amyloidosis?
options: Lifelong colchicine, which is the definitive therapy in all AA amyloidosis
Plasma exchange to remove circulating amyloid fibrils as initial therapy
Aggressive suppression of the underlying RA inflammation to lower serum amyloid A
Autologous stem cell transplant with high-dose melphalan as initial therapy
stages.explanation: AA amyloidosis is driven by chronically elevated serum amyloid A from the underlying inflammatory disease, so the cornerstone of therapy is aggressively controlling the source inflammation—here, escalating RA treatment to effective DMARD/biologic therapy (e.g., methotrexate plus a TNF or IL-6 inhibitor) to drive SAA toward normal and halt amyloid deposition. High-dose melphalan with stem cell transplant (choice 1) treats AL amyloidosis (a plasma cell disorder), not AA. Colchicine (choice 2) is the definitive amyloidosis prophylaxis specifically in familial Mediterranean fever, not the universal treatment for all AA amyloidosis driven by RA. Plasma exchange (choice 3) does not remove deposited tissue amyloid and is not a recognized therapy. Controlling the inflammatory driver is the key to stabilizing or improving organ function.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He reports early satiety, mild diarrhea, and orthostatic lightheadedness. How do these symptoms relate to his amyloidosis?
options: Amyloid can deposit in multiple organs
They are diagnostic of concurrent inflammatory bowel disease as the leading concern
They are unrelated medication side effects only as the leading concern
They indicate the amyloidosis has resolved as the leading concern
stages.explanation: AA amyloidosis is a systemic disease: beyond the kidneys, amyloid commonly deposits in the gastrointestinal tract (causing dysmotility, malabsorption, and diarrhea) and in autonomic nerves and vasculature (causing orthostatic hypotension), so his early satiety, diarrhea, and postural lightheadedness reflect extrarenal amyloid involvement and a heavier disease burden. Calling them unrelated medication effects (choice 0) ignores the systemic nature of the disease. They certainly do not indicate resolution (choice 2)—if anything they signal progression. They are not diagnostic of inflammatory bowel disease (choice 3); while RA patients can have other GI conditions, the unifying explanation here is multiorgan amyloid deposition. Recognizing extrarenal involvement guides supportive care and reinforces the urgency of controlling the inflammatory driver.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which of the following is the strongest predictor that this patient was at risk for developing AA amyloidosis?
options: His use of amlodipine with appropriate follow-up
His former smoking history with appropriate follow-up
Two decades of persistently active, poorly controlled inflammation with chronically elevated acute-phase reactants
His hypertension with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The dominant risk factor for AA amyloidosis is the cumulative burden of chronic, uncontrolled inflammation: decades of active RA with persistently elevated acute-phase reactants (ESR, CRP) keep serum amyloid A high, and sustained SAA elevation drives fibril deposition—exactly this patient's trajectory of intermittent adherence and never achieving remission. Hypertension (choice 0) contributes to chronic kidney disease through different mechanisms but does not cause amyloid deposition. A former smoking history (choice 1) is a risk factor for many conditions but is not the driver of AA amyloid. Amlodipine (choice 3) is an antihypertensive with no role in amyloidogenesis. This underscores why early, sustained control of inflammatory disease is the single most important preventive measure against AA amyloidosis.

cases.diagnosis: AA (secondary) amyloidosis complicating chronic rheumatoid arthritis
pearls: Nephrotic-range proteinuria in a patient with long-standing, poorly controlled inflammatory disease (RA, IBD, chronic infection) should prompt suspicion of AA amyloidosis.
Diagnosis requires tissue biopsy with Congo red staining showing apple-green birefringence; immunotyping distinguishes AA from AL amyloid.
A negative SPEP/UPEP, immunofixation, and free light chain ratio argue against AL amyloidosis and favor AA in the right clinical context.
Treatment centers on suppressing the underlying inflammation to lower serum amyloid A; colchicine is amyloid prophylaxis specifically for FMF.

cases.title: Sore Joints That Flare When the Bowels Act Up
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful, swollen joints and low back stiffness that worsen when her gut symptoms flare.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman with a 4-year history of Crohn disease presents with recurrent joint pain. She describes painful, swollen knees and ankles, often asymmetric, that tend to flare during periods of increased abdominal cramping, diarrhea (sometimes bloody), and weight loss—and that improve when her bowel disease is controlled. Separately, she reports the gradual onset of low back and buttock pain with prolonged morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with movement and worsens with rest, including waking in the second half of the night. She denies psoriasis, recent dysentery or urethritis, eye pain, or tick exposure. PMH: Crohn disease. Medications: azathioprine 100 mg daily, with prior intermittent prednisone tapers. No known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, works as a nurse, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history includes a father with ankylosing spondylitis. Review of systems is positive for occasional painful red eye in the past and mild fatigue, but negative for oral ulcers, nail changes, and genital lesions.
cases.exam: The patient appears thin but comfortable at rest. The right knee and left ankle are warm, swollen, and tender with effusions and painful range of motion; the small joints of the hands are spared. Spinal exam shows reduced lumbar flexion with a positive modified Schober test and tenderness over both sacroiliac joints; lateral spinal flexion is limited. Enthesitis is noted at the right Achilles insertion. The abdomen is mildly tender in the right lower quadrant without rebound; there is a healed perianal fistula scar. There is no psoriatic rash, no dactylitis, and no current conjunctival injection. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her low back pain pattern—morning stiffness over an hour, improvement with activity, worsening with rest, and night-time awakening—is best classified as which type?
options: Mechanical back pain
Inflammatory back pain
Referred pain from the bowel
Radicular pain from disc herniation
stages.explanation: Inflammatory back pain is defined by insidious onset before age 40, morning stiffness lasting more than 30–60 minutes, improvement with exercise but not rest, and nocturnal pain that improves on arising—exactly her pattern—indicating sacroiliitis/spondylitis. Mechanical back pain (choice 0) is the opposite: it worsens with activity, improves with rest, and lacks prolonged morning stiffness. Radicular pain (choice 2) radiates in a dermatomal distribution with neurologic signs (numbness, weakness, positive straight-leg raise), absent here. Referred bowel pain (choice 3) does not produce a positive Schober test, sacroiliac tenderness, or the inflammatory rhythm of axial symptoms. Recognizing inflammatory back pain plus peripheral arthritis in a patient with Crohn disease points toward enteropathic spondyloarthritis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How would you best characterize the relationship between her two arthritis patterns and her Crohn disease?
options: Peripheral arthritis tends to track with bowel activity, whereas axial disease runs an independent course
Neither pattern is related to the bowel disease as the primary diagnosis
Both peripheral and axial arthritis always parallel bowel activity as the primary diagnosis
Axial arthritis flares with bowel activity but peripheral arthritis is independent in this clinical context
stages.explanation: In enteropathic arthritis, peripheral type 1 arthritis (pauciarticular, asymmetric, large lower-limb joints) characteristically parallels intestinal disease activity and improves when the bowel disease is controlled, while axial involvement (sacroiliitis/spondylitis) and type 2 peripheral arthritis (polyarticular, symmetric) typically follow a course independent of gut activity. Choice 0 is wrong because axial disease does not reliably track bowel flares. Choice 2 inverts the relationship. Choice 3 ignores the clear temporal link between her large-joint flares and bowel symptoms. This distinction matters for management: treating the underlying IBD may resolve type 1 peripheral arthritis, whereas axial disease often needs dedicated spondyloarthritis-directed therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR: 46 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 2.9 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (microcytic)
Ferritin: low
WBC: 9.2 ×10³/µL
Platelets: 430 ×10³/µL
HLA-B27: positive
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP: negative
Fecal calprotectin: markedly elevated
Pelvic radiograph: bilateral sacroiliitis with erosions and sclerosis
Knee aspirate: inflammatory fluid, WBC 18,000/µL, no crystals, cultures negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has active Crohn disease with peripheral arthritis tracking her bowel flares and axial spondyloarthritis. Which therapy best addresses BOTH her gut and joint disease?
options: Sulfasalazine for the spine plus chronic prednisone
Long-term systemic NSAIDs as the mainstay
An anti-TNF biologic
Methotrexate monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An anti-TNF biologic such as infliximab or adalimumab effectively treats both moderate-to-severe Crohn disease and the associated peripheral and axial spondyloarthritis, making it the best single choice for this patient with active disease on both fronts. Long-term systemic NSAIDs (choice 0) are problematic in IBD because they can trigger bowel flares and are relatively contraindicated for sustained use, despite helping spondyloarthritis symptoms. Methotrexate (choice 2) has limited efficacy for axial spondyloarthritis and is not the optimal agent for the spine. Sulfasalazine (choice 3) may help peripheral arthritis and mild colonic IBD but is ineffective for axial disease, and chronic prednisone carries cumulative toxicity without controlling axial spondyloarthritis. TNF inhibition uniquely covers gut, peripheral, and axial manifestations.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She mentions prior episodes of a painful red eye with photophobia. Which extra-articular complication of spondyloarthritis should this prompt you to evaluate and counsel about?
options: Episcleritis, which is painless and self-limited as the leading concern
Cataract from the disease itself as the leading concern
Acute anterior uveitis, which requires urgent ophthalmologic evaluation
Bacterial conjunctivitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Acute anterior uveitis (iritis) is the classic ocular complication of HLA-B27-associated spondyloarthritis, presenting with a painful red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision; it requires urgent ophthalmologic evaluation and treatment (topical steroids and cycloplegics) to prevent synechiae and vision loss, and recurrences are common. Episcleritis (choice 0) is typically painless or mildly uncomfortable and self-limited and is more associated with IBD itself, but her painful, photophobic red eye fits uveitis. Bacterial conjunctivitis (choice 1) causes purulent discharge without significant pain or photophobia and is unrelated to spondyloarthritis. Cataract (choice 3) is not a direct manifestation of the disease (though chronic steroid use can contribute). Patients should be counseled to seek prompt care for any painful red eye.

cases.diagnosis: Enteropathic arthritis (spondyloarthritis associated with Crohn disease)
pearls: Enteropathic peripheral type 1 arthritis (oligoarticular, asymmetric, large lower-limb joints) tracks with IBD activity; axial disease and type 2 peripheral arthritis run independently.
Inflammatory back pain = insidious onset before 40, morning stiffness >30–60 min, better with activity, worse with rest, and nocturnal awakening.
Anti-TNF biologics treat both the gut and the joints; avoid chronic NSAIDs because they can flare IBD.
Acute anterior uveitis (painful red eye, photophobia) is a vision-threatening complication of HLA-B27 spondyloarthritis requiring urgent ophthalmology referral.

cases.title: Swollen Knee and Heel Pain After a Stomach Bug
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of a swollen, painful knee and ankle that started after a bout of food poisoning.
cases.history: A 25-year-old man presents with three weeks of pain and swelling in his right knee and left ankle, plus heel pain when walking. About three weeks before the joint symptoms began, he had a self-limited illness with fever, crampy abdominal pain, and several days of bloody diarrhea following a barbecue, which resolved on its own. He now has difficulty bearing weight and stiffness that is worse in the morning. He also reports mild burning with urination and gritty, red eyes that started around the same time as the joint pain. He denies new sexual partners in the past six months, recent antibiotic use, tick bites, or prior similar episodes. PMH is unremarkable. He takes only ibuprofen 400 mg as needed. No known drug allergies. He is a graduate student, an occasional smoker, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is notable for a cousin with ankylosing spondylitis. Review of systems is positive for a few painless lesions on the soles of his feet and mild low back stiffness, but negative for oral ulcers and skin psoriasis.
cases.exam: The right knee is warm, swollen, and tender with a moderate effusion and limited flexion; the left ankle is similarly swollen and tender, an asymmetric oligoarthritis of the lower limbs. There is tenderness at the left Achilles insertion (enthesitis) and a sausage-like swelling of the left second toe (dactylitis). Bilateral conjunctival injection is present without purulent discharge. The soles show a few hyperkeratotic, painless papules (keratoderma blennorrhagicum). The genital exam reveals mild meatal erythema. Spinal exam shows mild sacroiliac tenderness. The small joints of the hands are spared, and there is no psoriatic plaque or nail pitting.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings best characterizes this clinical syndrome?
options: Asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis with enthesitis, conjunctivitis,
Migratory polyarthritis with carditis after a sore throat as the first step today
Symmetric small-joint polyarthritis of the hands as the first step today
Acute monoarthritis of the first metatarsophalangeal joint as the first step today
stages.explanation: The picture—asymmetric oligoarthritis of the lower limbs, enthesitis/dactylitis, conjunctivitis, and urethritis arising weeks after an enteric infection—is the classic reactive arthritis triad (arthritis, conjunctivitis, urethritis) following gastroenteritis. Symmetric small-joint polyarthritis (choice 0) describes rheumatoid arthritis, which spares the post-infectious context and the eye/urethral features. First-MTP monoarthritis (choice 2) is gout, typically in older patients with hyperuricemia, not a young man post-dysentery with conjunctivitis. Migratory polyarthritis with carditis after pharyngitis (choice 3) is acute rheumatic fever following group A strep, not an enteric trigger, and features carditis and erythema marginatum rather than enthesitis and keratoderma blennorrhagicum. The post-enteric timing and extra-articular triad clinch reactive arthritis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which enteric pathogen is the most likely trigger given his bloody diarrhea after a barbecue, and what test best supports the diagnosis of reactive arthritis itself?
options: Norovirus; serum norovirus PCR confirms reactive arthritis in this clinical context
Clostridioides difficile; stool toxin assay confirms reactive arthritis
Salmonella; the diagnosis is clinical
Giardia; stool ova and parasites confirms reactive arthritis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Reactive arthritis classically follows enteric infection with Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, or Yersinia; bloody diarrhea after a barbecue points to Salmonella (or Campylobacter). Reactive arthritis is a clinical diagnosis—no single test confirms it—but HLA-B27 is frequently positive and provides supportive evidence and prognostic information (association with axial involvement and chronicity). C. difficile (choice 1) causes antibiotic-associated colitis and is not a classic reactive-arthritis trigger; its toxin assay diagnoses the colitis, not the arthritis. Norovirus (choice 2) is not a recognized trigger and there is no confirmatory serum PCR for reactive arthritis. Giardia (choice 3) causes non-bloody malabsorptive diarrhea and is not a typical trigger. The key concept is the clinical post-enteric diagnosis with HLA-B27 as a supportive marker.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR: 48 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 3.6 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
WBC: 9.6 ×10³/µL
HLA-B27: positive
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP: negative
ANA: negative
Uric acid: 5.1 mg/dL
Knee aspirate: cloudy fluid, WBC 22,000/µL (neutrophil predominant), no crystals, Gram stain negative, cultures negative
Urethral NAAT for chlamydia and gonorrhea: negative
Stool culture: positive for Salmonella species
Pelvic radiograph: mild asymmetric sacroiliitis

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The knee aspirate shows inflammatory fluid with negative Gram stain, no crystals, and negative cultures. What is the most important reason this arthrocentesis was performed?
options: To confirm reactive arthritis by identifying Salmonella in the joint fluid
To measure synovial fluid HLA-B27 as the immediate next step
To exclude septic arthritis
To inject corticosteroids as the first treatment step as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In any acute monoarticular or oligoarticular effusion, arthrocentesis is essential primarily to exclude septic arthritis (a joint-destroying emergency) and crystal arthropathy, since these are diagnosed by synovial fluid analysis and require different urgent treatment; only after excluding them can the inflammatory effusion be attributed to reactive arthritis. Intra-articular steroids (choice 0) may be used after infection is excluded but are not the reason for the diagnostic tap. Reactive arthritis is sterile and immune-mediated—the organism is NOT cultured from the joint (choice 2), distinguishing it from septic arthritis. Synovial HLA-B27 (choice 3) is not a meaningful test; HLA-B27 is measured in blood. The negative Gram stain, absent crystals, and sterile cultures confirm a sterile inflammatory effusion consistent with reactive arthritis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment and prognostic counseling for his reactive arthritis?
options: Prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics to eradicate the joint infection
Immediate initiation of a TNF inhibitor as initial therapy
Joint immobilization in a cast until symptoms fully resolve as initial therapy
NSAIDs for symptom control, with reassurance that most cases resolve within months
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for reactive arthritis is NSAIDs for symptom control (with intra-articular steroids for a persistently inflamed single joint), and counseling that the majority of post-enteric cases are self-limited and resolve within 3–6 months. Prolonged antibiotics (choice 0) do not help post-enteric reactive arthritis because the joint is sterile and the triggering GI infection has already resolved; antibiotics have a possible role only in confirmed chlamydia-induced disease, which his negative urethral NAAT excludes. A TNF inhibitor (choice 2) is reserved for severe, refractory, or chronic disease, not first-line for a new self-limited case. Casting/immobilization (choice 3) promotes stiffness and muscle atrophy; gentle range-of-motion and physical therapy are preferred. Reassurance about the favorable natural history is central.

pearls: Reactive arthritis follows GI (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia) or GU (Chlamydia) infection by 1–4 weeks: asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis, enthesitis, dactylitis, conjunctivitis, urethritis.
The classic mnemonic: 'can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree'—plus keratoderma blennorrhagicum and circinate balanitis.
It is a clinical diagnosis; HLA-B27 is supportive, and the joint fluid is sterile—always tap to exclude septic and crystal arthritis.
Treatment is NSAIDs with reassurance; most post-enteric cases resolve in months. Antibiotics don't help post-enteric disease (only Chlamydia-induced).

cases.title: Worsening Breathlessness in a Man with Crippled Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: Six months of progressive shortness of breath and a dry cough.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man with an 11-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents with six months of progressive exertional dyspnea and a persistent dry cough. He can no longer climb a flight of stairs without stopping. His RA has been moderately active despite therapy, with chronic synovitis and joint deformities. Current medications: methotrexate 20 mg weekly with folic acid, low-dose prednisone 5 mg daily, and naproxen as needed. He denies fevers, purulent sputum, chest pain, orthopnea, or leg swelling. He has had no recent infections or new exposures. PMH includes RA and gastroesophageal reflux. No known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (25 pack-years, quit 8 years ago), worked as a machinist, and denies known asbestos exposure. He drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable for lung disease. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and unintentional 3 kg weight loss, but negative for hemoptysis, rash, Raynaud phenomenon, dysphagia, and proximal muscle weakness.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly dyspneic at rest. Lung auscultation reveals fine, dry, 'Velcro-like' bibasilar inspiratory crackles that do not clear with coughing. There is no wheeze. Cardiac exam shows a normal S1/S2 without murmur, rub, or gallop, and no elevated jugular venous pressure. Musculoskeletal exam reveals ulnar deviation, MCP subluxation, and active synovitis of several wrist and MCP joints, with firm subcutaneous nodules over both elbows. There is early digital clubbing. There is no peripheral edema, no rash, no heliotrope or Gottron papules, and no sclerodactyly. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The bibasilar 'Velcro' crackles and clubbing in a patient with RA most strongly suggest which process?
options: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation
Interstitial lung disease
Decompensated heart failure
Acute bacterial pneumonia
stages.explanation: Fine, dry, 'Velcro-like' bibasilar inspiratory crackles that do not clear with coughing, together with digital clubbing and a chronic dry cough with progressive dyspnea, are classic for interstitial lung disease—a well-recognized extra-articular complication of RA. Heart failure (choice 0) would typically show elevated JVP, an S3 gallop, orthopnea, and peripheral edema, none present here. Acute bacterial pneumonia (choice 2) presents acutely with fever, productive cough, and focal consolidation, not six months of dry cough with diffuse basal crackles. A COPD exacerbation (choice 3) causes wheeze, prolonged expiration, and increased sputum, not fine inspiratory crackles and clubbing. The chronic restrictive pattern with clubbing points firmly to ILD, prompting high-resolution chest CT and pulmonary function testing.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 12.4 g/dL
WBC: 7.2 ×10³/µL
ESR: 56 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 3.1 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
Rheumatoid factor: 180 IU/mL (positive)
Anti-CCP: strongly positive
ANA: negative
BNP: 60 pg/mL (normal)
HIGH-RESOLUTION CHEST CT: bilateral, basal-predominant subpleural reticulation with traction bronchiectasis and honeycombing; usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern
Pulmonary function tests: FVC 64% predicted, restrictive pattern, DLCO 52% predicted
6-minute walk: desaturation to 88%
Sputum and respiratory viral panel: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The HRCT shows a UIP pattern with restrictive PFTs and reduced DLCO. Before attributing this entirely to RA-associated ILD, which alternative must be specifically considered given his medications?
options: Prednisone-induced pulmonary fibrosis
Methotrexate-induced pneumonitis/lung toxicity
Naproxen-induced bronchospasm
Folic acid hypersensitivity pneumonitis
stages.explanation: Methotrexate can cause drug-induced pneumonitis and is a recognized cause of lung disease in RA patients, so it must be specifically considered and often held while evaluating new or worsening interstitial changes; distinguishing drug toxicity from underlying RA-ILD critically affects management. Naproxen (choice 1) can provoke bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive asthma but does not cause a chronic UIP-pattern interstitial process. Prednisone (choice 2) does not cause pulmonary fibrosis; corticosteroids are in fact used to treat some inflammatory ILDs. Folic acid (choice 3) is a benign supplement and not a cause of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. While the UIP pattern and seropositivity favor RA-ILD, methotrexate's potential pulmonary toxicity makes reassessing it essential, and many clinicians hold methotrexate when progressive ILD is identified.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Multidisciplinary review concludes this is progressive RA-associated ILD with a UIP pattern. What is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Start high-dose long-term antibiotics as initial therapy
Reassess/hold methotrexate, treat RA with an ILD-appropriate agent,
Immediate referral for single-lung transplant as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Continue methotrexate unchanged and add no other therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management of progressive RA-ILD involves reassessing potentially pneumotoxic methotrexate (often holding it), controlling RA with an agent considered more appropriate in the setting of ILD (e.g., rituximab or another carefully chosen DMARD), and—because the UIP pattern is progressively fibrosing—considering antifibrotic therapy (e.g., nintedanib), which slows decline in progressive pulmonary fibrosis. Continuing methotrexate unchanged (choice 0) ignores both its pneumotoxic potential and the need to address progressive ILD. Long-term antibiotics (choice 2) are inappropriate without infection, which the negative workup excludes. Lung transplant (choice 3) is reserved for end-stage, refractory disease in selected patients, not first-line. A multidisciplinary approach (rheumatology and pulmonology) optimizes both joint and lung outcomes while minimizing drug toxicity.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which baseline feature most increases this patient's risk for RA-associated interstitial lung disease?
options: Female sex and seronegative RA with appropriate follow-up
Short disease duration of under one year
Male sex, smoking history,
Use of folic acid supplementation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The strongest risk factors for RA-ILD are male sex, a smoking history, older age, longer/more severe disease, and high-titer seropositivity (elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP)—all present in this patient, explaining his elevated risk. Female sex with seronegative RA (choice 0) is actually associated with lower ILD risk; ILD disproportionately affects seropositive men who smoke. Short disease duration (choice 1) is not a major risk factor; ILD tends to complicate longer-standing, more active disease, though it can occasionally precede joint symptoms. Folic acid (choice 3) is protective against methotrexate side effects and is unrelated to ILD risk. Recognizing these risk factors helps clinicians monitor at-risk RA patients for pulmonary involvement with symptom screening and, when indicated, PFTs and HRCT.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient had instead presented with acute, rapidly worsening dyspnea over days with new diffuse ground-glass opacities while on methotrexate, what would be the most important immediate action?
options: Start antifibrotic therapy emergently as the first step as the leading concern
Increase methotrexate to control the lung inflammation as the leading concern
Hold methotrexate, evaluate urgently for methotrexate pneumonitis
Continue methotrexate and add an inhaled bronchodilator as the leading concern
stages.explanation: An acute, rapidly progressive respiratory decline with new diffuse ground-glass opacities in a patient on methotrexate is a red flag for methotrexate-induced pneumonitis (a hypersensitivity reaction) or an opportunistic infection; the immediate steps are to hold methotrexate, evaluate urgently (HRCT, infectious workup including for opportunistic organisms such as Pneumocystis), and treat the identified cause—often with corticosteroids for confirmed pneumonitis once infection is excluded. Continuing or increasing methotrexate (choices 0 and 2) could be fatal if drug-induced pneumonitis is present. Emergent antifibrotic therapy (choice 3) targets chronic progressive fibrosis, not an acute pneumonitis or infection, and would not address the immediate threat. Rapid recognition and drug cessation are lifesaving in acute methotrexate lung toxicity.

cases.diagnosis: Rheumatoid arthritis-associated interstitial lung disease (UIP pattern)
pearls: Fine 'Velcro' bibasilar inspiratory crackles plus clubbing and progressive dyspnea in an RA patient suggest interstitial lung disease; confirm with HRCT and PFTs.
Risk factors for RA-ILD: male sex, smoking, older age, long-standing severe disease, and high-titer RF/anti-CCP seropositivity.
Methotrexate can cause drug-induced pneumonitis—reassess or hold it when evaluating new or worsening ILD.
Management combines RA control with an ILD-appropriate DMARD (e.g., rituximab) and antifibrotic therapy (e.g., nintedanib) for progressive fibrosing UIP.

cases.title: Frequent Infections and a Big Spleen in a Woman with Old Joint Disease
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent infections and fatigue in a woman with long-standing deforming arthritis.
cases.history: A 62-year-old woman with a 25-year history of severe, deforming seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents with recurrent infections over the past year—two episodes of pneumonia, recurrent skin abscesses, and persistent oral thrush—along with fatigue and early satiety. Her RA, though now less symptomatic in terms of pain, left her with marked joint deformities. She has had several leg ulcers that heal slowly. She denies fevers at present, weight loss apart from poor appetite, easy bruising, or bleeding. PMH: long-standing RA, hypertension. Medications: methotrexate 15 mg weekly, folic acid, low-dose prednisone 5 mg daily, lisinopril. No known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, retired librarian, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and a vague sense of left upper abdominal fullness, but negative for night sweats, lymph node swelling that she has noticed, jaundice, and dark urine.
cases.exam: The patient appears chronically ill but not acutely toxic. Musculoskeletal exam shows advanced RA with ulnar deviation, swan-neck and boutonnière deformities, MCP subluxation, and rheumatoid nodules over the elbows; active synovitis is minimal. The spleen is palpable 4 cm below the left costal margin. There is no significant peripheral lymphadenopathy. The skin shows two healing lower-extremity ulcers and hyperpigmentation over the shins; there is no palpable purpura. Oral exam reveals white plaques consistent with thrush. There is no jaundice. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of long-standing rheumatoid arthritis, splenomegaly, and recurrent infections should raise suspicion for which syndrome, and what hematologic abnormality is expected?
options: Sjögren syndrome with lymphopenia
Polycythemia vera with erythrocytosis
Reactive thrombocytosis
Felty syndrome with neutropenia
stages.explanation: The triad of long-standing seropositive RA, splenomegaly, and neutropenia defines Felty syndrome, and the recurrent bacterial and fungal infections (pneumonia, abscesses, thrush) are explained by the neutropenia. Sjögren syndrome (choice 1) causes sicca symptoms and can accompany RA, but it does not classically present with splenomegaly and infection-prone neutropenia. Reactive thrombocytosis (choice 2) is a nonspecific acute-phase response and does not cause splenomegaly with infections. Polycythemia vera (choice 3) is a myeloproliferative disorder with erythrocytosis and thrombosis, unrelated to RA-associated immune neutropenia. The expected lab finding is neutropenia; recognizing this triad is essential because infection is the major cause of morbidity and mortality in Felty syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC: 2.2 ×10³/µL
Absolute neutrophil count: 480 /µL (severe neutropenia)
Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (normocytic)
Platelets: 130 ×10³/µL
Rheumatoid factor: 320 IU/mL (high titer)
Anti-CCP: strongly positive
ANA: positive (1:160)
ESR: 60 mm/hr (ref <20)
Peripheral smear: neutropenia, no blasts, no large granular lymphocyte excess noted
Abdominal ultrasound: splenomegaly (16 cm), no focal lesions
HIV, hepatitis B/C: negative
B12 and folate: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The smear shows neutropenia without excess large granular lymphocytes. Which condition is the most important mimic to consider and exclude in this setting?
options: Iron deficiency anemia
Large granular lymphocyte leukemia
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia
Hereditary spherocytosis
stages.explanation: Large granular lymphocyte (LGL) leukemia is the key mimic of Felty syndrome: it occurs in patients with RA and causes neutropenia and splenomegaly, overlapping closely with Felty syndrome, so it must be excluded (with flow cytometry and T-cell clonality studies) because management differs. Iron deficiency anemia (choice 0) causes microcytic anemia, not neutropenia with splenomegaly. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (choice 2) would show circulating blasts and a markedly abnormal smear, absent here. Hereditary spherocytosis (choice 3) is a congenital hemolytic anemia presenting earlier in life with spherocytes and would not explain neutropenia and infections in this RA context. Because Felty syndrome and LGL leukemia share features and frequently coexist, distinguishing them via flow cytometry/clonality is a crucial diagnostic step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Flow cytometry shows no clonal LGL population, supporting Felty syndrome. What is the most appropriate management of her neutropenia and recurrent infections?
options: Stop all RA therapy and observe without immunosuppression as initial therapy
Immediate splenectomy as first-line therapy for all patients as initial therapy
Optimize DMARD therapy to control RA, with G-CSF reserved for severe neutropenia with active infection
Begin long-term high-dose corticosteroids indefinitely as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In Felty syndrome, controlling the underlying RA with effective DMARD therapy (methotrexate is commonly used and can improve the neutropenia) is the mainstay, with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) reserved for severe neutropenia accompanied by serious or recurrent infection. Stopping all therapy (choice 0) would worsen the underlying immune-mediated process driving neutropenia. Splenectomy (choice 2) can transiently raise neutrophil counts but is not first-line for everyone—responses are inconsistent and it carries infection risk; it is reserved for refractory, severe cases. Indefinite high-dose steroids (choice 3) increase infection risk and offer no durable benefit. Addressing the disease with appropriate DMARDs, plus targeted G-CSF and vaccination/infection prophylaxis, best reduces her infection burden.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Beyond bacterial infections, patients with Felty syndrome carry an increased risk of which long-term malignancy?
options: Colorectal carcinoma
Non-Hodgkin lymphoma
Melanoma as the leading concern
Renal cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: Patients with Felty syndrome (and with severe long-standing RA generally) have an increased risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, reflecting chronic immune stimulation; this also underscores the importance of excluding LGL leukemia and monitoring for lymphoproliferative disease. Colorectal carcinoma (choice 1) is not specifically increased in Felty syndrome. Renal cell carcinoma (choice 2) and melanoma (choice 3) are not characteristically associated with this syndrome. Awareness of the elevated lymphoma risk informs follow-up: persistent lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, or progressive cytopenias should prompt evaluation for lymphoproliferative disease. This malignancy association is a high-yield distinguishing feature of Felty syndrome and severe seropositive RA.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond treating the neutropenia, which preventive measure is most important to reduce her infection risk?
options: Ensuring appropriate vaccinations
Routine prophylactic systemic antifungals for life regardless of counts
Strict bed rest and isolation indefinitely with appropriate follow-up
Avoiding all dental and skin care to prevent breaks in the skin
stages.explanation: Because infection is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in Felty syndrome, the most important preventive steps are ensuring appropriate vaccinations (e.g., pneumococcal and influenza, with attention to live-vaccine cautions on immunosuppression) and educating the patient to seek prompt evaluation and early treatment of any suspected infection, alongside controlling the underlying RA. Indefinite bed rest and isolation (choice 0) are impractical, harmful, and do not address the immune defect. Lifelong systemic antifungals irrespective of counts (choice 2) are not standard and risk toxicity and resistance; antimicrobial prophylaxis is individualized. Avoiding all dental and skin care (choice 3) is counterproductive—good hygiene and routine care reduce infection sources rather than increase them. Vaccination and vigilant early infection management offer the greatest protective benefit.

pearls: Felty syndrome triad = long-standing seropositive RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia, with recurrent infections from low neutrophils.
Large granular lymphocyte (LGL) leukemia is the major mimic and can coexist—exclude it with flow cytometry and T-cell clonality studies.
Treat by controlling RA with DMARDs (methotrexate often improves counts); reserve G-CSF for severe neutropenia with infection and splenectomy for refractory cases.
Felty syndrome carries an increased risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma—monitor for lymphadenopathy and B symptoms.

cases.title: Neck Pain and Clumsy Hands Before a Planned Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening neck pain with tingling and clumsiness of the hands, admitted before elective surgery.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman with a 30-year history of seropositive, erosive rheumatoid arthritis is admitted for preoperative evaluation before an elective total knee replacement. She reports several months of progressively severe neck pain radiating to the occiput, along with new tingling in both hands, dropping objects, and a sense of unsteadiness when walking. She also describes intermittent electric-shock sensations down her spine when she flexes her neck. Her RA has been longstanding and deforming despite treatment. Medications: methotrexate 20 mg weekly, folic acid, prednisone 5 mg daily, and a TNF inhibitor. No known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker and lives with her husband. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for occipital headaches, hand clumsiness, gait imbalance, and rare urinary urgency, but negative for bowel incontinence, recent trauma, fevers, and prior neck surgery. The anesthesia and surgical teams requested rheumatology and neurology input before proceeding to the operating room.
cases.exam: The patient holds her neck stiffly and has marked tenderness at the upper cervical spine with limited, painful range of motion. Neurologic exam reveals hyperreflexia in both lower extremities, bilateral positive Hoffman sign, and an upgoing plantar response (Babinski) on the right—signs of upper motor neuron involvement (myelopathy). There is mild reduction in fine finger dexterity and a subtle decrease in vibration sense in the feet. Gait is mildly spastic and unsteady. A Lhermitte phenomenon is reproduced on neck flexion. Peripheral joints show advanced RA deformities. There is no acute respiratory distress. Cranial nerve exam is normal, and there is no facial weakness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient with long-standing RA, neck pain, and these upper-motor-neuron signs, which complication is most concerning?
options: Cervical radiculopathy from a single disc herniation
Atlantoaxial subluxation causing cervical myelopathy
Carpal tunnel syndrome as the first step today
Tension headache as the first step today
stages.explanation: Long-standing RA characteristically erodes the transverse ligament and the odontoid (dens) at the C1–C2 (atlantoaxial) level, producing atlantoaxial subluxation; when the dens or subluxated vertebrae compress the cervical cord, patients develop myelopathy—exactly her picture of neck pain, hyperreflexia, positive Hoffman and Babinski signs, gait spasticity, and Lhermitte phenomenon. A single-level disc herniation causing radiculopathy (choice 0) yields dermatomal sensory loss and lower-motor-neuron signs, not diffuse upper-motor-neuron findings. Carpal tunnel syndrome (choice 2) causes median-nerve sensory symptoms in the hand without cord signs or gait change. Tension headache (choice 3) does not explain hyperreflexia, Babinski, or myelopathic gait. Recognizing atlantoaxial subluxation is critical, especially before intubation, which involves neck manipulation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study is the most appropriate initial test to evaluate for atlantoaxial instability in this preoperative patient?
options: Carotid ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
Lateral cervical spine radiographs in flexion
Plain chest radiograph in this clinical context
Lumbar spine MRI as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Lateral cervical spine radiographs in flexion and extension are the appropriate initial screen for atlantoaxial instability: they reveal dynamic widening of the atlanto-dental interval (anterior atlantoaxial subluxation) that may not be apparent on static films, which is essential to identify before neck manipulation during intubation. MRI is then obtained to assess cord compression and the cervicomedullary junction. A chest radiograph (choice 1) does not evaluate the cervical spine. Carotid ultrasound (choice 2) assesses vascular stenosis, irrelevant to mechanical instability. Lumbar spine MRI (choice 3) evaluates the wrong region—her upper-motor-neuron signs localize above the lumbar cord. Flexion-extension cervical films are specifically requested preoperatively to detect occult instability that could cause catastrophic cord injury during airway management.

stages.label: IMAGING RESULTS
stages.content:
Lateral cervical flexion radiograph: anterior atlanto-dental interval 8 mm (normal <3 mm in adults) — widens on flexion
Extension radiograph: partial reduction of the interval
Findings: anterior atlantoaxial subluxation with dynamic instability
Cervical MRI: increased T2 cord signal at C1–C2 with cord compression by the odontoid; reduced posterior atlanto-dental interval (space available for the cord)
No fracture
ESR: 54 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 2.4 mg/dL (ref <0.5)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given confirmed atlantoaxial subluxation with cord compression and myelopathy, what is the most appropriate course regarding her planned elective knee surgery and airway management?
options: Increase the TNF inhibitor dose to reverse the subluxation as the immediate next step
Cancel all surgery permanently and treat with a soft collar only as the immediate next step
Defer the elective surgery
Proceed with the elective knee surgery as scheduled using routine direct laryngoscopy in this setting
stages.explanation: With confirmed atlantoaxial subluxation, cord compression, and clinical myelopathy, the elective knee surgery should be deferred and the unstable cervical spine addressed first: obtain neurosurgical/spine surgical evaluation for possible decompression and C1–C2 fusion, and ensure cervical spine protection with careful airway management (e.g., awake fiberoptic intubation with in-line stabilization) for any anesthesia. Proceeding with routine direct laryngoscopy (choice 0) risks catastrophic cord injury during neck extension. Permanently canceling all surgery and using only a soft collar (choice 2) abandons treatable, progressive cord compression that may need surgical stabilization. Escalating the TNF inhibitor (choice 3) cannot reverse established mechanical subluxation and bony erosion. Protecting the cord and stabilizing the spine take priority over the elective procedure.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most important reason flexion-extension cervical films are routinely considered before surgery in patients with long-standing RA?
options: To evaluate the thyroid gland with appropriate follow-up
To assess for osteoporosis of the spine with appropriate follow-up
To detect occult atlantoaxial instability that could cause cord injury during intubation/positioning
To screen for cervical lymphadenopathy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The chief reason for preoperative flexion-extension cervical films in long-standing RA is to detect occult atlantoaxial (and subaxial) instability that might be asymptomatic yet pose a grave risk during the neck manipulation, extension, and positioning required for general anesthesia and intubation—identifying it allows protective airway strategies and spine precautions. Assessing osteoporosis (choice 1) is done with DEXA, not dynamic cervical films. Screening for lymphadenopathy (choice 2) is unrelated to spinal instability and uses different modalities. Evaluating the thyroid (choice 3) is irrelevant to cervical spine stability. Because cervical instability can be clinically silent until a precipitating event, this preoperative screening prevents devastating, avoidable spinal cord injury in a high-risk population.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over time, RA can erode the odontoid and lateral masses, allowing the dens to migrate upward through the foramen magnum. What is this dangerous form of cervical involvement called?
options: Spondylolisthesis as the leading concern
Subaxial subluxation as the leading concern
Klippel-Feil anomaly as the leading concern
Vertical subluxation with potential brainstem compression
stages.explanation: Vertical subluxation—also called cranial settling or atlantoaxial impaction—occurs when erosion of the C1 lateral masses and odontoid lets the dens migrate superiorly through the foramen magnum, risking compression of the brainstem and cervicomedullary junction; it is the most dangerous form of rheumatoid cervical disease and can cause sudden death. Subaxial subluxation (choice 0) is real in RA and occurs below C2, but it is not the upward dens migration described. Spondylolisthesis (choice 1) is forward slippage of one vertebra over another, typically lumbar and degenerative or isthmic, not this craniocervical process. Klippel-Feil anomaly (choice 2) is a congenital fusion of cervical vertebrae, unrelated to RA erosion. Recognizing cranial settling is critical because it mandates urgent neurosurgical assessment and meticulous airway protection.

cases.diagnosis: Atlantoaxial subluxation with cervical myelopathy complicating rheumatoid arthritis
pearls: Long-standing RA erodes the transverse ligament and odontoid at C1–C2, causing atlantoaxial subluxation that can compress the cervical cord.
Myelopathic signs (hyperreflexia, Hoffman, Babinski, spastic gait, Lhermitte) plus neck pain in chronic RA should prompt urgent cervical evaluation.
Lateral flexion-extension cervical radiographs detect dynamic instability (atlanto-dental interval >3 mm); MRI assesses cord compression and space available for the cord.
Before any anesthesia, screen for cervical instability and use careful airway technique (awake fiberoptic intubation, in-line stabilization) to prevent cord injury.

cases.title: Painful Leg Ulcers and a Foot That Won't Lift
cases.chiefComplaint: Non-healing painful leg ulcers and new weakness with numbness of one foot.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man with a 30-year history of erosive, high-titer seropositive rheumatoid arthritis is admitted for painful, non-healing skin ulcers on his legs and new neurologic symptoms. Over six weeks he developed deep, punched-out ulcers on the lower legs and small dark lesions at several fingertips. In the last two weeks he noticed the sudden onset of right foot drop and numbness over the dorsum of the right foot, followed days later by weakness and tingling in the left hand in an ulnar distribution—each deficit appearing abruptly in a different nerve territory. His RA has long been poorly controlled with persistent active disease and nodules. Medications: methotrexate 15 mg weekly, prednisone 7.5 mg daily, and intermittent NSAIDs. No known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (40 pack-years). Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, low-grade fevers, and 4 kg weight loss, but negative for hemoptysis, sinusitis, hematuria, and oral ulcers.
cases.exam: The patient appears unwell. The lower legs have several deep, sharply demarcated, painful ulcers with surrounding livedo; there are small, tender nailfold and digital pulp infarcts and splinter hemorrhages. Musculoskeletal exam shows advanced RA deformities with subcutaneous nodules over the elbows. Neurologic exam reveals a right foot drop with weak ankle dorsiflexion and sensory loss over the dorsum of the right foot (common peroneal distribution), plus weak left finger abduction and sensory loss over the fifth digit (ulnar distribution)—asymmetric deficits in named peripheral nerves consistent with mononeuritis multiplex. There is no scalp tenderness or jaw claudication. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable, and there is no palpable purpura on the trunk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The pattern of stepwise deficits in separate named nerves (right peroneal, then left ulnar) is best described as which entity?
options: Mononeuritis multiplex
Symmetric distal sensorimotor polyneuropathy
Guillain-Barré syndrome
Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome
stages.explanation: Sequential, asymmetric involvement of multiple individual named nerves (here the right common peroneal causing foot drop, then the left ulnar) is mononeuritis multiplex, a hallmark of vasculitic neuropathy from ischemic infarction of the vasa nervorum. Symmetric distal sensorimotor polyneuropathy (choice 0) produces a gradual, length-dependent 'stocking-glove' pattern, not abrupt single-nerve deficits. Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome (choice 2) is confined to the median nerve distribution and does not cause foot drop or systemic illness. Guillain-Barré syndrome (choice 3) is an ascending, typically symmetric areflexic weakness following infection, not a stepwise multifocal nerve pattern with ulcers and constitutional symptoms. In a patient with severe seropositive RA, mononeuritis multiplex with skin ulcers and nailfold infarcts strongly suggests rheumatoid vasculitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the ulcers, digital infarcts, and mononeuritis multiplex in long-standing seropositive RA, which diagnosis best unifies the findings, and what investigation would best confirm it?
options: Rheumatoid vasculitis
Diabetic neuropathy; confirm with HbA1c as the primary diagnosis
Septic emboli; confirm with blood cultures alone as the primary diagnosis
Cholesterol embolization; confirm with echocardiography as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The unifying diagnosis is rheumatoid vasculitis—a small/medium-vessel vasculitis complicating severe, long-standing, high-titer seropositive RA—producing cutaneous ulcers, nailfold/digital infarcts, and mononeuritis multiplex; biopsy of affected tissue (skin, sural nerve, or muscle) demonstrating vasculitis confirms it. Diabetic neuropathy (choice 0) causes symmetric distal polyneuropathy and would not explain ulcers with digital infarcts and an acute multifocal nerve pattern. Cholesterol embolization (choice 2) can cause livedo and digital ischemia but typically follows vascular instrumentation and does not produce mononeuritis multiplex with this RA context. Septic emboli (choice 3) from endocarditis can cause digital lesions, but blood cultures alone would not explain the neuropathy, and the chronic RA picture with nodules and high titers points to vasculitis. Tissue confirmation is the gold standard.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR: 96 mm/hr (ref <20)
CRP: 8.2 mg/dL (ref <0.5)
Rheumatoid factor: 480 IU/mL (very high titer)
Anti-CCP: strongly positive
ANA: positive (1:160)
ANCA (PR3/MPO): negative
Complement C3/C4: low
Cryoglobulins: negative
Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (normocytic)
Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL
Urinalysis: trace protein, no RBC casts
Hepatitis B/C, HIV: negative
Blood cultures x2: no growth
Nerve conduction studies: asymmetric axonal sensorimotor neuropathy affecting right peroneal and left ulnar nerves
Skin/nerve biopsy: necrotizing vasculitis of small and medium vessels with fibrinoid necrosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms necrotizing vasculitis. What is the most appropriate treatment for this severe, organ-threatening rheumatoid vasculitis?
options: Aspirin and clopidogrel as monotherapy
High-dose corticosteroids combined with a potent immunosuppressant such as cyclophosphamide or rituximab
Topical wound care alone as initial therapy
Discontinue all immunosuppression and observe as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Severe, organ-threatening rheumatoid vasculitis (mononeuritis multiplex, deep ulcers, digital infarcts) requires aggressive immunosuppression: high-dose corticosteroids combined with a potent agent such as cyclophosphamide or rituximab to induce remission and halt ischemic nerve and tissue damage. Topical wound care alone (choice 0) addresses the ulcers superficially but ignores the systemic necrotizing vasculitis driving them and the neuropathy. Antiplatelet therapy alone (choice 1) does not treat the underlying inflammatory vessel destruction. Discontinuing all immunosuppression (choice 3) would allow the vasculitis to progress and is dangerous. Because rheumatoid vasculitis carries significant morbidity and mortality, prompt induction immunosuppression is the standard, followed by maintenance therapy and optimization of RA control.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which baseline feature most strongly predisposes a patient to developing rheumatoid vasculitis?
options: Recently diagnosed, mild seronegative RA with appropriate follow-up
Long-standing, severe, erosive, high-titer seropositive RA with rheumatoid nodules
Female sex with well-controlled disease with appropriate follow-up
Use of folic acid supplementation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Rheumatoid vasculitis classically develops in patients with long-standing, severe, erosive, nodular, high-titer seropositive (RF and anti-CCP) RA—often in those with poorly controlled disease and frequently in men and smokers—matching this patient's profile. Recently diagnosed mild seronegative RA (choice 0) is at low risk; vasculitis is a late complication of aggressive seropositive disease. Female sex with well-controlled disease (choice 1) is not a strong risk factor; disease severity and seropositivity, not sex alone, drive risk. Folic acid (choice 2) is a supplement used with methotrexate and is unrelated to vasculitis risk. Interestingly, the incidence of rheumatoid vasculitis has declined in the era of effective DMARDs, reinforcing that good disease control is protective.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The ANCA is negative and hepatitis serologies are unremarkable. Why is this workup important before attributing his vasculitis to RA?
options: Hepatitis serologies are irrelevant to vasculitis as the immediate next step
A negative ANCA proves the diagnosis is rheumatoid vasculitis with certainty as the immediate next step
Cryoglobulin testing is never indicated in vasculitis as the immediate next step
Mononeuritis multiplex
stages.explanation: Mononeuritis multiplex with cutaneous vasculitis has a broad differential—ANCA-associated vasculitides (GPA, MPA, EGPA), polyarteritis nodosa, cryoglobulinemia, and hepatitis B/C-related vasculitis—so testing ANCA, hepatitis serologies, complement, and cryoglobulins is essential to exclude these mimics before attributing the picture to rheumatoid vasculitis, since management and associations differ (e.g., PAN linked to hepatitis B, cryoglobulinemia to hepatitis C). A negative ANCA (choice 0) does not by itself prove rheumatoid vasculitis; it simply makes ANCA-associated vasculitis less likely. Hepatitis serologies (choice 2) are highly relevant because hepatitis B is associated with PAN and hepatitis C with cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. Cryoglobulin testing (choice 3) is indeed indicated in this differential. The diagnosis of rheumatoid vasculitis rests on the clinical context, biopsy, and exclusion of mimics.

pearls: Rheumatoid vasculitis complicates long-standing, severe, erosive, high-titer seropositive RA with nodules—often in male smokers with poorly controlled disease.
Hallmarks include cutaneous ulcers, nailfold/digital infarcts, and mononeuritis multiplex (stepwise asymmetric deficits in named nerves).
Diagnosis is confirmed by tissue biopsy (skin, sural nerve, or muscle) showing necrotizing small/medium-vessel vasculitis with fibrinoid necrosis.
Organ-threatening disease is treated with high-dose corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab; its incidence has fallen with effective DMARD therapy.

cases.title: Years of Worsening Neck and Upper-Back Stiffness
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive thoracic spine stiffness and mild dysphagia over several years.
cases.history: A 69-year-old retired accountant presents with several years of gradually worsening stiffness across his neck and upper back. The stiffness is worst after prolonged inactivity but, unlike his wife's inflammatory arthritis, it eases only modestly with movement and is not associated with prolonged morning stiffness exceeding 30 minutes. He denies peripheral joint swelling, eye redness, psoriasis, or inflammatory bowel symptoms. Over the past year he has noticed mild difficulty swallowing solid food and an occasional sensation of food 'sticking' in his throat. He has no night pain that wakes him and no buttock or alternating hip pain. PMH: type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin 1000 mg BID), hypertension, obesity (BMI 34), and hyperlipidemia. Medications also include lisinopril and atorvastatin. No known drug allergies. Social: never-smoker, two beers on weekends, no illicit drugs. Family history: hypertension; no ankylosing spondylitis, no psoriasis. He was told years ago by another physician that he 'might have ankylosing spondylitis' but never had formal evaluation.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, obese man in no distress. Marked reduction in cervical and thoracic spine range of motion with limited extension and rotation. Chest expansion is preserved at 5 cm. There is no spinal tenderness to palpation and no paravertebral muscle spasm. Schober test is normal (>5 cm increase). Sacroiliac joint provocation maneuvers (FABER, sacroiliac compression) are negative and non-tender. Peripheral joints show no synovitis, effusion, or enthesitis at the Achilles or plantar fascia. No dactylitis, psoriatic plaques, or nail pitting. Neurologic exam is intact with normal strength and reflexes. Oropharynx unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most argues AGAINST inflammatory axial spondyloarthritis (ankylosing spondylitis) in this patient?
options: Stiffness worst after inactivity as the first step today
Reduced cervical and thoracic range of motion as the first step today
History of dysphagia as the first step today
Preserved chest expansion, normal Schober test,
stages.explanation: Preserved chest expansion, a normal modified Schober test, and non-tender sacroiliac joints in a 69-year-old strongly argue against ankylosing spondylitis (AS), which typically begins before age 45, restricts chest expansion, limits lumbar flexion, and involves the sacroiliac joints early. Reduced spinal motion (option A) is nonspecific and occurs in both AS and other causes of spinal rigidity. Inactivity-related stiffness (option C) overlaps between mechanical and inflammatory processes but lacks the prolonged (>30 min) inflammatory morning stiffness of AS. Dysphagia (option D) is not a feature of AS but does not by itself exclude it. The constellation pointing away from sacroiliitis and the older age of onset is the key discriminator.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Lateral cervical/thoracic spine radiograph:
Flowing anterior ossification bridging ≥ 4 contiguous vertebral bodies (T7–T11)
Disc spaces preserved without disc-space narrowing
No erosions; apophyseal (facet) joints not fused
Sacroiliac joints: normal, no erosion, no sclerosis, no ankylosis
Lumbar spine: large flowing right-sided osteophytes
Barium swallow: smooth anterior indentation of esophagus at cervical level from osteophytes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Ankylosing spondylitis in this clinical context
Degenerative spondylosis with isolated osteophytes
Osteitis condensans ilii in this clinical context
Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis
stages.explanation: Flowing anterior ossification (a 'dripping candle wax' appearance) bridging at least four contiguous vertebral bodies, with preserved disc spaces and absent sacroiliac involvement, defines diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis by the Resnick criteria. AS (option A) produces thin vertical syndesmophytes ('bamboo spine'), with disc-space narrowing, facet fusion, and bilateral sacroiliitis—all absent here. Degenerative spondylosis (option C) causes disc-space narrowing and non-bridging osteophytes, not flowing ossification across multiple levels with preserved discs. Osteitis condensans ilii (option D) is sclerosis of the iliac side of the SI joint, typically in younger multiparous women, and does not explain spinal bridging.

stages.label: ASSOCIATIONS
stages.question: Which condition is most strongly associated with DISH and should prompt evaluation in this patient?
options: HLA-B27 positivity as the immediate next step
Inflammatory bowel disease in this setting
Metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes mellitus
Anterior uveitis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: DISH is strongly associated with metabolic syndrome—type 2 diabetes, obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia—and hyperinsulinemia is thought to promote the ossifying diathesis. This patient already has diabetes, obesity, and hypertension, fitting the typical phenotype. IBD (option A) and anterior uveitis (option B) are extra-articular manifestations of spondyloarthritis, not DISH. HLA-B27 (option D) is the genetic marker of ankylosing spondylitis and is NOT associated with DISH; checking it would only reinforce the distinction. Recognizing the metabolic association guides cardiovascular risk-factor optimization, which is central to management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this patient's DISH?
options: Start a TNF-alpha inhibitor as initial therapy
Begin high-dose glucocorticoids as initial therapy
Symptomatic care with analgesia/NSAIDs, physical therapy,
Urgent spinal fusion surgery as initial therapy
stages.explanation: DISH has no disease-modifying therapy; management is symptomatic—analgesics or NSAIDs for pain, physical therapy to maintain mobility, and aggressive control of associated metabolic risk factors. Dysphagia from large cervical osteophytes is managed with dietary modification and, only if severe, surgical osteophytectomy. TNF inhibitors (option A) treat inflammatory spondyloarthritis, not the non-inflammatory ossification of DISH. Systemic glucocorticoids (option B) have no role and would worsen his diabetes and osteoporosis risk. Routine spinal fusion (option D) is not indicated; surgery is reserved for refractory dysphagia or unstable fractures. Patients with DISH are also at higher risk of unstable spine fractures from minor trauma, warranting caution.

pearls: DISH (Resnick criteria): flowing anterior ossification across ≥4 contiguous vertebrae with PRESERVED disc spaces and NO sacroiliac or facet joint involvement.
Unlike ankylosing spondylitis, DISH spares the SI joints, is HLA-B27 negative, lacks inflammatory back pain, and presents in older patients.
Strongly linked to metabolic syndrome—type 2 diabetes, obesity, dyslipidemia—so manage cardiovascular risk factors.
Large cervical osteophytes can cause dysphagia; the rigid ankylosed spine is prone to unstable fractures after even minor trauma.

cases.title: Sharp Chest Pain in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp left-sided anterior chest pain for two days.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with two days of sharp, localized pain over the left anterior chest. The pain worsens with deep inspiration, coughing, and twisting movements, and she can point to the painful spot with one finger. She recently recovered from an upper respiratory infection with a lingering cough and also moved into a new apartment last weekend, doing heavy lifting. The pain does not radiate to the arm or jaw, is not associated with exertion, and is not relieved or worsened by meals. She denies dyspnea at rest, palpitations, diaphoresis, fever, leg swelling, or calf pain. No recent long travel or immobilization. PMH: mild intermittent asthma (albuterol PRN), otherwise healthy. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill, albuterol inhaler PRN. No known drug allergies. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as a graphic designer, no illicit drug use. Family history: no premature coronary artery disease, no clotting disorders, no sudden cardiac death.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable woman in no acute distress. Reproducible point tenderness over the left 4th and 5th costochondral junctions; palpation faithfully reproduces her presenting pain. No swelling, warmth, erythema, or visible mass over the costochondral joints (distinguishing from Tietze syndrome). Lungs clear bilaterally with no wheeze or pleural rub. Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distention. Calves are nontender and symmetric with no edema or Homans sign. No chest-wall ecchymosis or deformity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate FIRST step in the emergency department for this patient?
options: Reassure and discharge with NSAIDs without any testing
Order an urgent CT pulmonary angiogram as the first step today
Obtain an ECG and assess for life-threatening causes of chest pain
Administer sublingual nitroglycerin as the first step today
stages.explanation: Even when the history strongly suggests a benign musculoskeletal cause, the first ED priority for any chest pain is to obtain an ECG and consider life-threatening etiologies (acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary embolism, pneumothorax, aortic dissection). Immediate discharge (option A) is premature before ruling out dangerous causes. A CT pulmonary angiogram (option C) is not warranted up front—her PE risk is low and should first be stratified (e.g., Wells/PERC) before imaging that carries radiation and contrast risk. Nitroglycerin (option D) is inappropriate empiric therapy and could cause hypotension; reproducible positional, palpation-dependent pain is not anginal.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, rate 82, no ST-segment or T-wave changes
High-sensitivity troponin: <6 ng/L (undetectable)
Wells score for PE: 0 points (PE unlikely)
D-dimer (sent given OCP use): 0.3 µg/mL (normal <0.5)
Chest radiograph: clear lungs, no pneumothorax, no infiltrate, normal cardiac silhouette
CBC and basic metabolic panel: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the negative cardiac and pulmonary workup, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute coronary syndrome
Pulmonary embolism
Costochondritis
Tietze syndrome
stages.explanation: Costochondritis is the most likely diagnosis: reproducible, palpation-induced pain at multiple costochondral junctions WITHOUT swelling, in a young patient after viral illness and heavy lifting, with a negative cardiac and pulmonary workup. Tietze syndrome (option B) is closely related but features visible, localized swelling of a single (often 2nd or 3rd) costochondral junction—absent here. ACS (option C) is excluded by a normal ECG, undetectable troponin, and non-exertional reproducible pain. PE (option D) is excluded by a low Wells score, normal D-dimer, and clear chest film; her pleuritic-type pain is explained by chest-wall inflammation, not embolism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for her costochondritis?
options: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Admit for serial troponins and stress testing as monotherapy
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation as initial therapy
Reassurance plus NSAIDs, activity modification,
stages.explanation: Costochondritis is self-limited and managed conservatively with reassurance, NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) for inflammation and pain, activity modification, and local heat; symptoms typically resolve over weeks. Outpatient follow-up ensures resolution and reassesses if atypical features emerge. Admission for serial troponins and stress testing (option A) is unnecessary given a single undetectable high-sensitivity troponin, normal ECG, and clearly benign reproducible mechanical pain. Antibiotics (option B) have no role—this is inflammatory, not infectious. Anticoagulation (option D) is unjustified after PE has been excluded and would expose her to bleeding risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding, if present at follow-up, would most warrant reconsidering the diagnosis and further workup?
options: Pain reproduced by palpation of the costochondral junctions
New exertional pain with diaphoresis radiating to the left arm
Gradual improvement with NSAIDs over two weeks
Mild residual tenderness on deep inspiration
stages.explanation: New exertional chest pain with diaphoresis and arm radiation is a red flag for cardiac ischemia and mandates urgent re-evaluation—these features are not explained by costochondritis. Reproducible palpation tenderness (option A) is the hallmark of costochondritis and is reassuring, not concerning. Gradual improvement with NSAIDs (option B) confirms the expected benign course. Mild residual tenderness on inspiration (option D) reflects ongoing but resolving chest-wall inflammation and is expected. Teaching patients return precautions for exertional, radiating, or diaphoretic symptoms is the key safety net when discharging a young patient with musculoskeletal chest pain.

pearls: Costochondritis is the most common cause of musculoskeletal chest pain: reproducible palpation tenderness at multiple costochondral junctions WITHOUT swelling.
Tietze syndrome differs by having visible localized swelling, usually at a single upper costochondral joint.
Always exclude life-threatening causes (ACS, PE, pneumothorax, dissection) with ECG and risk stratification before attributing chest pain to the chest wall.
Treatment is conservative—reassurance, NSAIDs, activity modification—and the course is self-limited over weeks.

cases.title: Swollen, Red Elbow in a Tradesman
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful, swollen, red right elbow for three days.
cases.history: A 47-year-old plumber presents with three days of increasing swelling, warmth, and redness over the tip of his right elbow. He frequently kneels and leans on his elbows while working under sinks and recalls a small abrasion over the elbow about a week ago.
