The swelling is now tense and tender, and the overlying skin is red and warm with spreading redness up the forearm. He reports subjective fevers and chills since yesterday. He denies preceding gout attacks, psoriasis, or tick exposure. The elbow joint itself can be flexed and extended with only mild discomfort, but direct pressure over the swelling is very painful. PMH: well-controlled type 2 diabetes (metformin), no prior gout. Medications: metformin 500 mg BID. Allergies: none. Social: works as a plumber with frequent repetitive elbow trauma; smokes half a pack daily; drinks beer on weekends. Family history: noncontributory. No recent antibiotics or hospitalizations.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing but nontoxic man. Right posterior elbow shows a tense, fluctuant, exquisitely tender swelling over the olecranon with overlying erythema, warmth, and induration extending several centimeters onto the proximal forearm (cellulitis). There is a healing superficial abrasion at the apex. Active and passive elbow range of motion is nearly full with only mild discomfort at end-range, and there is no pain on axial loading or with mid-range motion (arguing against true septic arthritis). No joint-line effusion of the elbow joint proper. Distal neurovascular exam intact; radial pulse 2+, capillary refill brisk. Epitrochlear lymphadenopathy present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes septic olecranon BURSITIS from septic ARTHRITIS of the elbow in this patient?
options: Overlying erythema and warmth as the first step today
Nearly full, only mildly painful elbow range of motion despite the swelling
Tenderness to palpation of the swelling as the first step today
Presence of fever and elevated heart rate as the first step today
stages.explanation: In septic bursitis the inflammation is confined to the superficial bursa, so the underlying joint moves nearly fully with only mild discomfort—preserved range of motion is the key discriminator. Septic arthritis, by contrast, causes severe pain with any joint motion and marked limitation. Fever and tachycardia (option A) occur in both and are nonspecific. Overlying erythema/warmth (option C) reflects the cellulitis and superficial bursal infection but does not localize to bursa versus joint. Tenderness of the swelling (option D) is expected in bursitis but also nonspecific. Demonstrating that the joint itself moves freely is what points to bursitis rather than intra-articular infection.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Empiric antibiotics and discharge without aspiration
Aspiration of the elbow JOINT space as the primary diagnosis
MRI of the elbow before any intervention in this clinical context
Bursal aspiration for cell count, Gram stain, crystals,
stages.explanation: Aspiration of the bursal fluid is the key diagnostic step: it provides cell count, Gram stain, crystal analysis, and culture to confirm infection and identify the organism, guiding targeted therapy. Empiric antibiotics without aspiration (option A) forfeits the chance to confirm the diagnosis and tailor treatment, and can lead to inadequately treated or recurrent infection. MRI (option C) is not needed for a clinically obvious superficial bursitis and would delay care. Aspirating the elbow JOINT (option D) is inappropriate when the joint is uninvolved—and passing a needle through overlying cellulitis into a joint risks seeding it; the superficial bursa is the target.

stages.label: ASPIRATE & LABS
stages.content:
Bursal aspirate: turbid, purulent fluid
Bursal fluid WBC: 28,000 /µL (predominantly neutrophils)
Bursal fluid Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Crystal analysis: no monosodium urate or calcium pyrophosphate crystals
Bursal fluid culture: pending (later grows Staphylococcus aureus, MSSA)
Serum WBC: 13.6 ×10³/µL
ESR: 48 mm/hr | CRP: 72 mg/L
Serum uric acid: 6.1 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given gram-positive cocci in clusters on the bursal aspirate, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Intrabursal glucocorticoid injection as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic ointment alone as initial therapy
Drainage/aspiration plus antistaphylococcal antibiotics covering S. aureus
Oral colchicine for presumed gout as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Septic olecranon bursitis from S. aureus is treated with bursal drainage (aspiration, sometimes repeated, or surgical incision and drainage if loculated) plus systemic antistaphylococcal antibiotics. Empiric coverage should include MRSA (e.g., vancomycin or TMP-SMX/doxycycline) pending susceptibilities, then narrow to an agent like cefazolin or dicloxacillin once MSSA is confirmed. Colchicine (option A) is wrong—crystal analysis was negative and the Gram stain confirms infection. Intrabursal glucocorticoid injection (option C) is contraindicated in active infection and would worsen it. Topical antibiotics (option D) are inadequate for a deep purulent bursal infection with surrounding cellulitis and systemic signs.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor in this patient most supports a lower threshold for admission and IV antibiotics rather than outpatient oral therapy?
options: Absence of crystals on aspirate with appropriate follow-up
Preserved elbow range of motion with appropriate follow-up
His occupation as a plumber with appropriate follow-up
Systemic signs with spreading cellulitis in a diabetic
stages.explanation: Systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia), spreading cellulitis, and diabetes mellitus (a comorbidity that impairs healing and raises complication risk) collectively support admission for IV antibiotics and close monitoring rather than outpatient oral therapy. His occupation (option A) explains the mechanism (repetitive elbow trauma) but does not dictate disposition. Preserved range of motion (option C) is reassuring that the joint is spared—it argues against, not for, escalation. Absence of crystals (option D) helps confirm infection over gout but does not influence the inpatient-versus-outpatient decision. Severity of systemic illness and host factors drive disposition.

pearls: Septic bursitis preserves joint range of motion; septic arthritis causes severe pain with any joint movement—this is the cardinal clinical distinction.
Aspirate the bursa for cell count, Gram stain, crystals, and culture; S. aureus is the most common pathogen, often after a skin breach in those with repetitive elbow trauma.
Treat with drainage plus antistaphylococcal antibiotics; cover MRSA empirically, then narrow once susceptibilities return.
Never inject glucocorticoids into an infected bursa, and avoid aspirating a joint through overlying cellulitis.

cases.title: New Cough and Breathlessness on a Rheumatology Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and low-grade fever over one week.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis is admitted with one week of progressive exertional dyspnea, nonproductive cough, and low-grade fevers. Six weeks ago her rheumatologist started oral methotrexate 15 mg weekly (with folic acid 1 mg daily) for active disease; she had previously been on hydroxychloroquine alone. She reports no chest pain, no leg swelling, no orthopnea, and no sick contacts. She has had no recent travel. Her RA joint symptoms had been improving. PMH: rheumatoid arthritis (RF and anti-CCP positive, no prior known lung disease), hypothyroidism (levothyroxine), GERD. Medications: methotrexate 15 mg weekly, folic acid 1 mg daily, hydroxychloroquine 200 mg BID, levothyroxine, omeprazole. Allergies: none. Social: never-smoker, no alcohol, retired teacher. Family history: noncontributory. Functional status was independent until this illness; over the past three days she has become breathless climbing a single flight of stairs.
cases.exam: Mildly tachypneic woman using accessory muscles at rest. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral fine inspiratory ('Velcro') crackles, worse at the bases, without wheeze or focal consolidation. No clubbing. Cardiac exam: tachycardic, regular, normal S1/S2, no murmurs or gallops, no elevated JVP, no peripheral edema (arguing against heart failure). Hands show symmetric MCP and PIP synovitis consistent with RA but no new acute monoarthritis. No oral ulcers, no rash. Calves nontender. Mild hypoxia worsens with ambulation (desaturation to 84%).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial set of investigations for this hypoxic patient?
options: Chest imaging , ABG, CBC,
Discharge with oral antibiotics for presumed bronchitis as the first step today
Increase the methotrexate dose to control a presumed RA flare as the first step today
Immediate bronchoscopy with biopsy before any imaging as the first step today
stages.explanation: A hypoxic patient (SpO2 88%) needs supplemental oxygen plus a structured workup: chest radiograph and, given suspicion for interstitial disease, high-resolution CT; arterial blood gas; CBC; and an infectious evaluation (the leading mimics are pneumonia and opportunistic infection, especially PCP given methotrexate immunosuppression). Discharge on oral antibiotics (option A) is unsafe with significant hypoxia and tachypnea. Bronchoscopy (option C) may follow but is not the FIRST step; noninvasive imaging and labs come first, and bronchoscopy is reserved to exclude infection if the picture remains unclear. Increasing methotrexate (option D) is dangerous—the drug itself is a prime suspect for the pulmonary process.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 12.6 g/dL, eosinophils mildly elevated
ABG (room air): pH 7.46, PaO2 56 mmHg, PaCO2 32 mmHg (hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis)
CRP 64 mg/L | ESR 70 mm/hr | Procalcitonin 0.1 ng/mL (low)
BNP 60 pg/mL (normal) | Troponin negative
Chest radiograph: bilateral diffuse interstitial and patchy alveolar infiltrates
HRCT chest: bilateral ground-glass opacities with scattered areas of consolidation, no honeycombing, no effusions
Blood cultures and respiratory viral panel: negative | Beta-D-glucan: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the clinical picture and workup?
options: Bacterial community-acquired pneumonia
Acute decompensated heart failure
Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia
Methotrexate-induced pneumonitis
stages.explanation: Subacute dyspnea, dry cough, fever, hypoxemia, and HRCT ground-glass opacities developing weeks after starting methotrexate—with a low procalcitonin, negative cultures, negative viral panel, negative beta-D-glucan, and peripheral eosinophilia—are characteristic of methotrexate-induced pneumonitis, a hypersensitivity-type reaction. Bacterial pneumonia (option A) is less likely with a low procalcitonin, negative cultures, and diffuse bilateral ground-glass rather than lobar consolidation. PCP (option B) is an important mimic in immunosuppressed patients but is made unlikely by a negative beta-D-glucan. Heart failure (option D) is excluded by a normal BNP, absent edema, and no JVD. Methotrexate pneumonitis is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion supported by this constellation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important management step?
options: Continue methotrexate and add an antibiotic as initial therapy
Immediately discontinue methotrexate
Switch methotrexate to a higher dose given orally as initial therapy
Start a TNF-alpha inhibitor as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of treatment is prompt and permanent discontinuation of methotrexate, plus supportive oxygen; systemic corticosteroids are commonly added for moderate-to-severe or progressive disease and often accelerate recovery. Empiric antibiotics (± coverage for PCP) are frequently given initially until infection is excluded, but continuing methotrexate (option A) is wrong because the drug is the driver. Increasing or re-dosing methotrexate (option C) would worsen the reaction—and rechallenge is contraindicated. Starting a TNF inhibitor (option D) does not treat methotrexate pneumonitis and adds further immunosuppression and infection risk during an acute respiratory illness. Stopping the offending drug is the decisive intervention.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After recovery, what is the appropriate long-term plan regarding methotrexate?
options: Resume the same methotrexate dose with prophylactic steroids
Avoid methotrexate permanently and select an alternative DMARD
Rechallenge with methotrexate at half the previous dose
Resume methotrexate only with weekly chest imaging
stages.explanation: Methotrexate pneumonitis is an idiosyncratic hypersensitivity reaction with risk of severe or fatal recurrence on re-exposure, so methotrexate should be avoided permanently and an alternative DMARD (e.g., leflunomide, sulfasalazine, or a biologic such as a TNF inhibitor once stable) chosen for her RA. Rechallenge at a lower dose (option A) still risks life-threatening recrudescence and is contraindicated. Resuming the same dose with prophylactic steroids (option C) does not reliably prevent recurrence and is not standard. Surveillance imaging while re-dosing (option D) does not make rechallenge safe. The teaching point is that recurrence can be worse than the index event—avoidance is mandatory.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which feature most reliably helped distinguish methotrexate pneumonitis from an infectious cause in this case?
options: Elevated ESR and CRP as the immediate next step
Bilateral crackles on auscultation as the immediate next step
Low procalcitonin with negative cultures, negative beta-D-glucan,
The presence of fever as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The combination of a low procalcitonin, negative bacterial cultures, negative beta-D-glucan (arguing strongly against PCP), and peripheral eosinophilia—arising weeks after methotrexate initiation—is what best separates a drug hypersensitivity pneumonitis from infection. Fever (option A), bilateral crackles (option C), and elevated ESR/CRP (option D) all occur in BOTH infection and methotrexate pneumonitis and are therefore nonspecific. Because methotrexate pneumonitis is a diagnosis of exclusion, the value lies in tests that actively lower the probability of infection while the temporal link and eosinophilia raise the probability of drug toxicity. Empiric anti-infective cover is still reasonable until cultures finalize.

pearls: Methotrexate pneumonitis is a hypersensitivity reaction presenting with subacute dyspnea, dry cough, fever, hypoxemia, and HRCT ground-glass opacities, often within weeks to months of starting therapy.
It is a diagnosis of exclusion—always rule out infection (especially PCP) given concurrent immunosuppression; a negative beta-D-glucan and low procalcitonin help.
Management is immediate, permanent discontinuation of methotrexate plus supportive oxygen, with corticosteroids for moderate-to-severe disease.
Never rechallenge—recurrence can be more severe or fatal; switch to an alternative DMARD.

cases.title: Gradual Vision Changes on a Long-Term Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Difficulty reading and noticing missing patches in central vision.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman with longstanding systemic lupus erythematosus presents for routine follow-up and mentions she has noticed difficulty reading fine print and intermittent 'missing spots' just off the center of her vision over the past few months. She has trouble seeing words while keeping her eyes fixed and reports colors appearing slightly washed out. She has taken hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily (about 6.5 mg/kg/day at her weight) for the last 8 years with good control of her lupus. She has not had a dilated eye exam in over four years. She denies eye pain, redness, flashes, floaters, or headache. PMH: SLE (skin and joint predominant), chronic kidney disease stage 2, hypertension. Medications: hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily, lisinopril, low-dose prednisone 5 mg daily. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as a librarian. Family history: mother with macular degeneration. She weighs 62 kg.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-appearing woman. Visual acuity mildly reduced bilaterally (20/30) with subjectively diminished color saturation. Pupils equal and reactive without afferent pupillary defect. Extraocular movements intact; no eye redness, discharge, or proptosis. Anterior segment unremarkable. Confrontation visual fields suggest subtle paracentral scotomata. Amsler grid testing reveals paracentral distortion/missing areas without central involvement. The remainder of the neurologic exam is normal. Skin shows healed discoid lesions; no active malar rash. Joints without active synovitis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which medication-related complication should be highest on the differential given her history?
options: Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy
Optic neuritis from lupus
Open-angle glaucoma in this clinical context
Corticosteroid-induced posterior subcapsular cataract
stages.explanation: Paracentral scotomata, reduced color vision, and reading difficulty after years of hydroxychloroquine—especially at a dose near or above 5 mg/kg/day and with chronic kidney disease (which reduces drug clearance)—point to hydroxychloroquine retinopathy. Steroid-induced cataract (option A) causes a gradual diffuse blur and glare, not focal paracentral scotomata. Lupus optic neuritis (option C) typically presents acutely with pain on eye movement and an afferent pupillary defect, which she lacks. Open-angle glaucoma (option D) causes peripheral (arcuate) field loss and elevated intraocular pressure, not central/paracentral maculopathy. The drug history, dose, renal disease, and pattern of vision loss all converge on antimalarial retinal toxicity.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate screening/diagnostic test to detect early hydroxychloroquine retinopathy?
options: Slit-lamp examination of the cornea
Gonioscopy as the primary diagnosis
Tonometry for intraocular pressure
Spectral-domain OCT and automated visual field testing
stages.explanation: Modern screening for hydroxychloroquine retinopathy relies on spectral-domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) and automated 10-2 visual field testing, supplemented by fundus autofluorescence and multifocal ERG; these detect early parafoveal photoreceptor and RPE damage before irreversible vision loss. Slit-lamp corneal exam (option A) detects corneal verticillata (drug deposits), which are reversible and do NOT predict retinopathy. Tonometry (option C) screens for glaucoma, not maculopathy. Gonioscopy (option D) evaluates the anterior chamber angle for glaucoma. Early detection matters because retinopathy can progress even after the drug is stopped, so sensitive macular and functional testing is essential.

stages.label: OPHTHALMOLOGY
stages.content:
Dilated fundus exam: subtle bilateral parafoveal RPE changes; early 'bull's-eye' maculopathy on the right
10-2 automated visual field: bilateral paracentral scotomata
Spectral-domain OCT: parafoveal loss of the photoreceptor ellipsoid zone with RPE thinning ('flying saucer' sign)
Fundus autofluorescence: parafoveal ring of increased autofluorescence
Calculated dose: 400 mg/day ≈ 6.5 mg/kg/day (above recommended ≤5 mg/kg/day)
Cumulative use: ~8 years

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given confirmed early hydroxychloroquine retinopathy, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue the same dose and repeat screening in one year as initial therapy
Add an antioxidant vitamin to reverse the damage as initial therapy
Reduce to 200 mg daily and continue indefinitely without further screening
Discontinue hydroxychloroquine
stages.explanation: Once definite retinopathy is identified, hydroxychloroquine should be discontinued (in coordination with rheumatology to substitute alternative lupus control), because continued use risks progression to central, irreversible vision loss. Continuing the same dose (option A) is clearly unsafe given documented toxicity and supratherapeutic dosing. Simply lowering the dose without stopping and without continued monitoring (option C) is inadequate once toxicity is established, and abandoning surveillance is wrong because damage can progress even after cessation. Antioxidant vitamins (option D) do not reverse retinal toxicity. The damage is largely irreversible, underscoring why guideline-based screening to catch it early—and prompt cessation—are paramount.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which strategy best reduces the risk of hydroxychloroquine retinopathy in patients on long-term therapy?
options: Screening only if the patient reports symptoms as the immediate next step
Keeping dose ≤5 mg/kg actual body weight, baseline exam within the first year,
Routine corneal photography every visit as the immediate next step
Dosing by ideal body weight at >6.5 mg/kg/day as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Risk is minimized by limiting the dose to ≤5 mg/kg actual body weight per day, performing a baseline fundus exam within the first year of therapy, and beginning annual screening after 5 years—earlier and more frequently with risk factors such as renal impairment, high dose, or concurrent tamoxifen. Dosing above 6.5 mg/kg (option A) markedly increases toxicity risk. Screening only when symptomatic (option C) defeats the purpose, since early retinopathy is asymptomatic yet detectable and the goal is to intervene before central vision is lost. Corneal photography (option D) tracks reversible corneal deposits unrelated to sight-threatening retinopathy. This patient's high mg/kg dose and CKD were modifiable risk factors.

pearls: Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy causes paracentral scotomata and a 'bull's-eye' maculopathy; major risk factors are dose >5 mg/kg/day, duration >5 years, renal disease, and tamoxifen use.
Screen with SD-OCT and 10-2 visual fields (plus FAF and mfERG)—corneal verticillata seen on slit lamp are reversible and do NOT predict retinopathy.
Baseline eye exam within the first year, then annual screening after 5 years (sooner with risk factors).
Retinal damage is largely irreversible and can progress after stopping the drug, so prompt discontinuation once toxicity is detected is essential.

cases.title: Sudden Mid-Back Pain in a Woman on Long-Term Steroids
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute mid-back pain after bending to lift a laundry basket.
cases.history: A 64-year-old woman with polymyalgia rheumatica presents with sudden, sharp mid-thoracic back pain that began two days ago while bending to lift a laundry basket. The pain is worse with movement, coughing, and standing, and partially relieved by lying flat. She has lost about 3 cm in height over the last two years and notes a developing stooped posture. She has been on prednisone for 14 months, currently 10 mg daily, with prior periods at higher doses, and has never received bone-protective therapy or had a DXA scan. She denies fever, weight loss, bowel or bladder dysfunction, leg weakness, or numbness. PMH: polymyalgia rheumatica, hypertension, GERD, early menopause at age 46. Medications: prednisone 10 mg daily, amlodipine, omeprazole; takes no calcium or vitamin D. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), minimal alcohol, sedentary. Family history: mother had a 'dowager's hump' and hip fracture.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable woman who moves cautiously. Focal midline tenderness to percussion over the lower thoracic spine (approximately T11–T12) with associated paraspinal muscle spasm. Thoracic kyphosis is increased. There is no step-off deformity appreciated and no overlying skin changes. Neurologic exam of the lower extremities is normal: 5/5 strength, intact sensation, symmetric 2+ reflexes, downgoing toes, and normal perianal sensation and rectal tone (no cord compression). Straight-leg raise negative. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Cushingoid features noted: moon facies, central adiposity, and thin skin with easy bruising.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging to evaluate her acute focal back pain?
options: Non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis
Nuclear medicine bone scan
MRI of the lumbar spine with contrast
Plain radiographs of the thoracolumbar spine
stages.explanation: Plain thoracolumbar radiographs are the appropriate first-line imaging for suspected vertebral compression fracture—they are inexpensive, readily available, and demonstrate vertebral body height loss and wedging. MRI (option A) is reserved for red flags (neurologic deficit, suspected malignancy or infection, or to assess fracture acuity/cord compression), none of which are present given her normal neuro exam. A bone scan (option C) is nonspecific and slower, used mainly when fracture age is uncertain or to survey for metastases. CT abdomen/pelvis (option D) is not the test for a vertebral fracture and adds radiation without targeting the spine. Start simple and escalate only if red flags emerge.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Thoracolumbar radiograph: anterior wedge compression fracture of T12 with ~35% vertebral body height loss; diffuse osteopenia
No posterior cortical retropulsion; alignment otherwise maintained
DXA: lumbar spine T-score −2.9; total hip T-score −2.4 (osteoporosis)
Serum calcium 9.4 mg/dL | Phosphate 3.4 mg/dL | Albumin 4.0 g/dL
25-OH vitamin D: 18 ng/mL (low) | PTH 42 pg/mL (normal)
TSH normal | SPEP negative | CBC normal | ESR 22 mm/hr

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis underlying this fracture?
options: Primary hyperparathyroidism
Metastatic bone disease
Multiple myeloma
Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis
stages.explanation: A low-trauma vertebral compression fracture in a postmenopausal woman on chronic prednisone, with a DXA showing osteoporosis and a benign metabolic panel, is most consistent with glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis—the most common cause of secondary osteoporosis. Glucocorticoids reduce bone formation, increase resorption, and impair calcium balance, with fracture risk rising early and at relatively preserved BMD. Metastatic disease (option A) is less likely with no malignancy history, no posterior element involvement, and a normal exam, though imaging features should be reviewed. Myeloma (option B) is argued against by a negative SPEP and normal CBC/calcium. Primary hyperparathyroidism (option D) is excluded by normal calcium and PTH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In addition to calcium and vitamin D repletion and pain control, what is the most appropriate pharmacologic step?
options: Begin a thiazide diuretic for bone protection as initial therapy
Initiate testosterone therapy as initial therapy
Start an oral bisphosphonate as first-line anti-fracture therapy
Defer all therapy and repeat DXA in 2 years as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis with a fragility fracture and a T-score in the osteoporotic range, an oral bisphosphonate such as alendronate is first-line anti-fracture therapy, alongside calcium/vitamin D repletion (her vitamin D is low) and using the lowest effective glucocorticoid dose. Higher-risk patients may warrant teriparatide or denosumab. A thiazide (option B) reduces urinary calcium but is not anti-osteoporosis therapy and would not prevent fractures here. Testosterone (option C) is irrelevant in a woman. Deferring therapy (option D) is inappropriate after an established fragility fracture, which itself defines clinical osteoporosis and high imminent re-fracture risk—treatment should not wait.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: For patients starting long-term glucocorticoids, when should bone-protective measures be considered?
options: Only after a fracture occurs as the immediate next step
Early—assess fracture risk
Never, because the benefit is minimal as the immediate next step
Only if the daily dose exceeds 30 mg of prednisone as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend assessing fracture risk at the START of any glucocorticoid course expected to last ≥3 months and ensuring adequate calcium and vitamin D, with bisphosphonate therapy added for moderate-to-high-risk adults even at doses as low as 2.5–7.5 mg/day, because bone loss and fracture risk rise early in treatment. Waiting for a fracture (option A) misses the prevention window—exactly what happened here. There is no 30 mg threshold (option B); risk accrues at much lower doses. Saying the benefit is minimal (option D) is incorrect; prophylaxis meaningfully reduces vertebral fractures. Early risk stratification and prophylaxis are the teaching point.

cases.diagnosis: Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis with vertebral compression fracture
pearls: Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is the most common secondary osteoporosis; fracture risk rises early and at higher BMD than in postmenopausal osteoporosis.
Assess fracture risk and start calcium/vitamin D at the outset of glucocorticoid therapy expected to last ≥3 months; add a bisphosphonate for moderate-to-high-risk patients.
A fragility vertebral fracture defines clinical osteoporosis—treat regardless of T-score and do not defer therapy.
Always use the lowest effective glucocorticoid dose and repigment vitamin D deficiency before/while starting antiresorptives.

cases.title: Diffuse Rash and Fever Weeks After a New Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Spreading rash, fever, and facial swelling for several days.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man is admitted with five days of progressively spreading rash, daily fevers, and facial puffiness. Symptoms began about four weeks after his primary physician started allopurinol 300 mg daily for recurrent gout and mild chronic kidney disease. The rash started on the trunk and spread to the limbs and face, becoming confluent and slightly itchy; he reports malaise and decreased urine output. He denies new gout flares, recent infections, or other new medications besides allopurinol. He was not screened for HLA-B*5801 before starting. PMH: gout, chronic kidney disease stage 3 (baseline creatinine ~1.5 mg/dL), hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Medications: allopurinol 300 mg daily (recently started), losartan, metformin, amlodipine, hydrochlorothiazide. Allergies: none previously known. Social: of Han Chinese descent, retired, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol. Family history: gout in father. He has had no mucosal blistering but notes a sore throat and 'swollen glands.'
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, flushed man. Diffuse confluent morbilliform erythematous rash covering >50% body surface area with areas of edematous, infiltrated plaques; marked facial edema. No Nikolsky sign, no mucosal erosions or target lesions, no skin detachment. Bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy is present, and the liver edge is palpable and mildly tender. Lungs clear; heart tachycardic and regular. No joint effusions or active synovitis. Lower-extremity exam shows trace edema. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the timing and presentation, which diagnosis should be at the top of the differential?
options: Simple morbilliform drug eruption as the first step today
Acute viral exanthem as the first step today
Stevens–Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis as the first step today
Drug reaction with eosinophilia
stages.explanation: A confluent rash with facial edema, fever, lymphadenopathy, and organ involvement appearing 2–6 weeks after starting allopurinol is classic for DRESS (allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome). The delayed onset, systemic features (fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatic and renal involvement), and facial edema distinguish it. A simple morbilliform eruption (option A) lacks systemic organ involvement, facial edema, and eosinophilia. SJS/TEN (option C) features mucosal erosions, target lesions, a positive Nikolsky sign, and skin detachment—all absent here. A viral exanthem (option D) does not explain the strong temporal link to a high-risk drug with multiorgan involvement. Recognizing DRESS early is critical because it can be fatal.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 14.8 ×10³/µL with eosinophils 1,800 /µL (marked eosinophilia)
Atypical lymphocytes present on smear
Creatinine 3.2 mg/dL (up from baseline 1.5) — acute kidney injury
AST 210 U/L | ALT 245 U/L | ALP 160 U/L | total bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL
Uric acid 7.8 mg/dL
HLA-B*5801: positive (sent during admission)
Viral serologies pending; HHV-6 reactivation studies ordered
Urinalysis: mild proteinuria, few WBCs, no casts

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of findings best confirms the diagnosis of DRESS in this patient?
options: Mucosal ulceration with skin sloughing as the primary diagnosis
Rash, fever, eosinophilia, atypical lymphocytes,
Isolated rash with normal labs as the primary diagnosis
Positive blood cultures with leukocytosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: DRESS is supported by the RegiSCAR-type constellation: a delayed extensive cutaneous eruption, fever, peripheral eosinophilia (and/or atypical lymphocytes), lymphadenopathy, and internal organ involvement—here hepatitis (transaminitis) and acute kidney injury—temporally linked to allopurinol. An isolated rash with normal labs (option A) would be a simple drug eruption, not DRESS. Mucosal ulceration with sloughing (option C) defines SJS/TEN, a different severe cutaneous reaction. Positive blood cultures (option D) point to sepsis, not a drug hypersensitivity; this patient's picture is driven by an eosinophilic, multiorgan hypersensitivity reaction. The eosinophilia plus organ involvement is the diagnostic core.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important immediate management step?
options: Continue allopurinol and add an antihistamine as initial therapy
Immediately discontinue allopurinol
Start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics as sole therapy as initial therapy
Switch to a higher dose of allopurinol as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone is prompt discontinuation of the culprit drug—allopurinol—plus supportive care and systemic corticosteroids when there is significant organ involvement (his hepatitis and AKI qualify). Fluids, electrolyte management, and monitoring for evolving organ dysfunction are essential, and HHV-6 reactivation should be considered. Continuing allopurinol (option A) perpetuates a potentially fatal reaction. Increasing the dose (option C) is dangerous and contraindicated. Antibiotics alone (option D) do not treat a drug hypersensitivity and risk adding another sensitizing agent; they are reserved for proven superinfection. Stopping the offending drug is the single most important action.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which strategy could have reduced this patient's risk of allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome?
options: Giving prophylactic antihistamines as the immediate next step
Co-prescribing a diuretic as the immediate next step
Screening for HLA-B*5801 in high-risk populations
Starting allopurinol at the full 300 mg dose immediately as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Risk reduction includes screening for HLA-B*5801 in higher-prevalence populations (e.g., Han Chinese, Korean, Thai descent—this patient is of Han Chinese descent), avoiding allopurinol in carriers, and initiating allopurinol at a LOW dose with gradual titration, particularly in chronic kidney disease where higher starting doses raise hypersensitivity risk. Starting at full dose (option A) is precisely a major risk factor, compounded by his CKD. A diuretic (option B here, option 'co-prescribing a diuretic') does not prevent the reaction and thiazides may even contribute to hyperuricemia. Prophylactic antihistamines (option D) do not prevent this T-cell–mediated severe hypersensitivity. Genetic screening plus cautious renal-adjusted dosing is the preventive standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Weeks after recovery, which long-term consideration is most important for this patient?
options: Immediate restart of allopurinol at a lower dose as the leading concern
Rechallenge with allopurinol once labs normalize as the leading concern
No further follow-up is needed once the rash resolves as the leading concern
Permanent avoidance of allopurinol, monitoring for late autoimmune sequelae ,
stages.explanation: After DRESS, the culprit drug must be permanently avoided—rechallenge can cause rapid, severe recurrence—so allopurinol is contraindicated for life. Patients also need monitoring for delayed autoimmune sequelae (such as autoimmune thyroiditis and, less commonly, diabetes) that can emerge weeks to months later. For ongoing gout/hyperuricemia, alternatives (with caution and specialist input) may be considered, weighing cross-reactivity. Rechallenge or low-dose restart (options A and 2) are dangerous and contraindicated. Stopping follow-up (option D) is wrong given the risk of late autoimmune complications and the need to manage his gout safely without allopurinol.

pearls: Allopurinol DRESS presents 2–6 weeks after initiation with rash, fever, facial edema, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia/atypical lymphocytes, and multiorgan involvement (hepatitis, AKI).
HLA-B*5801 strongly predicts allopurinol hypersensitivity—screen high-risk populations (Han Chinese, Korean, Thai) and avoid the drug in carriers.
Start allopurinol low and titrate slowly, dose-adjusted for renal function, since high starting doses and CKD increase risk.
Management is immediate, permanent drug discontinuation plus supportive care and corticosteroids for organ involvement; watch for late autoimmune sequelae like thyroiditis.

cases.title: Chronic Pain in a Replaced Knee
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of progressive pain and stiffness in a previously replaced knee.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman is admitted for evaluation of several months of progressive pain in her right knee, which underwent total knee arthroplasty three years ago. The pain is now constant, worse with weight-bearing, and associated with a sensation that the knee 'gives way.' She has had no acute injury. There were no dramatic systemic symptoms—only intermittent low-grade malaise and occasional night discomfort—so the problem was initially attributed to mechanical loosening. She had transient bacteremia from a dental procedure last year. She denies high fevers, rigors, or a draining sinus. PMH: osteoarthritis, hypertension, hypothyroidism, osteoporosis on alendronate. Medications: levothyroxine, amlodipine, alendronate, acetaminophen PRN. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, nonsmoker, rare alcohol, lives independently. Family history: noncontributory. She reports the knee has felt 'not right' since within the first year after surgery, with low-grade aching that never fully resolved.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest but antalgic gait. Right knee with a mild effusion, slight warmth, and diffuse tenderness; range of motion limited by pain to 10–90 degrees. No erythema, no draining sinus tract, and no overlying skin breakdown. There is pain on rotation and a subtle sense of instability with stress testing. The surgical scar is well healed. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. Distal pulses intact, sensation normal. The contralateral knee and other joints are unremarkable. No other source of infection identified on general exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient with chronic pain in a prosthetic knee and signs of loosening, what is the most important diagnosis to exclude before attributing symptoms to aseptic loosening?
options: Patellar tendinopathy
Prosthetic joint infection
Gout in the prosthetic joint
Referred pain from the hip
stages.explanation: Prosthetic joint infection (PJI) must always be excluded before diagnosing aseptic loosening, because chronic, low-grade ('indolent') PJI—often from low-virulence organisms like Cutibacterium acnes or coagulase-negative staphylococci—mimics mechanical loosening and frequently presents only with persistent pain and radiographic loosening, without dramatic systemic signs. Missing it leads to failed revision and recurrent infection. Gout in a prosthesis (option A) is rare and would not explain progressive loosening. Patellar tendinopathy (option C) causes localized anterior pain, not effusion with instability. Referred hip pain (option D) would not produce a knee effusion or warmth. Ruling out infection drives all subsequent management.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to evaluate for prosthetic joint infection?
options: Inflammatory markers followed by image-guided joint aspiration for synovial cell count, differential,
Immediate one-stage revision without sampling as the primary diagnosis
Empiric oral antibiotics for two weeks and reassess as the primary diagnosis
MRI of the knee alone as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The standard workup begins with inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP), which are sensitive screens, followed by joint aspiration for synovial fluid WBC count and differential plus culture—ideally with antibiotics withheld to maximize culture yield, and with prolonged incubation to detect slow-growing Cutibacterium. Empiric antibiotics first (option A) sabotage culture yield and delay definitive diagnosis. Proceeding to revision without sampling (option C) risks operating on an unrecognized infection and choosing the wrong surgical strategy. MRI alone (option D) is degraded by metal artifact and cannot confirm infection. Tissue/fluid microbiology, especially with extended cultures, is essential for low-virulence organisms.

stages.label: LABS & ASPIRATION
stages.content:
ESR 52 mm/hr | CRP 38 mg/L (both elevated)
Plain radiograph: periprosthetic lucency and component loosening at the tibial baseplate
Synovial fluid WBC: 4,500 /µL with 78% neutrophils (elevated for a chronic prosthetic joint)
Synovial fluid crystals: none
Synovial fluid alpha-defensin: positive
Cultures held in extended incubation: at day 9, growth of Cutibacterium (Propionibacterium) acnes
Blood cultures: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How does the workup support chronic prosthetic joint infection rather than aseptic loosening?
options: Negative blood cultures rule out infection as the primary diagnosis
Elevated ESR/CRP, an elevated synovial neutrophil percentage, positive alpha-defensin,
Crystal-negative fluid confirms aseptic loosening as the primary diagnosis
Radiographic loosening alone proves the cause is mechanical as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Chronic PJI is supported by elevated ESR and CRP, a raised synovial neutrophil percentage (thresholds for chronic PJI are lower than for native septic arthritis), a positive alpha-defensin biomarker, and—diagnostically—delayed growth of Cutibacterium acnes on prolonged culture, a classic indolent shoulder/knee prosthesis pathogen. Negative blood cultures (option A) do not exclude a localized prosthetic infection. Crystal-negative fluid (option C) only excludes crystal arthropathy, not infection. Radiographic loosening alone (option D) cannot distinguish septic from aseptic loosening—the very pitfall this case illustrates. The composite microbiologic and biomarker data confirm infection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management for chronic prosthetic joint infection with a loose implant?
options: Surgical removal of the infected prosthesis plus prolonged organism-directed antibiotics
Repeat aspiration and observation as initial therapy
Lifelong oral antibiotic suppression alone as initial therapy
Arthroscopic washout with retention of the loose implant as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Chronic PJI with a loose prosthesis requires surgical removal of the infected hardware—most often a two-stage exchange (resection with antibiotic spacer, then reimplantation)—combined with prolonged organism-directed antibiotics, because biofilm on the implant cannot be eradicated by antibiotics alone. Suppression alone (option A) is reserved for patients who cannot undergo surgery and does not cure infection. Debridement with implant RETENTION (option C) is only considered for acute infections with a well-fixed implant—not a chronic infection with a loose component. Repeat aspiration and observation (option D) ignores a confirmed infection and risks ongoing bone destruction. Source control via hardware removal is essential.

pearls: Always exclude infection before calling a painful, loosening prosthesis 'aseptic'—indolent PJI from low-virulence organisms (Cutibacterium acnes, coagulase-negative staph) mimics mechanical loosening.
Workup: ESR/CRP, then aspiration for synovial WBC/differential and culture (hold antibiotics); use prolonged incubation and alpha-defensin to catch slow-growers.
Chronic PJI thresholds for synovial WBC and neutrophil percentage are lower than for native septic arthritis.
Definitive treatment of chronic PJI with a loose implant is hardware removal (often two-stage exchange) plus prolonged organism-directed antibiotics—biofilm defeats antibiotics alone.

cases.title: Months of Worsening Back Pain in a Recent Immigrant
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive lower thoracic back pain with night sweats and weight loss.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man who immigrated two years ago is admitted for several months of progressive, dull lower thoracic back pain that is now constant and wakes him at night. He reports drenching night sweats, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and about 6 kg of unintentional weight loss. He has noticed a slowly enlarging tender swelling in his right flank. He recalls a household contact with active tuberculosis in childhood and had a positive skin test years ago but never completed treatment. He denies acute trauma, bowel/bladder incontinence, or leg weakness, though he has mild numbness in both feet. PMH: latent TB (untreated), otherwise healthy. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: works in construction, lives in crowded housing, nonsmoker, no IV drug use, no alcohol. He has not been tested for HIV. Family history: noncontributory. The pain has been attributed to 'muscle strain' by two prior clinicians and treated with NSAIDs without benefit.
cases.exam: Thin, chronically ill-appearing man. Focal tenderness and a gibbus (angular kyphotic) deformity over the lower thoracic spine with paraspinal fullness. A fluctuant, nontender to mildly tender swelling tracks into the right flank/psoas region (suggesting a cold abscess). Lower-extremity strength is 5/5 proximally but with subtle bilateral 4+/5 ankle weakness; sensation mildly reduced in a stocking distribution; reflexes brisk at the knees with bilateral sustained ankle clonus and equivocal plantar responses (early myelopathy). Perianal sensation and rectal tone intact. Lungs clear; no lymphadenopathy. No overlying skin erythema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given chronic back pain with constitutional symptoms, a gibbus deformity, and a tracking abscess, which diagnosis is most likely?
options: Metastatic spinal tumor in this clinical context
Mechanical degenerative disc disease
Tuberculous spondylitis (Pott disease)
Pyogenic (bacterial) vertebral osteomyelitis
stages.explanation: Chronic insidious back pain over months with night sweats, weight loss, a gibbus (angular kyphotic) deformity, and a cold abscess tracking along the psoas in a patient with prior TB exposure is the classic picture of tuberculous spondylitis (Pott disease). TB characteristically causes anterior vertebral body destruction with relative disc sparing early, leading to wedging and gibbus. Pyogenic osteomyelitis (option A) is usually more acute with higher fevers and earlier disc destruction. Metastatic tumor (option C) is possible but less consistent with a tracking cold abscess and the exposure history. Degenerative disease (option D) does not cause constitutional symptoms, abscess, or vertebral destruction.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: MRI of the spine and image-guided biopsy/aspiration for histology, AFB stain, mycobacterial culture, and nucleic acid amplification testing
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for pyogenic osteomyelitis as the primary diagnosis
Start empiric four-drug anti-tuberculous therapy without sampling as the primary diagnosis
Plain radiographs alone as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: MRI is the best imaging to define the extent of vertebral and paraspinal/epidural disease and cord compression, and CT-guided biopsy or abscess aspiration provides tissue for histology (caseating granulomas), AFB smear, mycobacterial culture with drug susceptibility, and nucleic acid amplification testing (e.g., Xpert MTB/RIF)—essential to confirm TB and detect resistance. Empiric anti-TB therapy without sampling (option A) risks missing drug resistance and alternative diagnoses (tumor, pyogenic, fungal). Plain films alone (option C) underestimate disease and cannot confirm the organism. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (option D) target the wrong pathogen. Obtaining microbiologic and histologic confirmation, ideally before treatment, guides drug-resistant management.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI thoracic spine: destruction of T10–T11 vertebral bodies with anterior wedging and relative early disc preservation; large prevertebral and bilateral psoas collections (cold abscesses); epidural extension with mild cord compression
CT-guided biopsy: caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells
AFB smear: positive; Xpert MTB/RIF: Mycobacterium tuberculosis detected, rifampin resistance NOT detected
ESR 88 mm/hr | CRP 46 mg/L
HIV: negative | Chest radiograph: old apical scarring, no active cavity
Mycobacterial culture: pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the mainstay of treatment for confirmed tuberculous spondylitis without significant cord compromise requiring urgent surgery?
options: Immediate spinal fusion as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Short-course (2-week) antibiotics as initial therapy
Prolonged multidrug anti-tuberculous therapy , typically for a longer total course than pulmonary TB
Glucocorticoids alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The mainstay of Pott disease is prolonged multidrug anti-tuberculous therapy—an intensive phase of rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol followed by a continuation phase—generally for a longer total duration (commonly 9–12 months, individualized) than uncomplicated pulmonary TB. A 2-week antibiotic course (option A) is wholly inadequate for mycobacterial disease. Glucocorticoids alone (option C) do not treat TB and would worsen it without antimycobacterials. Immediate fusion (option D) is not first-line; surgery is reserved for significant neurologic deficit/cord compression, spinal instability, large abscesses not responding to medical therapy, or failure of drug treatment. Medical therapy is the backbone, with surgery for specific indications.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding in this patient would most strongly indicate the need for urgent surgical intervention?
options: Presence of a psoas cold abscess that is stable in this presentation
Elevated ESR as the leading concern
Progressive lower-extremity weakness with worsening cord compression
A positive AFB smear as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Progressive neurologic deficit—worsening motor weakness, sensory level, or new bladder/bowel dysfunction—from epidural extension and cord compression is the strongest indication for urgent surgical decompression and stabilization, as delay risks permanent paraplegia. An elevated ESR (option A) reflects inflammation but does not by itself mandate surgery. A stable psoas cold abscess (option C) can often be managed with drainage and medical therapy without urgent decompression. A positive AFB smear (option D) confirms the diagnosis but is not a surgical indication. Neurologic deterioration and spinal instability are the classic triggers for operative management in Pott disease.

pearls: Pott disease presents as chronic insidious back pain with constitutional symptoms, a gibbus deformity, and cold (psoas/paraspinal) abscesses; TB spares the disc relatively early and destroys the anterior vertebral body.

Confirm with MRI plus image-guided biopsy/aspiration for histology (caseating granulomas), AFB smear/culture, and nucleic acid amplification (Xpert MTB/RIF) to detect drug resistance before treatment when possible.
Treat with prolonged multidrug anti-TB therapy (RIPE then continuation), usually 9–12 months—longer than uncomplicated pulmonary TB.
Reserve surgery for progressive neurologic deficit/cord compression, instability, or large/refractory abscesses; isolated abscess or elevated markers alone are not surgical indications.

cases.title: Recurrent Bloody Swelling of One Knee
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent painful swelling of the right knee over more than a year.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man presents with more than a year of recurrent right knee swelling, pain, and a sensation of catching. The swelling builds over days, is sometimes accompanied by warmth, and has been aspirated twice elsewhere—both times yielding blood-tinged (serosanguineous) fluid—with temporary relief before recurrence. He recalls no significant trauma and no recurrent bleeding elsewhere (no nosebleeds, easy bruising, or hemarthroses in other joints). He has no fevers, rash, psoriasis, morning stiffness, or other joint involvement. The knee occasionally locks. PMH: healthy, no bleeding disorder, not on anticoagulants. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: recreational soccer player, nonsmoker, social alcohol, works in IT. Family history: no hemophilia or bleeding disorders, no inflammatory arthritis. He is frustrated that prior workups for gout and infection were unrevealing and the effusions keep returning to the same single joint.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man. The right knee has a moderate effusion with mild warmth and diffuse fullness, and boggy synovial thickening is palpable, especially in the suprapatellar pouch. Range of motion is mildly limited at terminal flexion with occasional catching; mild tenderness along the joint line. There is no erythema overlying the joint and no instability. The contralateral knee and all other joints are normal with no synovitis. No skin psoriasis, nail changes, tophi, or petechiae. Distal neurovascular exam intact. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Recurrent monoarticular knee effusions that are repeatedly BLOODY in a patient without trauma or a bleeding disorder should prompt consideration of which condition?
options: Gout as the first step today
Rheumatoid arthritis as the first step today
Reactive arthritis as the first step today
Pigmented villonodular synovitis
stages.explanation: Recurrent, spontaneous, BLOODY (hemorrhagic) effusions confined to a single large joint—typically the knee—in a young adult without trauma, anticoagulation, or a coagulopathy are characteristic of pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS), now termed diffuse-type tenosynovial giant cell tumor. The proliferative, hypervascular, hemosiderin-laden synovium bleeds repeatedly. Gout (option A) causes crystal-laden, not bloody, effusions and usually affects the first MTP. Rheumatoid arthritis (option B here 'RA') is symmetric and polyarticular with inflammatory features, not recurrent monoarticular hemarthrosis. Reactive arthritis (option D) follows infection and is typically oligoarticular with extra-articular features. The hallmark recurrent bloody monoarthritis points to PVNS.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to evaluate for PVNS?
options: Bone scan as the primary diagnosis
Ultrasound for effusion only
MRI of the knee
Plain radiograph only
stages.explanation: MRI is the imaging modality of choice for PVNS: it shows nodular synovial proliferation and characteristic low signal on both T1 and T2 sequences with 'blooming' artifact on gradient-echo sequences due to hemosiderin deposition, and it defines the extent (localized vs diffuse) for surgical planning. Plain radiographs (option A) may show soft-tissue swelling or juxta-articular erosions but are nonspecific and often normal early. Ultrasound (option C) can detect effusion and synovial thickening but cannot reliably characterize hemosiderin or extent. A bone scan (option D) is nonspecific. MRI's sensitivity to hemosiderin makes it both diagnostic and operative-planning tool.

stages.label: MRI & SYNOVIAL FLUID
stages.content:
Arthrocentesis: dark, serosanguineous (blood-tinged) synovial fluid
Synovial fluid WBC: 1,800 /µL, no crystals, Gram stain negative, culture negative
MRI knee: nodular synovial proliferation in the suprapatellar pouch with low T1/T2 signal and prominent 'blooming' artifact on gradient-echo sequences (hemosiderin)
No aggressive bone destruction; small juxta-articular erosion noted
Synovial biopsy (later): hemosiderin-laden macrophages, multinucleated giant cells, and lipid-laden histiocytes consistent with PVNS
CBC, coagulation profile, uric acid: all normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which biopsy findings confirm pigmented villonodular synovitis?
options: Caseating granulomas as the primary diagnosis
Hemosiderin-laden macrophages, multinucleated giant cells,
Monosodium urate crystals as the primary diagnosis
Neutrophilic infiltrate with bacteria as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: PVNS is confirmed histologically by proliferative synovium containing hemosiderin-laden macrophages (the source of the 'pigmented' brown synovium and recurrent bloody effusions), multinucleated giant cells, and lipid-laden (foamy) histiocytes; many cases harbor a CSF1 gene rearrangement driving the proliferation. Monosodium urate crystals (option A) indicate gout. Caseating granulomas (option C) indicate tuberculosis. A neutrophilic infiltrate with bacteria (option D) indicates septic arthritis. The combination of hemosiderin, giant cells, and foamy histiocytes in hyperplastic synovium is pathognomonic, correlating with the MRI hemosiderin blooming and the repeatedly bloody aspirates.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the primary treatment for diffuse pigmented villonodular synovitis of the knee?
options: Surgical synovectomy , with possible adjuvant radiation or CSF1R-targeted therapy for diffuse/recurrent disease
Systemic glucocorticoids alone as initial therapy
Long-term colchicine as initial therapy
Observation, since it resolves spontaneously as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The primary treatment for PVNS is surgical synovectomy to remove the proliferative synovium; diffuse disease often requires extensive (combined arthroscopic and open) synovectomy and has a notable recurrence rate, so adjuvant external-beam or intra-articular radiation, or systemic CSF1R-targeted therapy (e.g., pexidartinib) for diffuse/refractory or unresectable disease, may be added. Colchicine (option A) treats crystal arthropathy, not PVNS. Systemic glucocorticoids alone (option C) do not eradicate the proliferative synovium. Observation (option D) is wrong—PVNS does not spontaneously resolve and untreated diffuse disease can erode cartilage and bone, causing joint destruction. Synovectomy with vigilance for recurrence is the standard.

cases.diagnosis: Pigmented villonodular synovitis (diffuse tenosynovial giant cell tumor)
pearls: Suspect PVNS with recurrent, atraumatic, BLOODY monoarticular effusions—most often the knee—in a young adult without a coagulopathy.

MRI shows nodular synovial proliferation with low T1/T2 signal and 'blooming' on gradient-echo from hemosiderin; biopsy reveals hemosiderin-laden macrophages, giant cells, and foamy histiocytes (often CSF1-driven).
Treatment is surgical synovectomy; diffuse disease recurs frequently and may need adjuvant radiation or CSF1R inhibitors (e.g., pexidartinib).
Untreated PVNS can cause progressive cartilage/bone erosion and joint destruction—it does not resolve on its own.

cases.title: A Swollen, Warm Knee After No Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening pain and swelling in the right knee that began overnight without any injury.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man presents with one day of progressive right knee pain and swelling that began spontaneously while he was sleeping. He describes the joint as tight, warm, and tense, and he can no longer fully bend or straighten it. He denies any trauma, twisting injury, or recent illness. He has severe (factor VIII <1%) hemophilia A diagnosed in infancy and has had numerous joint bleeds, most often in this same knee and both ankles, with chronically reduced range of motion. He was previously on episodic factor VIII concentrate but admits poor adherence to recommended prophylaxis over the past year. He takes no other medications and specifically avoids NSAIDs and aspirin. No known drug allergies. He is a college student, does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and denies illicit drug use. Family history is notable for hemophilia A in a maternal uncle. Review of systems is negative for fever, rash, urethral discharge, prior similar swelling in other joints this week, or recent dental work.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and guarding the right leg. The right knee is markedly swollen, tense, and warm with a large effusion and a positive ballottement of the patella; overlying skin is intact without erythema or puncture wounds. The joint is held in slight flexion and any attempted flexion or extension is exquisitely painful, with a fixed flexion contracture limiting full extension. There is chronic quadriceps atrophy and crepitus with the limited motion attainable. Both ankles show reduced dorsiflexion and bony enlargement from prior bleeds. Distal pulses, capillary refill, sensation, and motor function are intact. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy and no other acutely swollen joints.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with severe hemophilia A and an atraumatic, tense, painful knee effusion, what is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Administer factor VIII replacement promptly to raise factor levels
Perform urgent arthrocentesis to rule out septic arthritis before any treatment
Give a single dose of intravenous ketorolac for pain control as the first step today
Order an MRI of the knee before initiating any therapy as the first step today
stages.explanation: In a patient with severe hemophilia A, a spontaneous tense joint effusion is an acute hemarthrosis until proven otherwise, and the priority is immediate factor VIII replacement, ideally within hours, to limit bleeding and joint damage; treatment should not be delayed for imaging or labs. Arthrocentesis (choice 0) is generally avoided as the initial step because it risks further bleeding and infection and is reserved for cases with strong suspicion of septic arthritis; if needed it must be performed only after factor correction. MRI (choice 2) delays time-critical treatment. Ketorolac (choice 3) is an NSAID that inhibits platelet function and is contraindicated, worsening bleeding in a hemophiliac.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the target factor VIII activity level when treating an acute hemarthrosis of this severity?
options: Raise factor VIII activity to approximately 10–20% of normal
Raise factor VIII activity to 100% of normal and maintain for two weeks
Raise factor VIII activity to approximately 50% of normal
Raise factor VIII activity to approximately 30–40% of normal
stages.explanation: For an acute hemarthrosis, the goal is to raise factor VIII activity to roughly 40–50% of normal; a target of about 50% is appropriate for a significant joint bleed, with repeat dosing as needed until symptoms resolve. Each unit of factor VIII per kilogram raises activity by roughly 2%, so dosing is calculated to reach the target. A target of 10–20% (choice 0) is too low to control a substantial hemarthrosis. A target of 30–40% (choice 1) is at the lower end and generally reserved for very early or mild bleeds. Sustained 100% correction for two weeks (choice 3) is reserved for major surgery or life-threatening bleeding, not a routine joint bleed, and represents unnecessary exposure and cost.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Factor VIII activity (pre-treatment): <1% (severe)
Factor VIII inhibitor (Bethesda assay): negative
Hemoglobin: 13.6 g/dL
WBC: 8.1 ×10³/µL
Platelets: 245 ×10³/µL
PT: 12.4 s (normal)
aPTT: 68 s (prolonged)
CRP: 0.4 mg/dL (normal)
Knee radiograph: large effusion; joint space narrowing, subchondral cysts, squaring of the patella, and widening of the intercondylar notch consistent with chronic hemophilic arthropathy
No lucency or gas suggestive of infection

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The radiograph shows joint space narrowing, subchondral cysts, and widening of the intercondylar notch. Together with the history, what do these chronic findings indicate?
options: Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (pseudogout)
Chronic hemophilic arthropathy from recurrent hemarthroses
Acute septic arthritis with early osseous destruction
Primary osteoarthritis unrelated to his bleeding disorder
stages.explanation: Widening of the intercondylar notch, subchondral cysts, epiphyseal overgrowth, and joint space narrowing in a target joint are classic features of chronic hemophilic arthropathy, caused by repeated hemarthroses leading to synovial iron deposition (hemosiderin), chronic synovitis, and cartilage destruction. Septic arthritis (choice 0) is acute and would show soft tissue swelling and rapid destruction with an elevated CRP/WBC, which are normal here. Primary osteoarthritis (choice 2) would be unusual at age 19 and does not explain the notch widening, which reflects chronic intra-articular bleeding during skeletal growth. CPPD (choice 3) produces chondrocalcinosis (linear cartilage calcification), not these findings, and is a disease of older adults.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After his acute bleed resolves, what is the most important long-term strategy to prevent further joint damage?
options: Regular prophylactic factor VIII to prevent recurrent bleeds
Episodic (on-demand) factor VIII only when bleeds occur
Early total knee arthroplasty regardless of disease activity
Daily low-dose aspirin to improve joint perfusion based on current risk
stages.explanation: Regular prophylaxis—scheduled factor VIII infusions or subcutaneous emicizumab (a factor VIIIa-mimetic bispecific antibody)—is the standard of care to prevent recurrent hemarthroses and the progressive arthropathy they cause, and is far superior to on-demand treatment for joint preservation. This patient's poor prophylaxis adherence drove his recurrent bleeds. Episodic treatment alone (choice 0) does not prevent the cumulative joint damage of subclinical and recurrent bleeds. Aspirin (choice 2) is contraindicated because it impairs platelet function and increases bleeding. Total knee arthroplasty (choice 3) may eventually be needed for end-stage arthropathy but is not a preventive strategy and is reserved for severe, refractory disease.

cases.diagnosis: Acute hemarthrosis with chronic hemophilic arthropathy in severe hemophilia A
pearls: In severe hemophilia, treat a spontaneous tense joint effusion as a hemarthrosis and give factor replacement immediately—do not delay for imaging or routine arthrocentesis.
Target ~50% factor VIII activity for a significant hemarthrosis; reserve sustained 100% correction for surgery or life-threatening bleeds.
Widening of the intercondylar notch, subchondral cysts, and epiphyseal overgrowth are radiographic hallmarks of chronic hemophilic arthropathy.
Regular prophylaxis with factor VIII or emicizumab—not on-demand therapy—is the key to preventing recurrent bleeds and progressive joint destruction; never give NSAIDs or aspirin.

cases.title: Aching Knuckles in a Middle-Aged Man
cases.chiefComplaint: About a year of stiffness and aching in the knuckles of both hands.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents with roughly 12 months of progressive aching and stiffness in the large knuckles of both hands, worse with gripping tools at his job as a carpenter. Morning stiffness lasts about 15 minutes. He denies joint swelling that comes and goes, but has noticed the knuckles look knobby. He reports several months of fatigue, decreased libido, and his wife notes he looks more tanned than usual despite little sun exposure. He has type 2 diabetes diagnosed two years ago that has been difficult to control, and mild hypertension. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, and acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. He drinks two to three beers most evenings, does not smoke, and uses no illicit drugs. Family history is notable for a brother with "liver problems" and a father who died of liver cancer. Review of systems is positive for occasional joint aches in the wrists and knees and mild dyspnea on exertion, and negative for rash, dry eyes, photosensitivity, alcohol bingeing, or jaundice.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing with a notably bronze-gray skin tone, most prominent on sun-exposed and flexural areas. Hand exam shows firm bony enlargement and tenderness of the second and third metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints bilaterally with mildly reduced grip, but no warmth, soft boggy synovitis, or erythema. The distal and proximal interphalangeal joints are relatively spared. Wrists and knees have mild crepitus. The liver edge is palpable 2 cm below the costal margin and is firm and nontender; there is no splenomegaly, ascites, or spider angiomata. Testicular volume appears mildly reduced. Cardiac exam is regular without murmurs; lungs are clear. There is no synovial swelling of the small joints and no rheumatoid nodules.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of bony second and third MCP arthropathy, bronze skin, diabetes, fatigue, and hepatomegaly should most prompt evaluation for which systemic disorder?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis as the first step today
Hereditary hemochromatosis with iron overload
Wilson disease as the first step today
Primary osteoarthritis from occupational overuse
stages.explanation: The constellation of arthropathy preferentially affecting the second and third MCP joints, bronze hyperpigmentation, diabetes ("bronze diabetes"), fatigue, hypogonadism, and hepatomegaly is the classic multisystem picture of hereditary hemochromatosis from iron overload. Rheumatoid arthritis (choice 0) causes symmetric soft, boggy synovitis with prolonged morning stiffness and would not explain the skin pigmentation, diabetes, or liver findings. Wilson disease (choice 2) presents earlier (often <40) with neuropsychiatric symptoms, Kayser-Fleischer rings, and low ceruloplasmin, not bronze skin and MCP arthropathy. Occupational osteoarthritis (choice 3) could explain isolated hand complaints but cannot account for the systemic features (skin, diabetes, hepatomegaly, hypogonadism) pointing to a unifying metabolic disease.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which initial laboratory tests are most appropriate to screen for the suspected iron-overload disorder?
options: Anti-CCP antibody and rheumatoid factor
Fasting transferrin saturation
Total iron-binding capacity alone
Serum iron alone as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The recommended initial screen for hemochromatosis is fasting transferrin saturation (serum iron divided by TIBC) together with serum ferritin; a transferrin saturation ≥45% (often higher) with elevated ferritin warrants HFE genetic testing. Serum iron alone (choice 0) is insufficient and fluctuates. TIBC alone (choice 2) is only one component of the saturation calculation and cannot screen by itself. Anti-CCP and RF (choice 3) evaluate for rheumatoid arthritis, which the systemic features argue against; even if mildly positive they would not establish the iron-overload diagnosis driving this presentation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Transferrin saturation (fasting): 78% (ref <45%)
Serum ferritin: 1240 ng/mL (ref 30–300)
Fasting glucose: 168 mg/dL
HbA1c: 8.4%
AST: 64 U/L  ALT: 71 U/L
Total testosterone: low
HFE gene testing: homozygous for C282Y mutation
Anti-CCP: negative   Rheumatoid factor: negative
Hand radiographs: joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis at the second and third MCP joints with characteristic hook-like osteophytes on the radial aspect of the metacarpal heads; scattered chondrocalcinosis

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The hand radiographs show hook-like osteophytes at the second and third MCP heads with chondrocalcinosis. Which crystal arthropathy is classically associated with hemochromatosis and explains the chondrocalcinosis?
options: Calcium oxalate deposition as the immediate next step
Basic calcium phosphate (hydroxyapatite) disease
Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease
Monosodium urate deposition (gout) in this setting
stages.explanation: Hemochromatosis predisposes to calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (CPPD), which produces chondrocalcinosis (linear calcification of cartilage, often in the wrist triangular fibrocartilage, knee menisci, and symphysis pubis) and can cause acute pseudogout attacks; this association, along with hook-shaped osteophytes at the second and third MCP joints, is a classic radiographic clue to underlying iron overload and should trigger iron-studies screening. Gout (choice 0) results from monosodium urate and produces tophi and erosions with overhanging edges, not chondrocalcinosis. Basic calcium phosphate disease (choice 2) is linked to large-joint destruction (e.g., Milwaukee shoulder) rather than the MCP/chondrocalcinosis pattern here. Calcium oxalate deposition (choice 3) is seen in primary oxalosis or end-stage renal disease, not hemochromatosis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given his C282Y homozygosity and ferritin >1000 ng/mL with elevated transaminases, which complication carries the greatest long-term mortality risk and warrants targeted surveillance?
options: Adrenal insufficiency
Progressive hand osteoarthritis
Cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma
Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease
stages.explanation: Hepatic iron overload can progress to cirrhosis, and cirrhotic hemochromatosis patients carry a markedly increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma—the leading cause of disease-specific mortality—so those with ferritin >1000 ng/mL, elevated transaminases, hepatomegaly, or evidence of fibrosis warrant hepatic assessment (often MR-based iron quantification, elastography, or liver biopsy) and, if cirrhotic, lifelong HCC surveillance with periodic ultrasound (with or without alpha-fetoprotein). His family history of liver cancer and his bronze skin with hepatomegaly reinforce this risk. Hand osteoarthritis (choice 0) causes morbidity but is not life-threatening. CPPD (choice 2) causes joint symptoms, not mortality. Adrenal insufficiency (choice 3) is not a typical feature; iron deposition affects the anterior pituitary (causing hypogonadotropic hypogonadism) and pancreas, but the dominant lethal organ complication is hepatic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for this patient's underlying iron-overload disorder?
options: Strict avoidance of all dietary protein
Lifelong oral iron supplementation
Therapeutic phlebotomy to deplete iron stores
Methotrexate to control the arthropathy
stages.explanation: Therapeutic phlebotomy is the mainstay of treatment for hereditary hemochromatosis: regular blood removal depletes excess iron, with the goal of reducing ferritin to roughly 50–100 ng/mL, which can improve fatigue, skin pigmentation, and slow organ damage; iron chelation (e.g., deferasirox) is reserved for those who cannot tolerate phlebotomy. Iron supplementation (choice 1) is exactly wrong and would worsen overload. Methotrexate (choice 2) treats inflammatory arthritis but does not address iron overload, and the hand disease here is a degenerative/CPPD-type arthropathy. Protein avoidance (choice 3) has no role; patients should limit alcohol, vitamin C supplements, and iron-rich foods/supplements, but eliminating protein is incorrect.

pearls: Arthropathy favoring the second and third MCP joints with hook-like osteophytes is a classic radiographic clue to hemochromatosis.
Screen with fasting transferrin saturation and ferritin; a saturation ≥45% with elevated ferritin should prompt HFE genetic testing.
Chondrocalcinosis and pseudogout (CPPD) are strongly associated with hemochromatosis and may be the presenting joint feature.
Therapeutic phlebotomy is first-line to deplete iron; the arthropathy may persist or progress even after iron normalization.

cases.title: A Hot, Swollen Foot That Doesn't Hurt
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of a red, swollen, warm right foot that is surprisingly not very painful.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents with three weeks of progressive swelling, warmth, and redness of the right midfoot. He is bothered mostly by the appearance and by difficulty fitting into his shoe; the foot aches only mildly, far less than he would expect for how swollen it looks. He recalls no specific injury but walks long distances at work. Over the past few days he feels the arch of the foot has "dropped" and the foot looks misshapen. He has type 2 diabetes of 14 years with known peripheral neuropathy, diabetic retinopathy, and chronic kidney disease. Medications are insulin glargine, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin, and gabapentin 300 mg three times daily for neuropathic pain. No known drug allergies. He is a warehouse worker, former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), and drinks rarely. Family history includes diabetes in both parents. Review of systems is negative for fever, chills, drainage, recent foot wound or ulcer, chest pain, or dyspnea, and positive for longstanding numbness and tingling in both feet.
cases.exam: The right foot is diffusely swollen, erythematous, and warm to touch compared with the left, with a notably flattened medial longitudinal arch giving a "rocker-bottom" contour. There is gross bony deformity and crepitus on gentle manipulation of the midfoot but only mild tenderness despite the dramatic appearance. The skin is intact with no ulcer, sinus tract, or drainage. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable and bounding. Monofilament testing reveals absent protective sensation across both forefeet, and ankle reflexes are absent bilaterally. When the leg is elevated for several minutes, the erythema substantially diminishes. The contralateral foot shows similar neuropathy but no deformity or swelling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A diabetic with dense peripheral neuropathy develops a warm, swollen, deformed but minimally painful foot with intact skin. Which diagnosis best fits?
options: Deep vein thrombosis of the calf
Osteomyelitis from a contiguous ulcer
Charcot (neuropathic) arthropathy
Acute gouty arthritis of the midfoot
